observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor: a. Require the GAD Focal Point System to prepare the GAD Agenda in accordance with the mandate of 4.C.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular 2019-04; and b. Empower the GAD Focal Point System to enhance their knowledge and build their competencies on GAD-related laws and international commitments, gender mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender-responsive planning and budgeting, and GAD-related tools among others.
observations.managementAction: They have coordinated with the Regional GAD Resource Pool to conduct trainings and workshops in 2024. The GFPS Executive Committee and TWG has undergone the first part of the series of intensive training/workshop towards the formulation of the GAD Agenda with planning period for CYs 2025-2028 last April 21-23, 2025 facilitated by the PCW GAD Resource Pool. Succeeding trainings/workshops is scheduled on the third quarter of the year.
observations.reason: GAD Agenda is yet to be prepared as they are aiming to formulate the GAD Agenda for the years 2025 to 2028 which is in concurrence with the incoming administration. The planned trainings and workshops were not conducted during the year due to conflicts of schedule with the resource person.

observations.recommendation: We praised the effort of the concerned Department Heads on the partial compliance made on the transfer of properties from its registry to their respective accounts. observations.reason: Considering the actions taken by Management, this is for preparation of RTS, hence the recommendation is no longer doable. Some infrastructure assets had been replaced or destroyed and records were lost (e.g., Typhoon Glenda).

observations.recommendation: We recommended that HGDG test be administered again at the end of project implementation and incorporate the results in determining the percentage of PPA expenditures attributable to GAD.

observations.reason: HGDG training is yet to be conducted; majority of TWG members have not undergone such training. The training is scheduled on the 3rd quarter of 2025 in preparation for GAD Plan and Budget 2027.

observations.managementAction:
Implemented Notice to Liquidate (NTL) is initially sent to AOs who fail to liquidate on time. After which, a Demand Letter (DL) is sent to AOs requiring immediate liquidation of their accountabilities. If the AO still fails to liquidate, a Final Demand Letter (FDL) is sent as a final notice to liquidate, otherwise a Notice to Salary Deduction (NSD) is sent to the AOs informing them that the unliquidated balances shall be deducted from their salaries in tranches. However, review of documents revealed that although such collection structure is in place, the same is not consistently practiced since only NTLs are sent to AOs, while the subsequent communication letters (DL, FDL and NSD) are sent only when the Accounting personnel has available time to initiate the same.

The LGU is reminded that allowing cash advances to remain unliquidated for extended periods increases the risk that the documents needed for liquidation will be misplaced, or lost along the process.

In a letter dated June 18, 2025, the Municipal Accountant assured that they are working hard to meet the audit recommendations pertaining to cash advances and informed that adjustments had already been made in the misclassified accounts and negative balance. She also informed that they will avoid granting cash advances near year-end to prevent build-up of unliquidated cash advances as at year-end. She also commented that they are stepping-up the monitoring of liquidation notices, demand letters and salary deductions. However, they are having trouble locating some individuals and there were times, when located, refused to acknowledge their outstanding cash advances.

In addition, she added that with regard to the receivables from the deceased employees, the necessary death certificates had already been gathered except for the former Municipal Accountant, for its eventual request for an authority to write-off to the Commission on Audit.
observations.reason: Procedural lapses on the grant, liquidation, accounting and monitoring of cash advances still persisted during CY 2024 due to Management’s disregard of the applicable rules and regulations.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Accountant to:

a. On the absence of documents on loans receivable:
• Exert more efforts in locating the documents/records pertaining to the loans granted to establish the validity, legality and rights over the claims against the debtors;

b. On the provision of receivables impairment, and the dormant and non-moving accounts:
• Conduct periodic and thorough monitoring, review and analysis of the outstanding balances of the Receivables, especially at year-end, to test whether or not such financial assets are impaired; and
• Conduct detailed collectability assessment and issue a policy on the impairment of receivables;

c. On the unaccounted settlements for final and executory disallowances:
• Submit to the Audit Team: (i) the copy of Official Receipts upon refund/payment/settlement by the persons liable; (ii) Employee’s payslip and payroll showing the amounts deducted, and Accountant’s Certification, for settlements made through salary deduction; and (iii) Certification of Accounts Affected every time there is settlement; and

d. Effect the necessary adjusting journal entries to present fairly the financial statements as of year-end.
observations.managementAction:
Not Retrieval of documents/records pertaining to the loans granted to establish the validity and legality of the claims against the debtors; Implemented

The Municipal Accountant recognized allowance for impairment at a 100 per cent level totaling P474,348.22 during CY 2024 for the following receivables: Loans and Receivable - TPLP-GF P456,000.00; Other Receivables - Agripino Aringa P13,348.22; Cash advances to Philippine National Police personnel for intelligence and confidential expenses P5,000.00. The provision for impairment was applied to the accounts based solely on the prior year’s audit recommendation, without supporting analysis or documentation showing a realistic assessment of the debtors’ capacity to pay or any attempt at recovery. The Accountant informed that the detailed collectability assessment and drafting of a policy for impairment measurement for receivables are still under process.

In a letter dated June 19, 2025, the Municipal Accountant reiterated that the amount of P13,781,337.69 without supporting documents, took place way back 2010 and the only record left was the aggregate amount in the general ledger and the absence of supporting documentation precluded the creation of individual/employee ledgers. Nevertheless, she assured that a diligent search for the necessary vouchers will be conducted although this process may require a considerable time. She also commented that they will commence analyzing and monitoring of receivables to formulate a comprehensive policy for impairment.

With respect to the disallowance, she committed to actively pursue the retrieval of the missing documents as payroll records from 2015-2019, although certain records remain incomplete, had already been retrieved.

Lastly, the Municipal Accountant assured that they are trying to find ways to comply with the audit recommendations and will prepare and effect the necessary adjustments needed and committed to inform/update the Audit Team of the developments.
observations.reason: Retrieval of documents/records pertaining to the loans granted to establish the validity pertaining to the loans was not facilitated due to lack of manpower. The provision for impairment was not based on a comprehensive collectability assessment. Documents evidencing settlements made during CYs 2015-2019 remained unsubmitted as the Accounting Office was constrained to retrieve them from their stock room.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor to implement cost-effective measures, and prudence in handling government funds, pursuant to Section 2 of PD No. 1445, to maintain its ability to meet current obligations and require the Municipal Accountant to: (a) effect the necessary adjusting entries to fairly present the affected accounts in the financial statements; (b) practice prudence in the review of transactions before the same are recorded in the books of accounts to avoid accounting errors; and (c) exhaust all means to retrieve the supporting documents of the unverifiable liabilities for proper accounting thereof.
observations.managementAction:
Not Adjusting journal entries were effected during CY 2024 to recognize (a) reversion to accumulated surplus/(deficit) and (b) other adjustments. Implemented

In a letter dated June 19, 2025, the Municipal Accountant commented that an adjustment pertaining to the P15,150.00 had already been effected in April 2025. Moreover, she added that they are undertaking efforts to identify liabilities from prior years that currently lack supporting documentation. While this poses verification challenges, she assured that efforts are underway to locate the relevant records as they are dedicating resources to search for the necessary documentation.
observations.reason: The inability to properly account for the unverifiable liabilities was due mainly to lack of manpower who will devote their time to retrieve the documents from their store room. The current condition of their store room is also not helpful as documents have already piled up and disorganized. Hence, they have no other option but to direct their limited manpower to tasks/works that will expedite the processing of the LGU’s transactions so as not to hamper the delivery of services to the public and stakeholders.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor exercise his primordial responsibility to require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer in coordination with other concerned municipal employees such as the former Municipal Accountants - Designee to continue exerting efforts in digging up records in order to render once and for all the actual and true status of the financial accounts of the Municipality. Moreover, the responsible officials should be held liable under the Revised Penal Code for their failure to render the accounts and reporting thereof.
observations.managementAction: Not Implemented

The financial reports pertaining to years 1994 to 2002 were still not submitted to the Audit Team due to unavailability of the pertinent documents needed in the preparation of the reports. There were still long outstanding cash advances.



Additional Cash Advances (CAs) to the AOs despite non-liquidation of their previous CAs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LGU observe strictly the prescribed minimum employee’s monthly net take-home pay under Section 47 of the General Provisions of the GAA of 2017.
observations.reason: Still, there were employees with net take home below the minimum.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:

To disclose the details of some line items in the Statement of Cash Flows in the Notes to Combined Financial Statements; and 

Prepare the required Reconciliation of Net Cash Flows from Operating Activities to Surplus/Deficit as required under PPSAS No. 1.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant-Designate failed to disclosure the said details in the Notes to FS and failed to prepare the required Reconciliation of Net Cash Flow from Operating Activities to Surplus/Deficit in CY 2024.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant- Designate to provide schedules or details to support the unliquidated balance and make the necessary disclosure in the Notes to FS.
observations.reason: No disclosure was made in the Notes to FS.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to instruct the (a) City Accountant and City Treasurer to periodically coordinate to determine the existence of stale checks; (b) City Treasurer to send written notices to the concerned payees at least one month before the checks become stale; and (c) City Accountant to take up the adjusting entries for the stale checks in the books.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the (a) City Accountant and City Treasurer to regularly reconcile reciprocal data on RPT and SET Receivables; and (b) City Treasurer to (i) sustain the CTO’s effective revenue collection efforts; and (ii) strategize specifically on how to collect from delinquent taxpayers and, if warranted, enforce the provision of Section 254(b) of RA No. 7160 in the collection of delinquent taxes.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation and the LCE agreed to instruct (a) all accountable officers concerned to liquidate their cash advances immediately; and (b) the CAO to pursue all available actions to enforce the (i) immediate settlement of outstanding cash advances, and (ii) timely liquidation of cash advances that will be granted in the future.

We also recommended that (a) Management refrain from granting additional cash advances to officers and employees unless the previous cash advances given to them are first settled; and (b) the CAO properly record transactions based on the account descriptions provided under the Revised Chart of Accounts.

We commend Management for successfully reconciling PPE records through the concerted efforts of the CAO and GSO personnel. Nonetheless, we still recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the (a) GSO and the CAO to (i) regularly reconcile the RPCPPE and GL balances; (ii) submit a request for write-off of the ₱1.690 million unidentified and fully depreciated PPE items availing of the one-time cleansing of PPEs in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006; and (b) GSO to (i) ensure that accountabilities on the City’s properties are well established and documented; and (ii) facilitate the registration with the LTO of the 110 identified vehicles with expired registrations.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the (a) City Engineer to (i) properly coordinate the completion of projects to the Accounting Unit to facilitate the necessary transfer of the CIP to the appropriate asset accounts for the fair presentation of balances in the financial statements; and (b) City Accountant to (i) record the necessary adjusting entries as mentioned above; and (ii) strictly comply with the recording requirements provided in Section 50 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended and the LCE agreed through the (a) CHRMO to ensure the continued updating of the leave records of the City’s employees to maintain accuracy and compliance with COA Circular No. 2024-009, dated July 16, 2024; and (b) CAO to update the provisions for earned leave credits on a quarterly basis in accordance with the recognition and measurement principles outlined in the circular.

We recommended and the LCE agreed through the City Accountant to ensure (a) full compliance with the disclosure requirements provided under IPSAS 3; and (b) that current-year expenses from prior-year prepayments are recorded in the current period, rather than adjusted against prior periods.

We recommended and the LCE agreed through the City Engineer and the Local Development Council to (a) ensure that all DF projects are implemented alongside certain requirements such as necessary ECC, turnover documents and other important requisites; and (b) sustain the generally high rate of project implementation.

We recommended and the LCE agreed through the SPMT to strictly require the PCs to (a) register with the SEC and BIR to comply with the regulatory requirements; (b) open bank accounts under the name of their respective PCs; (c) issue BIR receipts upon receipt of payment from the members; (d) update the monthly reading/collection report form using the attached suggested form in Appendix E to properly show the arrears, current month due, payment and balance of the members; and (e) timely submit the monthly financial reports.

We further recommended and the LCE agreed through the City Legal Officer, in coordination with the City Administrator, Liga of Barangays, and PCOs, to review and amend the EO No. 26-A or the POWAS Project Policy guidelines that would (a) consider the transfer of the POWAS to the concerned barangays through the integration of current PCOs into the barangay government structure; (b) include separate provision for sanctions in case of non-compliance with the Project Policy Guidelines such as revocation of grant or authority to manage the POWAS or termination of the erring officers; and (c) mandate the monthly submission of financial reports of the PCs to the SPMT.

We recommended and the LCE agreed through the CDRRMO and City Administrator to (a) ensure that PPAs are fully implemented and are identified based on reasonable needs assessment with the view of attaining efficiency in the use of government resources; and (b) monitor the capability of the City to respond to and prepare for disasters.

We recommended and the LCE agreed through the (a) Department Heads (i) maintain adherence to the terms and conditions outlined in current and future fund transfers from other NGAs; and (ii) ensure the full utilization of fund transfers through proper planning and coordination in order to maximize their intended benefits and achieve program objectives effectively; and (b) City Treasurer to facilitate the reversion of unutilized balance of the LGSF-COVID-19 Fund to the National Treasury.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the City Treasurer to (a) commence the review and update of the City Revenue Code; and (b) continue the advances made in business tax assessments and collections viewed vis-à-vis an updated Revenue Code and pertinent provisions of the Local Government Code.

We recommended and the LCE agreed through the HRMO and the heads of offices concerned to (a) require that all future JO contracts explicitly define the specific duties and responsibilities of the personnel being engaged, consistent with the service requirements of the hiring office; (b) ensure that accomplishment reports of JOs are reviewed and validated against their clearly defined duties before processing wage payments; and (c) prioritize the filling of existing plantilla vacancies to reduce dependency on JO/COS workers and avoid potential duplication of functions.

We recommended and the LCE agreed through the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) to (a) improve gender mainstreaming by complying with the basic requirements of GAD institutional mechanism and processes; and (b) ensure the complete implementation of activities indicated in the GPB to maximize the intervention in addressing gender-issues in the City.

We further recommended and the LCE agreed (a) to issue appropriate policy directives to disseminate and implement the GPB for the succeeding Fiscal Years, in compliance with Item C.7 of JMC No. 2013-01, as amended by Item 6.0 of JMC No. 2016-01; through the (b) City Budget Officer to ensure that PPAs under GPB were completely incorporated into the AIP and Annual Budget; and (c) GFPS to ensure that Annual GAD AR shall be accompanied by the reports and issuances required under Item C.8 (5) of the same JMC.

We recommended and the LCE agreed through the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to continue to comply with the BIR rules and regulations on the withholding and remittance of taxes on compensation, value added taxes, percentage taxes and expanded taxes.

We recommended and the LCE agreed through the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to ensure full compliance with RA No. 8291 or the GSIS Act of 1997 by (a) continuing to withhold GSIS premiums and loan amortizations from employees’ compensation and remitting the same together with the government’s share to the GSIS; (b) ensuring timely and full remittance of all contributions to the GSIS, including any unremitted balances from prior years; (c) regularly reconciling records with GSIS to prevent accumulation of unpaid obligations; and (d) promptly addressing any variances or delays to avoid penalties and protect the benefits and contributions of its officials and employees.

We recommended and the LCE agreed through the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to continue to comply with the requirements of RA No. 9679 or the HDMF law of 2009 on the withholding of contributions and loan amortizations from employees’ compensation and their remittance to the HDMF together with the government’s share.

We recommended and the LCE agreed through the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to continue to comply with RA No. 7875 as amended by R.A. Nos. 9241 and 10606 or the National Health Insurance Act of 2013 on the withholding of contributions from employees’ compensation and the remittance thereof together with the government’s share to PHIC.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the (a) persons liable to immediately settle the remaining balance of the disallowance; and (b) officials concerned to henceforth observe pertinent rules and regulations in the discharge of their fiscal functions to avoid disallowances on the use of government funds.

We recommended, and the Municipal Accountant agreed, to ensure the timely
remittance of withheld taxes to the BIR, in strict compliance with the deadlines prescribed
under the BIR RR No. 11-2018.

We recommended, and the Municipal Treasurer agreed, to:
a) Require the AF Custodian to maintain the Permanent Record Book of AFs and
ensure that all columns are properly labeled and filled out, with remarks on the
status of AFs for proper monitoring; and
b) Instruct the AO to:
i. Make certain that all RPT collections are remitted to the Municipal Treasurer
at the close of each business day;
ii. Cause the necessary adjustments on errors noted in the cashbooks; and
iii. Exercise due diligence in recording and reconciling transactions to prevent
recurring errors and ensure the completeness of reports.

We recommended, and the Management agreed, to reassess the bond requirements for
AOs and increase the bond amounts to adequately cover the maximum cash accountabilities
of each officer, in accordance with the schedule of cash accountability and bond requirements
set by the Bureau of the Treasury.

We recommended, and the Municipal Treasurer agreed, to ensure that only checks
actually released on the date of the report are included in the RCI. This will ensure accurate
recording of transactions in the Check Disbursement Journal by the Accounting Office.

We recommended, and the:
a) Municipal Treasurer agreed, to furnish the Accounting Office with a duly signed,
certified list of taxpayers and their property tax dues at the beginning of each year,
in accordance with Section 20 of Manual for LGU-NGAS;
b) Municipal Accountant agreed, to conduct a thorough investigation of the
discrepancies noted and prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct
the overstated balances of RPT and SET receivables in the books;
c) Accounting and Treasury Offices agreed, to coordinate closely to regularly
compare and reconcile their respective records of RPT and SET receivables, and
ensure that only the current year’s collectible accounts are recorded as receivables,
excluding prior years’ delinquencies to avoid duplication and overstatement.

We recommended, and:
a) The Municipal Accountant agreed, to require the immediate liquidation of the
outstanding cash advance for travel amounting to ₱91,045.65, which has remained
unliquidated beyond the prescribed period;
b) The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer agreed, to (i) provide a
written explanation and/or justification for the processing and approval of the DV,
and the release of the corresponding check for the cash advance, despite the
absence of an approved budgetary appropriation for foreign travel; and (ii) ensure
strict compliance with existing laws and regulations by verifying that all future
cash advances are properly authorized, supported by an approved budget, and in
accordance with COA rules, prior to approval and release; and
c) The Municipal Accountant agreed, to use the Advances to Special Disbursing
Officer account in recording cash advances intended for special purpose or time-
bound undertakings as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended, and the:
a) Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed, to authorize the creation of multiple Inventory
Committees to conduct semestral physical counts of inventories and submit the
resulting RPCI to the Audit Team and the MAO no later than July 31 and January 31
for the first and second semesters, respectively; and
b) MAO and designated Supply Officer agreed, to enhance existing systems and
procedures to ensure accurate recording of inventory movements, proper maintenance
of SLCs and SCs, and assignment of accountability for effective monitoring and
reconciliation of inventory balances in accordance with aforementioned guidelines;
c) Concerned offices agreed, to strictly comply with the submission of monthly
inventory issuance reports to the Accounting Office to ensure accurate and timely
recording of inventory transactions.

We recommended, and the Municipal Accountant agreed, to:
a) Prepare the necessary adjusting entries to close the previously recognized accumulated
depreciation for Communication Equipment and Other Property, Plant, and
Equipment items that were reclassified to semi-expendable property, pursuant to COA
Circular No. 2024-006. This will ensure that no negative PPE balances are carried in
the books, thus upholding the accuracy of reported asset values;
b) Determine and retrospectively recognize depreciation expenses for Other Land
Improvements, Power Supply Systems, Parks, Plazas and Monuments, and Other
Infrastructure Assets using the estimated useful life of PPE items under COA Circular
No. 2003-007. In the absence of guidance for specific assets, the Municipality may
adopt reasonable estimates based on historical experience or benchmarking from
other LGUs;
c) Coordinate with the Inventory Committee and Property Custodian to conduct a
comprehensive physical count of all PPE items, regardless of acquisition year, and
submit a complete RPCPPE on or before January 31 of each year.

We further recommended, and the LCE, through the Municipal Accountant and
Property Custodian, agreed to fully implement the one-time cleansing of PPE account
balances pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006 to ensure accurate PPE balances and
enhance the reliability of financial reports.

We recommended, and the Municipal Accountant agreed, to:
(i) update and maintain the subsidiary records for all CIP accounts to reflect the accurate
status of each project; and (ii) adjust PPE accounts to recognize completed projects, ensuring
that the CIP account contains only the accumulated costs of ongoing or partially completed
infrastructure projects.

We further recommended, and the Municipal Engineering Office agreed, to submit
quarterly status reports on all infrastructure projects to the Accounting Office to facilitate
regular reconciliation and the timely reclassification of completed project.

We recommended and the:
a) Bids and Awards Committee agreed, to: (i) submit the lacking procurement
documentation noted above so that these transactions may be subjected to
further audit evaluation; and (ii) strengthen internal controls by implementing
a systematic review process for DVs to ensure all transactions have complete
and proper documentation before approval;
b) Municipal Engineering Office agreed, to: (i) direct contractors to repair the
identified defects within the warranty period, in accordance with the contract
terms and conditions; and (ii) strengthen monitoring and supervision during
project implementation by requiring the MEO and concerned personnel to
conduct regular inspections, especially at critical stages of construction; and
c) Municipal Accountant agreed, to: (i) ensure the proper application of
accounting policies and procedures in the recording of fund transfers,
esspecially distinguishing between General and Trust Funds, in accordance
with the MNGAS for LGUs and prepare; and (ii) record the necessary
adjusting journal entries to reclassify the erroneously recorded collections
from the Due to NGAs – GF account to their appropriate liability accounts,
such as Due to LGUs – GF, Guaranty Deposits Payable – GF, and Due to NGAs
– TF.

We recommended, and the:
a) Municipal Accountant agreed, to (i) adopt a methodology based on
historical data to estimate the portion of leave credits expected to be used
or paid within 12 months and classify them as current liabilities, and the
remaining accumulated leave benefits, expected to be payable upon
separation, be recognized as long-term liabilities in compliance with
IPSAS 39; and (ii) recognize leave benefits yearly as they are earned,
rather than recording the entire liability in a single year; and (iii) prepare
the necessary adjusting journal entries to record the accrued leave benefits
under the Leave Benefits Payable account.
b) Municipal Budget Officer agreed, to establish an Employee Benefits
Reserve Fund on a yearly basis to ensure sufficient cash reserves for future
monetizations;
c) Human Resource Department agreed, to coordinate with the MAO and
Municipal Budget Office to ensure adherence to Rule XVI of the Omnibus
Rules Implementing Book V of EO No. 292 on the yearly accounting and
funding of leave benefits.

We recommended, and the MAO agreed, to: (i) reverse the ₱5,347,902.00
adjustment entries made to the Prior Period Adjustment account and restore the
corresponding balances to the affected inventory accounts—Drugs and Medicines
Inventory and Other Supplies and Materials Inventory—until complete and valid
supporting documents (such as inventory adjustment reports, consumption reports, or
distribution lists) are submitted and validated; and (ii) moving forward, refrain
from using the Prior Period Adjustment account for undocumented inventory
reductions or similar adjustments. Any such adjustments should only be made
following proper verification, reconciliation, and submission of complete supporting
documentation.

We recommended and the:
a) Municipal Engineering Office agreed, to: (i) require contractors to repair the
identified defects within the warranty period, in accordance with the contract
terms and conditions; and (ii) strengthen monitoring and supervision during
project implementation by requiring the Engineering Office and concerned
personnel to conduct regular and systematic inspections, especially at critical
stages of construction.
b) Bids and Awards Committee agreed, to: (i) submit the lacking procurement
documentation noted above so that these transactions may be subjected to
further audit evaluation; and (ii) strengthen internal controls by implementing
a systematic review process for DVs to ensure all transactions have complete
and proper documentation before approval;
c) Municipal Accountant agreed, to: (i) ensure the proper application of
accounting policies and procedures in the recording of fund transfers,
esspecially distinguishing between General and Trust Funds, in accordance
with the MNGAS for LGUs and prepare; and (ii) record the necessary
adjusting journal entries to reclassify the erroneously recorded collections
from the Due to NGAs – GF account to their appropriate liability accounts,
such as Due to LGUs – GF, Guaranty Deposits Payable – GF, and Due to NGAs
– TF.

Also, we reiterated our prior audit recommendations, and the management
agreed, to: i) indicate clear and specific implementation timelines in both the LDIP
and AIP to support timely execution and completion of development projects; and ii)
facilitate the submission of advance copies of contracts and their supporting
documents to allow timely audit review.

We recommended, and the MDRRMO agreed, to determine the appropriate
utilization of the unexpended LDRRMF balance and ensure that the identified PPAs
are incorporated into the LDRRMFIP or alternatively, prepare a revised AIP
reflecting the identified PPAs subject to the approval of the local sanggunian and LCE
in accordance with Item 6.5 of the NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1.

We further recommended, and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and
the Municipal Accountant agreed, to (i) coordinate with the concerned offices to
facilitate the submission of the required supporting documents; and (ii) provide
appropriate clarification or justification for the audit exception, particularly the
discrepancy in the ABC indicated in the PhilGEPS Bid Notice Abstract and the actual
contract amount.

We recommended, and the GFPS agreed, to: a) ensure timely submission of
the GPB and GAD AR to the Audit Team in accordance with COA Circular No. 2014-
001; b) continue to be capacitated on GAD, particularly on the use of gender
analysis/assessment tools such as GMEF, GeRL Assessment Tool, HGDG, and
GFAsT; and c) incorporate in the succeeding GPBs the results of gender
analysis/assessment using the prescribed GAD tools.

We recommended, and the Municipal Treasurer agreed, to:
i) conduct a comprehensive examination of the books and records of business
entities within the Municipality to ascertain, assess, and collect the correct
amount of the business taxes in accordance with Section 171 of the R.A.
No. 7160; and
ii) prepare a stratified schedule PIL to approximate the gross receipt of each
business classification as required under Section 2A. 03. of the
Municipality’s Revised Revenue Code, or while the revision of the
Municipal Revenue Code is still pending, publicize the current methods
used in determining the presumptive income level of businesses to ensure
that the taxpayers are properly informed.

We recommended, and the Officer-in-Charge (OIC) of the MENRO agreed,
to: (i) coordinate closely with the EMB to expedite the official turnover of the
shredding and decomposing machines, including consistent follow-up on the
execution of the required turnover documents; and (ii) consider conducting a
refresher orientation for concerned personnel to ensure that designated operators are
adequately trained on the proper use, handling, and maintenance of the machines.

We also reiterated our recommendation, and the LGU agreed, to strictly
enforce the mandatory use of personal protective equipment by all personnel involved
in waste collection. This may be carried out through the issuance of a formal directive
mandating compliance, along with the regular monitoring of field implementation to
ensure adherence.

We recommended, and the MHRMO, in coordination with the MAO, agreed
to ensure the accuracy of amounts deducted from employees and remitted to the
GSIS, HDMF, and PHIC. Specifically, the following actions are recommended: (a)
implement a more effective monitoring system to ensure the accuracy of
contributions, loan amortizations, and remittances; (b) facilitate regular
reconciliation of records with the concerned agencies to promptly identify and
address discrepancies between the respective records; (c) review and validate carry-
over balances from prior years and secure supporting documentation, or initiate
proper clearing procedures.

We reiterated that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. Municipal Budget Officer to include in the Municipality’s annual budget the
amount of premiums for the general insurance covering all insurable properties,
pursuant to COA Circular No. 2018-002; and

b. Municipal General Services Officer to insure with the GIF of the GSIS all of the
Municipality’s insurable assets and properties with the appropriate insurance
coverage, pursuant to RA No. 656 and COA Circular No. 2018-002.

We reiterated that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MDRRMO and Municipal Planning
and Development Coordinator (MPDC) to:

a. Submit within 30 days from receipt hereof the Municipality’s plan for the
implementation of the unutilized balance of LDRRMF amounting to
P20,255,673.30; and

b. Evaluate and review the LDRRMF Investment Plan with emphasis on the
proper identification and selection of specific priority programs/projects/activities (PPAs)
to accelerate their implementation and judiciously plan on how to significantly increase the
utilization of the LDRRM Fund to achieve the goal/target of the Municipality regarding
disaster relief, recovery, and risk reduction and management.

We likewise recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MDRRMO to prepare
the LDRRMF Investment Plan or LDRRM Plan which includes the list of projects and
activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years and submit a copy to
the COA Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Human Resource and
Management Officer (HRMO), in coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer, to
review the existing policy on the hiring and compensation of JO workers and ensure
that payment is in accordance at least with the latest minimum wage rate issued by the
DOLE, subject to the Municipality’s financial capacity, or if funds are available, a
comparable rate with the regular employees and similar items or positions having
similar rate for fair and equitable compensation.

We reiterated that the Municipal Mayor as the Chairperson of the Executive Committee
require the GAD Focal Point System and the Technical Working Group to:

a. Prepare the GAD Agenda as soon as possible and strictly comply with the
provisions on the preparation of the GAD Agenda to ensure that the PPAs
included in the annual GPB are aligned with it or with the identified gender
issues to be addressed during the three-year term of the LCE;

b. Strictly monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs and conduct regular
coordination with the implementing offices to ensure that programs are
undertaken within the target period; and

c. Prioritize gender mainstreaming efforts in GAD planning and budgeting by
using existing gender analysis tools such as the GMEF, HGDG, and PGA in
the identification, design, implementation, management and monitoring and
evaluation stages of the various PPAs to determine the extent of gender-
responsiveness and amount to be attributed to the GAD budget.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
require/instruct the:

a. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to closely coordinate and
review the unverifiable bank reconciling items to ascertain its nature to facilitate
proper recording of such items in the books of accounts;
b. Municipal Accountant to (i) observe proper cut-off of transactions in recording
collections and deposits by conducting strict and thorough review of RCDs and
its supporting documents before the same are recorded in the books of accounts;
(ii) ensure accurate preparation of BRS at every month-end to avoid
accumulation of unverifiable reconciling items; and (iii) prepare the necessary
adjusting journal entries to fairly present the affected accounts in the financial
statements; and
c. Municipal Treasurer to (i) send written notices to the payees one month before
the check becomes stale; (ii) for stale checks already issued to the payees,
immediately inform them to return the same to the Municipal Treasurer’s
Office, for cancellation and issuance of the necessary check replacements
thereof; (iii) for unclaimed stale checks still in his possession, immediately cancel
the same and furnish the Municipal Accountant with the related original DVs
and supporting documents, to facilitate adjustment in the books; and
(iv) henceforth ensure that all checks issued are properly monitored to avoid
accumulation of stale checks.

During the exit conference, the Municipal Accountant and the representatives from
the Municipal Treasurer’s Office concurred with the audit observations and
recommendations and committed to review and reconcile their records and prepare the
necessary adjusting entries for the fair presentation of the accounts in the Financial
Statements.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor:
(i) ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002
and CSC Memorandum Circular No. 12 s. 2012 on the grant, utilization and
liquidation of cash advances to avoid accumulation of outstanding cash
advances;
(ii) make a formal request for the writing-off of unliquidated cash advances for
deceased AOs supported by the necessary documents prescribed under
COA Circular No. 2016-005; and
(iii) instruct the Municipal Accountant to: (a) continue requiring immediate
liquidation from present employees with outstanding cash advances by
consistently sending demand letters; and (b) coordinate with the HRMO,
concerned government agencies, and individuals connected with the AOs, to
ascertain the home/business/office address of AOs no longer connected with
the LGU, connected with other government agencies, and those with
undetermined status, so that demand letters will be effectively served to
them;
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to: (i) prepare adjusting journal entries to correct the negative account
balance and properly classify the affected accounts; and (ii) conduct detailed
collectability assessment and draft a policy on the impairment measurement for the
accountabilities of the deceased AOs, for fair presentation of the accounts in the
financial statements.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
require the Accountant to:
a. On the absence of documents on loans receivable:
• Exert more efforts in locating the documents/records pertaining to the
loans granted to establish the validity, legality and rights over the claims
against the debtors;
b. On the provision of receivables impairment, and the dormant and non-
moving accounts:
• Conduct periodic and thorough monitoring, review and analysis of the
outstanding balances of the Receivables, especially at year-end, to test
whether or not such financial assets are impaired; and
• Conduct detailed collectability assessment and issue a policy on the
impairment of receivables;
c. On the unaccounted settlements for final and executory disallowances:
• Submit to the Audit Team: (i) the copy of Official Receipts upon
refund/payment/settlement by the persons liable; (ii) Employee’s payslip
and payroll showing the amounts deducted, and Accountant’s
95
Certification, for settlements made through salary deduction; and
(iii) Certification of Accounts Affected every time there is settlement, for
the preparation of NSSDCs; and
d. Effect the necessary adjusting journal entries to present fairly the financial
statements as of year-end.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
require the Municipal Accountant to: (a) effect the necessary adjusting journal entries
to fairly present the affected accounts in the financial statements; (b) practice prudence
in the review of transactions before the same are recorded in the books of accounts to
avoid accounting errors; and (c) exhaust all means to retrieve the supporting
documents of the unverifiable liabilities for proper accounting thereof.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
ensure that sound system of internal control for Inventories and PPE are installed,
implemented and monitored for the fair presentation of the affected accounts in the
financial statements.

We further reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require
the:
a. concerned Offices/Departments having custody of inventories to:
 o conduct a complete physical count of all Inventories and prepare the
 corresponding RPCI following the format prescribed in Annex 48 of the
 NGAS Manual for LGUs and submit the same to the GSO for
 consolidation; and
 o submit to the GSO and Municipal Accountant the certified distribution
 lists of inventories as soon as the same are issued to the ultimate
 users/beneficiaries/stakeholders, as well as the stock status report of
 inventories as of year-end;
b. GSO and the Municipal Accountant to:
 o build up their records of the Inventories and PPE;
 o investigate the discrepancies noted in the audit and effect the necessary
 adjustments in their records; and
 o upon receipt of RPCI and RPCPPE from the Inventory Committee and
 concerned offices/departments, conduct reconciliation between their
 perpetual inventory records;
c. Inventory Committee to:
 o conduct a complete physical count of all Inventories and PPE and
 prepare the corresponding RPCI and RPCPPE based on the perpetual
99
 inventory records of GSO and Accounting Unit; and
 o prepare the IIRUP and request for inspection from COA representative
 and thereafter undertake disposal proceedings for the unserviceable
 properties; and
d. Municipal Accountant to:
 o ensure strict compliance with the accrual basis of accounting in
 recording receipt, issuance and adjustments, if any, of Inventories; and
 o effect necessary adjusting journal entries to fairly present the affected
 accounts in the financial statements.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor to
require the:
(i) Municipal Treasurer to furnish the Municipal Accountant, at the beginning
of the year, a certified list of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible
for the year for the proper establishment of Real Property and Special
Education Taxes Receivables based on the Real Property Account
Register/Taxpayer’s Index Card;
(ii) Municipal Accountant to: (a) regularly take up in the books of accounts of
the Municipality the total RPT/SET tax dues based on the certified list of
tax due and collectible furnished by the Municipal Treasurer;
(b) coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer for proper reconciliation of
the discrepancy in the recorded balances of RPT and SET Receivables as
against the total amount of tax delinquencies indicated in the Real Property
106
Tax Owners Delinquency Listing and prepare the necessary adjusting
entry to present the correct and reliable balances of RPT and SET
Receivable accounts in the financial statements as at year-end; (c) ensure
that RPT and SET Receivables and are properly recognized only in the
year they were earned and advance payments are reclassified to income in
the year to which they pertain; and (d) analyze the net effect of the
foregoing observations in the financial statements and draw the necessary
adjusting entries in the General and Special Education Fund books to
reflect the correct balance of the affected accounts; and
(iii) Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to analyse and determine
the cause of the deficiencies between their records, prepare reconciliation
and draw the necessary adjusting journal entry, and submit to this Office
the actions taken to establish the correct balances of the subject
receivables.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct
the:
a. Municipal Planning and Development Officer, Municipal Budget Officer,
and the rest of Municipal Development Council to ensure strict compliance
with the provisions of Section 3.2.5 of the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1,
particularly on the expenditures not allowed to be charged against the Fund,
to ensure optimal funds available for development projects;
b. The Human Resource Management Officer, in coordination with the
Municipal Accountant, and the rest of heads of municipal offices to develop
and strictly enforce, clear, standardized operating procedures to improve
overall efficiency, and ensure more streamlined and compliant payroll and
DV process; and
c. The Municipal Accountant to immediately submit the lacking DVs/Payrolls
and supporting documents on the above-noted deficiencies, and henceforth
ensure that the same is complete and properly accomplished before
processing of payment in order to establish the validity, legality, and
propriety of transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. Concerned contractors to correct and rectify the aforementioned deficiencies
at their own expense, and provide pictures and/or proofs of compliance to the
Municipal Accountant for the eventual submission to the Audit Team.
Otherwise, require them to refund the equivalent cost of defects/deficiencies;
and
b. Municipal Engineer to closely monitor the project implementation and
ensure that projects are implemented in accordance with the approved plans and specifications.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant, upon submission of the
required technical documents from the Municipal Engineer, immediately submit the
same to the Audit Team to facilitate further review and evaluation.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
instruct the:
a. BAC and the TWG (a) to ensure that all procurement activities undertaken by the
Municipality are strictly in accordance with the provisions of the updated 2016
Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and Guidelines on the Use of Framework Agreement
by All Procuring Entities as approved by GPPB Resolution no. 27-2019 dated
December 10, 2019; (b) be cautious and exercise prudence in the examination of
eligibility and technical documents submitted by the prospective bidders in faithful
adherence to relevant provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 to prevent
the award of contract to ineligible contractors/suppliers; (c) immediately submit the
lacking bidding documents to the Audit Team to arrive at a decision in audit; and (d)
strictly comply with the prescribed period of furnishing the COA Auditor copies of
contracts including the documentary requirements to facilitate timely review and
evaluation as prescribed by COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009;
b. Municipal Engineer to: (i) ensure compliance with DPWH Department Order No. 197
s. 2016 on the revised guidelines in the preparation of ABC; and (ii) closely monitor
the project implementation and ensure that construction schedules are strictly
followed to ensure that projects are implemented efficiently and effectively in
accordance with the approved plans and specifications within the contract duration; and
c. Municipal Accountant to (i) immediately submit the lacking supporting documents
discussed in the preceding paragraphs, and ensure that all claims are duly supported
130
with proper and complete supporting documents, as required under Section 9.1.3.1 of
COA Circular No. 2012-001, before processing for payment; (ii) cause the
imposition/collection of liquidated damages to concerned suppliers; and (iii) prepare
adjusting entries in the noted errors in recording contract payments in the
Construction in Progress and Advances to Contractors Accounts.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the
(i) Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to expedite the reconciliation
of their records for the prompt transfer of the total unexpended/unutilized balances of
LDRRMF in the Trust Fund books under the Trust Liability-DRRM account and
ensure that the transfer thereof is supported with available cash; and (ii) concerned
officials to provide plausible explanation/justification on the noted discrepancies/
inconsistencies in various supporting documents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, with the assistance of the Local
Development Council, the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator,
maximize the utilization of the 20% DF by including those PPAs that are well-planned
and procurement-and-implementation-ready to expedite the procurement and
implementation of the identified development projects to attain the desirable socio-
economic development and environmental management outcomes of the LGU and for
the intended users/beneficiaries to enjoy the immediate benefits from said development
projects.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor exercise
his primordial responsibility to require the Municipal Accountant and the
Municipal Treasurer in coordination with other concerned municipal employees
such as the former Municipal Accountants - Designee to continue exerting
efforts in digging up records in order to render once and for all the actual and
true status of the financial accounts of the Municipality. Moreover, the
responsible officials should be held liable under the Revised Penal Code for their
failure to render the accounts and reporting thereof.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the
Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to exhaust all means in order to
substantiate the net unreconciled amount of P13,716,773.84. Then, if proven
futile, collate all previously available documents, reports, and correspondences
and consider requesting for authority to write-off for the purpose of fair
presentation in the FS and not for extinguishment of any accountability thereof.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Treasurer to prepare and send a (a) request for copies of bank statements
pertaining to the accounts with non-moving reconciling items in the SEF and
Trust Fund for proper reconciliation; and (b) written communication to the bank
for the error noted in CY 2016 for proper accounting thereof.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor
require the:
a. Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to (i) exert extra effort
in substantiating or locating the source documents of the supposed
receipt of funds amounting to P691,519.00 so that the fair and correct
balance of the CLT account would be presented in the FS; and (ii)
reconcile the balance of CLT reported in the cashbook and in the FS and
ensure that these records are always reconciled;
b. Municipal Treasurer (i) to instruct the collectors to adhere to Section
68(1) of P.D. No. 1445 by issuing official receipts for every
collection/receipt of fund; and (ii) together with the collecting officers to
properly accomplish the RCD in accordance with the instructions
provided in Annex 41 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II; and
c. Municipal Accountant to (i) verify the noted errors in the recording of
collections and prepare the necessary adjustments for fair presentation of
the CLT and other affected accounts in the FS; and (ii) observe proper
cut-off of transactions and ensure that transactions are recorded in the
period they are received/incurred.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended and management agreed that the
Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Accountant to (i) exert extra effort to locate the
records/documents to analyze the nature of non-moving items in the CIP
accounts for proper accounting thereof and fair presentation of the
balances of the affected accounts in the FS; and (ii) prepare and maintain
the PPE Ledger Cards for each category of assets and ensure that these
are reconciled with the PCs maintained by the Property Officer;
b. Municipal Treasurer to prepare the IIRUP for all returned unserviceable
properties to facilitate disposal thereof;
c. Disposal Committee to facilitate the disposal of the unserviceable
properties pursuant to Section 79 of P.D. No. 1445 and in accordance
with the Revised Manual on the Disposal of Government Properties
provided under COA-Department of Budget and Management (DBM)
Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2024-1 dated January 30, 2024;
d. concerned accountable officers to file a request for relief from
accountability for the lost properties pursuant to Section 73 of P.D. No.
1445 and Section 151 of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20,
1992. However, for lost properties which were not filed within the
prescribed period, concerned accountable officers are to pay the
depreciated replacement cost thereof pursuant to Item 7.11.b of COA
Circular No. 2020-006; and
e. Inventory Committee to (i) properly prepare/accomplish the RPCI and
RPCPPE by providing the complete information in the reports; and (ii)
submit the said reports to the Audit Team within the timeframe provided
under Section 124 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

With the foregoing, we reiterated our recommendation with some
revisions and management agreed that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
Municipal Accountant to:
a. exhaust all means to retrieve records or documents to support/establish the
claim of the municipal government against the accountable persons with long
outstanding cash advances;
b. if deemed futile, avail of the remedies set forth by COA on the guidelines
and procedures on the proper disposition of all dormant accounts prescribed
under COA Circular No. 2023-008;
c. continue to send demand letters to all accountable officers to liquidate or
settle immediately their outstanding cash advances, copy furnished the Audit
Team; and
d. ensure that all cash advances are fully liquidated at year-end by requiring the
concerned AO to refund any excess in accordance with Item 5.8 of COA
Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Mayor
instruct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the former Municipal
Accountant - Designee and:
a. prepare properly the SLs of each item in the GL following the
instructions provided in Annex 6 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II
and ensure proper maintenance thereof to be able to prepare and
maintain a Schedule based on the SLs;
b. ensure that the unexpended balances of fund transfers pertaining to
completed projects are returned to the source agency;
c. analyze the items in the Schedules of Inter-Agency Payable accounts that
are for adjustments and prepare the necessary adjustments thereto to
fairly present the balances of the accounts in the FS; and
d. for dormant and unsubstantiated items, consider the remedies set forth
by COA on the guidelines and procedures on the proper disposition of all
dormant accounts prescribed under COA Circular No. 2023-008.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
Municipal Treasurer to continue to adopt strategies (e.g., information campaign,
barangay caravan) to intensify the collection of real property tax and to persuade
taxpayers to take advantage of early payment discount.
We also reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor
instruct the Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the PGR regarding the
share of the LGU in the collection of additional one percent tax on real property
for SEF. Also, instruct the Municipal Accountant to investigate the nature of the
reconciling amount in the beginning balance of RPT and SET Receivables for
proper accounting thereof and prepare an adjusting entry to reclassify the
excess amount downloaded by the PGR to the appropriate income account to
fairly present the balances of affected accounts in the FS.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the
Municipal Assessor (a) together with the Municipal Accountant to complete and
submit to the Audit Team the noted lacking documents; and (b) register the
Deed of Absolute Sale with the Registry of Deeds for the processing of title in
compliance with Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445 and Item 13.1 of COA Circular
No. 2012-001.
We also enjoin the management to process the titling of the land in order to
protect the rights and ownership of the Municipality.

With the foregoing, we recommended and management agreed that the
Municipal Mayor require the BAC Chairperson to (a) submit a written
explanation for the continuous failure to strictly observe the provisions of the
Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and submit the lacking documents mentioned
above; and (b) furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the Contract of Agreement
and its attachments within five working days from the execution of the contract
as required under Item 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001.
For procurements made after the effectivity date (February 25, 2025) of the
IRR of R.A. No. 12009, or the New Government Procurement Act (NGPA), the
management is reminded to observe strictly its provisions and its IRR in the
procurement of infrastructure projects to ensure adherence to the principles of
transparency, competitiveness and public monitoring.
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Engineer to (a) submit all documents relative to the time
extensions granted to the contractors for proper validation/evaluation of the
reasonableness of the grounds for the grant of time extension as required
under Annex E of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and Annex E of COA
Circular No. 2009-001; and (b) be circumspect in validating and
recommending for approval of requests for time extension.
b. BAC, Municipal Engineer, and former Municipal Accountant - Designee
to submit the above-noted lacking documents and explain in writing the
deficiencies or incomplete information on the documents attached to the
DVs; and
c. Municipal Accountant, as the internal auditor, to ensure that
disbursements of funds are supported with complete and accurate
documentation pursuant to Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445.

With the foregoing, we recommended and management agreed that the
Municipal Mayor:
a. require the BAC through its Chairperson to (i) submit the above-noted
lacking documents in the procurements; (ii) explain in writing the
declaration of L/SCRBs despite the deficiencies in the submitted bid
documents or quotations; and (iii) furnish the Audit Team with copies of
the contracts/POs and their attachments within five working days from
execution/issuance thereof as required under Items 3.1.1 and 3.2.1 of
COA Circular No. 2009-001; and
b. instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) coordinate with the BAC relative
to the submission of the above-noted lacking documents; and (ii) as the
internal auditor, ensure that disbursements of funds are supported with
complete and accurate documentation pursuant to Section 4(6) of P.D.
No. 1445.

With the foregoing, we recommended and management agreed that the
Municipal Mayor, together with the concerned municipal employees:
a. closely monitor the implementation of development projects identified in
the AIP and see to it that these projects are promptly and timely
implemented in accordance with the schedule in the APP for the benefit
of the constituents and ensure that the development projects to be funded
out of the 20% DF are well-planned and procurement-and-
implementation-ready pursuant to Item 3.2.3 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC
No. 1; and
b. ensure that all PPAs incorporated in the AIP are specific or provided
with sufficient details, otherwise, secure authorization from the local
sanggunian for the implementation of PPAs with generic term
appropriations.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the Local Finance Committee to ensure that, in the preparation
of the budget, the estimated income and expenditures are based on
realistic and accurate projections for the smooth operation of the LGU
and to comply with other requirements, specifically the rules and
regulations on LDRRMF; and
b. direct the Municipal Accountant to (i) ensure that all the
unexpended/unobligated balances of QRF and MF-MOOE are
transferred to the STF pursuant to Item 5.1.10 of COA Circular No.
2012-002; (ii) reconcile the reporting of the balances of the continuing
appropriations of previous years’ capital outlay in Notes to FS and the
Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF by adjusting the amount
reported in the Notes to FS to avoid misleading the users thereof; and (iii)
ensure that disbursements of funds are supported with complete and
accurate documentation pursuant to Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445.

With the foregoing, we recommended and management agreed that the
Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. GAD Focal Person to (i) properly prepare the project proposals of PPAs
incorporated in the GPB; (ii) ensure that the actual expenditures and
actual attributed amounts reflected in the GAD AR and Project
Accomplishment Reports are correct and reconciled; and (iii) submit to
the Audit Team the GAD AR within five working days from the end of
January;
b. members of the GFPS to formulate the GAD Agenda and furnish a copy
to the Audit Team;
c. MPDC and GFPS to spearhead the setting up and maintenance of the
GAD database to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning,
programming, and policy formulation; and
d. Municipal Accountant to (i) submit the abovementioned lacking
documents, if available; and (ii) ensure that disbursements of funds are
supported with complete and accurate documentation pursuant to
Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended and management agreed that
the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. HRMO to indicate in the job order contract the specific function/s and/or
job description/s of the job order workers hired; and
b. Municipal Accountant to ensure that payments of wages and traveling
expenses to JOs/COS personnel are supported with complete and proper
documentation pursuant to Item 1.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and
Item B.5 of COA Resolution No. 2021-044.

We recommended that Management:
a) Instruct the Inventory Committee to conduct a complete annual physical
count, including assignment of property number stickers to all property,
plant, and equipment of the Municipality to prove their existence, determine
the condition and the valuation, and prepare the RPCPPE;
b) Direct the GSO and Municipal Accountant to reconcile the latest inventory
report with the PPELC, which shall serve as the basis of the Inventory
Committee as basis in the preparation of the PIP;
c) Implement the procedures prescribed under COA Circular 2020-006 dated
January 31, 2020, in the conduct of physical count of PPE, recognition of
PPE items found at the station, disposition of non-existing/missing PPE
items, and derecognition of non-existing/missing PPEs without an available
record of accountability, for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances
of government agencies to produce a reliable report and PPE balances;
d) Designate LGU personnel from the accounting and property offices who
shall maintain and update the PPE Ledger/Property Cards and regularly
reconcile their records, as well as maintain an updated ARE and safekeep
all documents relative to the acquisition of properties;
e) Direct the GSO to adhere to its mandated duties and functions. In relation
thereto, secure and maintain the aforementioned reports; and
f) Direct the GSO/Supply Office to secure and ensure that the AREs are
completely and correctly filled out with necessary details such as brand
name, color, model, applicable serial number, and other important
information about the property.

We recommended that Management:
a) Direct the Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and the
GSO with the complete description and cost segregation of road components
for road projects;
b) Require the Inventory Committee to conduct an inventory of local roads,
highways, and bridges within its area of jurisdiction which were acquired
through purchase, construction, or donations;
c) Direct the Municipal Accountant to record all road networks and their
components, based on the Registry of Public Infrastructures, or in its
absence, based on the complete Local Road Inventory Report provided with
a complete description and cost segregation of the road components;
d) Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the Local Road Network
Ledger Card in the prescribed form shown in Annex D of COA Circular No.
2015-008; and
e) Direct the GSO to maintain an updated Local Road Inventory and Road
Map and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card of all roads
within its area of jurisdiction; and
f) Utilize geotagging technology to enhance the accuracy and accessibility of
the local road inventory and mapping.

We recommended that Management:
a) Revisit the composition of the Inventory Committee and reiterate to its
members their mandated duties and responsibilities, particularly the
conduct of the actual physical inventory count and the timely preparation of
the corresponding reports, in accordance with existing rules and
regulations;
b) Require the Inventory Committee to conduct timely counts of Inventory
items and based on the timing prescribed by the Commission, and to ensure
proper and complete documentation of the results of the count to facilitate
reliable reconciliation between GSO records and Accounting Office records;
c) Ensure the preparation of RPCI, SSMI, and SCs to facilitate reliable
reconciliation between accounting and property records;
d) Direct the GSO, in coordination with the designated Supply Officer, to
establish an inventory control process, referring to Section 122 of the
Manual on the NGAS for LGUs as a guide;
e) Require the GSO and the Accounting Department to maintain SCs and
SLCs, respectively for proper monitoring of the balances of inventories; and
f) Coordinate with the concerned departments/offices and develop internal
control procedures that will ensure proper management of inventories to
prevent or minimize the risk of loss and misuse.

We recommended that Management:
a) Direct the Accountable Officers (AOs), through the issuance of a demand
letter, to submit the liquidation report together with the supporting
documents, to settle his/her cash advance; otherwise, require the immediate
return of the amount of cash advance that remained unliquidated or cause
the withholding of the payment of the AO’s salary;
b) Consider the one-time cleansing dormant Receivable accounts totaling
₱280,833.01, subject to documentary and procedural requirements under
COA Circular No. 2023-008.
c) Stop the grant of additional cash advances to personnel with unliquidated
cash advances until such time previous cash advances are fully settled;
d) Send demand letters to the accountable officers previously connected with
the LGU who still have unliquidated cash advances, otherwise officials
responsible for clearing said individuals from money accountability be held
liable for the unsettled cash advances; and
e) Monitor and establish controls to ensure timely liquidation of cash advances
such as requiring complete documentary requirements not only upon
liquidation but also upon granting of the same to establish accountability.

We recommended that Management:
a) Strictly adhere to the collection, remittance, and deposit process prescribed
in Sections 29 and 31 of the Manual on NGAS, Vol. I to ensure that all
collections are remitted and deposited regularly to the LGU’s accounts as
well as properly accounted and recorded in the books;
b) Demand the former Municipal Treasurer to settle all her accountabilities;
otherwise, she should not be cleared of money/property accountability
through Office Clearance process;
c) Immediately inform and coordinate with the Auditor in cases of retirement,
separation from office and other similar circumstances to facilitate timely
conduct of cash examination;
d) Instruct the Liquidating Officers to immediately settle their accountabilities
by remitting all undeposited collections;
e) Direct the OIC - Municipal Treasurer to enforce compliance with the
prescribed procedures and to strengthen the internal control mechanisms to
ensure that all collections are properly accounted for and deposited intact
and daily.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive coordinate with the bank to
confirm the status and balances of the LGU’s bank accounts, as the bank confirmed
that the LGU has no existing accounts with the bank, and to submit the necessary
documents needed to effect the any necessary adjustments.

We further recommended the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the
OIC-Municipal Treasurer to:
a) Reconcile the balance of the CIB-LCCA account once official confirmation
from the bank was obtained; and
b) Prepare the corresponding Bank Reconciliation Statements for the each
bank account and ensure the proper disclosure of these accounts in the Notes
to Financial Statements.

We recommended that Management direct the:
a) MHO and MSWDO to secure a copy of the Tripartite Memorandum of
Agreement signed on September 29, 2022;
b) Municipal Accountant to:
   i. Based on the amount arrived at in the preceding step, prepare adjusting
   journal entries necessary to record the amount under the appropriate
   liability and equity accounts as prescribed in Chapter 10 of the PMNP
   Project Operations Manual; and
   ii. Recognize the receipt of the next tranches of PBG, if any, as an outright
   income, and the subsequent utilization thereof under appropriate asset
   and expense accounts in compliance with the provisions of the
   Operations Manual.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Inventory
Committee to:
a) Prepare and submit the PIP to the COA Audit Team at least ten (10)
calendar days before the start of the inventory activities;
b) Use the ICF prescribed in Annex A of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated
January 31, 2020; and
c) Reconcile the difference of ₱72,894,810.00 between the accounting and
property records, and the result of the actual physical count pursuant to
COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to provide the
Municipal Accountant with a copy of the RPT records for CY 2024 and prior
years to establish the beginning balance for CY 2025. The Municipal
Treasurer also agreed to henceforth, furnish the Municipal Accountant at the
beginning of each year with a certified list of taxpayers with amounts due and
collectible for the current year as their basis for recording the RPT and SET
Receivables in compliance with Sections 19(b) and 20 of the NGAS Manual for
LGUs, Volume 1.

We recommended and the Municipal Agriculturist agreed to provide the
Municipal Accountant with an inventory of biological assets, along with the
prevailing market price for each item as of the reporting date and
information on the prevailing cost to sell these biological assets.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare JEVs
for valid reconciling items that require adjustment and correction in the GL,
in accordance with COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996.

We further recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to follow
the check disbursement process prescribed in Section 44 of the NGAS
Manual for LGUs, Volume I, to ensure that the JEVs are prepared and
recorded based on the RCI, together with the DVs and supporting
documents received from the Municipal Treasurer’s Office.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to make the
necessary adjusting entries to account for the stale checks.

We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to conduct a
thorough review and analysis of the list of outstanding checks and regularly
prepare the Journal Entry Voucher for the cancellation of stale checks to
reflect the correct monthly balances of the Cash in Bank accounts.

Furthermore, we recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to
regularly send a written notice to the payee informing him of the check’s
existence at least one month before it becomes stale.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to use the
appropriate account prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-009, dated
December 1, 2015, for garbage collection and disposal.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to improve the
processes within the accounting office to streamline the preparation and
timely submission of the required financial reports to the Audit Team.

Additionally, we recommended and the Municipal Accountant considered
the implementation of the Electronic New Government Accounting System
(eNGAS) to automate manual accounting processes and facilitate the timely
preparation of Financial Statements.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Assessor
agreed to conduct tax mapping to cleanse their records of real properties and
post the Notice of Delinquency in the Payment of RPT pursuant to Section 254
of R.A 7160.

We further recommended and Management agreed that, after the Notice of
Delinquency in the Payment of RPT is posted, the Municipality avail itself of
the remedies provided under R.A. No. 7160 to collect delinquent taxes.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a) Ensure that all future training and planning activities take place within
the Municipality's official station or territorial jurisdiction, as stipulated
by DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2011-059, thereby reducing
unnecessary expenses and ensuring compliance with regulations.
b) Choose venues within the Municipality or nearby towns to reduce costs
associated with transportation and venue rental.
c) Implement improved scheduling and communication strategies to
ensure higher attendance rates for training and planning activities, such
as coordinating with participants well in advance to avoid conflicts with
their professional duties.
d) Conduct a cost-benefit analysis before approving any training or
planning activities outside the Municipality to facilitate informed
decisions that align with the Municipality's financial constraints and
priorities.

We recommended and the LDRRMO agreed to utilize the LDRRMF in
accordance with NDRRMC, DBM, and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular
No. 2013-01 dated March 25, 2013, to strengthen the capacity of the LGU to
avoid or mitigate the adverse effects of disasters or calamities.

We recommended and the Local School Board (LSB) Chairperson and Vice-
Chairperson agreed to:
1. Ensure that the approved SEF Budget is stamped as “received” by the
Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Accountant, and Municipal
Treasurer within the indicative timeline or within seven (7) days after
approval of the SEF Budget for verification of compliance with Sec. 6.1
of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular, s. 2017;
2. Ensure the timely submission of the quarterly and annual SEF Utilization
Reports within the specified timelines stated in the above-mentioned
Joint Circular to the DepEd Central Office through appropriate channels
and copy furnished the local sanggunian and Regional Offices of the
DBM and DILG; and
3. Ensure that the quarterly and annual SEF Utilization Reports are posted
on the LGU website and on the three (3) Full Disclosure Policy Bulletin
Boards of the Municipality within their indicated timelines as specified
in the Joint Circular above.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report on Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) and to dispose all unserviceable properties at the earliest possible time pursuant to Section 79 of PD 1445 and Sections 379-381 of RA 7160.

Additionally, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor implement the One-Time Cleansing guidelines prescribed in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 and require the Inventory Committee to plan ahead the conduct of Physical Inventory to be used in the One-Time Cleansing and ensure that the RPCPPE will be submitted on time.

Furthermore, implement a regular assessment of the impairment of the LGU’s assets in line with IPSAS.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the:
Municipal Engineer to cross reference all the accomplishment reports and certificates of completion/inspection with the contracted scope of work or POW before approval.
MPDC and Engineer to implement a stricter verification process, including physical inspections and sufficient progress documentation, before certifying project completion
The Municipal Engineer to identify the undelivered work items so that the Municipal Accountant could appropriately effect the necessary adjustments in the recorded “Other Structure” account.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
