observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (MDRRMO) to judiciously plan how to maximize the utilization of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Funds including those financial assistance received from other government agencies and to accelerate their implementation in order to achieve the goal/target of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO implemented all 2019 PPA sourced from various NGAs. As of December 31, 2023, Due to NGAs account pertains to those received only in 2023.
observations.reason: Not Reiterated in Finding no. 2, Part 2, Page No. 45 to 47 of the 2023 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor that the Chairperson of the Executive Committee and the Technical Working Group to: a) exert active efforts in accomplishing the targeted and planned Organization-Focused PPAs and not just to sustain its compliance with minimum budget requirement on GAD programs, projects and activities as thus provided by the law, but to increase identification of gender-responsive PPAs to be covered by the annual budget; and b) strictly monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs and conduct regular coordination with implementing offices to ensure that programs are undertaken within the target period.
observations.managementAction: Endeavor not just to sustain its compliance with the minimum budget requirements on GAD programs, the agency initiated measures to increase identification of gender-responsive PPAs. They have implemented some activities but not all.
observations.reason: Not Reiterated in Finding no. 6, Part 2, Page No. 54 to 56 of this report.

observations.recommendation: 1. We recommended that the The Offices of the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant trace the differences between the Cash Local Treasury and Cash in Bank- LCCA accounts. We further recommended that Management, henceforth, conduct properly the quarterly reconciliation between the treasury records and accounting records in compliance with Section 143 (A.7) of the LTOM.
observations.managementAction: The Offices of the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant still had to trace the difference in cash account balances.

observations.recommendation: 9. We further recommended that the Management require the supplier/dealer to submit the tax and duty receipt corresponding to the equipment procured in accordance with Section 2 of A.O. No. 200 dated November 21, 1990.
observations.managementAction: The DRRMO still had to request the supplier/dealer for the tax and duty receipts required.

observations.recommendation: 16. We recommended that Management require the BAC, through its Secretariat, to post in the GPPB online portal the updated APP, NOA, and other information relative to the Municipality’s procurement projects in accordance with the provisions of GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020. We also recommended that the BAC Secretariat issue a certification on the posting of the NOA, Contract, or Purchase Order in any conspicuous place in the premises of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: 18. We recommended that the person immediately accountable for the unserviceable properties turn over the items to the Municipal Treasurer for cancellation of the Memorandum Receipt/Acknowledgement Receipt of Equipment.
observations.managementAction: Unserviceable properties had already been turned over to the Municipal Treasurer for cancellation of the Memorandum Receipt/Acknowledgement Receipt of Equipment.

observations.recommendation: 19. We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) create an Appraisal and Disposal Committee to appraise the value of unserviceable properties for the immediate disposal and drop thereof from the book of accounts in order to fairly present the PPE account in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Management had already committed to creating the Appraisal and Disposal Committee to facilitate the proper disposal of unserviceable properties.

observations.recommendation: 21. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant comply with all the conditions and requirements of COA Circular No. 2016-005 for the possible write-off of the specified dormant receivables, through the Local Chief Executive, to the Commission on Audit.
observations.managementAction: Not all dormant accounts identified were subjected to a request for write-off because the supporting documents still had to be obtained.

observations.recommendation: 23. We recommended that Management require the BAC to observe the provisions of IRR of RA No. 9184 in the posting of notice of award, notice to proceed, and the approved contract on the PhilGEPs website. We recommended further that the BAC Secretariat issue a certificate on the posting of the NOA on any conspicuous place in the premises of the Municipality.

26. Required the department heads concerned to process the titles of the land claimed by the Municipality and also requires the Municipal Accountant to record the said land in the books of accounts as soon as the titles have been issued.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and other local officials concerned to:

a.	Ensure that the PPAs prioritized under the 20% Development Fund are well-planned and implementation-ready to ensure full utilization of the fund within the period for which it is proposed; and

b.	Implement immediately all unimplemented PPAs that are in line with the Annual Investment Plan of the LGU for the promotion of the general welfare of the constituents and attainment of the desirable socio-economic and environmental management outcomes of the LGU.
observations.managementAction: The procurement and implementation of projects were expedited. Some items that are already impractical to be implemented was realigned to other 20% DF eligible projects.
observations.reason: Lack of personnel to oversee the simultaneous implementation of development projects. Reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to require the MDRRM Council through the MDRRMO to observe the proper utilization of the LDRRM Fund and carefully plan and execute the implementation of the approved programs, projects and activities to enhance the disaster risk reduction and management capabilities of the municipality.
observations.managementAction: None.
observations.reason: Non-coherence of the identified PPAs and the priority of the LGU. Reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.managementAction: Screenings of JOs were done and the hiring and need of JOs are based on the request and recommendation of the Head of Office concerned.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget Officer to indicate the prescribed account codes and information in the CAFOA, prepare the LDRRMF RAAO for MOOE, CO and QRF and provide the accounting office of the appropriations, realignments and supplemental, utilization and balances every end of the month. We also recommend that the Budget and Accounting Office reconcile the amounts of appropriations, utilization and balances so as to reflect reliable amounts in the FS.

On-going implementation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MENRO to look into the disposal and segregation of wastes in the MRF. It should supervise and consider the conduct of trainings and/or workshops on the proper handling and segregation of wastes to its Solid Waste Personnel. We further recommended that the Solid Waste Management Board devise measures consistent with the provisions of the R.A. 9003 and its IRR to prevent hazards or risks posed to the health of residents, the garbage collectors and the environment.
observations.managementAction: Discussed in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to instruct the City Accountant to (a) cancel the stale checks and restore in the books the corresponding cash and liability accounts; and (b) revisit the bank reconciliation made, and adjust the noted reconciling items and errors, as warranted.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the City Accounting Office to (a) identify the source of the variance and adjust the balances of affected accounts, as warranted; (b) regularly monitor and reconcile the corollary account balances of CIB-STF and Trust Liability- DRRM; and (c) ensure that trust funds are used exclusively for their intended purpose.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) continue complying with all the guidelines on the allocation, grant and documentation of cash advances for Confidential Fund; and (b) require the City Accountant to (i) timely submit all cash advance DVs and its supporting documents to COA Audit Team Leader within seven working days from the release of the check; and (ii) continue the proper recording in the books of CF transactions.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct (a) all accountable officers concerned to liquidate their cash advances immediately, and (b) the City Accounting Office to pursue all available actions to enforce the (i) immediate settlement of outstanding cash advances, and (ii) timely liquidation of cash advances that will be granted in the future.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require  (a) the  GSO and the CAO  to  continue working on the (i) elimination of the ₱34 million variance between RPCPPE and GL balances; (ii) identification of the accountable personnel on the 57 missing PPEs; or (iii) recommend for the disposition of the missing items, as warranted, in accordance with COA Circular 2020-006 on the one-time cleansing of PPEs; (b) CAO to  (i) derecognize from the books the 137 missing items and record in its place Due from Officers and Employees; and (ii) record impairment losses on unserviceable motor vehicles through journal entry as follows:
Prior Period Adjustment-Impairment Loss-PPE   ₱96,104.98
Accumulated Impairment Loss-PPE                                 ₱96,104.98
(c) GSO to (i) ensure that accountabilities on the City’s properties are well established and documented; and (ii) facilitate the registration with the LTO of the 51 identified vehicles.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the City Accounting Office to:
(a) reclassify the completed projects from CIP to its appropriate PPE accounts through the following entry:
Water Supply System			₱5,032,692.50
Computer Software				 ₱14,547,540.23
CIP – Infrastructure Asset   			₱5,032,692.50
CIP –Bldg. and Other Structures                     ₱ 14,547,540.23
(b) recognize depreciation and amortization costs from the period of its acquisition/completion up to the present through the following entry:
Prior Period Adjustment			₱15,100,778.64
Accum. Depreciation – Water Supply System       ₱553,238.41
Accum. Amortization – Computer Software       ₱14,547,540.23
(c) revisit the entries made for the final payment for potable water supply system at Zone 4, Caanawan, and adjust records, as warranted; and
(d) ensure that all acquired and completed PPEs are recorded to their appropriate asset account.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) pursue the request for an extension of the validity of the fund for the five biogas digesters to ensure maximum utilization of the funds, or return the funds to DENR if not approved; and (b) maintain adherence to the terms and conditions outlined in current and future fund transfers from other NGAs.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the City Engineer and the Local Development Council to (a) endeavor to resolve the impasse on the procurement of land for RCA; (b) subsequently ensure that adequate planning is applied before funds are committed to projects with the view of maximizing the use of the fund; and (c) sustain the generally high rate of implementation of its Annual Investment Plan.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the CDRRMO and City Administrator to (a) ensure that PPAs are identified based on reasonable needs assessment that is anchored on updated environmental scanning, with the view of attaining efficiency in the use of government resources; (b) tap alternative uses for the undistributed fertilizers; and (c) continue monitoring the capability of the City to respond to and prepare for disasters.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the CENRO to (a) enhance its planning processes and consider such factors that may affect the future implementation of the projects; (b) continue to include in the AIP the PPAs that will address the inadequacy of SWM facilities with consideration of the availability of funds; (c) recommend the hiring of technical person that will assist the CENRO in converting wastes to energy; and (d) sustain the satisfactory implementation of the five basic environmental regulations on preserving and protecting the environment as required under R.A. No. 9003.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the City Treasurer to (a) commence the review and update of the City Revenue Code; and (b) continue the advances made in business tax assessments and collections viewed vis-à-vis an updated Revenue Code and pertinent provisions of the Local Government Code.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the Gender and Development Focal Point System (GFPS) to continue conforming with the provisions outlined in PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 that ensures gender equality in public governance through mainstreaming of GAD in local policy-making, planning, programming, budgeting, implementation, monitoring, and evaluation processes.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the City’s Accounting and Treasury Offices to sustain their timely withholding and remittance of taxes in accordance with pertinent regulations, with the view of contributing in the revenue collection of the national government.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the City Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO), in collaboration with the City Accountant, to (a) continuously reconcile records with the GSIS EBCS and address any discrepancies accordingly; (b) facilitate coordination between former City employees and the GSIS to expedite the reconciliation and settlement of outstanding premiums and loan amortizations from prior years; and (c) continue to uphold compliance with the GSIS law concerning the deduction and timely remittance of contributions and premiums owed to the System.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the CAO to (a) adjust the ₱112,415.98 prior year’s error in the books; (b) determine the nature of the ₱5,213.48 unidentified deduction/reconciling items; and (c) continue its satisfactory compliance with the requirements of R.A. No. 9679 on the withholding and prompt remittance of HDMF contributions and loan repayments.
We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the City Accounting Office to continue in promptly remitting all collections due to PhilHealth.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the (a) persons liable to immediately settle the remaining balance of the disallowance; and (b) officials concerned to henceforth, observe pertinent rules and regulations in the discharge of their fiscal functions to avoid disallowances on the use of government funds.

3.14 We reiterated and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to instruct the: a)
Treasury Office to: i) submit to the Accounting Office the RCI daily; and ii) ensure
that payee has properly filled out the required information in Check Register and
Disbursement Voucher; and iii) ensure that Check Register is properly maintained
and updated; and b) Accounting Office to: i) use the RCI as basis in determining the
outstanding checks.

4.9 We recommended and the LCE agreed to: a) strictly adhere to Item No.
6.1.5 of the JC by limiting the cash advance for CF to a three-month requirement; and b)
require the Municipal Accountant to: i) submit all DVs for CF cash advances to the
COA ATL within seven working days from the release of check in a sealed envelope
with a visible label "CONFIDENTIAL - For the COA ATL Only"; and ii) submit
immediately to the Audit Team the lacking documents enumerated under Paragraph
4.8.

5.13 We reiterated and the LCE agreed to i) require the Inventory Committee to
conduct a complete physical count of all PPEs and submit the resulting RPCPPE on
or before January 31 of each year; and ii) consider the one-time cleansing of PPE
accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006. We further recommended and the
LCE agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure the recognition of
depreciation expenses for all depreciable PPEs.

6.15 We reiterated and the LCE agreed to instruct the personnel in charge of the
fund transfer from NGAs to: i) strictly observe the timeframe of the implementation
of the projects to ensure efficient delivery of public service to the intended
beneficiaries to avoid the recurrence of similar lapses in the future; and ii) ensure that
every fund transfer should be supported by MOA or its equivalent document and
submit the same to the Audit Team.

6.16 Also, we recommended and the Accounting Office agreed to: i) regularly
coordinate with the source agencies for the purpose of reconciling records; and ii)
adjust the balances of Due to NGAs accounts to reflect the correct amount based on
the confirmation provided by the source agencies.

7.21 We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the Municipal Planning and
Development Officer and all Department Heads involved in the planning process to:
i) be specific in including PPAs in the CDP and LDIP to ensure its inclusion in the
AIP for the constituents to enjoy benefits to be derived therefrom; ii) indicate the
specific date of implementation of PPAs in the LDIP and AIP to ensure the timely
delivery of the project to the intended users/beneficiaries; and iii) stop the practice of
charging improper PPAs in the 20% DF.

7.22 Lastly, we recommended and the Management agreed to continually maximize
the utilization of 20% DF for the continuous development of the municipality, which
will eventually improve the lives of the constituents.

8.12 We reiterated and the LCE agreed to instruct the MDRRMO to decide on the
use of the unexpended LDRRMF balance which shall then be incorporated in the
LDRRMFIP.

8.13 We further recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to i) identify
and determine the costs and accumulated depreciation of all equipment (excluding
heavy equipment) used for response and rescue activities and subsequently prepare
the necessary accounting entries to reclassify the identified equipment to the Disaster
Response and Rescue Equipment account; and ii) coordinate with concerned offices
to facilitate the submission of the documentary deficiencies and provide explanation/s
on audit exceptions enumerated under Paragraph 8.10.

9.6 We recommended and the Management agreed to: i) convert the existing
MRFs into operational in accordance with Section 32 of R.A. No. 9003; ii) consider
benchmarking with other LGUs with functional MRFs and ask assistance on how to
operate the shredding and decomposing machine; iii) coordinate with EMB on the
installation of the shredding and decomposing machines; iv) require the cooperation
of the Barangays in the implementation of Solid Waste Management particularly the
establishment of functional MRF and campaign for proper waste segregation; and v)
direct the garbage collectors to wear their protective gears at all times during garbage
collection.

10.14 We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the Municipal Treasurer to
i) examine the books and records of business entities within the Municipality to
ascertain, assess, and collect the correct amount of the business tax in accordance with
Section 171 of the R.A. No. 7160; and ii) prepare a stratified schedule PIL to
approximate the gross receipt of each business classification pursuant to Section 2A.
03. of the Municipality’s Revised Revenue Code.

11.13 We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the (i) BAC Chairman and
BAC Secretariat to invite Auditors and other observers, in writing, in all stages of the
procurement process, including the bid evaluation and post-qualification
proceedings; (ii) BAC Secretariat and concerned departments to submit an advance
copy of contract and supporting documents to the Audit Team; and (iii) TWG, BAC
Secretariat, and concerned departments to submit the lacking documentary
requirements to the Audit Team and/or provide justification if the lacking
document/s is/are not applicable to the project.

12.9 We reiterated and the LCE agreed to instruct the GFPS to ensure i) the timely
submission of GPB and GAD AR in accordance with COA Circular No. 2014-001; ii)
that gender analysis to determine the gender-responsiveness of regular PPAs be
conducted prior to their attribution in the GPB; and iii) during the preparation of the
GAD AR, the same PPAs be subjected to the HGDG test using the PIMME checklist
to determine the actual cost of expenditures that can be attributed to the GAD budget.

13.7 We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the Municipal Accountant
and Municipal Treasurer to ensure that monthly payments to HDMF and PHIC are
remitted within the prescribed periods by strictly monitoring the remittances of
withheld premium contributions and loan amortizations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Chairman of the Committee
on Disposal to initiate/facilitate the immediate disposal of the subject properties,
in accordance with the afore-cited provisions and with the Revised Manual on the
Disposal of Government Properties and the Municipal Accountant to provide the
necessary journal entries in the books of accounts after the disposal.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MDRRM Officer to:
a. submit within 60 days from receipt thereof the Municipality’s plan for the
implementation of unutilized balance of LDRRM Fund amounting to
P21,468,722.66; and
b. evaluate the feasibility of planned PPAs including the total amount of disaster
fund available and conduct strict monitoring to ensure its optimal
implementation within the plan, and identify projects which will no longer be
implemented for possible reversion and reprogramming to other disaster-
related PPAs.

We also recommended the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply to Section
5.1.10 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 on the transfer of
unspent disaster fund balance to the Special Trust Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:
a. instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure enough funds are appropriated
for property insurance of the Municipality; and
b. direct the GSO to insure with the GIF of GSIS all the Municipality’s insurable
assets and properties with the appropriate insurance coverage to protect the
Municipality thru indemnification in cases of damage or loss due to fortuitous
events.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) and the Municipal Budget Officer
to ensure that changes in the annual budget be made in accordance with Article
417 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA No. 7160, as amended by
AO No. 47.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to instruct the Municipal Development
Council, headed by the Municipal Mayor to assess the feasibility of the
implementation of development projects and and include projects that are well-
planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready pursuant to DBM, DOF and
DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2020-01.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. require the GFPS-TWG Chairperson to ensure the effective and efficient
implementation of the GAD PPAs and the judicious utilization of the GAD
budget; and
b. require the Technical Working Group (TWG) to monitor the implementation of
GAD-related PPAs and suggest corrective measures to improve their
implementation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. require the GAD Focal Point System to prepare the GAD Agenda in accordance
with the mandate of 4.C.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular
(JMC) No. 2013-01 and Sections 1 and 3 of the PCW Memorandum Circular
2018-04; and
b. empower the GAD Focal Point System to enhance their knowledge and build
their competencies on GAD-related laws and international commitments,
gender mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender-responsive planning and
budgeting, and GAD-related tools among others.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Inventory
Committee to:
a. Work on the reconciled difference of ₱56,187,136.63 between the
accounting and property records, and the result of the actual physical
count;
b. Adopt the uniform numbering system for property numbers of PPE and
require the Property Unit to update the property stickers based on the
prescribed format and to include the required information under
Section 5.6 and 5.7 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31,
2020; and
c. Conduct a physical count of all the PPE, whether acquired through
purchase or donation, using the prescribed forms in Annexes of COA
Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, and prepare and submit
the duly accomplished RPCPPE.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
a. Create an Inventory Committee to focus on the physical count of local
roads; and
b. Enjoin the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Accountant, and Municipal
Engineer to strictly comply with the accounting and reporting guidelines
on the local roads asset management system pursuant to COA Circular
No. 2015-008 dated October 23, 2015.

We recommended and Management agreed to revert to the unappropriated
surplus of the General Fund the balance of the undocumented Accounts
Payables with no valid claims pursuant to Section 98 of P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant revised the Notes to the
Financial Statements to include the principal amount, interest rate, maturity
date, purpose of the loan, and interest expense of the Loans Payable -
Domestic account.

We recommended and the Municipal Budget Officer, MPDO, Municipal
Engineer, and Municipal Accountant agreed to review and monitor the
balances of the continuing appropriations under the 20% DF, which shall be
the basis for the Local Finance Committee to recommend to the Local Chief
Executive the reversion of those pertaining to completed projects and those
no longer needed so that the funds may be appropriated and utilized for
other priority development projects.

We further recommended and Management agreed to address the causes of
delay, like insufficient funds, road ROW issues, and project plan and site
problems.

We recommended and Management agreed to revert the balances of
appropriations for completed and unimplemented projects that the
Municipality could no longer execute and use the funds to finance disaster
risk reduction and management activities pursuant to COA Circular No.
2012-02, dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended that Management utilize the procured rescue vehicle
exclusively for response and rescue/relief activities in accordance with DILG
Memorandum Circular No. 2022-105 dated August 23, 2022. Otherwise, the
LDRRMF should be reimbursed from the general fund for the cost of the
vehicle.

We further recommended that Management adhere strictly to the provisions
of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-73 dated April 17, 2012, and
NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated
March 25, 2013, on the utilization of the LDRRMF.

We recommended and the Municipal Agriculturist agreed to formally file to
this Office a request for relief from accountability for the death of biological
assets, together with the required documents enumerated in Section 151 of the
Rules and Regulations on Supply and Property Management.

We further recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare
an adjusting entry to reverse the losses recognized for biological assets in CY
2023 and wait for COA’s decision on the request for relief from
accountability filed by the Municipal Agriculturist.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Property Custodian and department heads concerned to furnish copy of the Property Transfer Report to the accounting office for proper recording and conduct inventory of donated/transferred property for record purposes and for inclusion in the inventory reports.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Inventory Committee to prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report on Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) and to dispose all unserviceable properties at the earliest possible time pursuant to Section 79 of PD 1445 and Sections 379-381 of RA 7160.

We recommended for the Municipal Officials concerned to coordinate and implement measures to improve the storage environment relative to the inventory management system to facilitate tracking, monitoring and replenishment of stocks.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive ensure that National Government Agency (NGA) employees were duly authorized by their mother agency to claim the food expenses from the LGU, duly supported with a certification that they have no funds for the said expense.

Further, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive refrain from approving/allowing assistance that it is not within the Municipality’s responsibility to finance and/or subsidized.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to verify thoroughly and review the supporting documents of claims for travel to ensure that only the reasonable amount of transportation/fare be allowed.

We further recommended to stop the practice of hiring public utility vehicle during official travels, instead, opt for the most economical and efficient mode of transportation. Otherwise, set a clear fare limits on the mode of transportation used when it is deemed justifiable and necessary under prevailing circumstances.

We recommended that the MPDC and Engineer regularly monitor the status/condition of the infrastructure projects of the LGU and recommend appropriate actions for its maintenance and/or repairs.

In the case of the structural defects and deficiency, we recommended that the contractor be required to immediately affect the necessary repair/restoration works considering that it is still within the defect liability period and/or warranty period.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Require the MPDC and Municipal Engineer to coordinate and intensify the monitoring of all on-going projects to minimize defects and ensure that the progress of construction are within the timetable and scope provided in the agreed construction works.

Instruct the Municipal Engineer to immediately inspect the Health Center, prepare the punch list for correction/completion and require the contractor to perform the satisfactory completion of the project.

Otherwise, enforce the sanctions and penalties to the defaulting contractor, such as imposition of administrative suspension for one to two years from participating in all the government bidding process, termination of the contract and forfeiture of the performance security and enforcement of the liquidated damages, as provided for in Republic Act 9184.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the:
Engineering Office to adequately conduct the detailed engineering and ensure that the PERT/CPM should include all the holidays and other instances that could adversely affect the construction works;
Municipal Engineer to validate the facts and extent of delay before recommending the grant of work suspension/ extension of contract time; and
Municipal Engineer and MPDC to regularly monitor/conduct ocular inspection during the construction period to appropriately assess the performance of the contractors in the entire infrastructure works of the LGU.

We recommended the following measures to the Local Chief Executive:
Instruct the MENRO to look into disposal and segregation of wastes in the Open Disposal Facility and Residual Containment Area operated by LGU.
Require the Solid Waste Management Board to devise measures consistent with the provisions of R.A 9003 and its IRR to prevent hazards or risks posed to the health of residents, the garbage collectors, and the environment.
Evaluate the suitability of the current location of the dumpsite and containment area, otherwise, consider relocating one of the facilities to a more suitable and environmentally responsible location.
Implement comprehensive monitoring and review processes on the collection and disposal of waste to ensure adherence to waste management regulations and standards and continuous improvement of its practices.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive thru the Solid Waste Management Board require all barangays to establish an operational MRF. Likewise, conduct regular inspection and monitoring of the established MRFs to ensure the operationalization of these facilities including waste diversion and effective record management. Further, enforce strict implementation of the sanctions provided in the Municipal ordinances and RA 9003 against individuals and entities that continuously violate the prohibited acts thereof.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive take the following courses of action to ensure the effective implementation of the DRRM Program:

Regularly review the annual LDRRM Plan, evaluate the readiness for implementation of the programmed PPAs and revise or recommend for re-appropriation, if warranted.

Expressly include the GAD aspect in the LDRRM Plan and Budget;

Establish a monitoring and evaluation scheme on the implementation of the DRRM PPAs so as to immediately address any hindrances and/or causes of delays.

Ensure that the minimum budgetary and reporting requirement are complied

1.15 We recommended, and Management agreed to:
a. Establish strong internal controls in the handling of cash particularly the
collections and remittances made by the Municipal Treasurer to the
AGDB, to prevent incurrence of unrecorded collections and/or over-
deposit of collections, which may result in an abnormal/negative balance
of the Cash-Local Treasury account;
b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant-Designate to make an extensive
review and evaluation of the accounting records to determine the cause
of the abnormal balances and draw a Journal Entry Voucher to correct
the deficiency; and
c. Require both the Municipal Accountant-Designate and the Municipal
Treasurer, to prepare and maintain under the TF a GL and a Cash Book,
respectively, and record all the cash receipts and deposits for funds
physically received by the Municipal Treasurer for a specific purpose or
undertaking.

2.13 We recommended, and Management agreed to require:
a. The Municipal Accountant-Designate to:
i. Facilitate the recording of the necessary journal entries to rectify
record the duplicate transfer of funds in the amount of
₱19,820,646.51 from the GF and the TF books; and
ii. Prepare the necessary BRS to immediately identify errors and be
able to make the necessary adjustments in the books prior to the
closing of the accounting period.
b. The Municipal Treasurer to issue a check for the reversion of the
aforesaid fund to the GF to correct the duplicate transfer of funds and
inform the Municipal Accountant-Designate to reflect the same in the
accounting records of the Municipality.

3.11 We recommended, and Management agreed to require the Municipal
Accountant-Designate to:
a. Conduct regular monitoring and analysis of the CIP account to ensure
that all completed projects are transferred to their appropriate PPE
accounts;
b. Reclassify the completed projects costing ₱16,022,996.37 under the CIP
account to their appropriate PPE account; and
c. Provide provision for depreciation on completed projects in accordance
with the existing rules and regulation.

4.16 We recommended, and Management agreed to require the Municipal
Accountant to:
a. Use the appropriate the accounts in the grant of cash advances and
recognition of the corresponding expenditures;
b. Record the liquidation of the fund transfers only upon submission of the
FUR to the Source Agency, and not on the date the cash advance was
liquidated by the Accountable Officer; and
c. Prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the errors noted.

a. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to:
i. Revert the amount of ₱4,498,500.00 to the GF to form part of the
CY 2023 Continuing Appropriation;
ii. Ensure that the charging of funds is only made against the
appropriate fund intended for that purpose; and
iii. Transfer the unexpended balance of the 30% QRF and DRRM-
MOOE totaling ₱4,026,817.35 to the STF.
b. Require the Municipal Accountant-Designate to:
i. Ensure that the unexpended balance of the 30% QRF and DRRM-
MOOE are transferred to the Trust Liability – DRRMF account.
ii. Record necessary adjustments in the books of account in order to
reflect the correct balances in the FS; and
iii. Draw the following journal entries relevant to the transfer of

6.6 We recommended, and Management agreed to require the Municipal
Accountant-Designate to:
a. Furnish the Audit Team with copies of the supporting documents in
recording the Prior Period Errors;
b. Identify the nature of the adjustments/errors to determine the extent of
the error if it requires prospective or retrospective restatement of the FS;
and
c. Fully disclose the Prior Period Errors in the Notes to FS, as required by
Paragraph 54 of IPSAS No. 3 to provide users of the FS adequate
information in understanding the transactions of the Municipality
through the FS.

7.14 We recommended, and the Management, through the Municipal Planning and
Development Coordinator (MPDC), agreed to provide specificity of schedules
in the Annual Investment Plan and Annual Procurement Plan in the
implementation of planned PPAs from the start of the project until its
completion dates to provide the basis for work plans and to serve as a guide in
procurement planning and to ensure further that all planned PPAs will be
implemented during the budget year.

8.14 We recommended, and Management agreed to:
a. Ensure that the implementation, utilization, and disbursement of the
LGSF-SBDP are strictly in accordance with the implementing guidelines
issued by the DILG;
b. Instruct the Municipal Engineer/MPDC and LGSF-SBDP Focal Person
to carefully monitor the implementation of the LGSF-SBDP projects and
ensure preparedness of available documents to support the progress
billing payments to the contract without undue delay;
c. Direct the Municipal Accountant-Designate to monitor the receipt and
utilization of the funds received by the Municipality and ensure that
disbursements are made within the validity of the fund; and
d. Require the Municipal Treasurer to immediately refund the unutilized
balance of the FY 2022 LGSF-SBDP fund to the DILG amounting to
₱380,956.92 and the corresponding FUR.

9.15 We recommended, and Management agreed to require the Municipal
Accountant-Designate to:
a. Ensure that disbursements for the release of mobilization, retention
money, and progress/final payment of infrastructure projects are duly
supported with complete documentation, and control over disbursements
of public funds are in place; and
b. Coordinate with the Municipal Engineering Office or any office
concerned, to comply with the lacking documentary requirements
prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-001 and immediately submit
to the Audit Team the nine (9) DVs under the GF and 17 DVs under the
TF totaling ₱25,720,043.85 and ₱8,242,944.76, respectively, for
evaluation.

10.9 We recommended, and Management agreed to direct:
a. The MPDC and the Members of the Bids and Awards Committee ensure
that all planned PPAs under continuing appropriation of the 20% DF
will be fully implemented in the succeeding year;
b. The Local Development Council, the MPDC and the Local Sanggunian
to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant-Designate and the
Municipal Budget Officer for the monitoring of the balances of
continuing appropriation under the 20% DF that pertain s to the savings
from completed and discontinued projects subject for reprogramming
and re-appropriation to other priority socio-economic and environmental
projects; and
c. The Municipal Engineer to conduct regular monitoring and inspection
on the implementation of infrastructure projects and ensure strict
compliance of the contractor with the terms of the contract and technical
specifications of the project and coordinate with the Municipal
Accountant-Designate on the release of the final payment.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation and the LCE agreed to instruct the 

(a) City Accountant in coordination with the City Health Office to determine and recognize in the books the full cost of the ending inventory of vaccines as at year-end and to henceforth record future receipts and issuances of the same;

 (b) the requesting office to prepare and submit the requirements of GPPB Circular No. 02-2021 in future procurements of ancillary supplies and services; and 

(c) City Health Office to continuously monitor the status of the COVID-19 morbidity in the community and provide updated information to senior management and oversight bodies as inputs in decision making.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to 

(a) request for the extension of validity or change in the purpose of the ₱1,400,057.00 received from DBM-NDRRM and DILG and the savings of ₱29,347.05, for a maximized use of the fund, or return the same to the agencies concerned; and 

(b) in case of favorable action on their request, instruct the City Planning and Development Officer, City Engineer and City Budget Officer to coordinate among the key officials of the City for the identification of PPAs where the unused funds could be applied.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the: 

(a) City Accountant to draw the necessary adjusting/ correcting entries for the following errors: (i) misstatement of amounts set up for RPT and SET Receivables for CY 2022; 

(ii) unrecorded collection of prior year’s SET Receivable; and 

(iii) non-recognition of receivables from cancelled properties; 

(b) City Accountant and City Treasurer to regularly reconcile their reciprocal data on RPT and SET Receivables, and 

(c) City Treasurer to (i) sustain its effective revenue collection efforts; and 

(ii) strategize specifically on how to collect from delinquent taxpayers and, if warranted, enforce the provision of Section 254(b) of the RA 7160 in the collection of delinquent taxes.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the HRMO and heads of the offices concerned to 

(a) identify the specific needs for JOs, Casuals and CoS; 

(b) define in the contract the service contractors’ duties and responsibilities; and 

(c) submit a copy of said contract to the Office of the Auditor within the prescribed period.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the GFPS to ensure that, 

(a) in coordination with the City Budget Officer, future appropriation for GAD funds meet the minimum mandatory appropriations; 

(b) the GPB and GAD AR are submitted timely to the AT; and 

(c) compliance with the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 is sustained for the overall promotion of gender equality in public governance.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the CAO and the CTO to sustain their commendable performance in observing the revenue regulations on the timely withholding and prompt remittance of the taxes to the BIR
We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the City Accountant to maintain the City’s strict observance of the GSIS law on the deduction and remittance of contributions and premiums due the System
We commended the CAO and City Treasurer's Office for their continuous compliance on proper deductions and remittances of Pag-IBIG fund in accordance with the provisions of RA No. 9679
We recommended and the LCE is amenable to continuously comply with the National Health Insurance Act by remitting all contributions withheld to the Philippine Health Insurance Corporation.

We recommended and the Local
Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to
instruct the:

submit the SSMI to the
Municipal Accountant; and
ii) Municipal Accountant to
attach the SSMI in preparing JEV to
recognize the expense portion of
inventory and refrain from doing
any adjusting journal entries without
proper supporting documents.

We recommended and the LCE
agreed to instruct the:

Office the RCIs daily; and

properly filled out the required
information in the Check Register and DV; and

and updated; and

checks;
ii) revert back to Accounts
Payable the stale checks
included in the BRS; and
iii) immediately prepare the
adjusting entry on the debit memo
pertaining to payment of check.

We recommended and the LCE
agreed to require the:
i) Municipal Treasurer to furnish the
Municipal Accountant a certified list
of taxpayers with the amount due and
collectible for the year pursuant to
Section 20, Volume I of the Manual on
the NGAS for LGUs; and

receipt of the said certification, to draw

RPT and SET Receivables as estimated.

We reiterated and the LCE agreed
to:
a) instruct the
i) Inventory Committee to
conduct a complete physical
count of all PPEs and submit
the resulting RPCPPE, in the
prescribed format, on or
before January 31 of each
year; and
ii) Supply Officer to maintain
complete PCs for all PPEs; and
b) strongly consider the one-
time cleansing of PPE accounts
balance pursuant to COA Circular
No. 2020-006.

We recommended and the LCE
agreed to instruct the MDRRMO
to:
i) warrant the optimal utilization of
the LDRRMF, particularly the 70%
MF, to achieve the objective of
reducing disaster risk and enhancing
disaster management capabilities; and
ii) determine the use of the
previous years’ unexpended
LDRRMF balances and
incorporate the identified PPAs
in the LDRRMFIP.

We recommended and the LCE
agreed to require the Municipal
Accountant, in coordination with
other Heads of Offices In-Charge
of the fund transfer from NGAs,
to:
i) ensure the submission to the
Audit Team of a copy of the MOA or
its equivalent on the fund transfers
received from NGAs;
ii) monitor the submission of
liquidation documents to the
concerned Source Agency for
funds received from NGAs; and
iii) strictly observe the time frame of
scheduled implementation of the
projects to ensure efficient delivery of
public services to the intended
beneficiaries.

We reiterated and the LCE agreed
to require the GFPS to:
i) submit the GPB and GAD AR to the
Audit Team within the periods
prescribed in COA Circular No. 2014-001; and
ii) administer the HGDG and PIMME tools
to measure the actual cost attributed to

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management to: a. Promptly comply the required annual preparation and updating of the LDRRMFIP with defined projects, or activities, and strictly comply with the requirement before the release and use of the unexpended previous year's DRRM balance accruing to the special trust fund; b. Provide the Audit Team proof of submission of the Annual LDRRMFIP to the OCD and DILG-LGOO within fifteen calendar days from receipt hereof in accordance with the provision of Section 5.1.6 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO have prepared Risk Profile and Risk Assessment to identify the specific needs of the Municipality. The MDRRMO transmitted the LDRRMFIP for CY 2022 to the OCD and DILG last March 31, 2022.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) and the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that changes in the annual budget be made in accordance with Article 417 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA No. 7160, as amended by AO No. 47.
observations.managementAction: The MPDC have provided justification and documents, that majority of the changes was due to the receipt of funding from NGAs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: a. Municipal Engineer to ensure that repair, rectification and correction of the noted deficiencies will be accomplished by the contractor and submit to the Audit Team the status of compliance for submission to the COA TAS, and if otherwise, immediately refund the noted deficiencies and plus liquidated damages, if warranted; and b. Municipal Accountant to deduct from the claims of the concerned contractors the deficiencies noted if no action was undertaken pursuant to 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
observations.managementAction: The LGU of San Jose have implemented the subject infrastructure status of compliance for submission to the COA TAS, the municipality collected the claim for the said noted deficiencies per OR No. 33533755 dated December 20, 2023.

observations.recommendation: Considering this, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer and Disbursing Officers to employ the use of ATM payroll system for the payment of casual and job order employees.
observations.managementAction: The LGU of San Jose entered into MOA with Land Bank of the Philippines last November 29, 2023 to avail ATM Payroll Facility for casual and job order employees.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to: a. consider the additional measures and conduct information drive and programs to intensify collections of taxes and coordinate with barangays in the implementation of its collection strategies; b. continuously coordinate with the Provincial Government to avail of guidance and assistance; and c. zero out unreconciled differences for the presentation of more reliable RPT and SET Receivables in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The MTO and Municipal Accountant have reconciled their records on RPT and SET as of December 31, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (MDRRMO) to: a. ensure the optimal utilization of the funds, closely monitor the implementation of the proposed PPAs and institutionalize measures in the planning and utilization of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund to answer the needs of the constituents; b. identify the specific needs of the Municipality in times of calamities and provide the necessary guide in the preparation of accurate and comprehensive LDRRMFIP; and c. make sure that the planned PPAs embodied in the investment plan must be detailed and specific and must support the disaster risk reduction and management activities of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO have prepared the Risk Profile and Risk Assessment to identify the specific needs of the Municipality. The MDRRMO transmitted the LDRRMFIP for CY 2022 to the OCD and DILG last March 31, 2022. The MDRRMO and council are thinking of plans to utilize LDRRMF Funds.
observations.reason: Not Reiterated in Finding no. 2, Part 2, Page No. 45 to 47 of the 2023 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant: a. to evaluate the validity of the long outstanding payables and seek authority, if needed from the Sangguniang Bayan for the reversion of the long outstanding payables to contractors and the Unappropriated Surplus of the General Fund to reflect the correct balance of the liabilities accounts as of year-end.
observations.managementAction: The concerned departments have evaluated majority of the outstanding obligations and seek contact to the respective supplier/contractor. The departments contacted the Sangguniang Bayan for the reversion of the long outstanding payables to a problem with the payee’s business name. The remaining unclaimed by the contractor. The majority were already released or reverted to Unappropriated Surplus.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and concerned officials devise additional strategies to intensify collection of taxes and coordinate with the Sangguniang Bayan and Provincial Government for guidance to avail remedies and sanctions as provided in RA No. 7160 to deter delay and non-payment of taxes; and continuously coordinate to zero out unreconciled differences for the presentation of more reliable RPT and SET Receivables in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant have reconciled their records on RPT and SET as of December 31, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. maintain database and establish the generation and review of sex-disaggregate data and consultations with gender advocacies; b. exert active efforts in accomplishing the targeted and planned Client-focused and Organization-Focused PPAs. Try not just to sustain its compliance with minimum budget requirement on GAD programs, projects and activities as provided by the law, but to increase identification of gender-responsive PPAs to be covered by the annual budget; and c. strictly monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs as required under the Joint Circular and conduct regular coordination with implementing offices to ensure that programs are undertaken within the target period.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Person has presented to the Audit Team their GAD database that was used to identify gender issues. The GFPS have implemented some activities but efforts to fully implement others are ongoing.
observations.reason: Not Reiterated in Finding no. 6, Part 2, Page No. 54 to 56 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer: a. and other concerned officials to implement various strategies to intensify collection of real property taxes and develop additional strategies to generate more income; b. coordinate with the Provincial Treasurer the transmitting status of the reported tax delinquencies and the action taken by the latter in the imposition of the required remedies; and further that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer reconcile their respective records for the immediate reconciliation of accounts.
observations.managementAction: The MTO and Municipal Accountant have reconciled their records on RPT and SET as of December 31, 2023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a) in coordination with the responsible departments aim to reduce unnecessary delays in implementatioin and strictly effect the necessary processes to expedite the completion of the projects to allow the early enjoyment of the benefits by the general public and the full utilization of the funds received from NGAs; and b) ensure the return/remittance of unused balances still remaining unreturned/unremitted to the source agencies or to the National Treasury.
observations.managementAction: The agency implemented all 2019 PPAs sourced from various NGAs. As of December 31, 2023, Due to NGAs account pertains to those received only in 2023. Unused balances amounting to P373,810.00 still remained unreturned/unremitted to the source agencies or to the National Treasury but actions were taken.
observations.reason: As of December 31, 2023, Due to NGAs account balance pertains to those received only in 2023. Unused balances still remained unreturned to the source agencies.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the GAD Focal Point System to attend training on how to administer the HGDG in its implementation and to administer HGDG test again at the end of project implementation and incorporate the results in determining the percentage of PPA expenditures attributable to GAD.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Focal Point System completed a 30 hours/5 days training on HGDG held last May 29 to June 2, 2023. The Agency initiated the HGDG test to be administered at the end of project implementation. The Agency has scheduled further trainings.
observations.reason: The GAD Focal Point System have completed training; implementation of HGDG test expected in succeeding years.

observations.recommendation: 2. We recommended that the BAC and Management submit a written explanation of the deficiencies noted. We also recommended that the BAC comply with the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to ensure integrity and transparency in the procurement process and in the implementation of procurement contracts.

3. We recommended that the BAC strictly comply with Section 18 of R.A No. 9184, on the prohibition of reference to brand names in the procurement of goods to obtain the most advantageous price for the government.

4. We recommended that the LDRRMC pass a resolution reverting the CYs 2013 to 2014 LDRRMF unspent balances to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund, for adoption by the Sangguniang Bayan thru the Local Development Council. We further recommended that the members of the Local Finance Committee prepare the necessary transfer of funds and accounting reports to facilitate the said reversion and certify the said funds for the subsequent reappropriation for the benefit of the constituents.
observations.managementAction: Management had already posted to the GPPB website/portal.

observations.recommendation: 6. We recommended that Management consider, in the preparation of the Municipality’s Annual Plan, PPAs which could be funded under the LCF share, such as health programs, medical assistance and services, and charities of national character. Henceforth, utilize the LCF share aligned with the intentions and purposes of the fund pursuant to Section 4 of EO No. 357, series of 1996, and Section 1 of RA No. 1169, as amended, for the benefit of the constituents.

7. We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant ensure the timely remittance of all Barangay shares from the RPT collections to the respective barangays in adherence to the prescribed period outlined in Section 271(d) of R.A No. 7160.

8. We recommended that Management henceforth, ensure that in the procurement of second-hand/reconditioned transport and heavy equipment, the requisitioning officer avoid stating in the PR and ITB the specification “Original Japan Surplus,” otherwise, the same shall be deemed brand-new transport equipment.
observations.managementAction: The DRRMO still had to request the supplier/dealer for the tax and duty receipts required.

observations.recommendation: 10. We recommended that, henceforth, the officials involved in the procurement of goods and services comply with laws and regulations, particularly R.A. No. 7581 or the Price Act, as amended, to protect the government’s interest.

14. We recommended that Management require the Barangay and the MSWDO to submit to the Auditor a photocopy of the ID with specimen signature of the beneficiaries pursuant to Item 9 provided under Item 9 of the MOA dated April 24, 2020 and Section VII.A.1 of DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 09, series of 2020, dated April 9, 2020.

15. We recommended that Management require the concerned personnel or authorized end-user to completely and properly fill up the relevant and required information in the PO pursuant to Item B of COA Circular No. 96-010 dated August 15, 1996 to allow comparison and to ensure that the government obtains the most advantageous prices.

17. We recommended that Management assign only regular employees as collecting officers and, henceforth, ensure strict adherence to existing laws and regulations that all accountable officers/employees shall be properly bonded to safeguard government funds.

20. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a thorough verification and analysis of the dormant receivable accounts and provide for an allowance for impairment in accordance with the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards.

24. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant evaluate the collectability of receivables, review and analyze the accounts and, if not possible due to the absence of records, the Local Chief Executive request for authority to write-off from the Commission on Audit.

25. Required the officials and employees concerned to immediately refund in full the productivity enhancement incentive received by them for CY 2010. observations.recommendation:

a)	Update the cashbooks regularly, daily or monthly whichever is practicable, based on the ROD and RCD.

b)	Reconcile the cashbook balances with the accounting records every end of the month or at least quarterly.

c)	Immediately take up necessary adjustments on the books to reflect the correct cash balances and to reconcile her cashbooks with the accounting records.

observations.recommendation:

b. Require the appointed Chairperson of the Oversight Committee, to recommend to the SB the immediate approval of the proposed revisions of the LGU’s Municipal Revenue Code; and,

c. Request from the Municipal Vice-mayor, as the Presiding Officer of the SB to facilitate the immediate issuance of a local revenue ordinance for the adoption of the revised rates on fees and charges of the LGU.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management enjoin the concerned department heads to strictly comply with the accounting and reporting guidelines on local roads asset management system, and to earnestly perform their respective duties and responsibilities set forth under COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015. Henceforth, we recommended that the Inventory Committee conduct an annual physical count of all its local road networks and report the results thereof in the RPCLRN to be submitted to the Auditor and Accounting Unit not later than January 31 of each year. The RPCLRN shall be reconciled with the accounting records and the necessary adjusting entries shall be prepared before the statement date.
observations.reason: Physical inventory of LRN 2019 was not yet conducted due to COVID-19 pandemic. Accordingly, the depreciation expense was not provided in the absence of the RPCLRN.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant require the PHWs to submit proof of actual exposure to specific health hazards and occupational risks, and documents required under Section 5.8 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012, to support their hazard pay. Henceforth, Management shall pay hazard pay based on the actual days of exposure to risks or danger in accordance with Section 7 of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 7305.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant had not submitted the lacking documentary requirements for the payment of hazard pay in CY 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Agriculturist to establish the actual status of the breeding stock dispersal program by tracing the whereabouts and condition of the original breeding stocks and their offspring and to render a report to the Municipal Accountant which includes the fair market value of each stock, for proper recording in the books of accounts. Henceforth, the Municipal Agriculturist shall keep systematic records of births, deaths, and disposals of breeding stocks. In the event of death or disposal, facilitate the request for relief of accountability in accordance with Sections 151 and 152 of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992, for dropping the account from the books. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant provide proper disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statements in consonance with PPSAS 27 to provide information that may affect the fairness of the presentation of the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Agriculturist (MAO) informed us that they could not establish the actual status of the breeding stock dispersal program by tracing the whereabouts and condition of the original breeding stocks and their offspring. Further, the MAO was not able to conduct an inventory of the breeding stocks and the prevailing market price of each stock purchased in CY 2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the accounting office should update the Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards. Likewise, the Municipal Treasurer’s office should maintain property cards. Accordingly, the two offices should make a periodic reconciliation of these records. Moreover, the inventory committee shall conduct a reliable physical inventory count at least once a year to determine which of their properties are actually existing. A team composed of the General Services and Accounting Department personnel should conduct the physical inventory count and reconcile the book and property records. Accordingly, the Accounting Department shall effect the correcting entries, where applicable, when properly supported with documentation. Finally, Management should drop the unserviceable properties from the inventory reports and dispose the same in accordance with the procedures outlined above.
observations.reason: The RPCPPE submitted by the Municipal Treasurer did not reconcile with the balance per book.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should see to it that the PCF is not used for regular and recurring expenses and only use for non-recurring, emergency, and petty expenses.
observations.managementAction: The BAC, in compliance with the existing provisions of RA No. 9184, conducted biddings on the procurement of fuel, oil, and lubricants. The procurement was duly published. However, despite the widest dissemination of information, suppliers operating within the territorial jurisdiction and from the adjacent municipalities never showed interest by way of participating in the public bidding. Further, the suppliers verbally disclosed that they have no tax clearance. Payments for fuel, oil, and lubricants were still made out of PCF.

observations.reason: Reiterated in AAR CY 2021

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the General Services Office ensures the conduct of physical count of the LGU’s inventories and PPE; the preparation and maintenance of Stock Cards and Property Cards. Likewise, the Municipal Accounting Office to prepare and maintain Supply Ledger Cards and PPE Ledgers Cards and ensure that the reconciliation of their records is done prior to the preparation of the annual financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review the contents of outstanding checks and coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to furnish their office with the list of checks staled and cancelled or checks staled but reissued together with the original disbursement vouchers and other supporting documents to facilitate accurate preparation of journal entry vouchers and ensure proper recording of the same.

We recommended that management require the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Secretariat to submit to the office of the Auditor all perfected contracts within five (5) working days upon approval, as mandated under COA Circular No. 2009-01 dated February 12, 2009, so that timely evaluation can be made by the office.

We recommended the Management to:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to evaluate and review the existing fund balances in order to determine the proper disposition of these accounts; and
Direct the Municipal Treasurer to return the excess or unexpended balances to the National Treasury or to the Source Agency in accordance with the above – cited provisions

We recommended the LGU management to

Exert more efforts in the negotiation and resolution of lot ownership conflict;
In the event that land ownership conflict cannot be resolved in favor of the LGU, make representation with the Source Agency, informing the latter of the difficulties encountered that hinders the project implementation;  and
Present other options and strategies in utilizing the funds, request for amendments and/or modifications of the terms and conditions of the MOA to fit the prevailing situation that implementation of the project can be facilitated in order to avoid reversion of the transferred fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive designate a Property Officer who shall prepare Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances, supported with Acknowledgement Receipt, Issuance Form and inventory of remaining undistributed items for in-kind donations received within ten (10) working days after the end of each quarter in compliance to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-009.

Refrain from delegating into one office the responsibility to receive and dispose the subject donations for check and balance purposes in order to achieve sound internal control and proper accounting thereof.

We recommended that Management properly plan, closely monitor, and evaluate the implementation of the identified and prioritized development programs/projects/activities (PPAs) funded by the 20% Development Fund,  so that any cause of suspension or problems that would delay the project implementation would be immediately addressed and more desired benefits could be delivered to the Municipality’s constituents.

We recommended that the MDRRM Officer prepare and furnish the revised/supplemental LDRRMFIP every after a new/revise program is made which includes the PPAs not embodied in the original plan and/or SB Resolutions, to be approved by the LDRRM Council. This would be the basis of the MPDC to update/revise the AIP of the Municipality to adopt the changes in the LDRRMFIP.

Also, adhere strictly to Items 4.4 and 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002, Section 2.5.6 of Local Budget Circular (LBC) No. 24 and Section 6.5 of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-1.

We praised the effort of the concerned Department Heads on the partial
compliance made on the transfer of properties from its registry to their respective
accounts. However, we reiterated our recommendation on the continuous
coordination among the departments for the retrieval of any available records and
information of those infrastructure assets for their appropriate transfer to the books
to fairly present the PPE accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant
to ensure that DRRM funds received from National Government and other
sources from the succeeding periods to be treated as special trust liability
recorded under Trust Liabilities – DRRMF account instead of Due to Other NGAs
account.

Prepare the necessary accounting entry to recognize the adjustment/ transfer of
the remaining P100,000.00 from Due to Other NGAs to Trust Liabilities – DRRMF
account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to
make proper adjustment in the books for the accumulated share from Philippine
Charity Sweepstakes Office (PCSO) totaling P1,388,652.22 and ensure that the
subsequent shares from the PCSO - Lotto Draws and Small-Town Lottery (STL)
of the Municipality is appropriately accounted as income and recorded using
“Share from PCSO (4-04-01-020)” account pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Acting Municipal
Treasurer to:
• consider the application of the legal remedies allowed by law and the
Municipal Revenue Code to enforce the collection of long overdue or
delinquent real property taxes and regularly communicate with the Sangguniang Bayan and Provincial
Government for guidance and assistance; and
• continuously coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to zero out the
unreconciled differences of P714.20 for the presentation of a more reliable
RPT and SET Receivables in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant:

a. to ensure that all subsequent unused balance of funds received from any
national government agencies shall be return immediately after the
completion of the program and/or project pursuant to COA Circular 94-013;
and
b. with the assistance of the Municipal Agriculturist, to continuously
coordinate with the Department of Agriculture for the proper action to be
done with the dormant balance of P373,810.00. If the Source Agency still
decline the offer to refund due to lack of pertinent records, return the same
to the National Treasury pursuant to DOF-DBM-COA Permanent Committee
Joint Circular No. 4-2012 dated September 11, 2012.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget Officer
in coordination with the Municipal Engineer to review the unexpended balances
and unobligated appropriations of PPAs carried over as CY 2021 Continuing
Appropriations to determine which are eligible as continuing PPAs pursuant to
the provisions of Sections 321 and 322 of RA No. 7160 and thereafter, consider the
reversion of these unobligated appropriations of completed PPAs which are no
longer to be pursued by the Municipality to be used for other potential
developmental projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Disaster
Risk Reduction and Management Officer to:
• submit justification on the sub-par accomplishments on the utilization of
the available LDRRM Funds and implementation of the proposed PPAs;
• ensure the optimal utilization of the LDRRM funds, closely monitor the
implementation of the proposed PPAs and institutionalize measures to
strengthen disaster preparedness and response capabilities at all levels to
answer the needs of its constituents;
• identify the specific needs of the Municipality in times of calamities and
disasters as guide in the preparation of an accurate and comprehensive
LDRRMFIP and avoid including generic-termed PPAs like stand-by funds in
the investment plans; and
• make sure that the planned PPAs embodied in the investment plan must be
detailed and specific and must support the disaster risk reduction and
management activities of the Municipality in compliance with the afore-cited
provisions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
• direct the Local Finance Committee to post on the Municipality’s website the
quarterly Report of Fund Utilization and Status of PPA Implementation of the
LGSF – COVID- 19 Financial Assistance to LGUs as part of their full
disclosure policy; and
• send written notice to the Secretary of DBM, Speaker of the House of
Representatives, President of the Senate of the Philippines, and the
Chairpersons of the House Committee on Appropriations and Senate
Committee on Finance upon posting of the 2nd and 3rd quarter reports on the
Municipality’s website.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure strict compliance with
pertinent laws, rules and regulations on the monitoring, utilization and reporting of the
status of funds and the implementation of COVID-19 related PPAs. Likewise, we also
recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Accountant to: (i) immediately submit to the Audit Team the lacking
supporting documents noted, to arrive at a decision in audit, and check the
completeness and propriety of supporting documents prescribed under COA
Circular No. 2012-001 and other COVID-19 related issuances before processing
claims for payments to establish the legality, validity and propriety of the
disbursements; and (ii) henceforth, ensure that purchases of medical supplies and
materials are recorded under the appropriate Inventory accounts upon purchase,
and expensed only upon issuance thereof, and all equipment to its appropriate PPE
accounts, for fair and accurate presentation in the Financial Statements;
a. BAC to: (i) submit all the originals/duly authenticated photocopies of the lacking
documents and proper justifications for non-compliance with the provisions of 2016
Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and other COVID-19 related issuances on the
procurement of COVID-19-related PPAs to arrive at a decision in audit; and
(ii) henceforth strictly comply with the provisions of the said 2016 Revised IRR, to
establish the validity and regularity of the procurement process and award of
contracts/POs; and
b. Municipal Treasurer to ensure that Box D portion of the DVs are duly signed by the
payees or his/her authorized representative before the release of payment thereof
and ensure that the printed names of the claimants are duly indicated in the Box D
portion of the disbursement vouchers to facilitate verification.

We hereby recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to make sure henceforth that the collections are deposited intact and timely; and
Require the Municipal Treasurer together with the Municipal Accountant to exert extra effort in substantiating the supposed receipt of funds amounting to P691,519.00 so that the fair and correct balance of the Cash Local Treasury account would be the amount presented in the Statement of Financial Position.

Instruct the Municipal Accountant to revisit and adjust the RPT and SET Receivable and its related accounts (i.e., deferred revenue, income, and discount) and adjust them in order to reflect the correct and fair amount of tax due and collectibles from the taxpayers; and
Require the Municipal Treasurer to (i) provide to the Accountant a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year which should be used as basis by the latter in setting up receivable account at the beginning of the year; and (ii) provide copies of summary of delinquencies as at December 31, 2021 to the Municipal Accountant and audit team to support the adjustments to be made for reference in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to (i) revisit the status of implementation of projects funded out by fund transfers to ensure prompt liquidation and adjustments for the already completed projects; and (ii) in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer, return the unexpended balances of completed projects to its source agencies.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer to (a) properly observe the provisions of DPWH D.O. No. 197 dated October 7, 2016 in the preparation of the ABC of future infrastructure projects; (b) review thoroughly the ABC, Program of Works, and Detailed Engineering Estimates to ensure that the basis and ceiling of bid prices are acceptable/reasonable and advantageous to the government; (c) ensure that details of each item of works are complete pursuant to Annex A of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184; (d) submit the above-mentioned lacking documents for proper evaluation of the COA TAS; and (e) submit written explanations on the variances to the contract cost noted by the COA TAS otherwise, deduct from the succeeding claims of the contractor.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Require the BAC and Municipal Engineer to (i) submit the noted lacking documents; (ii) explain the noted deficiencies; and (iii) ensure henceforth that the required postings and submissions are done on time; and
Instruct the Municipal Engineer to be circumspect in evaluating the contractor’s request for extensions and be guided with Item 11 of Annex E of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184, as updated as of March 21, 2021.

With the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Together with the Municipal Engineer, fast track the implementation of the identified PPAs under the 20 percent DF and henceforth see to it that all PPAs are started immediately after the issuance of Notice to Proceed and completed timely or within the targeted date of completion; and
