Municipal Treasurer to ensure that all portion of disbursement vouchers are properly filled out, and duly signed by authorized officials, before releasing the check to contractors/payees.
observations.managementAction: The Management had submitted several lacking documents noted in the audit observations such as, copies of Purchase Orders, posting of Invitation to Bid, Notice of Award and Notice to Proceed in PhilGEPS website, Annual Procurement Plan, Notice to Proceed, Minutes of Pre-bid Conference and Bid-Opening, certified photocopies of PCAB licenses of winning bidders, Certificate of Authority issued by Insurance Commission, invitations to observers and Performance Securities.
observations.reason: Procurements of goods, services and infrastructure projects were still not made strictly in accordance with the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184 due to the continued disregard of the concerned employees with the audit recommendation thus, reiterated in Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
In view thereof, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the MDRRM Council: (a) ensure the optimal utilization of the MDRRMF, including the unexpended balances from previous years, specially, during times of calamities to enhance the Municipality’s resilience and capacity to reduce vulnerabilities when exposed to hazards and calamities; (b) prepare a correct and updated LDRRMFIP which shall include Continuing Appropriations under the LDRRMF; and (c) refrain from appropriating and utilizing the fund for purposes outside the mandate of the NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

Municipal Accountant and Budget Officer to reconcile their records regularly to ensure the accuracy, completeness and reliability of the information contained in their respective reports for the proper guidance and information of stakeholders and users of the financial reports; and

Municipal Accountant to: (i) transfer the unexpended /unutilized balances of the LDRRMF in the Special Trust Fund under Trust Liability-DRRM account in the Trust Fund books solely for the purpose of supporting disaster risk reduction and management activities within the next five years; and (ii) ensure that purchases of relief items, supplies and materials are recorded under the appropriate Inventory accounts and recognize the appropriate expense accounts only upon its issuance.
observations.managementAction: The Management had already transferred the unexpended balance of LDRRMF for CYs 2020 and 2021 in the Special Trust Fund under Trust Liability-DRRM account in the Trust Fund.
observations.reason:
Unexpended balance of the continuing appropriations as of CY 2019 and previous years was still not transferred to Special Trust Fund under Trust Liability-DRRM account in the Trust Fund due to on-going reconciliation between Municipal Budget and Accounting Office records.

The Management still failed to prepare LDRRMFIP for the continuing appropriations since the actual/exact amount of the unexpended continuing appropriations was not established.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, with the assistance of the Local Development Council, the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, maximize the utilization of the 20 percent Development Fund to spur development, provide jobs to constituents and to achieve the desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the LGU so that the intended users/beneficiaries may enjoy the immediate benefits from said development projects.
observations.managementAction: The Management had implemented various identified PPAs and utilized P52,260,141.41 out of its continuing appropriations of P67,320,131.72, registering a financial delivery rate of 77.63 percent as at year-end.
observations.reason: Unimplemented projects, such as construction of drainage canals and concreting of road with drainage canal were re-aligned for the implementation of newly created PPA One (one unit Biomedical Waste Treatment Facility).

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor exercise his primordial responsibility to require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer in coordination with other concerned municipal employees such as the former Municipal Accountant - Designee who is still in the service to continue exerting efforts in digging up records in order to render once and for all the actual and true status of the financial accounts of the Municipality.  Moreover, the responsible officials should be held liable under the Revised Penal Code for their failure to render the accounts and reporting thereof.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer had already exerted extra effort in digging up such records.

The Municipal Mayor will seek legal advice/remedy regarding this matter. He also instructed Mrs. Emma V. Ambagan, former Municipal Accountant-Designee to coordinate with COA Provincial Office at Romblon, Romblon for the retrieval of such documents.
observations.reason: No financial reports pertaining to years 1994 to 2002 were submitted to the Audit Team due to unavailability of the related documents needed in the preparation of the reports.  The Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer have already exerted extra effort in digging up such records.

observations.managementAction:
The Inventory Committee conducted the complete physical count of all inventories and PPE.


The Inventory Committee prepared/accomplished the corresponding RPCI and RPCPPE and submitted them to the audit team on February 10, 2022.

The RPCI and RPCPEE were submitted on February 10, 2022, that was an improvement of one day compared to last year’s submission on February 11, 2021.


Verification of the General Journal revealed that the Municipal Accountant already made the necessary adjustments in the books to correct the noted erroneous recording/classification in CY 2020 audit.















The Inventory Committee and the Municipal Accountant already reconciled the result of the count with the property records and the accounting records.
observations.reason:
Some deficiencies were still noted in the submitted RPCI and RPCPPE particularly for the columns for description, stock number, whereabouts, and conditions.

Albeit the one-day improvement, the reports were still submitted late and thus incurred delay of 10 calendar days. 









The accountable officer concerned has still not explained the missing and lost properties.




Verification of the RPCPPE disclosed that details of some of the PPE were still not yet identified and provided in the report.





No IIRUP was prepared and submitted to the audit team.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that:

The Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant - Designee to exert extra effort to account the unreconciled items appearing in the Bank Reconciliation Statements to faithfully present the correct balances of the account for the period; and

The Municipal Accountant (i) ensure that the monthly BRS for all bank accounts are supported with debit/credit memos for verification as required under Item 3.4 of COA Circular No. 96-011; (ii) continue securing bank statements of prior years to establish the correct balance of the Cash in Bank; and (iii) submit the subsidiary ledgers of all the bank accounts.
observations.managementAction:
On September 17, 2021, the Municipal Treasurer sent another letter-request to DBP for previous years’ bank statements. On the same date, the DBP Kalibo Branch also sent an e-mail to DBP Main Branch forwarding the said request.



Debit/Credit memos were already attached to the monthly BRS of all bank accounts.  





Subsidiary Ledgers were already submitted.
observations.reason:
Verification of the submitted bank reconciliation statements for CY 2021 revealed that the unreconciled items still exist.  




Still failed to secure the Bank Statements of prior years to establish the correct balances of the Cash in Bank.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:

The Municipal Mayor (i) require all accountable officers and employees to immediately liquidate their outstanding cash advances giving more emphasis to the long outstanding cash advances; (ii) resort to the withholding of salaries of those accountable officers and employees who still fail or persistently refuse to settle/liquidate their cash advances pursuant to Item 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002; (iii) exert extra efforts to recover outstanding cash advances from accountable officers who are no longer in the service nor connected with the Municipality; and (iv) strictly require an Office Clearance from Money and Property accountabilities, pursuant to Sections 200 and 256, Volume I of the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual (GAAM) from all personnel who seek retirement, resignation, transfer or claim for death benefits in order that all accountabilities could be appropriately settled; and


The Municipal Accountant (i) maintain Subsidiary Ledgers and schedules for the accounts used for cash advances to facilitate monitoring of the amount outstanding per employee to ensure that no additional cash advance will be granted to those with outstanding balances and to support the amounts reflected in the financial statements and submit same to the Audit Team; (ii) refrain from granting additional cash advances to any official or employee unless previous cash advance given to him/her is first settled or a proper accounting thereof is made, 
pursuant to Item 4.1.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002; (iii) ensure that supporting documents attached to the liquidation reports are accurate and complete before processing the claims in accordance with Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445; and (iv) observe the existing rules and regulations and rates of expenses and allowances for official local travels of government personnel provided in E.O. No. 77.
observations.managementAction:
Verification of Payrolls disclosed that the LGU already withheld an amount of P200 to P700 from the salaries of officers and employees







For personnel who seek retirement and transfer of office, Office Clearance from Money and Property Accountabilities have always been required by the LGU.




The Municipal Accountant now maintains subsidiary ledgers for the cash advance accounts.


The Municipal Accountant now requires full liquidation of previous cash advance before granting a new one. 

As observed in the liquidation reports, the Municipal Accountant now ensures that supporting documents attached to the liquidation reports are accurate and complete before processing the claims.

Perusal of transaction documents revealed that the Municipal Accountant now observes the rules and regulations and the rates of travel expenses for official local travel of government personnel.
observations.reason:
Long outstanding cash advances were still unliquidated as at year end.






iii. Inquiry with the Municipal Accountant disclosed that no action was taken by the management in CY 2021 to recover outstanding cash advances from accountable officers who have separated already from the municipality or no longer in the service.






There was one instance on February 1, 2021 wherein the Municipal Mayor was granted cash advance despite having an unliquidated amount of P99,276.97.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and management agreed that:

The LDRRMO prepare and submit the LDRRMFIP and Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRM to the Audit Team in accordance with Items 5.1.2 and 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002; 

The Municipal Accountant strictly adhere with the guidelines set forth by (i) COA Circular No. 2012-002 and COA Circular No. 2014-002 in the recording of the utilization of the LDRRM Fund; (ii) Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 in the documentation of transactions; and (iii) submit the needed documents noted in the payments of insurance premium for further verification; and


The Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) strictly adhere to the requirements of Annex H of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 on the procurement of goods and services thru Small Value Procurement and Negotiated Procurement thru Two-Failed Bidding.
observations.managementAction:
The LDRRMO prepared and submitted the LDRRMFIP and Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRM to the audit team.


The Municipal Accountant has properly recorded the transactions under the General Fund.


Some lacking documents noted in CY 2020 audit were already complied with such as the Mayor’s Permit, Certificate of PhilGEPS Registration, proof of posting of invitation or request for submission of price quotation on the PhilGEPS website, and Notice of Award.

The needed documents noted in the payments of insurance premium for further verification were submitted to the Audit Team

The BAC has mostly complied with the requirements of Annex H of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 on the procurement of goods and services thru Small Value Procurement
observations.reason:
However, the expenditures totaling P363,349.80 charged against the Special Trust Fund were debited outright to Trust Liability – DRRMF instead of recognizing first the proper expense account.

Some documentary deficiencies were still noted in the disbursements of fund totaling P661,899.00 sourced from the LDRRMF. 


Some documentary deficiencies and procedural lapses were still noted in the procurement of supplies and materials through Negotiated Procurement under Emergency Cases and supply and delivery of Goods for LDRRMO Stockpile I, II and III procured through Public Bidding.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the BAC and its members be guided by the provision of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 in the procurement of goods and services and be always abreast with the issuances of the GPPB so that various requirements thereto are always complied with. 

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) properly record donations received by the Municipality in accordance with COA Circular No. 2014-002; (b) disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the procurement, issuance and inventory of goods and property plant and equipment procured by the Municipality including those acquired through donations either in cash or in-kind; and (c) be guided by the description of accounts in the Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.
observations.managementAction:
Based on perused documents, the BAC has strived to comply with the procedures and requirements of R.A. No. 9184 in the procurement of goods and services through small value procurement


The Municipal Accountant has already recorded the donations received by the municipality and disclosed them in the Notes to Financial Statements.
observations.reason: However, there were still some procedural lapses and documentary deficiencies noted in the procurement of goods and services through negotiated procurement under emergency cases.

observations.managementAction:
Auditorial review of contracts revealed that the BAC strived to comply with the processes and documentary requirements of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 





For projects constructed or implemented under the administration/supervision of the management, those were completed within the targeted date.


The Municipal Engineer already prepared and submitted some needed reports and documents. 




The Municipal Accountant already ensured that supporting documents attached to the vouchers are valid, proper, legal and complete before processing of claims.

Lacking documents noted in CY 2020 audit were submitted to the audit team.

The Municipal Accountant uses the Chart of Accounts provided by COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.
observations.reason:
However, some procedural lapses, documentary deficiencies, and delays were still noted in nine infrastructure projects totaling P9,092,767.69 in           CY 2021.





However, for projects constructed by contractors, those have repetitive request for time extension thus nine projects were completed beyond the targeted date. 


As already mentioned in the preceding evaluation, there were repetitive requests with similar reasons for time extensions which have stretched the contract period by a number of days. Those requests however were wanting of supporting documents to fully ascertain the veracity of the offered explanation.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Budget Officer strictly observe the general limitations on personal services cited under Section 325 (a) of R.A. No. 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 98.  The Municipal Mayor, being the Local Chief Executive, should always be guided by the conditions of the agency’s annual and supplemental budgets to ensure that the budgeting requirements are completely complied with. As part of his fiscal responsibility, he should allow only payments of personal services costs and other expenditures expressly authorized by law.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor together with the department heads increase its efforts in raising its revenues/receipts such as increasing tax collection efficiency and generating revenue related projects/activities to increase the base of PS limitation.
observations.managementAction:
Although the municipality was still noted with PS Excess  of  P1,479,875.87
 in the budget review of the Sangguniang Panlalawigan, the actual PS cost in CY 2021 no longer exceeded the PS cap and in fact they still have an allowance for PS amounting to P2,960,721.99.

With that, the Municipal Mayor together with the Municipal Budget Officer already observed the general limitations on personal services. 


The municipality’s total revenue increased from P65,434,339.87 in CY 2020 to P69,743,734.18 in CY 2021 or by P4,309,394.31. The upward movement was mainly due to the increase in the receipt of IRA of the municipality.
observations.reason: The local collections of the municipality have decreased in CY 2021. For instance, collection for permit fees was down by 20.92 percent. Likewise, collections for seaport system fees and garbage fees decreased by 73.37 and 81.75 percent, respectively.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:

The Municipal Mayor along with all the concerned officials and employees strictly follow the provisions of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1 in planning the programs/projects/activities funded by the 20% DF and their implementation.  See to it that the schedule of implementation as planned in the APP is strictly observed and those development projects identified in the AIP are fully implemented for the benefit of the constituents;

The BAC and its Secretariat and TWG (i) ensure compliance with the requirements prescribed under the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to prevent the same lapses in future procurements; and (ii) strengthen internal control over the post-qualification of bidders to ensure completeness and veracity of the post-qualified documents as reported in the Post- Qualification Evaluation Reports; and
The Municipal Accountant (i) ensure that supporting documents are thoroughly checked as to accuracy and completeness before processing claims pursuant to Section 474 b(5) of R.A. No. 7160; and (ii) submit the lacking documents mentioned above for verification.
observations.managementAction:
The municipality has utilized a total amount of P8,754,038.17 as at December 31, 2021 which translates to a relatively and commendable high utilization rate of 66.75 percent of the Development Fund for the year.






The BAC and its Secretariat and TWG already ensured compliance with the requirements prescribed under the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184




The Municipal Accountant (i) already ensured that the supporting documents are thoroughly checked as to accuracy and completeness before processing of claims and (ii) submitted the lacking documents noted in CY 2020 audit.
observations.reason:
However, it was noted that the three projects totaling P2,750,000.00 under the current year 20 percent Development Fund and five projects totaling P4,112,598.20 under the continuing appropriations were still not  implemented as at December 31, 2021.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:

The Municipal Mayor and LGU GFPS Technical Working Group (TWG) strictly comply with the comments and recommendations made by the DILG Provincial Office on the review of their GPB so as not to put the efforts of the Reviewing Officer in vain and accordingly the endorsement of the said Office could be secured;

The LGU GFPS TWG (i) conduct a gender analysis of their attributed programs and projects through the administration of the HGDG tool as prescribed under Item 3.0.C.4.1.a of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01; (ii) ensure compliance to Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 on the submission of the Annual GPB and GAD Accomplishment Report (AR) to the Audit Team; and (iii) prepare the Annual GAD AR in coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to avoid discrepancy in the report prepared; and 


The Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) spearhead the setting up and maintenance of the GAD database to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management install internal control mechanisms by using the RIS and keeping stock cards to ensure that relief goods are properly accounted for and safeguarded from possible theft, loss, and wastage.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Barangay and the MSWDO to submit to the Auditor a photocopy of the ID specimen signature of the beneficiaries pursuant to Item 9 of the MOA between the DSWD and the Municipality dated April 24, 2020 and Section VII.A.1 of DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 09, series of 2020, dated April 9, 2020. Hence, the effectiveness of the program could not be determined whether only those who suffered the greatest impact of the implementation of the community quarantine were given assistance.

observations.reason: Some of the beneficiaries of the ESP were locally stranded individuals and were back at work or out of town looking for jobs. As a result, they could not gather all the documentary requirements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that, henceforth, they will purchase their rice requirements from the NFA in accordance with EO No. 51 dated December 22, 1998, except upon issuance of a waiver from the latter.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality had already purchased their rice requirements from the NFA in CY 2021, except upon issuance of a waiver from the latter.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to stop the practice of procurement on a reimbursement basis and strictly observe the proper procedures in procurement as provided in the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and relevant policies issued by the GPPB. Further, we recommended that, henceforth, Management strictly comply with Sections 4.1.6 and 4.3.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 on the utilization of cash advances.
observations.managementAction: Management had already observed the proper procedures for the procurement of goods as provided under R.A. No. 9184.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that, henceforth, the BAC require the suppliers to submit the original notarized OSS before or after award of the contract but before payment is made and include a warranty clause in the contract or Purchase Orders in accordance with GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020. We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure the completeness of the documentary requirements for emergency procurement before payment is made.
observations.managementAction: The BAC Secretary submitted the notarized OSS of Frunie Mart and Payalin Trading Inc.
observations.reason: Examination of the submitted OSS disclosed that it did not conform with the revised OSS for Emergency Procurement under the Bayanihan Act, hence, additional provisions were not included therein.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to require the BAC, through its Secretariat, to post in the GPPB online portal the updated APP, NOA, and other information relative to the Municipality’s procurement projects in accordance with the provisions of GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 dated April 6, 2020. We further recommended that the BAC Secretariat issue a certification on the posting of the NOA, Contract or Purchase Orders in any conspicuous place in the premises of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The BAC Secretariat had already posted the Notice of Awards on the Municipality’s bulletin board.
observations.reason: The posting requirements in the PhilGEPs website were still not complied with.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer assign only regular employees as collecting officers and, henceforth, ensure strict adherence to existing laws and regulations that all accountable officers/employees shall be properly bonded to safeguard government funds.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer requested for regular employees to be assigned to the collection.
observations.reason: However, due to limited personnel, the JOs were still allowed to collect.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require the collectors to remit their collections daily in accordance with Sections 29 and 30 of the NGAS Manual, Volume I to safeguard government funds.
observations.managementAction: On December 23, 2019, the Municipal Mayor issued Memorandum No. 246-S-2019, assigning a JO the collectors to Maayo Shipping to supervise the collectors to remit their collections daily.
observations.reason: There were still collections which were not immediately remitted by the collectors to the Municipal Treasurer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require all collectors to prepare the RCDs and see to it that they are completely and properly filled up; check the accuracy of the Accountability for Accountable Forms by doing a physical count of all accountable forms; and carry forward the correct beginning balance to the succeeding RCDs.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer had already required the collectors to prepare the RCDs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management effect the increase of the fidelity bond commensurate to their accountabilities, in accordance with the Bureau of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009, in order to provide adequate protection for the Municipality’s resources.
observations.managementAction: Additional budget was requested by the Municipal Treasurer to increase the appropriation for fidelity bond premiums. The approved amount of bond for the DO-Designate was already increased to ₱350,250.00 with a minimum and maximum cash accountability of ₱500,001.00 to ₱750,000.00, respectively.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management fast track the implementation of all BuB projects to ensure that the desired benefits/services are delivered to the constituents on time. Otherwise, Management shall return the unspent fund to the source agency for other agencies to utilize the same for other equally important government projects/programs. We also recommended that the Project Monitoring Committee of the Local Poverty Reduction Action Team (LPRAT) or designated focal person continue to monitor the implementation of all BuB projects pursuant to the guidelines under DBM-DILG-DSWD-NAPC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 6 and 7. Lastly, we recommended that the BAC strictly observe the recommended timelines in the procurement activity under Annex C of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and GPPB Circular No. 05-2016 dated December 20, 2016 to avoid delay in the implementation of the projects.
observations.managementAction: Some of the unutilized funds under the BuB Program were already utilized, but there were projects which remained unimplemented due to the COVID-19 pandemic. On February 14, 2022, the Municipality returned to the Bureau of Treasury the unexpended balances of the BuB amounting to ₱484,400.00.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management refrain from charging expenditures not listed under Section 5.0 of NDRRMC, DBM, and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 to the LDRRMF and maximize the use of the 70% LDRRMF for more worthwhile projects that will really eliminate flood risks, lessen adverse impacts of hazards and calamities, and train and equip residents to be able to respond to any disaster or calamity.
observations.reason: In CY 2021, the 70% LDRRMF was partly utilized for fuel, oil, and lubricants, which could not be considered as disaster-related expenditure. Further, it was observed that 84.10% of the 70% LDRRMF was only utilized for the procurement of equipment while no programs and projects that would completely avoid or at least lessen or limit adverse impacts of hazards and related disasters were implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer or Head of the Inventory Committee complete fill up the RPCPPE and coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the balance per count with the balance per ledger card for each type of PPE. Any discrepancies between the actual count and the ledger card should be immediately verified and adjusted, if necessary, to fairly present the movable PPE account at year-end.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer submitted the RPCPPE as of December 31, 2021.
observations.reason: The conduct of a physical inventory taking of PPE was not completed because discrepancies were noted between the balance per book and the RPCPPE.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the person immediately accountable for the unserviceable properties turn over the items to the Municipal Treasurer for cancellation of the Memorandum Receipt/Acknowledgement Receipt for the cancellation of the Equipment. We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) create an Appraisal and Disposal Committee to appraise the value of unserviceable properties for the immediate disposal and drop thereof from the books of accounts in order to fairly present the PPE account in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Some of the persons immediately accountable for the unserviceable properties had already turned over the items to the Municipal Treasurer.
observations.reason: The assigned personnel in the Office of the Municipal Treasurer were still working out the inventory and inspection of unserviceable properties.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant evaluate the collectability of receivables, review and analyze the accounts and, if not possible due to the absence of records, the Local Chief Executive request for authority to write-off from the Commission on Audit.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant had already made the necessary journal entry on request for authority to write-off the dormant account amounting to ₱38,335.58 in accordance with COA Decision No. 2019-001 dated December 9, 2019.
observations.reason: The documentary requirements for the request for authority to write-off other dormant accounts were still not fully complied with.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LDRRMO coordinate with the MBO and the Municipal Accountant, and obtain data on the availability of the funds. Henceforth, require the MDRRM Council to include in its LDRRMFIP the PPAs to be funded by the unexpended LDRRMF. We also recommended that the LDRRM Officer submit the monthly report on the Sources and Utilization of Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund on or before the 15th day after the end of each month to the Office of the Auditor pursuant to Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 to monitor disbursements.
observations.managementAction: The quarterly utilization report of the LDRRMF was submitted to the Auditor on February 14, 2022.
observations.reason: Based on the report, the PPAs to be funded from the unexpended balances of the LDRRMF were still not included in the LDRRMFIP. The Monthly Report of Utilization of LDRRMF was not submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Council for the Protection of Children (MCPC) focus and prioritize on the formulation and implementation of the PPAs of the LCPC in accordance with Section 15 of RA No. 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012.
observations.reason: There was a lack of manpower to implement and handle the programs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management, henceforth, strictly adhere to the provisions of RA 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012 on the use of the one percent of IRA in the implementation of PPAs of the LCPC.

Based on the list of PPAs for LCPC, it was noted that there were programs which were still not in accordance with DILG Circular No. 2012-120, such as payment of the services of daycare workers.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare a certified list of taxpayers and their taxes due and collectible for the year to serve as basis for the Municipal Accountant in recording the Real Property Tax Receivables/Special Education Tax Receivables and their corresponding credits at the beginning of the year pursuant to Section 20 of the NGAS Manual, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer had submitted to the Municipal Accountant the certified list of taxpayers and their tax dues and collectibles as the basis for recording the RPT/SET receivables.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the officials and employees concerned immediately refund in full the productivity enhancement incentive received by them for CY 2010. Henceforth, the local chief executive should ensure that the grant of similar benefits complies with prescribed guidelines, specifically insofar as observance of the PS limitation is concerned, so that the disbursements will not be disallowed in audit.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the department heads concerned process the titles of the documents and require the Municipal Assessor to record the said land in the books of accounts as soon as the titles have been issued.

observations.reason: Due to the lack of evidence of a deed of conveyance, it was difficult for the Municipal Assessor to locate the documents required to title the lots.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the (a) City Treasurer to prepare a schedule of all unreleased checks every end of the year for submission to the City Accounting Office (CAO); and (b) City Accountant to make the necessary adjustments for the restoration to the respective Cash-in-Bank account of the total amount of unreleased checks to arrive at the correct balance of the Cash and other related accounts.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations  that the LCE (a) require the  City Accountant to (i) provide an allowance for impairment on receivable accounts based on an analysis of collection rating in the previous years to ensure the fairness of accounts presentation in the financial statements; (ii) file a request for authority to write-off the dormant and long outstanding receivable accounts to COA after the conditions and requirements are followed pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016; (iii)  refrain from granting cash advances during the last days of the year to ensure the liquidation of the same within the year; and (iv) strictly observe COA Circular No. 97-002 on the granting of cash advances and liquidation thereof.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the LCE require the City General Services Officer and the City Accountant  to (a)  reconcile  the recorded balances of Inventory accounts of the CAO  with  the RPCI maintained by GSO; (b) implement adequate controls  by documenting   the correct receipt and issuance of the inventories  pursuant to cited issuances of GAM for LGUs, Volume I; and  (c) maintain   SLCs and SCs pursuant to arrive at reliable and correct balances of the Inventory accounts.

We recommended that the LCE require the (a) City General Services Officer to (i) continuously update the IIRUP to serve  as  basis in dropping from the books all unserviceable property; (ii) exclude from the RPCPPE, properties/items below the ₱15,000.00 capitalization threshold and subsequently, issue the corresponding Inventory Custodian Slip (ICS) to establish accountability over these tangible items; and (iii) issue and update the PARs for all PPE items, starting with the current year’s acquisition until all items are covered with PARs and renew/update the previously issued ones at least once every three years or whenever there is a transfer of accountability; (b) City Accountant (i) to derecognize  as PPE  all issued tangible items  which fall below the ₱15,000.00  capitalization threshold;  and (ii) reconcile the discrepancy between the PPELCs and GL by properly maintaining the required PPELCs for each class of PPE; and (c) Inventory Committee (IC) to (i) conduct regular and complete physical count of all properties of the City for CY 2021; and  (ii) prepare the corresponding RPCPPE and  indicate the complete details on the whereabouts, condition and other relevant information relative to the PPE.
observations.managementAction:
Issuance of ICS is ongoing.







Management submitted an incomplete Physical Inventory Report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE require the (a)  City Treasurer to (i) continuously exert efforts in coordinating with the other concerned offices to improve the collection performance of the City by ensuring compliance with revenue regulations; (ii) comply with the submission of the Certified List of Real Property Tax Delinquencies on or before December 31 of the year and furnish  the Sanggunian  of the list of delinquent taxpayers  for the  enforcement of remedies for collection of the taxes delinquencies; and (iii) coordinate with the concerned offices in determining the taxability of printing and publication enterprises for imposition and collection of the taxes due them; and (b) City Assessor’s Office to maintain an updated Listing of Idle Lands to impose additional ad valorem taxes on such real property units.  

We also recommended that the (c) Sangguniang Panglungsod (SP)  fast track the approval of the Revised Local Revenue Code; and (d) LCE to  issue an Executive Order creating an oversight committee who shall continuously review, set and/or adopt reasonable local fees and charges in accordance with the prescribed rules and regulations to enhance the collection performance of the City and reduce its  high dependency on its IRA.

We recommended that the LCE (a) through the City General Services Officer, ensure the adequate insurance on all property, assets and interests with the GIF-GSIS; (b) require the budget committee to include in its budget the insurance expenses of all insurable properties of the City; and (c) direct the Property-in-Charge to prepare the Property Inventory Form (PIF) and facilitate the submission of the updated list of insurable properties with the GSIS.

We recommended that the LCE require the (a) authorized LGU representative, the CSWD Officer and other concerned officials to (i) impose strict measures in profiling the beneficiaries specifically in times of calamities; and (ii) conduct  strict house-to-house screening in accordance with the requirements of DSWD MC No. 9 series of 2020; and (iii) request assistance from the DSWD Regional Office No. III for the immediate implementation of Web Based DSWD Calamity Assistance Request and Monitoring System; and (b) City Accountant attach the payrolls and signed payrolls  to the cash advance DVs and  LRs, respectively.

We recommended that the LCE require (a) the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and the City Engineering Office (CEO) to strictly comply with the posting requirements on  the Report on Fund Utilization of Bayanihan Grant not only in the agency website, but also in three conspicuous places; and (b) the City Accountant to (i) comply with the documentary requirements in the  procurement of goods and services; and  (ii) to review the completeness of  supporting documents before the DVs  are processed for payment.

We recommended that the LCE require the City Human Resource Management Officer and the Department/Unit Head concerned to (a) submit documents to  establish the eligibility of personnel and validate the  authority of the names listed in the SRA payroll; and (b) payees to submit the DTRs, statement of duties and responsibilities, accomplishment reports signed by their supervisors indicating  that they directly catered to   or  were in contact with COVID-19 patients, persons under investigation, or persons under monitoring; copies of contracts for CoS/JOs and other pertinent documents to support the payment of such claims.

We recommended that the LCE require the City Accountant and the City Human Resource Development Officer to (a) require  the concerned offices to attach to the DVs for COVID-19 Hazard Pay the lacking supporting documents such as DTRs, Authority to report to work/Office Order, and Accomplishment reports; and (b) ensure sufficient documentation on disbursements to properly establish the validity and propriety of all the City government transactions.

We recommended that the LCE (a) require the General Service Officer to consistently oversee the receipt, distribution, and custody of all donated goods, supplies and equipment and shall establish a systematic process flow for such items; and (b) the City Accountant to account for the inventory of the remaining relief goods per category in the books or in the registries in accordance with the prescribed regulations.

We recommended that the   LCE require the (a) concerned City officials to fully implement the PPAs under the 20% DF in accordance  with  DILG and DBM JMC No. 01 and DILG/DBM JMC No. 2017-1; (b)  City Engineer  to  (i) cause the repair works on the noted deficiencies so as not to inflict further damage to the newly constructed projects pursuant to the defects liability period for infrastructure projects which shall be one year from project completion up to final acceptance by the Government; and (ii)  fast-track the turn-over of  the MRF Building to OLSJDM for their immediate use.

We recommended that the LCE require the  (a) BAC, General Services Officer and City Accountant to strictly observe the documentary requirements on alternative modes of procurement as provided by R.A No. 9184 and other pertinent GPPB issuances; (b) City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (CDRRMO) to (i) utilize the fund strictly for disaster risk management activities in accordance with Section III of COA Circular No. 2014-002; and (ii) to  include in the annual LDRRMFIP the detailed activities to be funded from the prior years’ unutilized LDRRMF; and (c) City Accountant to make the necessary adjustments in the GF and TF books to properly record the unexpended LDRRMF and financial assistance in compliance with the prescribed accounting policies and procedures.

We recommended that the LCE require  the BAC to (a) strictly adopt the rules and procedures under  competitive public bidding  under the IRR of RA No. 9184 except for Procurement Projects covered within the conditions and threshold of the Alternative Methods of Procurement;  (b) submit the 26 POs with complete supporting documents; (c) indicate in the PO the correct modality  of procurement adopted; and (d) post the procurement opportunities and awarded contracts in the City’s  website, PhilGeps and GPPB portal.

We recommended that the LCE advise the City Solid  Management Board to (a) implement the waste segregation at source by enforcing the No Segregation, No Collection policy and by assisting the Barangays in the conduct of IECs in the “new normal” scheme amidst the pandemic;  (b)  conduct  regular ocular inspection of the MRFs in order to determine the status of actual operations, and availability of the required facilities and equipment to  make the MRF fully functional; and (c) implement waste diversion activities through (i) wide dissemination to the Barangay constituents of information necessary to reduce, reuse, recycle and compost the wastes effectively and (ii) development of business opportunities to indigent households to lessen the huge volume of wastes in the disposal facilities and attain the waste diversion targets.

We recommended that the LCE require the members of the GFPS to (a) continually review all GAD-related regulations to enhance their knowledge in the priority issues to be addressed through the Annual GPB in a realistic and attainable manner within the budget year; (b) require concerned offices to  adopt strategies to fully implement  GAD PPAs through alternative modalities such as the use of technology and virtual means of conducting seminars and trainings to achieve the GAD-related targets amidst the new normal; and (c) set-up appropriate systems and mechanisms for the generation, processing, review, and updating of sex-disaggregated data for a functional GAD database.

We reiterate our previous year’s recommendations that the  LCE require the City Accountant and City Treasurer to (a) continue to comply  with the requirements of the regulatory agencies on the withholding and remittance of the employee’s contributions and loan amortizations within the prescribed period; and (b) adhere to the latest tax regulations through timely withholding and remittance of taxes due from salaries and wages of personnel and for purchases of goods and services to avoid incurring penalties and  further support the government’s revenue generation.
We recommended that the LCE require the (a) persons liable to immediately settle the disallowances; and (b) City Accountant to correct the erroneous recording of settlement made.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to require 

(a) the City General Service (CGS) Officer to (i) ensure the completeness, accuracy and proper classifications of the data encoded in the PIAS; 

(ii) separately account and classify PPE and inventories in the RPCPPE and RPCI, respectively; and 


(b) the City Accountant and the CGS Officer to reconcile their respective PPE records and reports.
observations.managementAction:
The Management created an inventory team thru EO No. 19, series of 2021 to ensure completeness and proper classification of PPE


Continuous coordination between GSO and CAO

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the LCE concurred to direct the City General Services Officer and the City Accountant to coordinate in the implementation of the Perpetual Inventory Method in accounting of inventories pursuant to the said Manual.
observations.managementAction: The Management employs Perpetual Inventory Method

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the City Mayor concurred to (a) continue the efficient use of its 20% DF; and 

(b) instruct the Local Development Council (LDC) to explore ways on how to align future development projects with the demands of the times with requisite consideration of applicable regulations.
observations.managementAction: The Management continue the efficient use of its 20% DF

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the LCE agreed to 
(a) clearly identify the offices dedicated to the management of donations and delineate their respective functions and accountabilities; and 

(b) instruct the office/s concerned to (i) ensure the completeness and correctness of the data being reported to the oversight bodies and the people in general; and 

(ii) timely submit the reports required under COA Circular No. 2020-009 to NDRRMC, through the OCD, copy furnished the respective COA Auditors.
observations.managementAction: The Donation Management Committee was created thru EO No. 15, series of 202 to oversee, facilitate, monitor and document all forms of donations received by the LGU.
City Accountant used the prescribe accounts for inventories in the subsequent transactions.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the concerned offices to 
(a) render a satisfactory explanation on the payment of Hazard Pay to JOs and CoS covered by appropriation for MOOE; 
(b) henceforth, ensure that funding of the Hazard Pay is aligned with the requirements of DBM Budget Circular No. 2020-01; and 
(c) submit to the Audit Team the required documents to prove the regularity of the payments made.
observations.managementAction: Submitted the explanation and required documents that proved regularity.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the LCE is amenable to instruct the CSWD Officer and City Treasurer to 

(a) fully examine the master list of SAP beneficiaries against the SAP payrolls to detect erroneous payment and make necessary actions and corresponding adjustments to the liquidation report; 


(b) require the complete documentation on the distribution of SAP; and 

(c) call the payees concerned to explain the differences noted in the specimen signatures and photos, and take appropriate actions to ensure that the SAP fund was used as intended.
observations.managementAction:
Examined and investigated the list of SAP beneficiaries against SAP payrolls and detected 54 double payment.


Investigation disclosed that some were received by the relatives.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the LCE concurred to continually adhere to the provisions of JMC No. 2013-01 of the PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA to remain committed to its GAD mainstreaming efforts.
We commended the CAO and Treasury Office for their continuous adherence to revenue regulations and prompt remittance of the taxes to the BIR.
We commended the City for its continuous compliance with the GSIS law on the deduction and remittance of contributions and premiums due the System.
We commended the CAO and City Treasurer's Office for their continuous compliance on proper deductions and remittances of Pag-IBIG fund in accordance with the provisions of RA No. 9679.
We recommended and the LCE is amenable to continuously comply with the National Health Insurance Act by remitting all contributions withheld to the Philippine Health Insurance Corporation (PHIC).

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed (a) to continue its efforts in observing the controls over the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002 including the submission of the complete supporting documents of liquidation reports; and (b) instruct the concerned personnel to process the liquidation of ₱174,000.00 in order to recognize the respective expense on its utilization.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the (a) Municipal Accountant to base the recognition of RPT and SET receivables on the duly certified list showing the name of the taxpayers and due and collectible submitted by the Treasury Office; and (b) Municipal Treasurer to continue to prepare and furnish the Accounting Office the duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year in accordance with Section 20 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the Accounting Office and the Municipal Treasurer /Supply Officer (a) to conduct annual physical count of PPE and submit the RPCPPE to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of each year in accordance with Section 124 of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs; (b) to strongly consider the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts in accordance with COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020; and (c) to maintain updated PPELCs and PCs, respectively, for all LGU properties and reconcile the noted discrepancies while taking into account the necessary adjustment in the books, as necessary.
observations.reason: Reiterated in AAR CY 2021

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the Municipal Treasurer together with the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Planning and Development Office (MPDO) to cause the proper titling of the lots, both acquired and received as donation, in the name of the Municipality and take up in the books of account the equivalent value of donated lot where portion of the Municipal Building is situated.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the Accounting Office recognize the cost of the Municipal Office Building – Annex and the Recreational Park Building funded under the PDAF amounting to ₱1,500,000.00 and ₱3,000,000.00 respectively, and accordingly make adjustments on the depreciation expense/government equity account.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to (a) submit the cash advance voucher with covering transmittal letter enumerating the supporting documents of the confidential fund within seven days after the release of the check to the ATL; and (b) be apprised with the requirements of the COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND JC No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015 on the grant and utilization of Confidential Fund for the LGU.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) require the Petty Cash Custodian to (i) set-up an amount for PCF sufficient enough for non-recurring/non-regular, emergency and petty expenses of the Municipality; (ii) replenish the PCF when the disbursement reached at least 75%, or as the need requires; (iii) prepare PCV with an attachment of supporting documents like ORs and Acknowledgement Receipts, as the case may be, for each petty operating expense; (iv) prepare DV duly supported by a list/summary of PCVs, the PCVs and its supporting documents for PCF replenishments; (v) be guided with the proper utilization of PCF by ensuring that all transactions threshed out of PCF are not regular expenses, such as the AICS, of the LGU; (vi) faithfully comply with the provisions of Section 48 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs and COA Circular No. 97-002; (b) consider designating a Petty Cash Custodian other than the OIC-Treasurer to prevent comingling of LGU funds; and (c) require the Municipal Accountant to maintain the PCF using Imprest System and ensure the completeness of the supporting documents of DVs for the replenishment of the PCF in accordance with Sections 48 and 52 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to instruct the concerned officials to (a) tap the services of a geodetic engineer/s in conducting a survey or joint survey so that, once and for all, the metes and bounds of the subject lots are established; (b) exhaust all the remedies, judicial or extra-judicial settlements, with the private owner for the benefit of all the parties; and (c) after which, facilitate the transfer of the donated lots under the name of the Municipality of San Jose.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the (a) concerned offices to capacitate the Inventory Committee and for the Committee to give specific instructions to formulate and employ a strategy in conducting annual physical count; (b) Municipal Treasurer/Supply Officer to come up with the Local Road Inventory and Road Map and Local Road Network Property Card for all roads under the Agency’s jurisdiction; and (c) Accounting Office to disclose information on Road Networks in the Notes to the FS in accordance with COA Circular 2015-008.
observations.reason: Reiterated in AAR CY 2021

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the LCE agreed that the (a) BAC together with the TWG (i) prepare the post-qualification reports properly; (ii) regularly post the NTP and Contract of the projects in the PhilGEPS and any conspicuous place in the premises of the Municipality all this in compliance with R.A. 9184; (iii) facilitate the submission of lacking documents as discussed; (b) Municipal Engineer for the project contractors observe the proper details to be provided on project signs including the update thereof in accordance with the project status; and (c) Municipal Accountant check and ensure the completeness of documents before effecting payment.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) direct the MSWMB to prepare an Annual Work Plan and Budget showing the SWM activities to be implemented and the financial counterpart for each SWM activity, taking into consideration the initiatives identified in the ten-year SWM Plan; (b) through the Sanggunian, facilitate the adoption of approved SWM plan through Municipal Ordinance; (c) organize IEC Team to facilitate the conduct of IEC campaigns; (d) organize M&E team and require them to prepare Progress Reports in line with the Annual Work Plan and the SWM Plan; (e) operationalize the existing MRFs into operational in accordance with Section 32 of R.A. No. 9003 and (f) direct the MENRO, SWM Focal Person, and MPDC to design contingency plans in order to put back on track the implementation of the Ten-Year SWM Plan.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the Accounting Office to (a) furnish immediately the deficient documents and the unsubmitted DV charged under SEF with its supporting documents; (b) ensure that all disbursements are supported with complete documentation and that necessary data/information are provided before payment; (c) secure from the DepEd Accountant a certification confirming that all expenses chargeable against the SEF are net of the budgetary provision for the same or related item(s) in the DepEd budget and (d) refund the amount ₱10,000.00 charged under SEF which was used for travel and meal allowance, an expense not allowed to be charged against the SEF.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the (a) Municipal Planning Chief and Municipal Engineer to continue their collaborative efforts in pursuing the remaining and future LGSF-funded programs in accordance with the guidelines set by the DILG or the DBM, as the case may be; (b) BAC and its Secretariat to (i) submit to the Audit Team the lacking documents as enumerated and warrant the completeness of supporting documents that need to be prepared/submitted in the procurement stages of projects in accordance with the provisions of DILG MC 2019-71, DILG MC 2020-088 and R.A. No. 9184 and its Revised IRR; (ii) prepare the quarterly reports of fund utilization of the future LGSF-FAs and post the same in public places in accordance with LBC No. 119 s. 2019; and (c) Municipal Accountant to ensure that all necessary documents to be attached to the DVs are present before making the payment/s.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) in coordination with the SB, formally constitute clear guidelines and criteria concerning the provision of reasonable communication expenses to qualified officials and employees by providing qualifications on who are entitled to claim, setting a limit on the allowable monthly communication expenses to be claimed, and providing provision on the ownership of mobile units in favor of the LGU. For the expense cap, the limit should be carefully reviewed and should be based on all available plans offered by the telecommunication companies choosing the most economic and efficient plan. The table presentation in paragraph 15.5 may be used as a reference; (b) direct the concerned officials and employees to (i) submit the SOA in support to claiming of mobile expenses; (ii) provide certifications that all NDD, National Operated Assisted Calls and International Operated Calls are official calls to only verified official calls by the qualified officials/employees; and (iii) refund the excess payment of mobile allowance for CY 2020.

We recommended and the Management agreed to comply with the provisions of COA Circular No. 77-61 by ensuring that: (a) claims for payment of diesel/fuel are supported with duly accomplished Driver’s Trip Tickets and approved RIV or its equivalent; (b) Charge Slips which support the RIV bear the approval of the concerned officials and the plate number of the vehicle issued with diesel/fuel; (c) summary of gasoline expenses submitted by the supplier is verified prior to processing and payment ; and (d) provide an explanation why BAC Resolution under DV No. 101-2020-12-1371 was only signed by two members of the BAC.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) in coordination with SB, formulate clear policies, procedures and guidelines as basis in the grant of AICS, in accordance with the provisions set forth in the DSWD MC No. 02 S. 2014; (b) designate a bonded special disbursing officer who will manage the release of outright cash assistance given to AICS beneficiaries; and (c) direct the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer (MSWDO) to ensure that the necessary documents required in the DSWD MC are prepared and submitted.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the (a) Acting Municipal Treasurer (i) strictly observe the mandatory daily deposit/remittance of collections of the Agency; (ii) ensure that the collections are deposited intact; (iii) properly prepare the RCD supported with ORs and machine-validated deposit slips; (iv) prepare the monthly RAAF and furnish copy to the Audit Team; (v) fill-out the ORs properly upon issuance to the taxpayers/clients and (vi) strictly comply with the processes laid down by the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs and Section 69 of P.D. No. 1445 with respect to preparation of reports and remittance of collections; and (b) Acting Municipal Accountant ensure that all deposits made by the Collecting Officer is validated against the bank statements of the Municipality.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that the (a) Municipal Accountant, and the Municipal Treasurer (i) seriously address the persisting issue on the delay in the submission of mandatory reports to COA by requiring that only those DVs with complete supporting documents are processed and paid; and (ii) strictly comply with the submission of DVs and supporting documents within the reglementary period as prescribed by COA Circular 2009-006 and Manual on the NGAS for LGUs.
We recommended and LCE was amenable that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer remit intact all taxes withheld within the prescribed period.

We recommended and the LCE agreed that HDMF monthly premiums should be continuously deducted by the Agency Authorized Officer (AAO) from HRMO due from employees and fully remitted to the HDMF by the Municipal Treasurer within the reglementary period set by the said law.
We recommended and the LCE concurred that the AAO from HRMO continue to ensure the monthly withholding and timely remittance of premiums due to the PHIC by the Municipal Treasurer.
We recommended and the LCE agreed that the (a) AAO from HRMO, Municipal Accountant and Treasurer continue deducting and remitting GSIS premiums and amortizations within the prescribed timeline.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) direct the BAC and its Secretariat (i) to submit the lacking documents noted on the BGCM fund utilization to the Audit Team; (ii) provide a reasonable explanation as to the unsigned/incompletely/improperly filled-out forms and supporting documents; (iii) faithfully adhere with the GPPB Circulars and other relevant laws, rules and regulations specifically in the emergency procurement of goods; (b) require the Municipal Accountant to (i) recognize/classify expenses to their appropriate accounts/purpose; and (ii) provide the list of recipients or any other equivalent documents for the distribution of relief goods.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to (a) direct the concerned offices to completely and properly fill-out the PO/PR/IAR; and (b) through the BAC and its Secretariat (i) submit pictures, certifications or any documents to support the posting of NOA and PO in conspicuous place in the premises of the Municipality; (ii) secure the printed name of the contractor in NOA and NTP; and (iii) ensure that the date of receipt of NTP is provided.

We recommended and the LCE was amenable that the Municipal Accountant (i) submit immediately the DVs with their supporting documents pertaining to the noted transactions; and (ii) strictly comply with the submission of DVs and supporting documents within the reglementary period as prescribed by COA Circular 2009-006.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the (a) listed officers/officials/employees to immediately refund the overpaid hazard pay and SRA with a combined total of ₱470,297.50; (b) Acting HRMO and other concerned offices to submit the lacking supporting documents/records/orders to the Audit Team for payments of hazard pay and SRA and accordingly observe compliance on the documentary requirements for these kinds of personnel incentives; and (c) Municipal Accountant to ensure that (i) all necessary supporting documents are attached to these claims before approving the payments; (ii) correct computation based on the guidelines set by Administrative Orders and DBM Circulars are effected in coming-up with total amount to be paid to the claimants; and (iii) proper account expense accounts are used in recording hazard pay.

We recommended that Management enjoin the concerned department heads to strictly comply with the accounting and reporting guidelines on local roads management system, and to earnestly perform their respective duties and responsibilities set forth under COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated October 23, 2015. Henceforth, we recommended that the Inventory Committee conduct annual physical count of all its Local Road Networks and results thereof reported in the RPCLRN to be submitted to the Auditor and Accounting Unit not later than January 31 of each year. The RPCLRN shall be reconciled with the accounting records and the necessary adjusting entries be prepared before the statement date.
observations.reason: Physical inventory of LRN was not yet conducted due to COVID-19 pandemic. Accordingly, the depreciation expense was not provided in the absence of the RPCLRN.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require the collectors to remit their collections daily in accordance with Sections 29 and 30 of the NGAS Manual, Volume I to safeguard government funds against loss or misappropriation.
observations.reason: The Agency Action Plan (AAPSI), Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD) and Cash Receipts Journal (CRJ) were not submitted to the Auditor, hence, compliance on the audit recommendation could not be validated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require all collectors to prepare the RCDs and see to it that these are completely and properly filled up; check the accuracy of the Accountability for Accountable Forms by doing physical count of all accountable forms; and carry forward the correct beginning balance to the succeeding RCDs.
observations.reason: The Agency Action Plan (AAPSI) and the Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD) were not submitted to the Auditor, hence, compliance on the audit recommendation could not be validated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant require all the PHWs to submit proof of actual exposure to specific health hazards and occupational risks, and documents required under Section 5.8 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to support their hazard pay. Henceforth, Management pay hazard pay based on the actual days of exposure to risks or danger in accordance with Section 7 of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 7305.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant and the PHWs had not yet submitted the lacking documentary requirements for the payment of hazard pay in CY 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer or Head of Inventory Committee completely fill up the RPCPPE and coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the balance per count with the balance per ledger card for each type of PPE. Any discrepancies between the actual count and the ledger card should be immediately verified and adjusted, if necessary, to fairly present the movable PPE account at year-end.
observations.reason: Physical count of government properties was not conducted in CY 2020 due to the pandemic, hence, the RPCPPE was not prepared and submitted to the Auditor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Agriculturist to establish the actual status of the breeding stock dispersal program by tracing the whereabouts and condition of the original breeding stocks and their offspring, if any, and render a report to the Municipal Accountant which includes the fair market value of each stock, for proper recording in the books of accounts. Henceforth, the Municipal Agriculturist shall keep systematic records of births, deaths, and disposal of breeding stocks. In case of death or disposal, facilitate the request for relief of accountability in accordance with Sections 151 and 152 of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992, for dropping the account from the books, if warranted. We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant provide proper disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statements in consonance with PPSAS 27 to provide information that may affect the fairness of the presentation of the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Agricultural Officer (MAO) informed that they could not establish the actual status or condition of the biological assets because there was no proper turnover from the former. However, she assured that, henceforth, monitoring of newly acquired biological assets shall be made in accordance with the audit recommendations.
observations.reason: The Municipal Agricultural Officer (MAO) was not able to conduct an inventory of the breeding stocks and the prevailing market price of each stocks, hence, the biological assets were not stated at its fair market value in accordance with PPSAS No. 27.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the accounting office should update the Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards. Likewise, the municipal treasurer’s office should maintain property cards. Accordingly, the two offices should make a periodic reconciliation of these records. Moreover, the inventory committee shall conduct a reliable physical inventory count at least once a year to determine which of their properties are actually existing. A team composed of the General Services and Accounting Department personnel should conduct the physical inventory count and reconcile the book and property records. Accordingly, the Accounting Department shall effect the correcting entries, where applicable, when properly supported with documentation. Finally, Management should drop the unserviceable properties from the inventory reports and dispose the same in accordance with the procedures outlined above.
observations.reason: A physical count of government properties was not conducted in CY 2020 due to the pandemic. As a result, reconciliation of records could not be made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant see to it that the PCF is not used for regular and recurring expenses and only use it in the non-recurring, emergency and petty expenses.
observations.managementAction: The BAC, in compliance with the existing provisions of RA No. 9184 had conducted biddings on the procurement of fuel, oil and lubricants. The procurement was duly published, however, despite the widest dissemination of information, suppliers operating within the territorial jurisdiction and from the adjacent municipalities never shown interest by way of participating in the public bidding. Further, the suppliers verbally disclosed that they have no tax clearance. Payments of fuel, oil and lubricants were still made out of PCF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant evaluate the collectability of receivables, review and analyze the accounts and, if not possible due to the absence of records, the Local Chief Executive request for authority to write-off from the Commission on Audit.
observations.reason: The subject receivable accounts were still outstanding as of December 31, 2020 because there were no adequate records to support the accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare a certified list of taxpayers and their taxes due and collectible for the year to serve as basis for the Municipal Accountant in recording the Real Property Tax Receivables/Special Education Tax Receivables and their corresponding credits at the beginning of the year pursuant to Section 20 of the NGAS Manual, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer started processing the documents required for the titling of lots. The Municipal Accountant was advised to record the said land in the books of accounts as soon as the titles shall have been issued.
observations.reason: The setting up of RPT/SET Receivables was still not based on the certified list of taxpayers from the Municipal Treasurer.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and management agreed that the City Accounting Office  

(a) adjust the above noted reconciling items and errors and take appropriate action on items that could no longer be traced/supported; and 

(b) cancel the stale checks and restore the balances of corresponding cash and liability accounts.
observations.managementAction: On-going verifications on the details of the reconciling items
POWASS are intended to be turned over to barangays. Turn-over documents are still in process and will be submitted to CAO cc: City Auditor, for derecognition in the books

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to immediately settle the remaining balance of disallowance and to henceforth observe pertinent rules and regulations in the discharge of its fiscal functions.
observations.managementAction: Subsequent payments still in process

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Management agreed to:

a) instruct the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and other concerned officials and employees to coordinate with each other to facilitate the completion of supporting documents to be attached to disbursement vouchers;

b) require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to ensure that only disbursement vouchers with complete supporting documents and signatures are processed and paid; and

c) institute measures to improve the agency’s performance in terms of submission of documents and accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2009-006 and NGAS Manual for LGUs.
observations.reason: Reiterated in CY 2020 AAR under Obs. No. 19 page No. 94

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal Accountant give priority to the submission of the accounts and financial reports within the reglementary period to enable the Audit Team to conduct timely audit thereof and promptly inform the management of the deficiencies noted, if any.
observations.reason: For CY 2020, the Municipality still incurred delay in the submission of accounts, monthly trial balances and quarterly financial reports.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management fill in the plantilla positions, or designate permanent staff in a concurrent capacity, or reassign LGU personnel to the LDRRMO as required by the existing regulations who will perform the function of research and planning.
observations.managementAction: The management designated only Job Order personnel to perform the research and planning functions of LDRRMO.
observations.reason: Due to funding requirements the management was not able to implement the recommendation.

observations.recommendation: To correct the procedure, we recommended that the payment for registration fees for CLUP trainings should be acknowledged by an Official Receipt of the government (AF51) in accordance with the provisions of Executive Order No. 292 and Section 68(1) of PD 1445.
observations.reason: The management did not act the audit observation and recommendation

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:

The Accounting Office and MGSO shall perform reconciliation between their records of Road Networks. Any discrepancies found shall be verified, investigated and adjusted in the books of accounts, if necessary. Henceforth, perform periodic/regular reconciliation of property cards to ensure that all discrepancies are immediately investigated, cleared and reconciled with the books of accounts.  The LRN Ledger Card and Property Card shall always be reconciled with each other in compliance COA Circular 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015;

Municipal General Services Office shall prepare and maintain a Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components; and 


Accounting Office shall prepare and maintain a Local Road Network Property Card Property which serves as Subsidiary Ledgers to support the PPE accounts in the General Ledger.
observations.managementAction: Report of physical count was not submitted for CY 2020
observations.reason: No Property Card being maintained by the agency. The finding was first raised in CY 2019 AAR.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to instruct the Accounting Office to take actions on the following: 

Exert effort to analyze the account and immediately prepare an adjusting entries to recognize in the books unrecorded audit disallowances on the basis of NFD, if any, and drop in the books of accounts unrecorded settlement thereof under the NSSDC to obtain fair presentation of the account in the financial statements;

Always ensure that audit disallowances that have become final and executory, on the basis of the NFD are recorded in the books of accounts and settlement thereof under the NSSDC are dropped therefrom; and

Maintain an updated subsidiary ledger/records for each official/employee determined to be liable for the amount disallowed to ensure that audit disallowances including their settlements, are properly monitored and reconciled with the SASDC issued by the Auditor.
observations.reason:
Amount in the Financial Statement did not reconcile with SASDC.
The finding was first raised in CY 2019 AAR.

No records provided by the agency.
The finding was first raised in CY 2019 AAR.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Government adopt a system of payroll payment through the ATMs of banks.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipality is still using cash advance payroll system.

Will work out on or before the end of FY 2021.
The required report was not submitted to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.reason: Findings raised in CY 2017 AAR.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following

The Municipal Accountant demand from NGOs/POs the unliquidated fund transfers as of December 31, 2016 through submission of the Fund Utilization Report together with the required supporting documents for proper evaluation and recording in the books; and (Fully Implemented in CY 2019).

Submit liquidation or fund utilization reports with complete supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.managementAction: Liquidation reports were not submitted as of report date.
