observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to review and analyze the said account. Exert extra efforts in recovering the said receivable by exhausting all possible means and seeking legal advice. Likewise, adequately document the actions taken thereon. Should all efforts fail, request for a write-off to the COA of the subject account and support the letter of request with (a) proof or evidences that could substantiate efforts exerted to review and validate the account, and enforce collection thereof; (b) list of available records and extent of validation made on the said account; and (c) a certification ascertaining the reasons why pertinent records and documents could not be located, as the case may be.
observations.reason: Management is on the process of seeking legal advice in the effort of recovering the said receivables. These accounts, furthermore, were during the time of the late Municipal Treasurer Arsenia Orejas.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Agriculturist to establish the actual status of the breeding stocks dispersal program by tracing the whereabouts and condition of the original breeding stocks and their fair value less cost to sell for proper recording in the books of accounts. Likewise, the Municipal Agriculturist shall keep systematic records of births, deaths, and disposal of breeding stocks. In case of death or disposal, facilitate the request for relief of accountability in accordance with Sections 151 and 152 of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992, for dropping the account from the books, if warranted. We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant provide proper disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statements in consonance with PPSAS 27 to provide information that may affect the fairness of the presentation of the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2018
observations.reason: The Inventory Report of the subject biological assets were not yet submitted by the Municipal Agriculturist, hence, the actual status of the breeding stocks and its fair value less cost to sell could not be ascertained. Henceforth, the Municipal Agriculturist shall keep systematic records of births, deaths, and disposal of breeding stocks. In case of death or disposal, facilitate the request for relief of accountability in accordance with Sections 151 and 152 of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992, for dropping the account from the books, if warranted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management maximize the use of the 70% LDRRMF for more worthy projects that will really eliminate flood risks, lessen adverse impacts of hazards and calamities, and train and equip residents to be able to respond to any disasters or calamity. We likewise required the submission of the first quarter accomplishment report of the JO personnel and the Municipal Accountant to see to it that wages of JOs are completely supported with the documentary requirements enumerated under COA Circular No. 2012-01 before payments are made.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2018
observations.reason: As of June 30, 2019, the LDRRMF for CY 2019 was not utilized for more worthy projects as recommended. Also, there were still activities which could not be considered as pre-disaster related that were charged to the LDRRMF of CY 2018 which were paid in the first quarter of CY 2019. Moreover, the accomplishment reports of the JO workers on the subject transaction were not yet submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that henceforth the Municipality appropriate and utilize the 20% DF only for viable and sustainable development programs and projects enumerated under DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant see to it that payrolls of the JOs are supported with accomplishment reports and to refrain from charging their wages against the 20% DF. These expenditures, however, may be charged to the General Fund Proper pursuant to existing rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2018
observations.reason: Appropriations for Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses were still included in the 20% DF based on the Status of Appropriations, Allotments, Obligations and Balances submitted by the Municipal Budget Officer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management submit the GPB and AR to the PPDO and DILG Provincial Office within the prescribed period in accordance with Section 4.0 of PCW-DBM-DILG-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01 dated January 12, 2016.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2018
observations.reason: The GPB was only submitted to the DILG-Provincial Office on February 12, 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the BAC to observe the provisions of IRR of RA No. 9184 in the posting of notice of award, notice to proceed and the approved contract in the PhilGEPs website. We further recommended that the BAC Secretariat issue a certification on the posting of the Notice of Awards in any conspicuous place in the premises of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2018
observations.reason: The Notice of Award, Notice to Proceed and Approved Contract were still not posted in the PhilGEPs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality utilize and implement its PPAs under the LDRRMF to strengthen the capacity of the Municipality to avoid or mitigate the adverse effects of disaster or calamity.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2017
observations.reason: The LDRRMF was still not fully utilized for projects that would strengthen the capacity of the Municipality to avoid or mitigate the adverse effects of disaster or calamity.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should see to it that the PCF is not used for regular and recurring expenses and only use it in the non-recurring, emergency and petty expenses.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2017
observations.reason: Payments for fuel, oil and lubricants were still made out of the PCF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Council for the Protection of Children (MCPC) should focus and prioritize on the formulation and implementation of the PPAs of the LCPC in accordance with Section 15 of Republic Act No. 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2017
observations.reason: As of June 30, 2019, there were still activities not implemented. The status of implementation of PPAs in CY 2019 was still not submitted to the Auditor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management, henceforth, strictly adhere to the provisions of RA 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012 on the use of the one percent of IRA in the implementation of PPAs of the LCPC.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2016
observations.reason: The Annual Budget for CY 2019 per Ordinance No. 19-01 dated January 28, 2019 did not include allotments for the protection of children under the LCPC.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant evaluate the collectability of receivables, review and analyze the accounts and, if not possible due to the absence of records, the Local Chief Executive request for authority to write-off from the Commission on Audit.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2015
observations.reason: The subject receivable accounts were still outstanding as of December 31, 2019 because there were no adequate records to support the accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare a certified list of taxpayers and their taxes due and collectible for the year as basis for the Municipal Accountant in recording the Real Property Tax Receivables/Special Education Tax Receivables and their corresponding credits at the beginning of the year pursuant to Section 20 of the NGAS Manual, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2011
observations.reason: The setting up of RPT/SET Receivables was done based on the assessment of the Municipal Assessor as of December 31, 2018 instead of the certified list of taxpayers to be issued by the Municipal Treasurer. The recommendation is to be implemented at the beginning of the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management consider the disposal of its unserviceable properties in order to realize additional income, otherwise, if left unattended the subject unserviceable properties may eventually deteriorate and lose their salvage values.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2009
observations.reason: The identified unserviceable properties were still not disposed by Management.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to 

(a) check and validate the dormant receivable accounts; 


(b) exhaust all possible remedies to collect receivables and to demand liquidation of fund transfers; and 

(c) request for authority for the write-off of accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
observations.managementAction: Items were not requested for write-off for lack of supporting documents

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive direct the Accounting Office and the implementing offices to adhere with the provisions on documentary requirements and submit to the Audit Team the required supporting documents in order to establish the regularity of the claims.
observations.managementAction: The Management ensured that the concerned offices will produce the necessary documents on the subject transactions

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to immediately settle the remaining balance of disallowance and to henceforth observe pertinent rules and regulations in the discharge of its fiscal functions.

We reiterated the audit recommendations that the Management should:

a.	create an inventory team to conduct physical count of agency’s properties;

b.	instruct the Municipal Treasurer to (i) prepare and maintain the required property cards, pursuant to the provisions of New Government Accounting System and (ii) tag the properties with property number; and

c.	require the Municipal Accountant to adhere to COA Circular 2015-008 with regard to the use of residual value of the road networks.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant immediately submit (a) all cash advance vouchers and their supporting documents together with the transmittal letter to the Auditor and (b) Liquidation Reports and their supporting documents together with the transmittal letter to the ICFAU. We further recommended that management henceforth fully comply with the provisions of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular (JC) No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this Report.
Reiterated in Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendations that the Accounting Office (a) coordinate with the HR and Treasury Office on the monthly reconciliation of the amount due for remittance of premiums and loan amortizations to the GSIS; and (b) trace the cause in detail the discrepancies on withheld and remitted amounts, including previous years, which consisted the ending balance of Due to GSIS account and make the necessary adjustments, if warranted.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated that (a) the Municipal Treasurer coordinate with the Municipal Accountant on monthly preparation of remittances to Pag-IBIG and PHIC; and (b) the Municipal Accountant reconcile the discrepancies from the remitted amounts to the recorded withheld contributions and loan amortizations which accumulated to the ending balances of Due to Pag-IBIG and Philhealth accounts and make the necessary adjustments, if warranted.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated the audit recommendations that the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer should (a) ensure that only disbursement vouchers with complete supporting documents are processed and paid; and (b) strictly complied with the guidelines on the submission of accounts and reports.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:    (a) Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all PPEs of the Municipality, prepare and submit the corresponding RPCPPE and henceforth ensure that said physical count are strictly conducted every year; (b) OIC-General Services Officer and the Municipal Accountant to update their respective records and analyze the unreconciled balances between the RPCPPE and accounting records amounting to P424,349,795.48; and (c) OIC-General Services Officer to:       (i) update and monitor all issued PARs so that accountabilities thereof can be easily determined; and (ii) prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP), together with the required supporting documents to facilitate the disposal of any unserviceable items.
observations.reason:
The GSO still failed to conduct physical count on all PPEs of the Municipality, due to lack of proper turnover of reports and records from the previous GSO and the unexpected outbreak of the COVID-19 and the implementation of the Enhanced Community Quarantine thus, required RPCPPEs were still not submitted. 

Likewise, the IIRUP, together with its supporting documents were still not submitted. As such, the observations were reiterated in Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: 
(a) MDC to specifically identify the priority development projects that will contribute to the realization of capital development of the Municipality and see to it that the Annual Investment Plan is prepared with the required information such as the specific location and appropriation for each projects; and (b) Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to reconcile their respective records. Discrepancies that may arise, if any, should be investigated accordingly for accurate and reliable reporting. 

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor refrain from placing available funds in time deposit accounts instead ensure that priority programs, projects and activities, beneficial to the public, are fully implemented so that the needed services of the constituents may not be delayed or sacrificed in favor of interest earned in time deposits.
observations.reason: Projects, programs and activities were still not specifically identified in the AIP; Discrepancies between the reports of the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer were still observed; and funds under the 20% Development Fund were still placed in a time deposit account due to continued disregard of the concerned Municipal Official to act on the matter. Related observations were discussed in Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer ensure the optimal utilization of the 70% mitigation fund of LDRRMF to strengthen the resilience of the Municipality and its constituents in times of disasters and calamities as required under RA No. 10121. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant transfer the unexpended/unutilized amount of the Quick Response Fund (QRF) and the Disaster Risk Reduction and Management – Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (DRRM-MOOE) as Trust Liability-DRRM account in the trust fund books solely for the purpose of supporting disaster risk reduction management activities of the LDRRM Council within the next five (5) years.
observations.reason: Lack of available cash to fully implement the PPAs embodied in the LDRRMF Investment Plan and the transfer of unexpended balance QRF and DRRM – MOOE in the Trust Fund Books hence, this finding in reiterated in the Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal Accountant give priority to the submission of the accounts and financial reports within the reglementary period to enable the Audit Team to conduct timely audit thereof and promptly inform the management of the deficiencies noted, if any.
observations.reason: For CY 2019, the Municipality still incurred delay in the submission of accounts, monthly trial balances and quarterly financial reports for as long as 105 days due to lack of manpower and voluminous transactions.

The Municipality still incurred lapses in the allocation and implementation of GAD programs, projects and activities (PPAs) for CY 2019 as follows: (a) failure to revise the Annual GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) based on the comment/s and recommendation/s issued by the DILG Provincial Office; (b) failure to conduct gender analysis through the administration of the Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines (HGDG) tool; (c)lapses in the preparation and submission of the GAD Accomplishment Report; and (d) lapses in the submission of purchase orders, documentation and charging of various expenditures.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit all her collections intact and daily or not later than the next banking day to avoid the risk of possible loss of government money. 

We likewise recommended that Management institute appropriate legal charges against the erring accountable officers who failed to deposit collections particularly those who are retiring and/or retired from service and those officials who allowed them to retire.
observations.managementAction: No new, effective mechanisms were incorporated in Municipal Treasurer’s Office since previous audit in order to facilitate timely, intact collections and deposit thereof.
observations.reason:
Cash in Local Treasury account increased to ₱4,182,892.41.


Reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.managementAction: Executive Order No. 005 series of 2018 released last April 2018, was presented as proof  designation of Appraisal and Disposal Committee.
observations.reason: Corresponding RPCPPE for CY 2019 was not finalized, thus, could not proceed with reconciliation with Accounting records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management ensure that all projects identified under the 20% Economic Development Fund are fully implemented within the budget year.
observations.reason:
Remarked projects remained unimplemented as of 2019.

Reiterated in Part II of this report.

conduct regular monitoring of on-going private construction to ensure that appraisal and assessment on buildings are issued upon completion of construction; 

conduct regular inspection of real properties to validate actual or predominant use of property to provide assurance that collections are based on correct, accurate and credible assessments; and

issue Notices of Tax Delinquency to those real properties with no payment from 2017 and earlier years and post the notices in accordance with Section 254 of 1991 LGC, to optimize collection of RPT.
observations.managementAction: The said office promised to “conduct a property record validation in anticipation of General Revision on property assessment on agricultural land, residential and commercial buildings.”
observations.reason: Per communication letter to ATL, Office of the Municipal Assessor was still waiting for implementation of 2016 Revised Schedule of Fair Market Values of Residential and Commercial Lands in Province of Camarines Sur.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management study the packages offered by mobile networks and evaluate which would provide the best price favorable to the local government. 

We likewise recommended that Management refrain from paying excessive expenditures as required under COA Circular No. 2012-003 and see to it that claims are supported with adequate documentation.
observations.managementAction: The Telephone Expenses increased to ₱718,690.00 in 2019 because the personnel from National Gov’t assigned in the municipality were allocated load allowance. But the rates given to local officials were the same in CY 2017.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
a) Require the accounting personnel to exert extra efforts in following up the demand letters sent to concerned NGOs/POs to settle their accountabilities and determine if the said funds were already spent by the said NGOs/PO and have it documented as basis for recording in the books and/or requesting for write-off;
b) Direct the assigned personnel of every program to be diligent in safe keeping and tracking of documents/records not only with regard to this case but each transaction of the municipality; and in case of transfer, resignation or reassignment, to turn-over the documents/record to the incoming/relieving officer; and henceforth,
c)Adhere strictly with the provisions stipulated in Sections 5.2 to Section 5.4 of COA Circular No. 2007-001.
observations.reason: Liquidation reports not yet submitted and Management found it difficult to enforce liquidation of fund transfers.

observations.recommendation:
Instruct the Municipal Assessor to register all land owned by the Municipality under the Torrens Title System in conformity with COA Circular No. 92-386 and submit tax declarations which are duly signed by the Provincial Assessor to erase the doubts as to the genuineness of those documents.

Likewise, reconciliation between the records of the municipal accountant and the municipal assessor should be regularly made to accurately establish the valuation of land in the book of accounts of the Municipality.
observations.reason: Management found difficulty in registering the lands due to untraceability of source documents needed for the same.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management enjoin the concerned department
heads to strictly comply with the accounting and reporting guidelines on local
roads asset management system, and to earnestly perform their respective duties and
responsibilities set forth under COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated October 23, 2015.

Henceforth, we recommended that the Inventory Committee conduct annual physical
count of all its Local Road Networks and the results thereof reported in the RPCLRN
to be submitted to the Auditor and Accounting Unit not later than January 31 of each
year. The RPCLRN shall be reconciled with the accounting records and the necessary
adjusting entries be prepared before the statement date.

We recommended that Management assign only regular employees as collecting
officers and henceforth, ensure strict adherence to existing laws and regulations that
all accountable officers/employees shall be properly bonded to safeguard
government funds.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to require the
collectors to remit their collections daily in accordance with Sections 29 and 30
of the NGAS Manual, Volume I to safeguard government funds against loss or
misappropriation.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed require all
collectors to prepare the RCDs and see to it that these are completely and
properly filled up; check the accuracy of the Accountability for Accountable
Forms by doing physical count of all accountable forms; and carry forward the
correct beginning balance to the succeeding RCDs.

We recommended and Management agreed to effect the increase of the
fidelity bond of the DO commensurate to her accountabilities, in accordance
with the Bureau of Treasury Circular No. 02-2009, in order to provide adequate
protection on the Municipality’s resources.

We recommended and Management agreed to fast track the
implementation of all BuB projects to ensure that the desired benefits/services
are delivered to the constituents on time to address the needs of the poor sectors
of the Municipality. Otherwise, Management shall return the unspent fund to
the source agency for other agencies to utilize the same for other equally
important government projects.

We also recommended that the Project Monitoring Committee of Local
Poverty Reduction Action Team (LPRAT) or designated focal person continue to
monitor the implementation of all BuB projects pursuant to the guidelines under
DBM-DILG-DSWD-NAPC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 6 and 7 dated
February 27, 2015 and November 3, 2015, respectively.

Lastly, we recommended that the BAC strictly observe the recommended
timelines in the procurement activity under Annex C of the 2016 Revised
Implementing Rules and Regulations (RIRR) of R.A. No. 9184 and GPPB
Circular No. 05-2016 dated December 20, 2016 to avoid delay in the
implementation of the projects.

We recommended and Management agreed to refrain from charging
expenditures not listed under Section 5.0 of NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 to the LDRRMF and
maximize the use of the 70% LDRRMF for more worthy projects that will
really eliminate flood risks, lessen adverse impacts of hazards and calamities,
and train and equip residents to be able to respond to any disasters or calamity.

We recommended and Management agreed to comply with the rules and
regulations on the grant of Uniform/Clothing Allowance, whether in cash or in
kind, and desist from providing uniforms to JO personnel in accordance with
Budget Circular No. 2018-1 dated March 8, 2018 to avoid incurring irregular
expenditures.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed require all the
PHWs to submit proof of actual exposure to specific health hazards and
occupational risks, and documents required under Section 5.8 of COA Circular
No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 to support their hazard pay. Henceforth, we
recommended that Management pay hazard pay based on the actual days of
exposure to risks or danger in accordance with Section 7 of the Revised IRR of
R.A. No. 7305.

1.6	We recommended and management agreed that the City Accounting Office  (a) adjust the above noted reconciling items and errors and take appropriate action on items that could no longer be traced/supported; and (b) cancel the stale checks and restore the balances of corresponding cash and liability accounts.

2.10 	We reiterated our recommendation and management agreed that the (a) Inventory Team complete the physical validation of PPEs; (b) GSO  review the integrity of the data uploaded in the PIAS; (c) GSO and Accounting offices  reconcile their respective PPE records and submit to the Audit Team the report thereon; and (d) GSO facilitate the early disposal of unserviceable properties.

3.5 	We recommended and management agreed that the City Accountant (a) determine once and for all the status and nature of the dormant receivables and to take appropriate action as applicable in accordance with regulations to cleanse the books of dormant accounts; and (b) demand for the liquidation of advances and apply all legal remedies to collect said accounts.

4.5 	We recommended and Management agreed to continue (a) complying with the provision of Section 324 (b) of the Local Government Code in appropriating for debt servicing; and (b) observing the provisions of Philippines Public Sector Accounting Standards (PPSAS) I in accounting and reporting of loans payable.

5.15 	We recommended and the Management agreed that the City Mayor instruct the City Planning and Development Office, City Engineering Office, City Budget Office and the BAC to coordinate efforts with the end view of ensuring that (a) all critical inputs in planning are considered to ensure that adequate funding are provided; (b) all programmed and funded PPAs are implemented as planned; (c) PPAs are adequately described and identified in the AIP; and (d) budget augmentations pass through the required processes and are clothed with requisite authority.

6.5 	We recommended and the Management agreed to (a) follow up from OCD its request for funds for the construction of an evacuation center while seeking other ways to augment its financial resources; and (b) continue its compliance with the guidelines on the proper management of LDRRMF.

7.23 	We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed that the (a) CSWMB review and revise the City’s existing 10-year ESWMP; (b) concerned official  (i) continue to address the environmental issues on the closed dump site and prioritize the fund support to SWM; (ii) designate/appoint barangay ordinance enforcers in all barangays; (iii) strictly enforce the sanctions and penalties for violations of environmental ordinances; (iv) find better strategies to improve waste diversion rate; and (v) ensure integrity of data being reported.

8.19 	We recommended and the Management agreed to (a) instruct the City Treasurer to improve collection on delinquent accounts by addressing the identified causes in accordance with the options provided by law; (b) remind the City Assessor to regularly assess the valuation of RPUs; and (c) request the SP to (i) legislate on the imposition and collection of ad valorem tax on idle lands; and (ii) facilitate legislative action on the new SFMV of the real properties in the City.

9.15 	We recommended and the Management agreed to (a) secure an SP ordinance to legalize the imposition and collection of fees by POWAS; (b) establish an internal control system for POWAS operation with the end view of securing collections from loss or misuse and ensuring that government funds are legally and properly utilized.

10.6 	We reiterated our recommendation to which management agreed to instruct the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to strictly conform with COA Circular 2009-006 on the timely submission of the transaction documents.

11.19 	We recommended and  the City Mayor agreed that the GFP  (a) create M&E team that will assess the overall effectiveness and efficiency of the LGU’s gender mainstreaming efforts; (b) use HGDG tool to properly identify the PPAs or its portion that can be attributed to GAD activities; (c) prioritize the conduct of required trainings/seminars to the members of GFPS to aid them in the proper discharge of their duties and responsibilities; and (d) strictly adhere with the pertinent rules and regulations on the timely submission of the reports to the COA Audit Team and other oversight bodies.

13.4   	We commended management and recommended that the City Accountant and the City Treasurer continue to be compliant with the GSIS law on the deduction and remittance of contributions and premiums due the System.
We recommended and Management agreed to continuously comply with the National Health Insurance Act by remitting all contributions withheld to the Philippine Health Insurance Corporation.

16.3 	We recommended and Management agreed to immediately settle the remaining balance of disallowance and to henceforth observe pertinent rules and regulations in the discharge of its fiscal functions.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the Municipal Treasurer to prepare and furnish the Accounting Office the duly certified list of taxpayers showing their tax dues as required under Section 20 Volume I of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs.

We recommended that the MTO (a) establish clear schedule and cut-off date and time for depositing collections to facilitate better monitoring and reconciliation of daily deposits; (b) deposit intact all collections to the authorized depository bank based on the established cut-off date; and (c) account for the shortage of ₱5,428.85 determined in this report. We further recommended that the collectors prepare RCDs daily with corresponding report number.
The Management agreed to implement the audit recommendations.

We reiterated that the Municipal Accountant require all Accountable Officers to immediately liquidate their cash advance by submitting required report and settlement documents to the Accounting Office and refunding any unexpended balance to the Municipal Treasurer. If settlement documents are no longer available, the Accountable Officers must return the full amount of cash advance they so received.
The Management agreed to implement the audit recommendations.

We reiterated the previous audit recommendations for (a) the Municipal Accountant, GSO and Municipal Treasurer to prepare and maintain in their file property records such as ledger cards and stock cards in compliance with the directives of the NGAS Manual for LGUs; (b) the Inventory Committee to start conducting the physical count of PPE for CY 2019 and submit copy of the RPCPPE to the Audit Team; (c) the Municipal Treasurer to prepare PAR/ARE for PPE procured by the agency and maintain a copy thereof, and require the accountable officer to keep a copy of said ARE/PAR for assets issued to him; and (d) the Municipal Accountant to avoid providing residual value for the depreciable road network system and to correct the accumulated depreciation computed for the said account.
The Management agreed to implement the audit recommendations.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
instruct the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer and other concerned officials and employees to coordinate with each other to facilitate the completion of supporting documents to be attached to disbursement vouchers;
require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to ensure that only disbursement vouchers with complete supporting documents and signatures are processed and paid; and
institute measures to improve the agency’s performance in terms of submission of documents and accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2009-006 and NGAS Manual for LGUs.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Engineer to (a) immediately implement the LGSF-AM funded project of Local Access Road at Barangay Villa Aglipay; (b) strictly adhere to the provisions of DILG Memorandum Circular 2019-71 for the policy, guidelines and procedures in the implementation of FY 2019 LGSF-AM; and (c) upon receiving the fund from LGSF-FA, immediately start the process of procurement of the proposed project in the Municipality.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Implement the remaining programs, projects and activities under development fund included in the Municipality’s Annual Investment Plan for CY 2019; and
Instruct the Bids and Awards Committee and its Secretariat Office to submit the lacking documents as mentioned in paragraph 8.6 and to strictly comply with the provisions of RA No. 9184 and COA Circular 2012-01.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the:
GFPS ensure that the GAD PPAs are well-planned and coordinated in order to give effect to its full implementation in accordance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint JMC No. 2013-01 and 2016-01; and
BAC and its Secretariat submit the lacking documents supporting the post-audited disbursement transactions to avoid issuance of Notice of Suspension.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the (a) Municipal Accountant (i) revert to general fund the unused CY 2011-2013 LDRRMF amounting to ₱6,182,996.18 and transfer the unutilized LDRRMF fund for CY 2019 of ₱3,630,274.31 to the Special Trust Fund; (ii) prepare or cause to prepare a revised Utilization Report using the appropriate classification of expenses such as equipment and traveling expenses as discussed; (iii) avoid the practice of paying suppliers through cash advance when direct payment is possible; (iv) record future purchases of standby-goods by recognizing the appropriate inventory account and prepare the requisite supplies ledger cards; (v) take up in the books the equivalent value of standby-goods which remained undistributed as at year-end; and (vi) in coordination with the BAC Secretariat, submit the lacking supporting documents for the procurement of one rescue service vehicle; (b) LDRRMO (i) prepare the LDRRMFIP in accordance with COA Circular 2012-002; (ii) prepare and submit monthly report on sources and utilization in coordination with the Municipal Accountant; and (iii) comply with the minimum standards recommended by the DILG and LGA to strengthen the agency’s capacity in disaster preparedness and response; and (c) BAC and its Secretariat produce the lacking documents mentioned in the report and strictly adhere to the provisions of RA No. 9184 and its IRR, particularly the use of public bidding whenever the threshold amount is breached.

We recommended that the MTO continue the monthly remittance of employees’ premium contributions and loan amortizations to the GSIS. We also recommended that the MAO reconcile their records of under remitted balances with the GSIS and adopt measures that would ensure the correct calculation of the required premiums and loan amortizations to be withheld against the actual billing statement.
The Management agreed to implement the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the MAO, MTO and HRMO (a) adopt an effective and efficient procedure for ensuring the correctness of amount withheld against actual remittances; and (b) reconcile the unremitted amounts from previous years to Pag-IBIG and PHIC. We further recommended to continue remitting the mandatory contributions and loan amortizations to Pag-IBIG and PHIC.
The Management agreed to implement the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the MTO remit immediately the unremitted balance of ₱72,363.68 to the BIR and to furnish the Audit Team copy of official receipt issued by the BIR for the remitted amount. We further recommend that the MAO and MTO continue to withhold taxes from the employees’ salaries and from payments to suppliers and remit the same to the BIR within the stipulated timeline.
The Management agreed to implement the audit recommendations.

We recommended and Management agreed to follow up the settlement of the Suspensions and Disallowance incurred by the previous Municipal Officials and payors in accordance with the prescribed rules under COA Memorandum No. 2009-084 dated November 16, 2009.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to:
a) instruct the Municipal GSO to completely fill up and include the vital information on
the RPCPPE pertaining the actual result of the physical inventory count undertaken
for the reliable comparison of the report to that of the records of the Accounting;
b) cause the Municipal Accountant and Municipal GSO to evaluate the causes of the
immaterial differences of ₱0.70 and ₱3,374.99 for the Buildings and Office
Equipment, respectively, and devise an alternative method to include those PPE
items left out on the RPCPPE since they were already recorded on the books and
owned by the Municipality; and
c) direct the Chairman of the Committee on Disposal, if any, to initiate/ facilitate the
immediate disposal of those properties in accordance with afore-cited provisions
in order not to expose those properties to elements that cause further
deterioration or loss of salvaged value.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer:
a. and other concerned officials to implement more effective, efficient and aggressive
synergized tax campaign and develop additional strategies to improve tax revenue
generation and to intensify collection of Real Property Taxes not only current but
also the delinquent taxes in order to generate more income.
b. coordinate with the Provincial Treasurer the status of the rest of the reported tax
delinquencies and the action taken by the latter in the imposition of the required
remedies in the collection of real property taxes against said delinquent
taxpayers; and
c. regularly provide the Office of the Municipal Accountant the Report of Delinquent
Real Property Tax as the basis of the latter on the reconciliation of their accounts.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant
and the Municipal Treasurer to verify the existing difference between their respective
records for the immediate reconciliation of accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) in coordination with the responsible departments aim to reduce unnecessary
delays in project implementation and strictly effect the necessary processes to
expedite the completion of the not-yet started and on-going projects to allow the
early enjoyment of benefits by the general public and the full utilization of the
funds received from National Government Agencies (NGAs);
b) provide to the Audit Team the requested Memorandum of Agreements (MOA)
which would be the basis of the Team for the proper validation of the
implementation of the afore-cited programs and projects;
c) instruct the Municipal Engineer to perform in-depth planning and detailed
engineering including thorough site inspections to anticipate and consider
interruptions that may probably arise during implementation of the projects; and
d) instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with
the source agencies for the proper return of the said unutilized funds pursuant to
Section 4.9 of COA Circular No. 94-013 and DOF-DBM-COA Permanent Committee
Joint Circular No. 4-2012 to avoid possible fund misuse.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Local School Board to
evaluate carefully the programs and projects identified during the year in accordance
with the provisions stated in Section 272 of the R.A. 7160 and DepEd, DBM and DILG
Joint Circular No. 01 s. 2017 dated January 19, 2017 and cause its effective
implementation in order to achieve fully its purpose of providing quality education in
the public schools.
Likewise, we recommended that the balances of the finished/implemented programs/
projects/activities be reverted to unappropriated account and made available for
budgeting to priority programs/projects/ activities for the benefits of the students.

While we took cognizance of the relentless efforts of the Municipality to support gender
equality and women empowerment, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor as the
Chairperson of the Executive Committee and the Technical Working Group:
a) Ensure the proper mainstreaming of GAD related activities in all departments
/offices of the Municipality;
b) Secure first the Certificate of Endorsement of the Annual Gender and
Development (GAD) Plan and Budget (GPB) from the DILG Provincial Office before
incorporating to the Municipality’s Annual Budget to properly ascertain the
relevance of the gender issues identified and gender-responsiveness of the PPAs
indicated in the GPB;
c) Exert active efforts in accomplishing the targeted and planned Organization-
Focused PPAs. Endeavor not just to sustain its compliance with the minimum
budget requirement on GAD programs, projects and activities as provided for by
the law, but to increase identification of gender-responsive PPAs to be covered by
the annual budget until such time that all major programs of the Municipality are
attributed to GAD as envisioned under the PCW-NEDA-DBM Joint Circular;
d) Strictly monitor the implementation of GAD PPAs as required under the Joint
Circular and conduct regular coordination with the implementing offices to ensure
that programs are undertaken within the target period; and
e) Coordinate with the Municipal Human Resources Management Officer to require all
personnel to undergo training on gender sensitivity, human rights and gender
responsiveness especially those holding positions involving protection and defense
of women against gender-based violence, counselling and trauma healing.

Since the Municipality opted to attribute a portion of its budget to the GAD Budget,
we recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the GAD Focal Point System
to attend a training program on how to administer the Harmonized Gender and
Development Guidelines (HGDG) in its GAD Plan and Budget to assure the proper
determination of the PPAs that may be attributed to the GAD Budget.
Likewise, we recommended that HGDG test be administered again at the end of
project implementation and incorporate the results in determining the percentage of
PPA expenditures attributable to GAD.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor direct the MSWDO/focal person and other
concerned persons to implement immediately the required activities on
Supplementary Feeding Program based on the set timeline in order to improve and
sustain the nutritional status of the target children beneficiaries and ultimately
contribute to the success of the program as a whole.
We likewise recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MSWDO and the
General Service Officer to submit justification why there were variances in the prices
of the procured items based on the prices per Purchase Order and compared from the
canvass of prices submitted by the General Service Officer and the prices obtained
by the Audit Team from confirmation with the supplier of goods and refund the
excess amount of P532,576.19, if warranted.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the General Services
Officer to regularly conduct canvass of prices of items to be procured before the
preparation of any purchase request and before the conduct of public bidding and
consider only those that are reasonable to guide and allow interested bidders,
specifically the cooperatives, to offer also just and fair prices that are advantageous
to the procuring entity.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Barangay Officials in
coordination with the Municipal Agriculture Office to prioritize helping the barangays
to operate again their MRFs and allot funding for the re-establishment in their
respective Barangays Material Recovery Facilities in compliance with Section Rule XI
of the IRR of RA No. 9003 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2009-168.
We also recommended that the Mayor instructed the municipal officials concerned to
initiate intensive information drive and awareness campaign on regulations on solid
waste management in coordination with the Barangay officials.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Human Resource Management
Officer, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to control the accommodation
given to financing institutions and ensure that minimum monthly take-home pay of all
officials and employees shall not be lower than Five Thousand Pesos (₱5,000.00)
pursuant to General Appropriations Act.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as the chairperson of the Municipal
Council for the Protection of Children (MCPC):
a) instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to specifically allocate 1% share from IRA for
LCPC as provided in DILG MC No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012 for the
implementation LCPC;
b) direct the members of the Council to prepare a separate Local Development Plan
for Children (LDPC), Local Investment Plan for Children (LIPC) and
Accomplishment Report to facilitate validation and maximize the utilization of the
allocated LCPC Fund to identified programs, projects and activities (PPAs)
provided for in the afore-mentioned Circular to ensure full strengthening and
implementation of the LCPC; and
c) require the Municipal Social Welfare Officer, being the focal person, to ensure that
the identified PPAs geared for promotion of rights and welfare of children are
carried out effectively and efficiently and continuously monitor the
implementation of children’s programs and projects in the locality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) direct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and Technical Working Group (TWG)
to:
• scrutinize the sufficiency of the winning bidder’s manpower and equipment on
all the projects to be awarded and not on an individual project only in cases
where the latter is the lowest bidder in several projects which have similar or
overlapping implementation period. This is to oblige the winning contractor to
supplement its manpower and/or equipment to be utilized in the projects
awarded to it or to disqualify it from obtaining several awards or contracts;
• monitor contractors with on-going projects with other government agencies
and to coordinate with them involved in implementing infrastructure projects
not disclosed in the Statement of On-going and Completed projects to avoid
overlapping and delay in project accomplishment and/or substandard quality of
work;
• to conduct the required processes and procedures in all the stages of the bidding
in the procurement of infrastructure and comply strictly with all the documentary
requirements as mandated by R.A. No. 9184. Non-compliance of the required
documents by the prospective bidder shall be a cause for automatic
disqualification in the eligibility and award of contract.
b) instruct the Municipal Engineer to:
• strictly and closely monitor all projects simultaneously implemented by the
contractors and prepare an individual status report per project to be submitted
to the Honorable Mayor for monitoring purposes and in order to check, verify
and evaluate the quality of work and efficiency of the said contractors; and
• consider re-evaluation of estimate of projects duration based on experience
from actual completion of previous projects and reduce the stipulated contract
time, if proper. This would allow the Municipality to implement more projects
during the year given the shorter time provided to the contractors to complete
the projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer to publicize
the projects/programs/activities in conspicuous places within the agency premises and in
the project venue or notify the public on its essential features/elements in compliance
with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2014.
Moreover, the Municipal Engineer require the contractor/s in their on-going and future
projects to install enough safety signages in the construction sites and provide protective
gears to the laborers. Finally, explain/justify the non-installation of project billboards and
signboards with essential features/elements and the failure to provide safety and health
items, despite the availability of the fund and require the contractor to make a refund of
the said amount.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: (a) Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all PPEs and Inventories of the Municipality, prepare and submit the corresponding RPCPPEs and RPCIs and henceforth ensure that said inventory reports are regularly and promptly conducted every year; and (b) General Services Officer to fast track the preparation of the IIRUP, together with the required supporting documents to facilitate the disposal of the unserviceable items.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular 97-002 and the CSC Memorandum Circular No. 12 s. 2012 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances to avoid accumulation of outstanding cash advances. Moreover, the Municipal Mayor is advised to make the formal request for the writing-off of unliquidated cash advances of deceased AOs, with the necessary supporting documents.

We also recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant to: (i) issue demand letters requiring the immediate settlement of the remaining outstanding cash advances of those who are no
longer connected with the agency but are still active; (ii) strictly monitor the grant of cash advance by issuing a certification that previous cash advances have been liquidated and accounted for in the books to prevent the grant of additional cash advance to those employees and officers with outstanding cash advances; (iii) practice prudence in the grant of cash advances to casual/job order employees whose connection and tenure with the Municipality is uncertain there being no employee-employer relationship; and (iv) ensure that the grant and liquidation of cash advances are duly supported with proper and complete supporting documents, ObR included, to establish the validity, propriety and legality of the transactions; and
b. Municipal Budget Officer to duly prepare ObRs for all transactions including cash advances to ensure that valid and legal expenditures and obligations are taken up in the accounts of the current fiscal year pursuant to Section 119 of PD No. 1445.

In view thereof, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to conduct a thorough review/analysis of the outstanding balances of the Receivables account amounting to P21,533,840.90 and institute appropriate legal actions to enforce collection/settlement thereof to recover the amount due thereon.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant: (i) exert efforts in locating the documents/records pertaining to the outstanding loans receivables under the Tulong Kabuhayan Loan Program to establish the validity and legality of the claims against the debtors and facilitate collection/settlement thereof; (ii) analyze the unreconciled balances of the Due from and Due to Other Funds account, prepare a reconciliation statement therein and make the necessary adjustments in the books for the affected funds; and (iii) make the necessary adjustments on the reclassification of the Due from Officers and Employees, Other Receivables and Loans Receivables - Others accounts amounting to P60,108.00, P220,000.00 and P500,000.00, respectively, to its proper Receivable accounts depending on the nature of the transactions for fair and accurate presentation in the Statement of Financial Position to provide a reliable and relevant information to the users of the Financial Statements.

In view thereof, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Finance Committee to: (i) conduct thorough study in the reasonableness of the projected income to be collected to arrive at a more realistic appropriation; (ii) conduct periodic review of the collection efficiency to keep track of the revenue/income accounts that do not meet the revenue targets for appropriate remedial action; and (iii) adjust the financial and physical targets for the budget year in times of under-collection to prevent any cash overdraft;
b. Sangguniang Bayan to thoroughly review and scrutinize any proposed measure/s to increase the Municipality’s target income for the budget year to ensure whether the same are realistic and feasible to support the budget prior to approval thereof; and
c. Municipal Treasurer to: (i) regularly prepare cash programs in order to determine the realistic cash inflows every month and help the MFC to adjust the cash disbursement programs in case of foreseeable under-collection of target revenues; and (ii) ensure that there are available funds, taking into consideration the funds for mandatory appropriations, before certifying fund availability in the Purchase Requests/Disbursement Vouchers.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MDC to: (i) specifically identify the priority development projects that will contribute to the realization of capital development of the Municipality and see to it that the Annual Investment Plan is prepared with the required information, such as the specific name and location of the projects, the intended beneficiaries, the intended scope of the program, and the standard within which the results of the project can be measured; and (ii) ensure that discrepancies between the AIP and Annual Budget is resolved.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor refrain from placing available funds in time deposit accounts instead ensure that priority programs, projects and activities, beneficial to the public, are fully implemented so that the needed services of the constituents may not be delayed or sacrificed in favor of interests earned in time deposits.

Likewise, the Municipal Mayor is advised to instruct the:
a. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to reconcile their respective records. Discrepancies that may arise, if any, should be investigated accordingly for accurate and reliable reporting;
b. BAC and its Technical Working Group (TWG) to immediately submit the lacking documents to the Audit Team to arrive at a decision in audit, and henceforth ensure that perfected contracts are properly and completely documented before submitting the same to the Audit Team and be vigilant in all stages of the procurement process to warrant that procedures undertaken is strictly in accordance with the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184;
c. Municipal Accountant to ascertain that all succeeding payments are supported with complete and proper documents as required in COA Circular No. 2012-001 before processing claims for payment; and
d. Municipal Budget Officer to issue ObR in full amount of the contract upon issuance of Notice of Award/payment of mobilization/first progress payment instead of partial issuance per amount claimed by supplier/contractor.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor refrain from investing funds out of the Share from Tobacco Excise Tax to a time deposit account and ensure the efficient utilization and implementation of the projects and programs duly authorized by the Sangguniang Bayan pursuant to RA No. 8240, as amended by RA No. 10351.

We also recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan, ensure that authorities given to concerned Municipal officials for the placement of high yield savings/time deposit accounts should be specific and complete with details as to source of fund and the amount to be invested. Also, ascertain that funds deposited in fixed-term deposits are idle funds from the General Fund to guarantee that special purpose funds are not being used pursuant to Section 21 of COA Circular No. 92-382 and Section 309 (b) of RA No. 7160.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor  exercise
   his primordial responsibility to require the Municipal Accountant and the
   Municipal  Treasurer  in  coordination  with  other  concerned  municipal
   employees such as the former Municipal Accountant-Designee who  is  still
   in the service to continue exerting efforts  in  digging  up  records  in
   order to render once and for all  the  actual  and  true  status  of  the
   financial  accounts  of  the  Municipality.   Moreover,  the  responsible
   officials should be held liable under the Revised Penal  Code  for  their
   failure to render the accounts and reporting thereof.

Based  on   the   foregoing   observations,   we   reiterated   our
   recommendations that:


     a. The Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer  and Municipal
        Accountant-Designee to  exert  extra  effort  to  account  for  the
        unreconciled items appearing in the Bank Reconciliation  Statements
        to faithfully present the correct balances of the account  for  the
        period; and


     b. The Municipal Accountant (i) submit the monthly BRS  for  all  bank
        accounts regularly together with the paid checks  and  debit/credit
        memos for verification as provided for under Section 74 of  PD  No.
        1445 and COA Circular  No.  96-011;  (ii)  continue  securing  bank
        statements of prior years to establish the correct balance  of  the
        Cash in Bank; and (iii) submit the subsidiary ledgers  of  all  the
        bank accounts.

Based on the foregoing audit observations, we recommended that:


     a. The Municipal  Mayor  (i)  require  all  accountable  officers  and
        employees to immediately liquidate their outstanding cash  advances
        giving more emphasis to the long outstanding  cash  advances;  (ii)
        resort to the withholding of salaries of those accountable officers
        and  employees  who  still   fail   or   persistently   refuse   to
        settle/liquidate their cash advances pursuant to Item 5.1.3 of  COA
        Circular  No.  97-002;  (iii)  exert  extra  efforts   to   recover
        outstanding cash advances from  accountable  officers  who  are  no
        longer in the service nor connected with the Municipality; and (iv)
        require strictly  an  Office  Clearance  from  Money  and  Property
        accountabilities, pursuant to Sections 200 and 256, Volume I of the
        Government Accounting and Auditing Manual (GAAM) from all personnel
        who seek retirement,  resignation,  transfer  or  claim  for  death
        benefits in order that all accountabilities could be  appropriately
        settled; and


     b. The  Municipal  Accountant  (i)  maintain  Subsidiary  Ledgers  and
        schedules for the accounts used for long outstanding cash  advances
        to facilitate monitoring of the amount outstanding per employee  to
        ensure that no additional cash advance will  be  granted  to  those
        with outstanding balances and to support the amounts  reflected  in
        the financial statements and submit same to the  Audit  Team;  (ii)
        refrain from granting additional cash advances to any  official  or
        employee unless previous cash advance given  to  him/her  is  first
        settled or a proper accounting thereof is made, pursuant to Section
        4.1.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002; and (iii) ensure that  supporting
        documents attached to the  liquidation  reports  are  accurate  and
        complete before processing the claims in  accordance  with  Section
        4(6) of PD No. 1445.

The  Municipal  Accountant  and  Municipal  Treasurer  promised  to
   prepare adjusting  entries  of  the  foregoing  lapses  in  recording  of
   collections  and  promised  to  work  back  and  trace   the   difference
   particularly that pertains to General Fund amounting to P289.74.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended the following:


     a. The LDRRMO to (i) prepare and submit the  LDRRMFIP  and  Report  on
        Sources and Utilization of DRRM to the  Audit  Team  in  accordance
        with Items 5.1.2 and 5.1.5  of  COA  Circular  No.  2012-002  dated
        September 12, 2012; (ii) consider the requisitions of items  needed
        to maintain pre-positioned food packs and hygiene kits for at least
        500 families at any given  time  in  compliance  with  the  minimum
        standards and pursuant to NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1; and (iii)
        attend trainings on the conduct of Rapid  Damage  Assessment  Needs
        Analysis (RDANA);


     b. The Municipal Mayor consider the provision of  additional  manpower
        at the LDRRM Office pursuant to Section 5.2 of the JMC  No.  2014-1
        dated April 4, 2014 bearing in mind the provision of Section 325(a)
        of R.A. No. 7160;


     c. The Property and Supply Officer always  ensure  that  issuances  of
        small tangible items with serviceable life of more  than  one  year
        and property, plant  and  equipment  are  supported  with  Inventor
        Custodian Slip (ICS)  and  Acknowledgement  Receipt  for  Equipment
        (ARE), respectively, pursuant to  COA  Circular  No.  2005-002  and
        Section 42 of the MNGAS, Volume II for LGUs;



     d. The Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere with the guidelines set
        forth by (i) COA Circular No. 2012-002 and COA Circular  No.  2014-
        002 for proper accounting and recording of the utilization  of  the
        LDRRM Fund; (ii) Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and COA  Circular  No.
        2012-001  dated  June  14,  2012  for   proper   documentation   of
        transactions; and (iii) COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1,
        2015 for proper recording of transactions of the Municipality; and


     e. The Bids and Awards Committee  (BAC)  to  strictly  adhere  to  the
        requirements of Annex H of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 on
        the procurement of goods and services thru Small Value Procurement.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended  that  the  Municipal  Mayor
   instruct the BAC to (a) furnish  the  Audit  Team  with  a  copy  of  the
   Purchase Order/Contract of Agreement and its attachments within five  (5)
   working days from the execution of the contract as required under Section
   3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 23, 2009; (b) make sure
   that  Purchase  Orders/Contracts  contain   basic   data/information   as
   prescribed  by  COA  Circular  No.  96-010;  (c)  observe  strictly   the
   provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to  ensure  adherence
   to the principles of transparency, competitiveness and public monitoring;
   (d) strengthen internal control over the evaluation of the bids and post-
   qualification of bidders by requiring the  BAC  and  the  TWG  to  ensure
   timeliness, completeness and veracity of the post-qualified documents  as
   reported in the Bid  and  Post-  Qualification  Evaluation  Reports;  (e)
   submit the lacking documents cited above to the Audit Team to comply with
   the requirements of the Revised IRR of R.A.  No.  9184  and  address  the
   deficiencies enumerated; and (f) prepare various  reports/documents  with
   complete information and with extra care  to  avoid  errors  which  could
   mislead the readers/users of the reports.


        We also recommended  that  the  Municipal  Accountant  ensure  that
   liquidated damages are imposed to suppliers for the  delays  incurred  in
   accordance with Item 3.1 of Annex D of the 2016 Revised IRR of  R.A.  No.
   9184.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Budget  Officer
   strictly observe the general limitations on personal services cited under
   Section 325 (a) of R.A. No. 7160 and Local Budget Circular No.  98.   The
   Municipal Mayor, being the Local Chief Executive, should always be guided
   by the conditions of the agency’s  annual  and  supplemental  budgets  to
   ensure that the budgeting requirements are completely complied  with.  As
   part of his fiscal responsibility,  he  should  allow  only  payments  of
   personal services costs and other expenditures  expressly  authorized  by
   law.

        The Municipal Mayor  together  with  the  department  heads  should
   increase its efforts in raising its revenues/receipts such as  increasing
   tax   collection    efficiency    and    generating    revenue    related
   projects/activities to increase the base of PS limitation.

        We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant take  extra  care
   in recording the transactions of the Municipality in accordance with  COA
   Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that the  Municipal  Mayor  instruct  the  Municipal
   Accountant and the concerned employee  who  prepares  the  payroll to
   adhere to the provisions of LBC No. 103  in  the  computation  of  the
   RATA  of municipal officials and  employees.   Also, direct the concerned
   officials and employees to refund the excess RATA of P311,852.28 that were paid  to
   them.

        We further recommended that the Municipal  Accountant  ensure  that
   required supporting documents of the payrolls are  complete  pursuant  to
   Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445.  Also, submit the  unsubmitted  Applications
   for Leave.  Moreover, department heads entitled to RATA  should  properly
   accomplish their  DTRs  and  AFL  and  provide  the  required  supporting
   documents to avoid confusion as to the nature of the absences.

We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the Municipal
   Accountant give priority to the submission of the accounts and  financial
   reports within the reglementary  period  to  enable  the  Audit  Team  to
   conduct timely audit thereof and promptly inform the  management  of  the
   deficiencies noted, if any.

We recommended that Management direct the collectors to remit daily all collections to the Liquidating Officer; and the designated Liquidating Officer to remit to the Municipal Treasurer the cash turned over by the collectors, to be deposited daily and intact or not later than the next banking day.
Management assured that collectors will be reminded to turn over their collections daily to the Liquidating Officer and  all collections shall be deposited daily.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer as the PCF Custodian to:
immediately submit to the Acting Municipal Accountant the Report of Disbursements together with the necessary documents to liquidate the Petty Cash Fund amounting to ₱70,000.00 and to avoid the disallowance of the noted deficiencies in the amount of ₱39,657.25; and
strictly comply on the full liquidation of the PCF every end of the year, disburse from the fund those non-recurring, emergency and petty expenses and adhere to the policies on PCF cited in Section 48 of MNGAS for LGU, Volume I.
During the exit conference, the Municipal Treasurer promised to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that Management demand from the concerned Accountable Officers the submission of the liquidation reports, Credit Advice from COA ICFAU or a copy of the transmittal letter duly stamped received by COA’s ICFAU, otherwise Notice of Suspension or Disallowance will be issued to the Accountable Officer.
Management promised to demand from the concerned accountable officer the transmittal letter or liquidation report or  refund of the amount.

We recommended that Management:
(a) direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately prepare and submit the required monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) for these bank accounts as of  December 31, 2019, and monthly thereafter;
(b) comply with the requirements of Treasury Circular 01-2017 and  Department Circular No.001-2015 to maintain account with PNB otherwise close the accounts and  transfer them to AGDB such as Land bank of the Philippines (LBP) or Development Bank of the Philippines (DBP); and
(c) Recoup the amount of ₱144,281.00 from the defunct Philippine Postal Savings Bank or file claim with Philippine Deposit Insurance Corporation.
Management promised to complete the Bank Reconciliation Statement as soon as possible and promise to close dormant account with PNB and DBP.

We recommended that Management make representation with the source national government agencies to facilitate the speedy implementation of the three unimplemented projects totaling ₱1.7 million, otherwise return the amount to the funding NGAs.
We also recommended that Management return the unused balances of all completed projects totaling ₱1.299 million in accordance with COA Circular 94-013.
Management promised to make representation with  the source/funding agency(DA/BFAR) if they could implement other project which is similar in nature or beneficial to target beneficiaries; follow up with DILG the purpose of the fund; and will refund the excess fund transferred.

We recommended that Management conduct formal communication with National Solid Waste Management Commission to hasten the request for review and approval of the SWMP.
We also recommended that a timetable or schedule of compliance specifying the remedial measures and eventual compliance be included in the plan as prescribed in Paragraph 2, Section 16 of RA 9003.
According to Management since the municipality has not located a suitable  sanitary landfill  compliant with the requirement  of EMB,  they will focus on  material recovery facility (MRF) and conduct seminar on waste segregation. They also promised to follow-up  the approval of their SWMP.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to properly maintain all cashbooks for cash advances, collections, and bank depository accounts in accordance with Section 26, 27 and 28 of NGAS for LGUs.
The Municipal Treasurer promised to comply the procedure  for  the proper maintenance of cashbooks.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to submit immediately the original copies of 174 cancelled checks, and strictly observe Section 59 of NGAS Manual for LGUs, Vol. I in the proper treatment of cancelled checks.
The municipal treasurer after lapsed of four (4) months was able to submit the cancelled checks and promised to turn-over regularly cancelled checks to COA.

We recommended that Management ensure that all projects identified under the 20% Economic Development Fund are fully implemented within the budget year to effectively and timely extend to the constituents the benefits of the agency’s development projects.
During the exit conference Management promised  to see to it that all proposed EDF Projects will be implemented during the year they are appropriated.

We recommended that Management strictly observe the provisions in DILG-DBM JMC 2017-1 on the appropriation and utilization of EDF and paragraph 2 of Section 5 thereof otherwise the amount shall be disallowed in audit.
The Management alleged that this practice was done before his term but promised to discontinue the practice  of  charging Engineering Design, Supervision, Administrative and Other Cost  (ESAO) with local projects.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to: (a) intensify  tax collection campaigns by sending treasury personnel to barangays especially during barangay assemblies for tax information drive in order to invite more taxpayers to pay taxes on time; and (b) reinforce deputation of barangay officials to assist the LGU in delivering tax bills and delinquency notices  to encourage  taxpayer to pay their taxes promptly.
Management promised to exert extra efforts in conducting tax campaigns to every barangay and to deputize barangay officials in distributing tax bills.

1.6	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain subsidiary ledgers for the CIP account based on the   List of On-going Infrastructure Projects and other pertinent records relative to the projects to have a meaningful and informative information that would serve as basis for decision-making by Management.

2.7	We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant  submit the financial reports, together with the supporting documents,  within the prescribed period for timely review and evaluation of transactions as to its propriety, completeness and validity and to inform Management of any defects found thereon so that  corrective measures can be  made immediately.

3.9	We recommended that the Municipal Assessor, in coordination with the Provincial Assessors Office, update the assessed values of the 187 real properties taking into consideration the issues encountered during the latest revision and ensure that the same will be addressed.
4.5	We recommended that the Solid Waste Management Coordinator properly utilize and dispose the pulverized plastics and bottles in accordance with the SWM plan.

5.5	We recommended that Management avoid assigning functions of regular personnel to job orders in the RHU and other departments. If this cannot be avoided due to inevitable circumstances, fill in the vacant regular positions and if warranted, to consider hiring these contract of service/job order workers as regular employees to meet the demands of their services especially during emergency cases.
6.5	We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to strictly observe the ₱5,000.00 net take home pay for each employee in compliance with the above-mentioned guideline.

7.6	We recommended that Management compel the Barangay Officials concerned to effect immediate liquidation of the fund transferred thereto by not making subsequent fund transfers to their favor unless previous fund transfers were liquidated / refunded.

8.7	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant compel the concerned Punong Barangay of its failure to submit the required documents and to inform in writing after 5 days the Auditor if the same are not complied despite receipt of the Municipal Accountant’s letter.

9.5	We recommended that the MPDO, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer, keenly monitor and fast track the implementation of the projects to achieve desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes that would benefit the community.
10.5	We recommended that the Local School Board maximize utilization of the SEF Funds in conformity with the foregoing regulations to attain fully the SEF objectives
11.4	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant compel the previous LCE concerned the immediate settlement/liquidation of the said cash advances.

12.7	We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer retrieve all stale checks and the corresponding DVs together with all supporting documents, and furnish the Municipal Accountant of the said documents for the preparation of the journal entry voucher.  Replacement checks shall be issued following the procedures in accordance with the above provision of the MNGAS, Vol. I.

13.5	We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the entity’s Annual Procurement Plan to avoid excessive expenditures and/or to revise its APP to conform to any changes in the items to be procured during the year, if needed.

14.5	We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Engineer to compel the concerned contractor to perform the actual quantity of the said item of works, as programmed, in accordance with the contract agreement, plans and specifications and inform the Auditor of the action taken thereon.
15.4	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant inform the contractor concerned that the said deficiency will be deducted from its   final billing and inform the Auditor of the action taken.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the MPDO to establish the GAD database through storage of necessary information in aid of effective gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation in accordance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-1 to ensure that the PPAs are aligned with GAD-related undertakings so that the intended beneficiaries are assured of the services, protection, and other assistance that may be provided.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the accounting office should update the Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards. Likewise, the Municipal treasurer’s office should maintain property cards. Accordingly, the two offices should make a periodic reconciliation of these records. Moreover, the inventory committee shall conduct a reliable physical inventory count at least once a year to determine which of their properties are actually existing. A team composed of the General Services and Accounting Department personnel should conduct the physical inventory count and reconcile the book and property records. Accordingly, the Accounting Department shall effect the correcting entries, where applicable, when properly supported with documentation. Finally, Management should drop the unserviceable properties from the inventory reports and dispose the same in accordance with the procedures outlined above.
observations.managementAction: AAR CY 2017
observations.reason: The Inventory Committee submitted the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment account (RPCPPE) of the movable properties as of June 30, 2019. However, the reconciliation of the results of the physical count with the books of accounts was still on-going.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management, henceforth, strictly implement projects programmed in the current year. Also, some of the projects had no site ownership issues while others needed additional budget.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Local Development Council and the Municipal Engineer to:
