a. ensure that the mandatory 20% of Internal Revenue Funds for Development was optimally utilized to implement identified priority programs and activities to help achieve the desirable socio-economic and environmental outcomes; 
b. properly plan and coordinate with DPWH and other concerned officials which have projects that coincide with the programmed infrastructure projects to ensure the timely completion of the projects; and
c. come up with well-defined guidelines in identifying priority programs and projects in order to project a feasible and appropriate results that can be delivered efficiently and effectively to the public on time.
observations.managementAction: Coordination was made by the Municipal Engineer.
observations.reason: There were still municipal projects that coincide with the implementation of National Government agencies resulting to full implementation of the planned Development projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Local Development Council and the Municipal Engineer to communicate with National Government agencies such as DPWH and other concerned government agencies to coordinate the proper scheduling of infrastructure projects planned for Development Fund for the timely accomplishment of the projects to ensure that the full intention of development would benefit all the constituents, and carefully choose each project to be included in the planned program.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Engineering Office only added additional inputs.  For the local access road project at Brgy. Pinagtung-ulan, they clarified that most of the times, LGUs are not thoroughly informed on the project procurement timeline of the national government, resulting to unforeseen events on the implementing of projects locally funded, mostly those adjacent or succeeding theirs, and frequently they are party to adjust during the unexpected occurrences. Subsequent coordination was undertaken.
observations.reason: There were unforeseen events by National Government Agency’s implementation of their project which caused suspension/delay and subsequently completed; and that the projects were implemented/ completed within the period thereafter.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: a) General Services Officer to procure government insurance coverage for all government insurable buildings to guarantee protection from fire, earthquake, storm and/or other acts of nature; and b) Municipal Budget Officer to include the premiums for the general insurance covering all insurable properties in the Municipality’s annual budget.
observations.managementAction: The concerned offices have complied with the recommendation; the budget for premiums was included in the current year appropriations.
observations.reason: Not all insurable properties were insured due to insufficiency of funds. Appropriations for insurance premiums was included in the CY 2019 Annual Budget but insufficient to cover all insurable properties.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a) Instigate a standard hiring process for engaging in job order contracts based on a Work Program with identified requirements/necessities of the particular office or department and the duties and responsibilities of the position to ensure that all job order contracts actually augments an existing need for manpower; and b) Enjoin the HRMO and Municipal Accountant for the proper and complete the documentation before payment of wages of the job order personnel.
observations.managementAction: Starting July 01, 2019, all Job Order Contracts were based on a Work Program with identified requirements/necessities of the particular office or department and the duties and responsibilities of the position. The Municipal Accountant and HRMO complied with the recommendation.
observations.reason: Implemented. The Municipality complied with the recommendation and job orders were based on Work Programs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to process and remit shares from the RPT collections to the Barangays within 5 days after the end of each quarter.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer already ensured that Barangays shares from the RPT collections were remitted within 5 days after the end of each quarter.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned Municipal employees and officers to cause the immediate settlement of cash advances. Likewise, instruct the Municipal Accountant to:           (i) strictly monitor the grant and liquidation of cash advances and ensure that they are liquidated within the prescribed period in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002; (ii) ensure that cash advances are granted only for a duly authorized specific purpose supported with approved list of payees and expenses to be incurred; and (iii) refrain from granting cash advances to Job Order and/or Contracts of Service employees.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accountant issued three (3) notices to liquidate and one (1) demand letter to concerned employees and officials with long outstanding cash advances and those still connected with the Municipality. 

Moreover, salary deductions amounting to P1,000.00 were imposed to various accountable officers to gradually reduce the long outstanding cash advances
observations.reason:
Lapses in the internal control of the LGU in the processing, granting and liquidation of cash advances were still observed in CY 2019;

Likewise, the lack of initiative on the part of the concerned Municipal Officials to enforce liquidation of the long outstanding cash advances, resulted in the same observations in CY 2019, as reiterated in Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) ensure strict compliance with the requirements of COA Circular Nos. 75-6, 75-6a and Administrative Order No. 239 series of 2008 on the proper use of government vehicle particularly the proper use of Driver’s Trip Tickets and the preparation of required monthly reports pursuant to the requirements of the Manual on Audit for Fuel Consumption of Government Motor Vehicle to avoid wasteful, excessive and/or unnecessary expenditures relating thereto; (b) initiate the conduct of a thorough investigation regarding the vehicular accident and determine persons accountable therefore which shall be held personally liable for the acquisition cost of the service vehicle amounting to P1,350,000.00; and (c) assure that all government vehicles are properly insured with the GSIS so that the Municipality is protected in case of vehicular accidents and third party claims.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Instruct the OIC-GSO to:       (i) put markings For Official Use Only on all vehicles owned by the Municipality and ensure that all bears plate prescribed for all government vehicle in accordance with COA Circular No. 75-6 dated November 7, 1975;          (ii) ensure that properties are covered with duly accomplished Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) before issuance to end-users to easily pinpoint accountabilities therein;

b. Require all government officials who are assigned with service vehicles, including their designated drivers, to park all vehicles within the premises of the Municipal Compound and/or Motor Pool during weekends, holidays and after regular office hours, except those that are covered with approved travel orders falling within the specified prohibited days and time; and

c. Require the Municipal Accountant to submit Monthly Reports of Official Travels and Monthly Reports of Fuel Consumption after the end of each month for proper control and monitoring on the usage of service vehicles and the related expenditures thereto such as, fuels and lubricants.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-GSO initiated the markings of various government service vehicle with “Official Use Only” and the name of the Municipality as embodied in the unnumbered memorandum dated July 2, 2019. Moreover, issuances of service vehicle to various Municipal Officials were already covered with Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) starting July 1, 2019.
observations.reason:
Municipal Officials with issued motor vehicle were still allowed to park at the residence of the personnel with assigned vehicles due to lack of covered parking space within the Municipal Compound. 

Likewise, the attached markings on the service vehicles were made of magnetic stickers only, which can be easily removed thus, defeating the purpose of marking government vehicles. 

Moreover, Monthly Reports of Official Travels and Monthly Reports of Fuel Consumption and the Report and/or explanation regarding the service vehicle involved in a car accident were still not submitted to the Audit Team for proper evaluation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Municipal Mayor that Representation and Transportation allowances must be given after actual services have been rendered pursuant to Section 1 of LBC No. 103. Likewise, payments thereof must be supported with complete documentation to prove the validity and regularity of the claims especially the reimbursable RATA. 

Moreover, the Municipal Mayor was advised to require all incumbent and OICs of regular positions who are assigned with or uses government motor transportation to refund all claimed transportation allowances and henceforth, refrain from granting full transportation allowances to said government officials. Accordingly, fuel expenses incurred and paid by the Municipality either through reimbursements or direct payments involving Municipal Officials claiming transportation allowance must also be refunded.
observations.managementAction:
After the issuance of the Audit Observation Memorandum (AOM), payment of Transportation Allowance was already made at the end of each month when services have already been rendered.  

Incumbents and Officers-in-Charge (OICs) of regular positions with issued service vehicles were no longer paid with Transportation Allowance pursuant to LBC No. 103 dated May 15, 2013.
observations.reason: Reimbursable Transportation Allowance is still not supported with complete documentation.

observations.managementAction: The Management was able to submit some of the lacking documents to the Audit Team.
observations.reason:
Most of the lacking documents were still not submitted by the concerned personnel, as these were misplaced/lost due to improper filing/sorting of related supporting documents. 

Likewise, other lapses in the procurement and implementation of various goods and infrastructure projects were still observed, hence, this finding is reiterated in Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned officials to submit the financial reports/statements of calendar years 1994 to 2002 to the Audit Team. The Municipal Accountant - Designee and the Municipal Treasurer in coordination with the former Municipal Accountant – Designee must render once and for all the actual and true status of financial accounts of the Municipality, otherwise, the responsible officials should be held liable under the Revised Penal Code for their failure to render the accounts and reporting thereof.
observations.managementAction: Continuous efforts in digging up records are being made by the Municipal Accountant-Designee and Municipal Treasurer.
observations.reason: No financial reports pertaining to years 1994 to 2002 were submitted to the Audit Team due to unavailability of the related documents needed in the preparation of the reports.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that:

The Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to exert extra effort to account for the unreconciled items appearing in the Bank Reconciliation Statements to faithfully present the correct balances of the accounts for the period; and

The Municipal Accountant prepare the Monthly BRS for all bank accounts and submit same regularly together with the paid checks, debit/credit memos for verification as provided for under Section 74 of PD No. 1445.  Continue securing bank statements of prior years to establish the correct balance of the Cash in Bank and update and submit the subsidiary ledgers of all the bank accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer had already sent a request letter to Development Bank of the Philippines (DBP) Kalibo for previous years’ copy of Bank Statements as per advice of the Municipal Mayor.  In addition, the Municipal Accountant-Designee has already prepared and submitted Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements and paid checks for all bank accounts.  Likewise, subsidiary ledgers of all the bank accounts were already maintained and updated.
observations.reason: The unreconciled items amounting to P13,640,085.05 still exist in the Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRSs) as of December 31, 2019.  Likewise, delay in the submission of BRSs was still incurred and the subsidiary ledgers of all the bank accounts were not yet submitted to the Audit Team.  Moreover, letter request to Land Bank of the Philippines (LBP) Odiongan was not yet sent.

observations.managementAction: Demand letters were already sent to the accountable officers to settle their obligations particularly the long outstanding balances.  Likewise, the Municipal Accountant-Designee has already maintained subsidiary ledgers and schedules of cash advances.   Moreover, Certificates of Expenses not Requiring Receipts were already attached to the Liquidation Reports.  Additional claims for travel were already supported with Itinerary of Travel.  Lastly, the Municipal Mayor had already secured the approval of the Governor for his claims for travel outside the province.
observations.reason: For CY 2019, long outstanding cash advances still exist.  The municipal officials and employees still failed to liquidate their cash advances for travel within the prescribed period resulting to accumulation of unliquidated cash advances as of December 31, 2019 in the amount of P21,485,624.99.  In addition, claims for lodging of some municipal officials and employees in excess of the allowed rates of P750.00/P900.00/P1,100.00 per day totaling P17,680.00 were not supported with the certification as absolutely necessary in the performance of an assignment from the head of the agency concerned.  Likewise, claims for motorboat and taxi fare of almost all of the municipal officials and employees totaling P41,530.00 were not supported with the corresponding official receipts. Lastly, the date on the boat and terminal tickets of one of the municipal officials and employees did not jibe on the date of his actual travel.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to provide depreciation on Infrastructure Assets so that the annual decrease in value could be deducted from the original cost and PPE could be presented correctly in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Allowances for depreciation on Infrastructure Assets totaling P14,325,819.82 were already provided.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a.	instruct the BAC to a) furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the Contract of Agreement and its attachments within five (5) working days from the execution of the contract as required under Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 23, 2009; b) observe strictly the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to ensure adherence to the principles of transparency, competitiveness and public monitoring; c) strengthen internal control over the evaluation of the bids and post-qualification of bidders by requiring the BAC and the TWG to ensure timeliness, completeness and veracity of the post-qualified documents as reported in the Bid and Post- Qualification Evaluation Reports; d) submit the lacking documents cited above to the Audit Team to comply with the requirements of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and address the deficiencies enumerated; and e) prepare various reports/documents with extra care to avoid errors which could mislead the readers/users of the reports; and

b.	instruct the Municipal Engineer to observe DPWH D.O. 197 dated October 7, 2016 which provides the revised guidelines in the preparation of the Approved Budget for the Contract and Annex A of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 relative to the detailed engineering for the procurement of infrastructure projects.  Likewise, prepare PPMP for the 20% development projects for incorporation in the APP.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality has already submitted some of the lacking documents noted in CY 2018 audit.  Likewise, some of the audit recommendations from prior year’s audit were already complied with.
observations.reason: Various deficiencies and lapses in the procurement and implementation of infrastructure projects, as well as their payments and recording were still noted.

observations.managementAction: The Municipality has already submitted some of the lacking documents noted in CY 2018 audit.  Likewise, some of the audit recommendations from prior year’s audit were already complied with.
observations.reason: Although no procurement for consulting services was made during the year, similar observations were still noted in the procurement of infrastructure projects.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:

Municipal Disaster Risk and Reduction Officer (MDRRMO) to prepare the LDRRMFIP in accordance with the format prescribed in Annex A of COA Circular No. 2012-002. It should also include all the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years;

Bids and Awards Committee to strictly adhere to the requirements of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 for the submission of contracts/purchase order and for procurement of various goods and services of the municipality, respectively; and

Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere with the guidelines set forth by COA Circular No. 2012-002 for proper accounting of the utilization and transfer of LDRRM Fund as well as proper recording of various transactions in the Special Trust Fund and Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 for proper documentation of transactions. She should also be guided by COA Circular No. 2015-009 in the proper recording of transaction in accordance with Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards (PPSAS).
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO had already included in the LDRRMFIP the list of some projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years.
observations.reason: The Municipality still incurred lapses in the planning and utilization of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF) for CY 2019 as follows: (a) failure to include in its CY 2019 LDRRMF Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) the list of all projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years; (b) lapses in the preparation and submission of the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF; (c) lapses in the acknowledgement, distribution and accounting of In-Kind Donations from various donors;  (d) failure to comply with the minimum standards on service delivery relative to disaster preparedness; and (e) lapses in the procurement, documentation, recording and charging of various expenditures.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer ensure timely deposit of collections and issue OR for the transfer of unexpended balance of LDRRMF to Trust Liabilities – LDRRMF Account and transfer of fund to 20% DF bank account.
observations.managementAction: Undeposited collections noted in CY 2018 were already deposited by the Municipal Treasurer in January 2019.  In addition, for CY 2019, official receipts for the receipts of funds transferred from the General Fund Proper to 20% DF bank account were already issued.  And timely deposit of collections was already practiced in CY 2019.

Inquiry with the Solid Waste Focal Person revealed that continuous efforts are being made to fully comply with the provisions of R.A. No. 9003 or the Ecological Solid Waste Management Act of 2000 such as a) proper segregation of reusable or recyclable materials was encouraged from the barangay level; b) the LGU’s temporary dumpsite was surrounded with fence and it was properly covered with soil which plays important role in sanitation, fire prevention, odor and vermin control; and c) the five barangays have already constructed their own Material Recovery Facility (MRF).
observations.reason: The Municipality still has no final disposal facility or sanitary landfill.

observations.managementAction: Programs/projects/activities which do not partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures were already realigned to other development projects.  In addition, the MPDC and BAC already insured that that all projects under the 20% development fund were included in the Annual Procurement Plan (APP) of the agency in compliance with the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184.
observations.reason:
The Municipality still (a) failed to fully implement the projects funded under the current and continuing appropriations of the 20% Development Fund; (b) charged expenditures which do not partake the nature of capital expenditures; and (c) incurred lapses in the recording of transactions and in the preparation of various reports and Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts (SCBAA).
unicipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant commented that the unremitted amount to GSIS, PHILHEALTH.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

strictly enforce the settlement of all unliquidated cash advances as of December 31, 2018;

stop the granting of additional cash advances to Accountable Officers, officials and employees unless the previous ones are fully settled, to avoid accumulation of the same; and

institute appropriate legal actions/sanctions against those erring accountable officers/employees for continued non-compliance with the existing laws, rules and regulations on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances, if warranted.
observations.reason:
Net decrease of ₱510,537.07 was noted in three accounts e.g. Advances for Payroll, Advances to Special Disbursing Officer & Advances to Officers and Employees.

Reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management fully implement all projects whose funds were granted by the national agencies to effectively and timely extend to the constituents the benefits of the projects.

We recommended that the Sanggunian Bayan enact an ordinance to update the Local Revenue Code and every five years thereafter in accordance with RA 7160, as basis for the collection of taxes, fees and charges.
observations.managementAction: Office of the Sangguniang Bayan conveyed a letter to revenue-concerned offices in LGU-San Jose last Jan. 21, 2020 in order to perceive proposed new rates of taxes, fees and charges.
observations.reason: Ongoing comparison analysis with other same-class LGUs.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management direct:
adherence to the accounting standards and method of recording supplies and materials in accordance with the above cited provisions;

the implementation of requisition procedures and the reporting on issuance of supplies/materials as required under Sec. 114 and 121 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs; and

the Municipal Accountant to record the appropriate accounts upon submission of the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) and the appropriate supporting documents.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the Supply Officer has submitted partial report of issuance of supplies and materials.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
implement feasible solid waste management projects such as construction of the MRF and perimeter fence around the open dump site


	direct the MSWMB to coordinate and assist all barangay in solid waste management, such as waste segregation, sorting, recycling and composting at their respective Material Recovery Facility (MRF); and

conduct Information Education and Communication campaign to the barangays, schools, private and business sectors and other institution.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal 10- Year Solid Waste Management Plan was submitted to the NSWMP on January 31, 2018 for review. The management is awaiting for the NSWMP’s notice for the latter to defend its plan.
observations.reason: Pending approval of the said plan.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Municipal MDDRM Officer to ensure that preparedness programs such as stockpiling be procured in time when disaster actually hit the province and mass feeding be implemented to answer the needs of families in the evacuation centers during and immediately after disaster.
observations.managementAction: No corresponding balance for inventory held for distribution was noted in the financial statements.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately prepare and submit the required monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) for the 12 bank accounts of the Municipality as of December 31, 2016;

require that the personnel assigned to prepare the BRS shall not be given other duties other than the preparation of BRS, to enable him/her to finish the assigned task promptly; and

comply with the requirements of Treasury Circular 01-2017 and Department Circular No. 001-2015 to maintain account with PNB and PPSB otherwise close the accounts and transfer them to AGDB such as LBP or DBP.
observations.managementAction:
Ongoing reconciliation.

                                A staff in the accounting section is updating b the BRS.

                             
                                Account with PNB and PPSB is now closed.
observations.reason: Ongoing reconciliation.       Reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds Accounts to present fairly the financial position of the municipality.
Direct Management to include in the LDRRMP of the ensuing year activities and projects under disaster rehabilitation and post recovery which are not yet included in the previous plan and budget.

Direct Management to desist and stop hiring teachers charged against the SEF whose names are not included   in the registry of qualified applicants as required.  Likewise, a written explanation on why the violation was committed is requested to be submitted for us to make a decision in audit.

Direct the concerned officials and employees to be extra careful in selecting projects to be indorsed for funding by the national government to avoid duplication of projects and the return of the amount of grant to the source agency.

We recommended that management require the GAD Focal Point System to include in the submission of the GAD AR the reports/documents enumerated in Section 4.C.8.5 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular 2013-01 to facilitate the monitoring, evaluation and substantiation of the reported accomplishments.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Point Systems appropriately identify PAPs that are gender-responsive and gender-sensitive for inclusion to its GAD Plan and stop disbursing GAD Funds to PAPs that are not related to gender and development.
We also recommended that payment for philhealth premiums of the indigents be charged to the appropriate fund of the Municipality.

Direct Management to prioritize inclusion in the Project Procurement Management Plan of the ensuing year the personal protective equipment and supplies of the garbage collectors in compliance with Section 23(a) of RA 9003.

Direct the Management to prioritize inclusion in the Project Procurement Management Plan of the ensuing year the purchase of a new garbage compactor, within the first quarter, to address the problem of frequency in collecting and handling solid wastes.

We recommended that Management include the utilization of the unexpended balance for LDRRM fund in the LDRRMFIP in each year for programs/projects/activities related to disaster risk management and prevention.
We recommended that Management require the barangays to immediately submit the liquidation reports and refund the unutilized fund transfers for completed projects, if any.
observations.reason: Not all funds are liquidated as ₱539,886.06 remained outstanding.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the municipal accountant not to allow payment of utilities and communication expenses on a reimbursement basis in conformity with Item 8, Section 4 of P.D. 1145.
We recommended that Management instruct the Treasurer and/or his authorized representative to stamp “PAID” all disbursement vouchers and their supporting documents after the release of checks.
Direct the MPDO to submit the AIP regularly to the DILG Regional Office and DBM in compliance with Section 6 of the afore-cited JMC.

We recommended that management stop the practice of granting cash support directly to the heads of agencies but instead any disbursements for mobilization activities of the said NGAs should be charged to the appropriate funds allocated for the purpose and subject to the usual accounting and auditing rules and regulations.
Likewise, require the foregoing agencies to include in their respective PPMPs the PAPs related to the said activities.

Direct the Municipal Agriculture Office to monitor and device a feedback or reporting mechanism on the progress of funded projects to be implemented in accordance with the Memorandum of Agreement. Consider to return the unexpended balance of the released funds if it is improbable that the project could continue.

We recommended Management to:

a.	Direct the Municipal Planning and Development Office to monitor the implementation of the projects as embodied in the Annual Investment Plan;

b.	Instruct the Municipal Engineering Office to coordinate with the departments concerned to fast track the implementation of the projects; and 

 c.	Remind the Mayor’s Office to ensure compliance with the responsibilities provided for in paragraph 5.0 of DBM and DILG JMC No. 2017-1 to achieve desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes.

Instruct the LCPC Secretariat to exert effort in the establishment and maintenance of a complete database for children in order to create an effective/responsive LCPC PAPs for the best interest of the children.

Direct the concerned officials and employees to place the eight (8) computer units at the appropriate and safe area. Likewise, fast track the utilization of the purchased IT equipment according to its intended purpose.
observations.reason: Computers are already in an appropriate safe area but still unutilized by the intended users.

observations.recommendation: Require the employees concerned to conduct regularly the reconciliation of PPE balances on both records of the Municipal Accountant and the Property Custodian. If there is a difference identified between the records, the same shall be adjusted in the books of accounts and/or reflected in the RPCPPE.
observations.reason: Other PPE accounts not yet adjusted.
Documentary requirements not yet submitted.

observations.recommendation:
Require Management to dispose immediately the six (6) unserviceable vehicles in conformity with Section 79 of PD 1445 by following the guidelines of Section 503 of GAAM Vol. I.
Likewise, other unserviceable properties aside from those vehicles be disposed of properly also.
Instruct the MPDC to construct a ground water monitoring well system in the appropriate locations within the sanitary landfill to continuously monitor the quality of ground water.
observations.reason: Coordination with relative National Government Agencies were done and feedback not yet communicated by management.

observations.recommendation: Direct the concerned officials and employees to place the eight (8) computer units at the appropriate and safe area. Likewise, fast track the utilization of the purchased IT equipment according to its intended purpose.
observations.reason: Awaiting the completion of training center and MOA/MOU with the DICT.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to review and analyze the said account. Exert extra efforts in recovering the said receivable by exhausting all possible means and seeking legal advice. Likewise, adequately document the actions taken thereon. Should all efforts fail, request for a write-off to the COA of the subject account and support the letter of request with (a) proof or evidences that could substantiate efforts exerted to review and validate the account, and enforce collection thereof; (b) list of available records and extent of validation made on the said account; and (c) a certification ascertaining the reasons why pertinent records and documents could not be located, as the case may be.
observations.reason: Management is still in the processed of collating the required documents from the concerned Accountable Employees/Individuals. A request for write-off was already done by Management to COA.

observations.recommendation:
1.16 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, Treasurer/General Services
Officer, and Engineer to comply with the Accounting and Reporting Procedures on
Local Road Asset Management System, to perform their mandated duties and
responsibilities in the submission of reports and maintenance of records, and to
coordinate with each other.
1.17 Submit copies of the Report on the Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE), and Property,
Plant and Equipment Ledger Card (PPELC)
1.18 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw Journal Entry Vouchers
to correct the errors in the books of the LGU relative to the computation of
depreciation.

2.12 We recommended that the Head of the Procuring Entity require the BAC to
submit the lacking documents and explanation on why these were not submitted.
Strictly comply with the provision of RA 9184, its RIRR, and its relevant annexes
and GPPB issuances in all procurements of the LGU. Moreover, we
recommended that LGU submit the aforementioned contracts and pertinent
documents for evaluation and review. Henceforth, strictly comply with the
provisions of COA Circular No. 2009-01.

2.13 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant should attach complete
documentation on disbursements to ensure the validity as well as the correctness
of the transactions.

3.13 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to instruct:
a) The Municipal Budget Officer to prioritize specific PPAs, which are to
be implemented in the budget year; and
b) The Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC), Municipal
Engineer, and other implementors to coordinate with each other to ensure
the full implementation of the 20% DF PPAs during the budget year.

We recommended that the Revenue Collection Clerks, regardless of the nature of the
collections, should remit their collections on a daily basis. They should also
properly accomplish the RCDs to facilitate the verification of the same by the
Municipal Treasurer upon remittance.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer should verify the RCDs of the
collectors upon remittance as to correctness and completeness of the needed
information in the report.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer be
required to remit in full the taxes withheld on compensation and payments to
contractors and suppliers on or before the due dates.

6.6 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant
and the Municipal Treasurer to observe the due dates of the remittances of premium
and loan amortization to GSIS.

7.7 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Person and
Municipal Budget Officer to strictly comply with the provisions of PCW-DILG-
DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 on prioritization, and training of personnel
responsible for planning and implementation of GAD programs, projects and
activities to ensure that the PPAs are aligned with GAD-related undertakings so that
the intended beneficiaries are assured of the services, protection, and other assistance
that may be provided.

We recommended that management fill in the plantilla positions, or designate
permanent staff in a concurrent capacity, or reassign LGU personnel to the
LDRRMO as required by the existing regulations who will perform the function of
research and planning.

We recommended that management should strictly comply with the provisions of
RA 9003 in the establishment of a sanitary landfill and in the formulation of
necessary education promotion and information campaign strategies on the source
reduction, segregation, recycling, composting of solid wastes, and the establishment
of a Sanitary Landfill.

11.8 We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant
and all responsible officials to immediately submit the disbursement vouchers,
together with their supporting documents, and cancelled checks, to support the
recorded check disbursements. This is without prejudice to any notices of
suspension, charges or disallowances that may be issued as a result of our audit.

11.9 Ensure that all disbursement vouchers, pertinent supporting documents and copies
of the checks be submitted on a regular and timely basis.

11.10 This is without prejudice to any notice of suspension, charges or disallowances that
may be issued as a result of our audit.

12.5 We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to require the identified Persons Liable
in the Notice of Disallowance to settle their disallowance.

12.6 We recommended further for the Municipal Accountant to send copy of the Notices
to the Persons Liable for their information on their unsettled disallowance.

13.6 We recommend that the Municipal Mayor should direct the Municipal Engineer
and Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to strictly implement the
provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant see to it that the PCF is not used for regular and recurring expenses and only use it in the non-recurring, emergency and petty expenses.
observations.reason: Payment for fuel, oil and lubricants were still made out of the PCF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management, henceforth, strictly adhere to the LCPC provisions of RA 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012.
observations.reason: There were still PPAs programmed under the LCPC which were not in accordance with DILG MC 2012-120, such as protective services of battered women and youth and welfare program.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant evaluate the collectability of receivables, review and analyze the accounts and, if not possible due to the absence of records, the Local Chief Executive request for authority to write-off from the Commission on Audit.
observations.reason: The subject receivable accounts were still outstanding as of December 31, 2018 because there were no adequate records to support the accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management submit its GAD Plan and Budget to the Provincial Planning and Development Office (PPDO) to ensure the alignment of the Municipality’s GAD PPAs with the priorities of the province and for submission to the DILG Regional Office for its review and approval. Moreover, management is required to prepare and submit their Annual GAD Accomplishment Report based on DILG-approved GPB.
observations.reason: The GPB was still not submitted to the PPDO within the prescribed period for endorsement to the DILG.

observations.recommendation: Required the Municipal Treasurer to prepare a certified list of taxpayers and their taxes due and collectible for the year to serve as basis for the report of the Municipal Accountant in recording the Real Property Tax Receivables/Special Education Tax Receivables and their corresponding credits at the beginning of the year pursuant to Section 20 of the NGAS Manual, Volume I.
observations.reason:
As of December 31, 2018, a difference was noted between the Certified List of Delinquent Real Property Tax and the total amount in the financial statements RPT/SET (GF & SEF)- ₱2,943,006.89
List of RPT Deliq. 1,306,434.16
Difference ₱ 1,636,572.73

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management consider the disposal of its unserviceable properties in order to realize additional income, otherwise, if left unattended the subject unserviceable properties may continue to deteriorate and eventually lose their salvage values.
observations.reason: The identified unserviceable properties were still not disposed by the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated the audit recommendation that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer (PCF Custodian) strictly adhere to the provision of Section 48 Volume I of the NGAS for LGUs and relevant rules and regulations on the proper maintenance and recording of transactions of Petty Cash Fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct Treasury and Accounting Offices to comply with all the laws, rules and regulations on cash management.
observations.reason: The Accountable Officer in-charge started implementing the recommendation in CY 2019.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated the audit recommendation that the Municipal Accountant require all Accountable Officers to immediately liquidate their cash advances by submitting required report and settlement documents to the Accounting Office and refund the unexpended balance to the Municipal Treasurer. We likewise recommended that the Municipality avoid granting cash advances to non-regular employees and additional cash advances to accountable officers with outstanding account.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated the audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive ensure the submission of all disbursement vouchers for confidential cash advances. We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant (i) certify the disbursements only after the completion of supporting documents before any payment of confidential funds is effected, and (ii) submit immediately all cash advances vouchers and their supporting documents to the Auditor.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this report.
Reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Accounting Office (i) raise immediately the detected discrepancy in the billing or statement of account; (ii) regularly reconcile its records with the GSIS; and (iii) make the necessary adjustments, if warranted.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated the audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Treasury and Accounting Offices to reconcile their records.  We further recommended that the Treasury Office should prepare remittance schedules based on checks issued only for the Accounting Office will only recognize taxes withheld from the issued checks for a certain month.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Treasury Office (i) exert effort in preparing accurate remittances on time; and (ii) inquire from Pag-IBIG and PHIC on the proper treatment of contributions withheld from salary differential which were belatedly paid to employees.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal General Services Officer to carry on and accelerate the process by which these unserviceable properties could be disposed of, in accordance with the abovementioned rules and regulations, so that the balances of Property, Plant and Equipment accounts presented in the financial statements would only represent those assets that are in existence and servicing the needs of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal OIC-General Services Officer stated that their Office already sought canvasses to the registered junkshops in the Municipality as to the cost per kilo of metal and scraps as basis of their appraisal and will start the actual appraisal upon disposal of the unserviceable ICTE.
observations.reason: The Municipal General Services Office stated that due to the volume of the unserviceable items and considering the insufficient space in their storage space where all unserviceable ICTE are still located, they are encountering difficulties in its appraisal. They also sought canvasses to the registered junkshops in the Municipality regarding the cost per kilo of metal and scraps as basis of their appraisal. The Municipal GSO committed to process the disposal of the subject unserviceable properties amounting to P942,396.25 after the disposal of the unserviceable ICTE costing P385,429.80.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Accountant transfer the unexpended/unutilized amount of QRF and DRRM-MOOE as Trust Liability-DRRM in the trust fund books solely for the purpose of supporting disaster risk reduction management activities of the LDRRM Council within the next five years.
observations.reason: Lack of available cash and continued disregard of the concerned agency officials to ensure that cash set aside for disaster risk and reduction and management activities are used specifically for the purpose for which it was appropriated.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) ensure that the Engineering technical staff conduct detailed engineering investigation, survey designs for infrastructure projects and duly approved in accordance with the standards and specifications prescribed by the HoPE and with Annex A of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; (b) refrain from issuing unjustifiable suspension orders and time extensions to cover up the real cause of slippage or negligence on the part of the Management or the contractor; and (c) direct the Municipal Engineer to (i) strictly monitor the implementation of projects to ensure their timely completion to enable early enjoyment of benefits by intended users; and (ii) observe compliance with COA Circular No. 2013-003 on the display of signboards for infrastructure projects. 

We further recommended that the (a) Bids and Awards Committee ensure that procurement of infrastructure projects are made strictly in accordance with the requirements of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR by verifying the regularity and completeness of eligibility requirements submitted by the bidders; (b) OIC – Municipal Accountant ensure that all claims are duly supported with proper and complete supporting documents required under Section 9.1. of COA Circular No. 2012-001; and (c) Municipal Treasurer ensure that checks are drawn only on duly approved disbursement vouchers and to require written authorization if the claimant is other than the payee.
observations.reason: Continued disregard of the concerned officials to act on the matter hence, this finding is reiterated in the Part II of this Report.
Continued disregard of the concerned officials to act on the matter hence, this finding is reiterated in the Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal School Board strictly comply with the prioritization of expenditures to be charged against the SEF based on DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 01, s. 2017 and Section 272 of the Local Government Code of 1991.
observations.reason: Minimal amount of ineligible expenditures were charged against SEF which was communicated to the agency during exit conference.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, with the assistance of the Local Development Council, the Municipal Development Officer and the Municipal Engineer optimize the utilization of the 20% development fund through: a) proper prioritization and planning of projects including the timing of execution; b) strict monitoring of project implementation to ensure prompt completion of the projects;         c) ensure that projects charged against the 20% Development Fund partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures that will contribute to the attainment of socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the Municipality; and d) ensure that expenditures such as, repair and maintenance of various vehicles and equipment, purchase of petroleum products and payment of salary of job order/casual employees are not charged against the fund in compliance with Item 4.0 of the DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-001 dated February 22, 2017.

We also recommended that the a) MBO and the Municipal Accountant coordinate with each other and reconcile their records regularly to ensure the accuracy and completeness of information reflected in their respective reports; and                b) Municipal Treasurer and/or Municipal Accountant submit the 40 DVs totalling P9,969,031.95 and its supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor for verification and audit.
observations.reason: Related discussion in observation No. 5, Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following courses of action:

The Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (i) ensure the optimal utilization of 70% mitigation fund of LDRRMF to strengthen the resilience of the constituents in times of calamities as required under RA 10121; (ii) see that the stockpile procured are distributed/utilized only for the purpose for which it was purchased; and (iii) prepare the LDRRMFIP correctly showing the 30% allocation for QRF and the details of PPAs to be funded by the 70% mitigation fund as well as the PPAs charged to unexpended LDRRMF of previous years; and

The Bids and Awards Committee observe the procedure and requirements of Section 29 and 35.1 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 with regard to bid opening and failure of bidding, including the procedures in negotiation (two failed bidding) provided under Annex H of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.
observations.reason: Related discussion in observation No. 7, Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BAC Secretariat, or other assigned as the case may be ensure the timely submission of copies of the contracts and purchase orders within five (5) working days from execution and issuance, to ensure timely review and evaluation of same, so that management could be immediately notified of any defects/deficiencies noted. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant review the purchase orders and its supporting documents before preparation of vouchers to determine completeness of the documentary requirements as required under the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.
observations.reason: The BAC still failed to submit copies of Purchase Orders (POs) and Contracts within the prescribed period; and support the Purchase Orders with complete documentary requirements. These were discussed in AOM. No. 2019-006 dated April 8, 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management study the packages offered by mobile networks and evaluate which would provide the best price favorable to the local government. We likewise recommended that Management refrain from paying excessive expenditures as required under COA Circular No. 2012-003 and see to it that claims are supported with adequate documentation.
observations.reason: The Telephone Expenses increased to P697,137.52 in 2018.
Reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Accountant should be instructed to comply with required supporting documents prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 in the granting of cash advances for payments for salaries, wages, allowances, honoraria and other similar expenses.
observations.managementAction: No action
observations.reason: Supporting documents of the DV of the cash advance were being attached to the liquidation.

observations.recommendation: To correct the procedure, we recommend that the payment for registration fees for CLUP trainings should be acknowledged by an Official Receipt of the government (AF51) in accordance with the provisions of Executive Order No. 292 and Section 68(1) of PD 1445.
observations.reason: Implementation could not be verified since there were no disbursement for CLUP trainings in CY 2018.

observations.recommendation:
Since fiscal responsibility rests upon the Head of the Agency, we recommend that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to render her accounts and submit the required financial transactions together with the supporting documents to the Accounting Division, and the Municipal Accountant to submit the accounts and reports to COA on or before the 10th day of the succeeding month.

Likewise, the accountable officer and/or other officials concerned are reminded of the sanctions imposed under Section 122 of PD 1445 in relation to Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code on the automatic suspension of payment of salaries for failure to render accounts in a period of two (2) months.
observations.managementAction: LGU submits accounts on a quarterly basis.
observations.reason: Submission of reports is still beyond the reglementary period.

observations.recommendation: To correct the deficiencies, we recommend that the Municipal Mayor reorganize the LCPC/MCPC and thru the concerned officials to appropriate one (1%) from its IRA in the LGUs Annual Budget in compliance with RA 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012.   We further recommend that the MCPC propose and implement Programs/Projects/Activities that advocates child rights, plan and conduct monitoring/interventions on children’s programs and projects.
observations.managementAction: No action taken
observations.reason: No allocation for the PPAs of the Local Council for the Protection of Children (LCPC) was made in CY 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that procurement of materials for the construction of shed houses be made in bulk thru competitive public bidding in compliance with Republic Act 9184 or the Government Procurement Reform Act and its revised implementing rules and regulations.  Avoid splitting of contract in the guise of on a per shed house basis.
observations.reason: Evaluation of the status could not be verified due to non-implementation of additional shed houses in CY 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that management strictly observe Section 9.1.3.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 on the documentary requirements for maintenance and repairs of government vehicles.
observations.managementAction: No Action
observations.reason: During the year 2018, repairs and maintenance of government vehicles were still done on shopping and reimbursement since according to them they only procure when the need arises and sometimes it was through credit from the local stores.

observations.recommendation: Direct the concerned officials and employees to place the eight (8) computer units at the appropriate and safe area. Likewise, fast track the utilization of the purchased IT equipment according to its intended purpose.
observations.managementAction: No Action taken.

observations.recommendation:
Instruct the Municipal Assessor to register all land owned by the Municipality under the Torrens Title System in conformity with COA Circular No. 92-386 and submit tax declarations which are duly signed by the Provincial Assessor to erase the doubts as to the genuineness of those documents.

Likewise, reconciliation between the records of the municipal accountant and the municipal assessor should be regularly made to accurately establish the valuation of land in the book of accounts of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: No Management Action.

observations.recommendation: Instruct the MPDC to construct a ground water monitoring well system in the appropriate locations within the sanitary landfill to continuously monitor the quality of ground water.
observations.managementAction: No Management action.

observations.recommendation: Direct the concerned officials and employees to place the eight (8) computer units at the appropriate and safe area. Likewise, fast track the utilization of the purchased IT equipment according to its intended purpose.
observations.managementAction: Awaiting the completion of training center and MOA/MOU with the DICT.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer or Head of the Inventory Committee agreed to completely fill up the RPCPPE and coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the balance per count with the balance per ledger card for each type of PPE. Any discrepancies between the actual count and the ledger card should be immediately verified and adjusted, if necessary, to fairly present the movable PPE account at year-end.

We recommended and Management agreed that the person immediately accountable for the unserviceable properties turn over the items to the Municipal Treasurer for cancellation of the Memorandum Receipt/Acknowledgement Receipt of Equipment.

We also recommended and the Local Chief Executive (LCE) agreed to create an Appraisal and Disposal Committee to appraise the value of unserviceable properties for the immediate disposal and dropping thereof from the book of accounts in order to present fairly the PPE account in the financial statements.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the Municipal Agriculturist to establish the actual status of the breeding stock dispersal program by tracing the whereabouts and condition of the original breeding stocks and their offspring, if any, and render a report to the Municipal Accountant which includes the fair market value of each stock, for proper recording in the books of accounts.

Henceforth, the Municipal Agriculturist shall keep systematic records of births, deaths, and disposal of breeding stocks. In case of death or disposal, facilitate the request for relief of accountability in accordance with Sections 151 and 152 of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992, for dropping the account from the books, if warranted.

We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to provide proper disclosure in the Notes to the Financial Statements in consonance with PPSAS 27 to provide information that may affect the fairness of the presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to conduct a thorough verification and analysis of the dormant receivable accounts and provide for an allowance for impairment in accordance with the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards or Philippine Financial Reporting Standards.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant comply with all the conditions and requirements of COA Circular No. 2016-005 for the possible requests for write-off of the specified dormant receivables, through the Local Chief Executive, to the Commission on Audit.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) City Accountant and the BAC to submit the required supporting documents to the payments made which we will evaluate; (b) City Development Council to properly plan activities pertaining to development projects; and (c) City Engineering Office to regularly monitor the status of implementation the projects so that necessary interventions may be done.

We recommended and Management agreed to (a) immediately address the environmental issues on the closed dumpsites and the inadequacy of necessary facilities and equipment through provision of prioritized fund support to SWM; (b) enforce the sanction and penalties for the violators of environmental ordinances; and (c) continue the conduct of information dissemination concerning the harmful effects of littering, illegal dumping, mixing wastes and burning of agricultural wastes.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive require the City Accountant to (a) adjust the book reconciling items including the stale checks; and (b) ensure that subsequent reconciling items are adjusted in the books immediately.

4.11.   We recommended and Management agreed that the City Mayor instruct the (a) Inventory Team to complete the physical validation of PPEs; (b) Accounting Office to (i) review the reconciliation and adjustments made in the books of accounts; and (ii) determine the status of payments and the transfer of ownership of the fully paid units, and adjust the accounts affected, as warranted;  (c) concerned offices to submit the reconciled report to the Audit Team not later than January 31 of the ensuing year; and (d) GSO to facilitate disposal of the unserviceable properties to maximize the returns that could be generated therefrom.

5.6.     We recommended and Management agreed that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to (a) check and validate the dormant receivable accounts; (b) exhaust all possible remedies to collect receivables and to demand liquidation of fund transfers; and (c) request for authority for the write-off of accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

6.26.     We recommended and Management agreed to (a) equip itself with the required equipage; and (b) continue its observance of the provisions of JMC No. 2013-1 and satisfactory performance in disaster risk reduction and management.

7.15.    We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive and the GAD FPS Technical Working Group (a) strictly adhere with the pertinent rules and regulations on the timely submission of the reports to the COA Audit Team and other oversight bodies; and (b) continuously adhere with Republic Act No. 9710 and relevant circulars including the City’s GAD Code.

8.15.   We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive through the OIC-PMO (a) maximize income opportunities from vacant market stalls; (b) ensure stallholders’ compliance with the payment of current and prior period rentals; and (c) consider other strategies to enforce collections from delinquent stallholders.

9.8.    We recommended and Management agreed to continue to (a) observe and comply with the provision of Section 324 (b) of the Local Government Code in the appropriation for debt servicing; and (b) adhere with the provisions of Philippines Public Sector Accounting Standards (PPSAS) I in accounting and reporting of Loans Payable.

10.11.    We recommended and the Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive direct the Accounting Office and the implementing offices to adhere with the provisions on documentary requirements and submit to the Audit Team the required supporting documents in order to establish the regularity of the claims.

11.5.  We commended and recommended that Management particularly the Accounting and Treasury Departments to continue to adhere to revenue regulations on the withholding and timely remittance of taxes to the Bureau of Internal Revenue.

12.10.  We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Accountant to (a) request for the authority from the Sangguniang Panglunsod to revert the said amount to unappropriated surplus after ascertaining the absence of any liability with the GSIS; and (b) the City Accountant and City Treasurer to continually adhere with the timely remittance of GSIS contributions withheld by the City.

13.4.   We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief Executive direct (a) the City Accountant to request for the authority from the Sangguniang Panglunsod to revert the said amount to unappropriated surplus; and (b) the City Accountant and City Treasurer to continually adhere with the timely remittance of PAG-IBIG contributions withheld by the City.
14.3.    We recommended and Management agreed to comply with the National Health Insurance Act by remitting all contributions withheld to the Philippine Health Insurance Corporation.

15.3.   We recommended and Management agreed to immediately settle the remaining balance of disallowance and to henceforth observe pertinent rules and regulations in the discharge of its fiscal functions.

We reiterated the audit recommendations that the Management should:
create an inventory team to conduct physical count of agency’s properties;
instruct the Municipal Treasurer to (i) prepare and maintain the required property cards, pursuant to the provisions of New Government Accounting System and (ii) tag the properties with property number; and
require the Municipal Accountant to adhere to COA Circular 2015-008 with regard to the use of residual value of the road networks.
We recommended that Management continue the payment of loan amortization in accordance with the terms and conditions.

We reiterated the audit recommendations that the (a) Municipal Accountant require all Accountable Officers to immediately liquidate their cash advance by submitting required report and settlement documents to the Accounting Office and/or refund the unexpended balance to the Municipal Treasurer; and (b) Management to avoid 	the granting cash advances to non-regular employees of the auditee and additional cash advances to accountable officers with outstanding accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant immediately submit (a) all cash advance vouchers and their supporting documents together with the transmittal letter to the Auditor and (b) Liquidation Reports and their supporting documents together with the transmittal letter to the ICFAU. We further recommended that management henceforth fully comply with the provisions of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular (JC) No. 2015-01 dated January 8, 2015.

We recommended that:
the Municipal Accountant check the completeness of supporting documents before effecting the payments of obligations in view of the contracts’ deliverables; and
the Local Chief Executive direct the Bids and Awards Committee and its Secretariat Office to submit the lacking documents as mentioned in paragraph 5.9 and to strictly comply with the provisions of RA 9184 and COA Circular 2012-01.

We recommended that the LCE:
direct the Bids and Awards Committee and its Secretariat to submit the lacking documents; and
strictly adhere to the above-cited provisions of RA No. 9184 and its IRR and the DILG Memorandum Circular in the post-completion activities of the project.

We reiterated the audit recommendations that (a) Management comply with the minimum standards prescribed by the LGA and (b) LDRRMO coordinate with the Municipal Accountant in the preparation of monthly report on sources and utilization of DRRMF. We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant revert back the Special Trust Fund transferred from CY 2011 and 2012 to General Fund, pursuant to RA No. 10121.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the GAD Focal Person and concerned offices to (a) cause the update and maintenance of GAD Database which will serve as basis for planning, formulating GAD responsive projects, programs and activities; and (b) observe the timely submission of the GAD Plan and Budget and GAD Accomplishment Report, pursuant to PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01 and COA Circular No. 2014-001.

We reiterated the audit recommendations that the Treasury Office coordinate and reconcile with the Accounting Office for the correct amount of taxes before the monthly remittance is paid. We further recommended that the Accounting Office trace the details of the remaining balance of Due to BIR account.

We reiterated our recommendations that the Accounting Office (a) coordinate with the HR and Treasury Office on the monthly reconciliation of the amount due for remittance of premiums and loan amortizations to the GSIS; and (b) trace the cause in detail the discrepancies on withheld and remitted amounts, including previous years, which consisted the ending balance of Due to GSIS account and make the necessary adjustments, if warranted.

We reiterated that (a) the Municipal Treasurer coordinate with the Municipal Accountant on monthly preparation of remittances to Pag-IBIG and PHIC; and (b) the Municipal Accountant reconcile the discrepancies from the remitted amounts to the recorded withheld contributions and loan amortizations which accumulated to the ending balances of Due to Pag-IBIG and Philhealth accounts and make the necessary adjustments, if warranted.

We reiterated the audit recommendations that the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer should (a) ensure that only disbursement vouchers with complete supporting documents are processed and paid; and (b) strictly complied with the guidelines on the submission of accounts and reports.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
General Services Officer to carry on and accelerate the process by which these
unserviceable properties could be disposed of, in accordance with the
abovementioned rules and regulations, so that the balances of Property, Plant and
Equipment accounts presented in the financial statements would only represent those
assets that are in existence and servicing the needs of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
a. Municipal Assessor and Municipal General Services Officer to exhaust all possible
means to document the legality of ownership of the three recorded lots without
titles through a Transfer Certificate of Title (TCT);
b. Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Assessor include in the appropriations
the titling of land owned by the Municipal Government; and
c. Municipal Accountant to monitor the status of the above instructions to the
Municipal Assessor, General Services and Budget Officers in order to assure
reconciliation of the records in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Local Development Council
and the Municipal Engineer to communicate with National Government agencies such
as DPWH and ensure the proper scheduling of infrastructure projects planned for 20%
Development Fund for the timely completion of the projects to ensure that the full
intention of development would benefit all the constituents, and carefully choose each
project to be included in the planned program.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Local Development Council
and the Municipal Engineer to conduct proper detailed engineering before project
implementation to ensure timely accomplishment of all planned programs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a) General Services Officer to procure insurance coverage for all insurable
government buildings to guarantee protection from fire, earthquake, storm and/or
other acts of nature; and
b) Municipal Budget Officer to include in the Municipality’s annual budget the amount
of premiums for the general insurance covering all insurable properties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Instigate a standard hiring process for engaging in job order contracts based on
identified requirements/necessity of a particular office or department and the
duties and responsibilities of the position to ensure that all job order contracts
actually augments an existing need for manpower; and\nb. Enjoin the HRMO and Municipal Accountant for the proper and complete
documentation before payment of wages of the job order personnel.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the: (a) Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of all PPEs of the Municipality, prepare and submit the corresponding RPCPPE and henceforth ensure that said physical count are strictly conducted every year; (b) OIC-General Services Officer and the Municipal Accountant to update their respective records and analyze the unreconciled balances between the RPCPPE and accounting records amounting to P424,349,795.48; and (c) OIC-General Services Officer to: (i) update and monitor all issued PARs so that accountabilities thereof can be easily determined; and        (ii) prepare the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP), together with the required supporting documents to facilitate the disposal of any unserviceable items.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned Municipal employees and officers to cause the immediate settlement of cash advances. Likewise, instruct the Municipal Accountant to: (i) strictly monitor the grant and liquidation of cash advances and ensure that they are liquidated within the prescribed period in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002; (ii) ensure that cash advances are granted only for a duly authorized specific purpose supported with approved list of payees and expenses to be incurred; and (iii) refrain from granting cash advances to Job Order and/or Contracts of Service employees.

In view thereof, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (a) ensure strict compliance with the requirements of COA Circular Nos. 75-6, 75-6a and Administrative Order No. 239 series of 2008 on the proper use of government vehicle particularly the proper use of Driver’s Trip Tickets and the preparation of required monthly reports pursuant to the requirements of the Manual on Audit for Fuel Consumption of Government Motor Vehicle to avoid wasteful, excessive and/or unnecessary expenditures relating thereto; (b) initiate the conduct of a thorough investigation regarding the vehicular accident and determine persons accountable therefore which shall be held personally liable for the acquisition cost of the service vehicle amounting to P1,350,000.00; and (c) assure that all government vehicles are properly insured with the GSIS so that the Municipality is protected in case of vehicular accidents and third party claims.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
Instruct the OIC-GSO to: (i) put markings For Official Use Only on all vehicles owned by the Municipality and ensure that all bears plate prescribed for all government vehicle in accordance with COA Circular No. 75-6 dated November 7, 1975; (ii) ensure that properties are covered with duly accomplished Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) before issuance to end-users to easily pinpoint accountabilities therein;
Require all government officials who are assigned with service vehicles, including their designated drivers, to park all vehicles within the premises of the Municipal Compound and/or Motor Pool during weekends, holidays and after regular office hours, except those that are covered with approved travel orders falling within the specified prohibited days and time; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to submit Monthly Reports of Official Travels and Monthly Reports of Fuel Consumption after the end of each month for proper control and monitoring on the usage of service vehicles and the related expenditures thereto such as, fuels and lubricants.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor that Representation and Transportation allowances must be given after actual services have been rendered pursuant to Section 1 of LBC No. 103. Likewise, payments thereof must be supported with complete documentation to prove the validity and regularity of the claims especially the reimbursable RATA.
Moreover, the Municipal Mayor was advised to require all incumbent and OICs of regular positions who are assigned with or uses government motor transportation to refund all claimed transportation allowances and henceforth, refrain from granting full transportation allowances to said government officials. Accordingly, fuel expenses incurred and paid by the Municipality either through reimbursements or direct payments involving Municipal Officials claiming transportation allowance must also be refunded.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: (a) MDC to specifically identify the priority development projects that will contribute to the realization of capital development of the Municipality and see to it that the Annual Investment Plan is prepared with the required information such as the specific location and appropriation for each projects; and (b) Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to reconcile their respective records. Discrepancies that may arise, if any, should be investigated accordingly for accurate and reliable reporting.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor refrain from placing available funds in time deposit accounts instead ensure that priority programs, projects and activities, beneficial to the public, are fully implemented so that the needed services of the constituents may not be delayed or sacrificed in favor of interest earned in time deposits.

We recommended that the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer ensure the optimal utilization of the 70% mitigation fund of LDRRMF to strengthen the resilience of the Municipality and its constituents in times of disasters and calamities as required under RA No. 10121. Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant transfer the unexpended/unutilized amount of the Quick Response Fund (QRF) and the Disaster Risk Reduction and Management – Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (DRRM-MOOE) as Trust Liability-DRRM account in the trust fund books solely for the purpose of supporting disaster risk reduction management activities of the LDRRM Council within the next five (5) years.

We recommended for the settlement of audit suspension within 90 days from receipt of the Notice of Suspension, pursuant to Section 9.4 of the Rules and Regulations on Settlement of Accounts.  The head of the agency is responsible for ensuring that the requirements of transactions suspended in audit are complied with.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor exercise
his primordial responsibility to require the Municipal Accountant and the
Municipal Treasurer in coordination with other concerned municipal employees
such as the former Municipal Accountant – Designee who is still in the service to
continue exerting efforts in digging up records in order to render once and for
all the actual and true status of the financial accounts of the Municipality.
Moreover, the responsible officials should be held liable under the Revised Penal
Code for their failure to render the accounts and reporting thereof.

Based on the foregoing observations, we reiterated our recommendations
that:
a. The Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to exert extra
effort to account for the unreconciled items appearing in the Bank
Reconciliation Statements to faithfully present the correct balances of
the accounts for the period; and
b. The Municipal Accountant prepare the Monthly BRS for all bank
accounts and submit same regularly together with the paid checks,
debit/credit memos for verification as provided for under Section 74
of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 96-011. Continue securing
bank statements of prior years to establish the correct balance of the
Cash in Bank and update and submit the subsidiary ledgers of all the
bank accounts.
