Based on the foregoing audit observations, we recommended that:
1. The Municipal Mayor:
a. require all accountable officers and employees to immediately
liquidate their outstanding cash advances giving more emphasis to
the long outstanding cash advances;
b. resort to the withholding of salaries of those accountable officers
and employees who still fail or persistently refuse to
settle/liquidate their cash advances;
c. exert extra efforts to recover outstanding cash advances from
Accountable Officers who are no longer in the service nor
connected with the municipality;
d. require strictly an Office Clearance from Money and Property
accountabilities, pursuant to Sections 200 and 256, Vol. I of the
Government Accounting and Auditing Manual from all personnel
who seek retirement, resignation, transfer or claim for death
benefits in order that all accountabilities could be appropriately
settled; and
e. secure written approval from the Governor for any official travel
outside the province in accordance with Section 96 of R.A. 7160.
2. The Municipal Accountant
a. maintain Subsidiary Ledgers and schedules for the cash advances
accounts to facilitate monitoring of the amount outstanding per
employee, to ensure that no additional cash advance will be
granted to those with outstanding balances and to support the
amounts reflected in the financial statements. Submit copies of the
same to the Audit Team;
b. refrain from granting additional cash advances to any official or
employee unless previous cash advance given to him is first settled
or a proper accounting thereof is made, pursuant to Section 4.1.2
of COA Circular No. 97-002;
c. ensure that travel orders are attached to the liquidation reports
for cash advances of travel of the employees;
d. ensure that Certificate of Expenses not Requiring Receipts
pursuant to COA Circular No. 2017-001 and certification as to the
absolute necessity of the expenses in excess of the prescribed rate
per day as prescribed in Section 4 of E.O No. 298 are attached to
the liquidation reports, if necessary; and
e. ensure that additional claims for travel are supported with
Itinerary of Travel pursuant to Item 1.2.4.1 of COA Circular
2012-001 and Section 47, Volume I of the Manual on the New
Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for LGUs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to provide depreciation on Infrastructure Assets so that the annual
decrease in value could be deducted from the original cost and PPE could be
presented correctly in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. instruct the BAC to a) furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the
Contract of Agreement and its attachments within five (5) working days
from the execution of the contract as required under Section 3.1.1 of
COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 23, 2009; b) observe
strictly the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 to
ensure adherence to the principles of transparency, competitiveness and
public monitoring; c) strengthen internal control over the evaluation of
the bids and post-qualification of bidders by requiring the BAC and the
TWG to ensure timeliness, completeness and veracity of the post-
qualified documents as reported in the Bid and Post- Qualification
Evaluation Reports; d) submit the lacking documents cited above to the
Audit Team to comply with the requirements of the 2016 Revised IRR
of R.A. No. 9184 and address the deficiencies enumerated; and e)
prepare various reports/documents with extra care to avoid errors
which could mislead the readers/users of the reports; and
b. instruct the Municipal Engineer to observe DPWH D.O. 197 dated
October 7, 2016 which provides the revised guidelines in the
preparation of the Approved Budget for the Contract and Annex A of
the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 relative to the detailed
engineering for the procurement of infrastructure projects. Likewise,
prepare PPMP for the 20% development projects for incorporation in
the APP.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC to:
a. furnish the Audit Team with a copy of the Contract of Agreement and
its attachments within five (5) working days from the execution of the
contracts as required under Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001
dated February 23, 2009;
b. observe strictly the provisions of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No.
9184 to ensure adherence to the principles of transparency,
competitiveness and public monitoring;
c. strengthen internal control over eligibility checking, evaluation of the
bids and post-qualification of bidders by requiring the BAC and the
TWG to ensure timeliness, completeness and veracity of the post-
qualified documents;
d. submit the lacking documents mentioned above to the Audit Team to
comply with the requirements of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No.
9184 and address the deficiencies noted; and
e. prepare various reports/documents with extra care to avoid errors
which could mislead the readers/users of the reports.

In view of the foregoing audit observations, we recommended that the
Municipal Mayor direct the:
a. Municipal Disaster Risk and Reduction Officer (MDRRMO) to
prepare the LDRRMFIP in accordance with the format prescribed in
Annex A of COA Circular No. 2012-002. It should also include all the
list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of
previous years;
b. Bids and Awards Committee to strictly adhere to the requirements of
COA Circular No. 2009-001 and the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No.
9184 for the submission of contracts/purchase order and for
procurement of various goods and services of the municipality,
respectively; and
c. Municipal Accountant to strictly adhere with the guidelines set forth
by COA Circular No. 2012-002 for proper accounting of the utilization
and transfer of LDRRM Fund as well as proper recording of various
transactions in the Special Trust Fund and Section 4(6) of PD No.
1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 for proper documentation of
transactions. She should also be guided by COA Circular No. 2015-009
in the proper recording of transaction in accordance with Philippine
Public Sector Accounting Standards (PPSAS).

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer ensure timely deposit of
collections and issue OR for the transfer of unexpended balance of LDRRMF to
Trust Liabilities – LDRRMF Account and transfer of fund to 20% DF bank
account.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor:
a. as the Local Chief Executive, should have the political will to strictly
implement its solid waste management plan;
b. through the Liga ng mga Barangay, encourage active participation of
barangay officials to support the solid waste management program of
the government by educating their respective constituents on the proper
segregation of reusable or recyclable materials from compostable
wastes;
c. through SWMB, comply with the requirements of the law on waste
segregation by placing trash bins with proper labels;
d. recommend to the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) policies and possible
measures that will ensure full compliance by the barangays on the
provisions of R.A. No. 9003; and
e. through SWMB to fast track the processing of various requirements for
the operation of a sanitary landfill in accordance with Sections 40 and
41 of R.A. No. 9003 the soonest possible time to avert adverse
environmental consequences.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit all her collections intact and daily or not later than the next banking day to avoid the risk of possible loss of government money. We likewise recommended that Management institute appropriate legal charges against the erring accountable officers who failed to deposit collections particularly those who are retiring and/or retired from service and those officials who allowed them to retire.

We  recommended that Management: (1) create an Inventory Committee that will conduct on a full-time basis the annual inventory of all property, plant and equipment of the Municipality to prove their existence, determine the condition and  the valuation, and prepare  the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE); (2) direct the Municipal Accountant to update the PPE Ledger Cards, and the acting GSO to also update the Property Cards and the prompt renewal of all  Acknowledgment Receipt for Equipment (ARE)  pursuant to existing regulations; (3) the Accounting and the GSO of the Municipality to reconcile their records; (4) facilitate the assignment of property number stickers on all LGU property for proper monitoring and reporting of PPE accounts; and (5) direct concerned LGU’s personnel that all pertinent documents relative to acquisition of properties should be submitted to GSO for proper recording.
We recommended that Management ensure that all projects identified under the 20% Economic Development Fund are fully implemented within the budget year.
We recommended that Management fully implement all projects whose funds were granted by the national agencies to effectively and timely extend to the constituents the benefits of the projects.

We recommended that Management:
conduct regular monitoring of on-goingprivate construction to ensure that appraisal and assessment on  buildingsare issued upon completion of construction;
conduct regular inspection of real properties to validate actual or predominant use of property to provide assurance that collections are based on correct, accurate and credible assessments; and
issue Notices of Tax Delinquency to those real properties with no payment from 2017 and earlier years and post the notices in accordance with Section 254 of 1991 LGC, to optimize collection of RPT.

We recommended that the Sanggunian Bayan enact an ordinance to update the Local Revenue Code and every five years thereafter in accordance with RA 7160, as basis for the collection of taxes, fees and charges.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepares the Report of Disbursement to liquidate her cash advance for each nature of cash advance and should be prepared in three (3) copies.
We also recommended that the Human Resource Officer submit to Treasurer’s Office and Accounting Office complete copies of DTRs, application for leave, travel documents and Minutes of Sessions attended/conducted and Resolutions prepared/passed for elective officials for each pay period in support of the payrolls for salaries and wages.

To address the issue, we recommended that management under the initiative of the Local Chief Executive should complete the conduct of physical inventory and prepare inventory reports of all their properties. The GSO/Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant should exert extra efforts in reconciling available records to come up with a reliable amount so that necessary adjustments in the books could be effected especially those PPE which are no longer serviceable.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to show the correct balances of the Property, Plant and Equipment and the Due to NGAs and Trust Liabilities account.
On partial payments in the implementation of infrastructure projects, the journal entry should be debit to Construction in Progress and credit to Cash in Bank only. No corollary entry should be made to adjust the balance of the liability account before completion of the project.
The recognition of infrastructure asset and its transfer to the General Fund should only be made once the project is completed.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should trace the nature and to document these obligations, and furnish the Office of the Auditor the schedule of these obligated vouchers as of December 31, 2018 to validate its existence.
Further, we recommended strict compliance to the pertinent provisions of Presidential Decree 1445. Future reports evidencing failure of compliance hereon may be construed as resorting to blind certification of accounts payable, an act in violation of the existing regulation.

In order to facilitate delivery of public services and to attain the inclusive growth and poverty reduction and other special purpose of the funds as envisioned by these Programs, we recommended that management should:
Expedite the implementation of the projects in accordance with Section 5 of the  DBM-DILG-DSWD-NAPC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 7 dated November 3, 2015; and
For projects with insufficient funds, submit the revise detail, design, specification and location of the project for approval in accordance with Section 5. 8 thereof

We recommended that the MSWDO fast track the submission of the complete reportorial requirements required under the Program discussed above for the subsequent release of funds in order to fully implement the Program in accordance to the guidelines of SFP to see the impact/effectiveness in addressing the under nutrition problem among children in the Child Development Centers (CDC) and Supervised Neighborhood Play (SNP).
The audit team requires the submission of the pertinent documents of the winning bidder and other documents relative to the liquidation of the Supplemental Feeding Program.

To address the issues and concerns, we recommended that:
The Local Chief Executive (LCE) as the chairman and all other members of the Local School Board (LSB) to prepare the Annual SEF Budget following the procedure set forth in the Joint Circular and furnish the Local Accountant, Local Treasurer and Local Budget a copy of the budget using the prescribed format;
The Local Chief Executive (LCE) observe the policies and guidelines contained in the joint circular to ensure a more strategic and efficient utilization of resources for priority programs and projects which would complement the budgeting priorities at the different levels of decision-making for the welfare of its constituents; and
The Local Chief Executive (LCE) to ensure that all public schools within its jurisdiction should equally avail of the Special Education Fund (SEF) for the benefits of all public students thereof.

We recommended that the Municipality utilize the 20% Development Fund in accordance with the implementing rules and regulations set forth in DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1.
Moreover, stop using the fund for PPAs not allowed in the Circular.

To institutionalize the DRRM Office for the attainment of the objectives of the fund, we recommended that:
The Municipal Accountant use the prescribed format provided in Annex B of COA Circular No. 2012-002 for the preparation of the Report on Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF and submit the same by the LDRRM Officer through the LDRRMC and Local Development Council (LDC) to the auditor on or before the 15th day after the end of each month.
The LCE through the LDRRM Office to maximize the use of the funds for the implementation of priority programs, projects and activities for prevention and mitigation and promote disaster preparedness.
The Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entries to book up the remaining unexpended balance of the LDRRM Fund to a special trust fund.

We recommended that management provide a budget for GAD equivalent to at least 5% of the Municipality’s total annual appropriation as required in DBM-NEDA-NCRFW Joint Circular No. 2004-1 dated April 5, 2004 and Section 30 of the General Provisions of General Appropriations Act of CY 2018 and fully utilize the funds which intend to contribute to poverty alleviation, economic empowerment especially of marginalized women, protection, promotion, and fulfillment of women’s human rights, and practice of gender-responsive governance are considered.
Likewise, the Municipality should come up with an accomplishment report showing among others, the amounts utilized in the implementation of the projects that were lined up in its formulated GAD Plan.

We recommended that management make earnest efforts to have the Solid Waste Management Plan for 2017-2026 approved by the National Solid Waste Management Commission to provide an efficient and effective system of solid waste collection and disposal within its area of jurisdiction. Moreover, make use of the allotted fund to achieve its vision in ensuring the protection of public health and environment.

1.7  	We recommended that Management:
a)	Require the accounting personnel to exert extra efforts in following up the demand letters sent to concerned NGOs/POs to settle their accountabilities and determine if the said funds were already spent by the said NGOs/PO and have it documented as basis for recording in the books and/or requesting for write-off;
b)	Direct the assigned personnel of every program to be diligent in safe keeping and tracking of documents/records not only with regard to this case but each transaction of the municipality; and in case of transfer, resignation or reassignment, to turn-over the documents/record to the incoming/relieving officer;  and henceforth,
c)	 Adhere strictly with the provisions stipulated in Sections 5.2 to Section 5.4 of COA Circular No. 2007-001.
2.7 	We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds Accounts to present fairly the financial position of the municipality.

We recommended that the accounting office should update the Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger Cards. Likewise, the municipal treasurer’s office should maintain property cards. Accordingly, the two offices should make a periodic reconciliation of these records. Moreover, the inventory committee shall conduct a reliable physical inventory count at least once a year to determine which of their properties are actually existing. A team composed of the General Services and Accounting Department personnel should conduct the physical inventory count and reconcile the book and property records. Accordingly, the Accounting Department shall effect the correcting entries, where applicable, when properly supported with documentation. Finally, Management should drop the unserviceable properties from the inventory reports and dispose the same in accordance with the procedures outlined above.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee submitted the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) for CY 2018 on January 31, 2019.
observations.reason: The report submitted was not completely filled up as to the balance per card and on hand per count, thus, the existence and condition of the movable PPE at year-end still could not be fully relied upon. Moreover, PPE account balances still included unserviceable PPEs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management refrain from using the 20% EDF for the payment of wages to casual and job order (JO) personnel assigned in the different offices of the Municipality in accordance with DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017. We, however, recommended that these expenditures may be charged to the General Fund Proper pursuant to existing rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction: Two appropriation ordinances for the payment of wages to job order personnel amounting to ₱1,163,021.66 from new revenue sources-environmental fees were enacted by the Sangguniang Bayan (SB).
observations.reason: Wages of some JOs amounting to ₱1,040,754.90 were still charged to the 20% DF in CY 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipality utilize and implement its PPAs under the LDRRMF to strengthen the capacity of the Municipality to avoid or mitigate the adverse effects of disaster or calamity.
observations.managementAction: Management had utilized 73.53% of the 70% allocation for LDRRMF in CY 2018.
observations.reason: Mitigation Fund of CY 2017 and prior years transferred to the Special Fund were still not utilized.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to comply with the documentary requirements under COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 before granting honoraria to its officials and employees. We further recommended that delivery plans, specific duties and expected deliverables with timeframes shall be prepared and used as basis for computation of honoraria.
observations.managementAction: The required documents to support the honoraria granted to the MIAC were submitted to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.reason: The delivery plan submitted did not contain the specific duties and responsibilities of each personnel. The expected deliverables per personnel at a specified timeframe which shall be the basis in the computation of honoraria was also not indicated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant analyze and review the composition of the balance of the Due to Philhealth account. We further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer immediately remit to Philhealth the amount withheld, or for the Municipal Accountant to revert the long outstanding balance, if warranted.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant reviewed and validated the composition of the account and made the necessary adjustments in the books.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management create a GFPS and a GFPS-TWG and prepare its GAD Plan and Budget in accordance with Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of the PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA so that gender issues and concerns of the Municipality may be substantially addressed.
observations.managementAction: The GFPS was established on March 24, 2017 through Executive Order No. 8, series of 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Council for the Protection of Children (MCPC) should focus and prioritize on the formulation and implementation of the PPAs of the LCPC in accordance with Section 15 of Republic Act No. 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality issued Ordinance No, 17-04 enacting the Children’s Code.
observations.reason: The utilization of the fund was only 34% of the amount appropriated.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management evaluate and monitor the status of implementation of the Kilos Asenso Program, and if the agency program is already completed, return the unused balance to the national government.
observations.managementAction: The unexpended balance of the Kilos Asenso Fund was already returned to the source on July 17, 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management substantially implement projects programmed in the current year. For those planned in the past years, the former has to inspect the status of projects regularly and for the Municipal Accountant to review the balances of the Municipality’s continuing appropriations; and to recommend to the local chief executive, if warranted, for reversion, those amounts covering balances of completed projects and those which may no longer be implemented, in order that the funds may be utilized for other economic development projects.
observations.managementAction: Some projects were already implemented.
observations.reason: The projects were not implemented because the Municipal Engineer was not able to cope up with the implementation schedule due to the volume of work and the complexity of its requirements consuming much of his time, not to mention the various seminars and trainings, meetings and conferences he needed to attend. Also, some of the projects had site ownership issues while others needed additional budget.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the concerned officials and employees concerned immediately refund in full the productivity enhancement incentive received in CY 2010. Henceforth, the local chief executive should ensure that the grant of similar benefits complies with prescribed guidelines specifically insofar as observance of the PS limitation is concerned so that the disbursements will not be disallowed in audit.
observations.managementAction: The concerned officials and employees already started refunding the PEI received in CY 2010.
observations.reason: Refunds were made through salary deduction.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: 1. Management should appropriate at least five percent (5%) of the total budget appropriations for Gender and Development Programs pursuant to Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2004-01. 2. Require the Social Welfare Officer in coordination with the budget officer to prepare the GAD Plan and Budget and Accomplishment Report in accordance with the form prescribed in DBM, NEDA and NCRFW Joint Circular No. 2004-001 dated April 5, 2004.
observations.managementAction: Management gender perspective were mainstreamed in the different PPAs of the agency.

observations.recommendation: Required the department heads concerned to process the titles of the land claimed by the Municipality and require also the Municipal Assessor to record the said Title land in the books of accounts as soon as the titles shall have been issued.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Assessor had started the processing of the titles. The Municipal Assessor started the processing of the titles of the land claimed by the Municipality.
observations.reason: The Municipal Assessor had difficulty retrieving the documents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:

(a) instruct the 

(i) City Treasurer and City Assessor to continuously coordinate for the updating of the list of taxpayers; and 


(ii) City Accountant to make appropriate adjustments in the receivable balances of each taxpayer, as warranted; and

(b) address the inadequacy of personnel at the City Treasurer’s Office but taking into consideration the Personal Services limitations in the Local Government Code
observations.managementAction: Full coordination of the offices of Treasurer, Accounting and Assessor is strictly being observed

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that: 

Management instruct the (a) GSO to 

(i) fast track the recording of individual PPEs in the property cards; and 
 
(ii) based on PCs, extract a list of all PPEs that will be used in the physical validation of these assets; and 

(b) Inventory and Disposal Committee to facilitate the 

(i) conduct of physical count in CY 2018 in accordance with prescribed rules and timelines; and 

(ii) disposal of unserviceable items based on the guidelines set. 

Likewise, we advised, to which Management concurred, to ensure that all the functionalities of the proposed PPE inventory system meet their actual requirements.

We recommended that:

 Management direct the 

(a) City Engineer, City Budget Officer and the City Treasurer to resolve the identified causes of the missed timelines in project implementation and to act on these accordingly; and 

(b) City Planning and Development Officer or the concerned officer to henceforth ensure that the documentary requirements of future and similarly-funded projects are timely provided.

We recommended that:

Management instruct the 

(a) LDRRM Officer to monitor, evaluate and enhance performance on DRRM, as warranted, based on the Disaster Preparedness Minimum Standards on Disaster Risk Reduction and Management; 

(b) City Accountant to 
(i) cause the refund to LDRRMF from appropriate trust fund the amount paid for BAC honoraria; and 

(ii) refrain from using DRRMF for programs/projects/activities which are not directly related to disaster preparedness, mitigation, response and rescue operations; and 

(c) BAC to explore other legal procurement options such as “Ordering Agreement” in the procurement of relief goods and food packs.

We commended the effort exerted by the Management in the implementation of the SWM Plan to ensure protection of the environment and the general welfare of the City’s constituents.  Also, we recommended and Management agreed, thru the CENRO, to continue ensuring clean and healthy environment through waste reduction, recycling, diversion, proper disposal, segregation, collection, transport, storage, and treatment of solid waste as well as the formulation and adoption of the best environment practice in ecological waste Management as required under R.A. No. 9003.

We recommended the:

Management that the Members of the City Local School Board  

(a) ensure that future plans and budgets for SEF are consistent with DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 01, s. 2017 dated January 19, 2017 and other pertinent rules that may be issued henceforth; 

(b) secure certification from the DepEd Budget Officer that the charges to SEF were already net of the approved DepEd Budget;  and 

(c) refrain from accommodating expenditures for activities for which the DepEd has the direct mandate to execute. We also advised that the invalid charges to SEF be restored to the fund from the General Fund after the requisite legislation is secured.

We recommended and Management agreed to:

(a) reconstitute the LCPC after ensuring that the required membership qualification are met; 

(b) instruct the LCPC to 

(i) prepare a comprehensive plan on delinquency prevention, and to oversee its proper implementation; 

(ii) continuously ensure that at least 1% of the City’s annual IRA is attributed to child protection related activities and in the development of a child-friendly environment; and 

(iii) prepare accomplishment 
reports and other necessary documents required under the MC Nos. 2008-126 and 2012-120.

We recommended and Management agreed to look into the reason for, and act accordingly to address, the continued failure of the LGU in the timely submission of the reports, records, accounts, contracts, POs, DRs, and other documents required by regulations.
We commended the Accounting and Treasury Departments for their continuous adherence to revenue regulations on the withholding and prompt remittance of taxes to the BIR.

We recommended and Management agreed that the City Accountant and City Treasurer continue to be compliant in the deduction of GSIS premium contributions and the remittance thereof including the government share within the prescribed period.

We commended the City Accounting Office and City Treasurer's Office in their continuous compliance with existing laws, rules and regulations on proper deductions and remittances of Pag-IBIG fund contributions and loan amortizations in accordance with the provisions of RA No. 9679. We also recommend the Accounting Office to verify the negative balance signifying over-remittance and make the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Treasury and Accounting Offices to submit to the Audit Team the disbursement vouchers for the twenty paid checks together with all the relevant supporting documents to avoid disallowance or suspension in audit.
observations.reason: For CY 2017, all vouchers related to checks were submitted. Meanwhile, for CY 2016, the check no. 1147895 amounting to ₱3,206.00 was found to be cancelled and vouchers totaling ₱475,366.84 was already submitted, leaving a balance of ₱23,471.43

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated the audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive:

a.	instruct the Treasury Office to prepare and furnish the Accounting Office the duly certified list required under Section 20 Volume I of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs; and

b.	instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) base the recognition of RPT and SET receivables on the list submitted by the Treasury Office; and (ii) make the necessary adjustments in the misstatements of the RPT/SET Receivables.

observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management: (i) stop the practice of paying fixed allowance for communication without the submission of proof on the incurrence of expense showing or justifying the actual usage or consumption by the concerned officials and employees and (ii) consider the availment of contracts subscriptions with telephone companies offering discounts or service promotions.
observations.reason: The officials/ employees availed subscriptions to different telecom companies but did not consider subscriptions to companies offering discounts or service promotion.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and other concerned Officers (i) submit clarification on the designation of an employee with a first level item as OIC-HRMO and justification for the grant of RATA to said OIC-HRMO; and (ii) for future and similar personnel movement, observe the guidelines set forth by the CSC in the designation of personnel as OICs.

We recommended that the Management:

a.)	draft individual contract of services showing the rates, functions and expected outputs to its JOs and Casual employees;

b.)	ensure that each JO and Casual employee has DTRs and ARs duly approved and certified by their supervisors every payroll period; and

c.)	ensure the completeness of details in the DTRs of the said employees.
observations.reason: The management agreed to implement the audit recom-mendations on drafting of individual contract of services and ensuring that the Accomplishment Reports and DTRs of JOs were approved by their supervisors every payroll period.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality submit explanations/information/ documents for the issues stated above.
observations.reason: The Management submitted explanations for the issues regarding the transaction. As for the submitted tax clearance, the management answered: “As per submitted Tax Clearance by Benguet Builders as one of the required document for the bidding conducted on March 6, 2016, the BAC and TWG checked the submitted Tax clearance and notice that it was issued on November 20, 2015 valid until May 20, 2016 as stated in the annotation below the Tax Clearance submitted. So we assumed that the Tax Clearance was authentic. We admit that we are not familiar about the reference number of what it means. About the date of payment which is May 7, 2015, we assumed that the contractor paid early but differed or late issuance of the Clearance by the BIR. Furthermore, based on the submitted Om-nibus Sworn Statement by the contractor under 3 states that “each of the documents submitted in its satisfaction of the bidding requirements is an authentic copy of the original, complete, and all statements and information provided there in are true and correct”. Also, we take into consideration that the contractor submitted his 2015 Income Tax Return together with the Audited Financial Statement, we assumed that all the Liabilities of the said contractor with regards to tax was already settled.”
The submission of incomplete sup-porting documents was reiterated and included in Part II of this report.

The audit recom-mendation on property tagging was reiterated and included in Part II of this report on PPE accounts. Other recom-mendations re-garding the submission of monthly report on utilization and the compliance with the minimum standards of equipment were reiterated and included in Part II of this report.
The BAC and its Secretariat are still in the process of complying with the audit recom-mendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the BAC and its Secretariat, the Accounting Office and the Engineering Office to submit the lacking documents in order to avoid the Auditor’s issuance of Notice of Suspension.
observations.reason: The BAC and its Secretariat are still in the process of complying with the audit recom-mendation.
The Municipality is still in the process of complying with the audit recom-mendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct lead offices for the programs under trust to secure and maintain complete copies of relevant documents for monitoring and evaluation purposes. We recommended further that the MOA with the DOT and the corresponding approved project/program, if any, be submitted immediately to facilitate the proper evaluation of the project/program’s implementation.
observations.reason: The Municipality is still in the process of complying with the audit recom-mendations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:

a.	exert efforts to secure a copy of the MOA relative to the Community Based Livelihood Program funded by DSWD-RO III and submit the same to the Audit Team to further evaluate the implementation of the said program; 

b.	submit the corresponding detailed project proposal for the livelihood project; and

c.	explain/justify the discrepancies in the items procured and the items distributed and provide specific specifications of the items procured for audit purposes.
observations.reason: The Municipality is still in the process of complying with the audit recom-mendations.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated the audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive instruct concerned offices to establish a mechanism necessary to operate and manage the harvester including the scheme on income generation that could both benefit the farmers and the Municipality. We further recommended that the concerned officials conduct monitoring to ensure that the programs’ activities are implemented in accordance with the agreement or MOA.
observations.reason: The Municipality is still in the process of complying with the audit recom-mendations.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated the previous audit recommendation that the:

a.	GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) orient the Barangay VAW Desk officers on the preparation and submission of quarterly reports to the DILG and the MSWDO for the latter to take appropriate action based on the reports.

b.	GFPS coordinate with other competent agencies on the conduct of formal training of the Barangay VAW Desk Officers.

c.	Local Chief Executive review the scholarship program of the Municipality and establish more detailed procedures to ensure the achievement of its objective which is to help poor but intellectually deserving students and addressing the problem on the out-of-school youth.

d.	Local Chief Executive and the Sangguniang Bayan formulate the Municipality’s own implementing guidelines, policies and procedures regarding AICS, in conformity with the provisions set forth by the DSWD MC No. 02 S. 2014, as basis in the granting of financial or in-kind assistances to its constituents.

We further recommended that (1) the LCE instruct the GFPS to properly monitor the GAD PPAs and coordinate with respective offices on implementing and reporting GAD plan and accomplishments; and (2) the BAC and its Secretariat and other concerned offices to submit explanation/justi-fication/documents for the issues stated in paragraph 20.7.
observations.reason: The Management is still in the process of complying with the audit recom-mendations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipality continue to allocate the mandatory appropriation or funding for LCPC. We also recommended that the Municipality prepare its LDPC and LIPC in consideration of other relevant and comprehensive programs for children. 

We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive (1) instruct the LCPC to consider in the formulation of plans and programs those which are culturally relevant and responsive and (2) direct the concerned officers to submit explanation/ documents/ information for the post audit findings indicated under paragraph 22.8.
observations.reason: The concerned officers are still in the process of complying with the audit recom-mendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management to settle the above audit suspension through compliance with the requirements indicated on paragraph 26.2.
observations.reason: The concerned officials and employees are still in the process of complying with the requirements of the audit recom-mendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the persons responsible and liable to fully settle their respective accountabilities.

We recommended that the Accountant (i) strengthen his review controls over disbursements and ensure the completeness and propriety of the supporting documents before certifying the DVs; and (ii) deliver immediately the accounts and reports that are due for submission as of date. We further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer defer the approval or processing of the check if the DV does not bear signatures of the key officials.
observations.reason: Reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive to:

a.	instruct the Municipal Engineering Office to conduct comprehensive evaluation and implement necessary corrective measures so that the Municipality could benefit from capital investment put to the project; and

b.	ensure that all projects are properly planned taking into consideration the competence and technical knowledge of the involved officers/employees.
observations.reason: The Municipality is in the process of complying with the audit recom-mendations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal concerned to strictly observe the austerity measures being implemented by the government in terms of trainings and seminars and exercise due diligence on the use of the limited government funds entrusted to them. Further, avoid expenditures that may qualify to otherwise be disallowed in audit. We further recommended that the Municipal officials and employees to attach Itinerary of Travel together with the Travel Order and other required documents in support to cash advances for travel, and the Certificate of Travel Completed together with the tickets, e-tickets, boarding pass on the corresponding liquidation vouchers in accord with COA Circular Nos. 96-004 and 2005-003.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant already corrected and modified the procedure in granting of cash advance and liquidation for the travel, and implemented a strict policy on trainings and travelling expenses.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Local Development Council and the Office of the Municipal Engineer to: a. ensure that the mandatory 20% of Internal Revenue Allotment be optimally utilized to implement identified priority programs and activities to help achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes and pursuant to the DILG-DBM Joint Circular No. 2011-1; b. properly plan and coordinate with DPWH and other concerned officials which have projects coincide with the programmed projects of the Municipality; and c. come up with a well-defined planning guidelines in identifying priority programs and projects in order to project feasible and appropriate results that can be delivered efficiently and effectively to the public on time.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality, through the Municipal Engineer, coordinated with the DPWH and other agencies and undertook measures to avoid recurrence by constantly communicating with other agencies with regards to their proposed projects and committing not to implement any project in the barangay unless all documents are in place and other issues are resolved.
observations.reason: During the planning and implementation stage, projects were delayed or not started due to lack of coordination with national agencies, overlapping projects (DPWH), requests from barangay officials and affected stakeholders, pending right-of-way/landowner permissions, and other issues which prevented timely implementation; the LGU has since committed to improve coordination and documentation.

observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Accounting Office has already prepared PPE Ledger Card for each category of PPE with complete details upon acquisition thereof starting in CY 2016 under Special Education Fund and in CY 2018 for other three Funds. The 25 parcels of land was already recorded in the financial statements thru Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) no. 5227, dated July 19, 2018. 

Meanwhile, the OIC-GSO had maintained records of newly acquired PPE in their office, however, it is not the Property Card required under Section 119 of the Manual on New Government Accounting System.
observations.reason:
Lack of man power to conduct complete physical count of all PPE including the Local Road Networks of the Municipality. 

Continued disregard of the OIC-GSO to assign property number for each PPE acquired by the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the Management the following courses of action:

The Inventory Committee conduct complete physical count of all inventories and submit the complete report thereon to the Auditor, copy furnish the Municipal Accountant to enable reconciliation with their records/ledgers;

The Municipal Accountant    (i) record the receipt of supplies in the Supplies Ledger Cards including the complete details of the Inventories; (ii) record the receipts of medicines from the DOH; (iii) reclassify the Float Buoy Markers from Inventory account to proper PPE account, subject to depreciation, for the fair presentation of affected accounts in the financial statements; and 

The OIC-General Service Office (i) assign stock number for each type of supplies upon receipt thereof and maintain stock cards per stock number   of Inventories;                      (ii) require/prepare RIS for issuance of inventories to end-users and consolidate weekly all RIS using the SSMI for submission to the Municipal Accountant to enable recognition of expenses totaling P15,237,424.75 in the books of accounts; and        (iii) regularly reconcile the balance of stock cards with the ledger cards of the Municipal Accounting Office to promptly address discrepancies, if any.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office recorded the medicines received from DOH thru JEV No. 020 dated June 29, 2018. The Float Buoy Markers were reclassified to PPE account thru JEV No. 058 dated December 3, 2018 and was transferred to General Fund under JEV No. 066 dated December 28, 2018.
observations.reason: Lack of man power to conduct physical count of inventories.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following courses of actions:

the Municipal Treasurer        (i) submit records to Municipal Accountant regarding the accurate tax liability due from OMECO for proper recording in the books of accounts; and                   (ii) communicate with OMECO the adjustments made on its business taxes for reconciliation of their records with that of the Municipality; and

the Municipal Accountant         (i) compute the amount of unpaid electric bills due to OMECO and prepare the necessary Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the exact amount of obligation due to them; (ii) prepare JEV to adjust the  Other Deferred Credits account balance for its fair presentation in the Statement of Financial Position; and (iii) exert efforts in locating the documents/records pertaining to the outstanding loans receivables totalling P13,048,824.04 under Tulong Pangkabuhayan Loan Program to establish the validity and legality of claims against the debtors, facilitate its collection  and to prevent the possible loss of funds.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant coordinated with the Municipal Treasurer upon receipt of Audit Observation Memorandum (AOM) in CY 2018 for the correct claims and payable to OMECO. In April 2018, JEV No. 11 was issued to record the adjustments to Other Deferred Credits Account.
observations.reason: Documents to support claims against the debtor of the Long-outstanding receivables could no longer be located due to constant turn-over of employees in the Accounting Office for three consecutive years.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the         (a) Municipal Accountant          (i) strictly monitor the grant of  cash advance by preparing certification that previous cash advances have been liquidated and accounted for in the books to prevent the grant of additional cash advances to those employees and officers with outstanding balances; (ii) practice prudence in the grant of cash advances to casual/job order employees whose connection and tenure with the Municipality is uncertain there being no employee-employer relationship;  and      (iii) ensure that the grant and liquidation of cash advances are duly supported with proper and complete supporting documents, ObR included, to establish the validity, propriety and legality of the transactions; and                  (b) Municipal Treasurer pay regular and operating expenses by check and that only payments in small amount should be made through the petty cash fund.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accounting Office issues certification every time an accountable officers settled/liquidated their cash advance. Moreover, three (3) notices to liquidate cash advance were issued and withholding of salaries is already enforced to certain employees and officials.
observations.reason: Lapses in the internal control system in processing, granting and liquidation of cash advances.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management, through the          (a) Municipal Mayor and the OIC-Municipal Agriculturist       (i) exercise prudence and restraint in granting financial assistance to NGOs/POs, NGAs and barangays; and (ii) adhere strictly with the guidelines provided under COA Circular Nos. 2007-001 and  94-013  on the availment, release and utilization of fund transfers to NGOs/POs and implementing agencies, respectively, to ensure the proper implementation of projects and the attainment of its objectives for the benefit of the intended beneficiaries. 


Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant          (i) review the regularity and completeness of supporting documents as required by COA Circular No. 2012-001, as amended by COA Circular No. 2016-002; and (ii) record funds released to NGOs/POs, NGAs and LGUs as receivable accounts subject to liquidation; and        (iii) recognize expenses only after its liquidations to facilitate monitoring and ensure that public funds are used for its intended purpose. 

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer be more cautious in releasing checks by requiring official receipt to acknowledge receipt of payment especially from the barangay treasurers and to require written authorization and certified photo copy of valid Identification Card if the claimant is other than the payee or the authorized representative of the association.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality has established guidelines on the grant of Financial Assistance to different barangays and NGOs/POs. The transfer thereof duly covered with MOA which consist of specific guidelines and was supported with SB Resolution No. 4603.
observations.reason: Transfers to NGOs/POs were not adequately supported with complete documentary requirements.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, to remit all unused balance of funds to the source agencies and the Bureau of Treasury as soon as cash becomes available, pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013 and Supreme Court Ruling dated November 19, 2013.
observations.managementAction: The unutilized balances of funds released by the National Government Agency were already remitted to Bureau of Treasury except for unutilized balance of PDAF.
observations.reason: Lack of available cash

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management, through the Solid Waste Management Board

Hasten the finalization of the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan that will place primary emphasis on the implementation of all feasible re-use, recycling, and composting programs of the Municipality;

Encourage the barangay officials  to establish MRF in their respective barangays which must be strategically located in  a certain parcel of land  where they can receive mixed waste for final sorting, segregation, composting and recycling before the resulting residual wastes are transferred to a long term storage or disposal facility or sanitary land fill; and

Allocate funds for the effective implementation of Ecological Solid Waste Management Plan especially on the construction of Materials Recovery Facility and conversion of its open dumpsite to long term disposal facility or sanitary landfill to ensure the health and safety of the people and the environment.
observations.managementAction: The 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan of the Municipality was already approved by the National Solid Waste Management Commission on December 6, 2018. There were ongoing preparations for the conversion of the open dumpsite into a sanitary landfill. The cluster MRF was being renovated to sufficiently accommodate waste collected from the Barangays its covering.
observations.reason: No funds were appropriated to support other Barangays that could not acquire lot for the construction of their own MRF due to significant high market value within their area.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned officials to submit the financial reports/statements of calendar years 1994 to 2002 to the Audit Team. The Municipal Accountant - Designee and the Municipal Treasurer in coordination with the former Municipal Accountant – Designee must render once and for all the actual and true status of financial accounts of the Municipality, otherwise, the responsible officials should be held liable under the Revised Penal Code for their failure to render the accounts and reporting thereof.
observations.managementAction: Continuous efforts in digging up records are being made by the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer.
observations.reason: No financial reports pertaining years 1994 to 2002 were submitted to the Audit Team due to unavailability of the related documents needed in the preparation of the reports.

observations.recommendation: Based on the foregoing observations, we advised that: a. the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to exert extra effort to account for the unreconciled items appearing in the Bank Reconciliation Statements to account for the period; and b. the Municipal Accountant prepare the Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements for all bank accounts and submit same regularly together with the paid checks, debit/credit memos for verification as provided for under Section 74 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 96-011. Continue securing bank statements of prior years to establish the correct balance of the Cash in Bank and update and submit the subsidiary ledgers of all the bank accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer was advised by the LCE to make a request to DBP Kalibo and LBP Odiongan for previous years’ copy of Bank Reconciliation Statements. In addition, OIC-Municipal Accountant has prepared and submitted Bank Reconciliation Statements for all bank accounts.
observations.reason: The unreconciled items amounting to P13,716,773.84 still exist in the Bank Reconciliation Statements submitted to the Audit Team as of December 31, 2018. Likewise, subsidiary ledgers of all the bank accounts were not submitted. Reiterated in Observation No. 2 of this report.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Mayor: a. require all accountable officers and employees to immediately liquidate their outstanding cash advances giving more emphasis to the long outstanding cash advances; b. resort to the withholding of salaries of those accountable officers and employees who still fail or persistently refuse to settle/liquidate their cash advances; c. exert extra efforts to recover outstanding cash advances from Accountable Officers who are no longer in the service nor connected with the municipality; d. require strictly an Office Clearance from Money and Property accountabilities, pursuant to Sections 200 and 256, Vol. I of the Government Accounting and Auditing Manual from all personnel who seek retirement, resignation, transfer or claim for death benefits in order that all accountabilities could be appropriately settled; and e. secure written approval from the Governor for any official travel outside the province in accordance with Section 96 of R.A. 7160. The Municipal Accountant: a. maintain Subsidiary Ledgers and schedules for the cash advances accounts to facilitate monitoring of the amount outstanding per employee, to ensure that no additional cash advance will be granted to those with outstanding balances and to support the amounts reflected in the financial statements. Submit copies of the same to the Audit Team; b. refrain from granting additional cash advances to any official or employee unless previous cash advance given to him is first settled or a proper accounting thereof is made, pursuant to Section 4.1.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002; c. ensure that travel orders are attached to the liquidation reports for cash advances of travel of the employees; d. ensure that Certificate of Expenses not Requiring Receipts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2017-001 and certification as to the absolute necessity of the expenses in excess of the prescribed rate per day as prescribed in Section 4 of E.O No. 298 are attached to the liquidation reports, if necessary; and e. ensure that additional claims for travel are supported with Itinerary of Travel pursuant to Item 1.2.4.1 of COA Circular 2012-001 and Section 47, Volume I of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for LGUs.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor and the other Department Heads sent demand letters to the Accountable Officers to settle their obligations especially the long outstanding balances. The Municipal Accountant commented that she had already required the concerned officers and employees to settle their obligations especially the long outstanding balances.
observations.reason: Accountable officers still have long outstanding balances. Various lapses in the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances were noted, resulting to accumulation of unliquidated cash advances as of December 31, 2018 in the amount of P20,232,236.29. Details were discussed in Observation No. 3 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Management strictly observe the general limitations cited under Section 325(a) of R.A. No. 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 98 dated October 14, 2011. The municipality should also increase its efforts in raising its revenues/receipts such as increasing tax collection efficiency and generating revenue related projects/activities to increase the base of PS limitation.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor, being the Local Chief Executive, should always be guided by the conditions of the agency’s annual and supplemental budgets to ensure that the budgeting requirements are completely complied with. As part of his fiscal responsibility, he should allow only payments of personal services costs and other expenditures expressly authorized by law. The municipality should also increase its efforts in raising its revenues/receipts such as increasing tax collection efficiency and generating revenue related projects/activities to increase the base of PS limitation.
observations.reason: The Municipality still exceeded its fifty-five percent (55%) Personal Services Limitation/Cap. No increase in actual salaries and allowances in CY 2018. This was noted in AOM No. 2019-006 dated April 8, 2019.

observations.recommendation: In view of the aforementioned observations, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to: a. strengthen internal control over eligibility checking, evaluation of the bids and post-qualification of bidders by requiring the BAC and the TWG to ensure timeliness, completeness and veracity of the post-qualified documents; b. ensure compliance to the procurement schedule, including deadlines and timeframes for different activities prescribed in Annex C of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184; c. ensure mathematical accuracy on the evaluation of bids; d. comply strictly with the requirements of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184; and e. comply strictly with Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 in the submission of copies of contracts to the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: Some of the lacking documents in the municipality’s procurements were already complied in the procurements made in CY 2018. These were discussed in Observations Nos. 5 and 6 of this report.
observations.reason: Various procedural lapses which were discussed in Observations 5 and 6 of this report were still noted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a. the Municipal Mayor instruct the LDRRMO to prepare and submit the LDRRMFIP to the Audit Team in accordance with Item 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002; b. the LDRRMO refrain from incurring and charging expenses not related to disaster risk reduction against the DRRM fund; and c. the Municipal Accountant be guided with the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units prescribed by COA Circular No. 2015-009 in recording the transactions of the municipality.
observations.managementAction: In CY 2018, the LDRRMO has already included in the LDRRMFIP the previous years’ unexpended/unobligated balances of LDRRMF in the Special Trust Fund and the list of projects and activities to be charged therefrom. The LDRRMO also refrained from incurring and charging expenses not related to disaster risk reduction against the DRRM fund. The Municipal Accountant is guided with the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units in recording the transactions of the municipality.
observations.reason: The amount of unexpended/unobligated balances of LDRRMF in the Special Trust Fund included in the LDRRMFIP for CY 2018 was only P3,454,220.59 instead of the total amount of P3,745,742.11 which is the balance of Trust Liabilities – Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund reported in the Financial Statements of CY 2017. Various lapses in documentation, accounting and utilization of the Municipality’s LDRRMF for CY 2018 were noted. Details were discussed in Observation No. 7 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all the RCDs and validated deposit slips be prepared with the Journal Entry Voucher and recorded in the Cash Receipt Journal to avoid unrecorded collections and deposits. Likewise, ensure the accuracy of the amounts of transactions and adjust the balance of the accounts affected by the miscalculated collections noted in CY 2016 audit.
observations.managementAction: In CY 2018, the OIC-Municipal Accountant has already forwarded RCDs and copies of ORs and deposit slips and recorded in the Journal of Entry Voucher as indicated in the audit recommendations of CY 2017.
observations.reason: The Municipal Treasurer failed to deposit intact collections totaling P2,579,622.56 as of December 31, 2018. Details were discussed in Observation No. 8 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. as the Local Chief Executive, should have the political will to strictly implement its solid waste management plan; b. through the Liga ng mga Barangay, encourage active participation of barangay officials to support the solid waste management program of the government by educating their respective constituents on the proper segregation of reusable or recyclable materials from compostable wastes; c. through SWMB, comply with the requirements of the law on waste segregation by placing trash bins with proper labels; d. recommend to the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) policies and possible measures that will ensure full compliance by the barangays on the provisions of R.A. No. 9003; and e. through SWMB to fast track the processing of various requirements for the operation of a sanitary landfill in accordance with Sections 40 and 41 of R.A. No. 9003 the soonest possible time to avert adverse environmental consequences.
observations.managementAction: In CY 2018, the municipality’s Solid Waste Management Plan covering the period CY 2015-2024 has already been updated/revised and submitted for review and approval to the National Solid Waste Management Commission (NSWMC). The municipality already conducted the Waste Analysis and Characterization Study (WACS). The open dumpsite operating before was already closed and the municipality is operating a Sanitary Landfill Level I. However, mandatory waste segregation at source is not yet fully enforced.
observations.reason: Mandatory waste segregation at source was still not fully enforced in the municipality and some prior year audit observations were still not fully addressed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor a) direct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records with GSIS, PHILHEALTH and Pag-IBIG and make the necessary adjustments, if any; and b) instruct the Municipal Treasurer to identify the employees whose contributions and loan amortizations were still not remitted and effect immediate remittance.
observations.managementAction: In CY 2018, the withheld contributions from employees were already remitted on time to avoid penalties. However, some amounts retained in the municipality’s books still exist. The Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant are reconciling and coordinating with the concerned agencies.
observations.reason: The unremitted amount due to government agencies exists in the books of the municipality. Also, an under remittance due to incorrect deduction of loan amortization amounting to P3,806.84 was noted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a. The Municipal Mayor along with the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) and Municipal Engineer strictly follow the provisions of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 in planning the programs/projects/activities funded by 20% Development Fund and their implementation; b. The Municipal Mayor along with the MPDC ensure that programs/projects/activities partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures; c. The Municipal Accountant ensure that transactions are properly recorded in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009; d. The MPDC and BAC ensure that all projects under the 20% development which will undergo procurement be included in the Annual Procurement Plan (APP) of the agency in compliance with the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and the Municipal Engineer prepare the PPMP ahead of time; e. proper coordination be made between the Municipal Accountant and MPDC in order to generate accurate and reliable reports; f. the Municipal Accountant include projects under the continuing appropriation of the 20% Development Fund in the preparation of reports and submit documents to warrant transfers; g. the Municipal Treasurer ensure that the 20% Development Fund and all other PPAs are always backed-up by cash; and h. the Municipal Mayor allow only charges on the programs, projects and activities under the 20% DF that are in line with the guidelines.
observations.managementAction: In CY 2018, the Municipal Accountant has already included projects under the continuing appropriation of the 20% Development Fund in the preparation of reports and the Municipal Treasurer ensured that the 20% Development Fund was backed–up by cash.
observations.reason: Various lapses were still noted in the audit of the 20% Development Fund for CY 2018. Details were discussed in Observation No. 10 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a. The GAD Focal Person ensure that all expenditures charged against GAD projects be identified and verified to establish reliability of the amount being charged to the projects; b. The GAD focal person ensure that the result of HGDG Assessment is accurate; c. The GFPS Technical Working Group (TWG) prepare the Annual GAD Accomplishment Report in coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant as well to avoid discrepancies in the reports prepared; d. The Municipal Mayor require the GAD focal person to strictly monitor if GAD PPAs during the year are being implemented; e. The GAD Focal Point Members establish and maintain a GAD database through gathering sex-disaggregated data and GAD-related information to guide them in the planning and implementation of the projects; and f. The GAD Focal Point members undergo GAD seminars and trainings.
observations.managementAction: Based on the foregoing observations, the GFPS has already prepared the GAD Accomplishment Report in coordination with the Municipal Budget and Municipal Accountant.
observations.reason: There were still lapses noted in the implementation of GAD Fund for CY 2018. Details were discussed in Observation No. 11 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate the required 1% of the total Internal Revenue Allotment for the LCPC programs, projects and activities.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Budget Officer has already allocated 1% of the total Internal Revenue Allotment for the LCPC programs, projects and activities.
AAR 2017
observations.reason:
Unliquidated balance as of December 31, 2017 for Advances to Officers & Employees pertains to previous officials and employees were reassigned to other agencies and some were already deceased.

Reiterated in Part II of this report.
The Office of the Supply Officer has submitted partial report of issuance of supplies and materials.

Still on reconciliation process.

Reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to compute the appropriate depreciation of these PPEs and to make the necessary accounting entries.
We recommended that Management make an allocation of one percent (1%) for Local Councils for the Protection of Children (LCPC) Programs in the Municipality’s Annual Budget for the ensuing year.

We recommended that Management:
implement feasible solid waste management projects such as construction of the MRF and perimeter fence around the open dump site;
direct the MSWMB to coordinate and assist all barangay in solid waste management, such as waste segregation, sorting, recycling and composting at their respective Material Recovery Facility (MRF); and
conduct Information Education and Communication campaign to the barangays, schools, private and business sectors and other institution
observations.reason: The Municipal 10- Year Solid Waste Management Plan was submitted to the NSWMP on January 31, 2018 for review. The management is awaiting for the NSWMP’s notice for the latter to defend its plan.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Municipal MDDRM Officer to ensure that preparedness programs such as stockpiling be procured in time when disaster actually hit the province and mass feeding be implemented to answer the needs of families in the evacuation centers during and immediately after disaster.

We recommended that Management:
direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately prepare and submit the required monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) for the 12 bank accounts of the Municipality as of December 31, 2016;
require that the personnel assigned to prepare the BRS shall not be given other duties other than the preparation of BRS, to enable him/her to finish the assigned task promptly; and
comply with the requirements of Treasury Circular 01-2017 and Department Circular No. 001-2015 to maintain account with PNB and PPSB otherwise close the accounts and transfer  them to AGDB such as LBP or DBP.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2016

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require:
the Municipal Treasurer deposit immediately to the authorized depository bank all her undeposited collections as of December 31, 2016 and strictly comply with Section 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382;
reconcile the records of the Municipal Treasurer with that of the Accounting Section from date of assumption as Municipal Treasurer up to December 31, 2016, to determine the correct amount of the undeposited collections; and
institute legal remedies to collect the undeposited collections from the three (3) accountable officers, namely: M.O. Millares, F.L. Bombase and Marietta V. Olivares.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2016
observations.reason:
Undeposited collections from prior years were not yet reconciled against the records of the Municipal Treasurer and the accounting office.

Reiterated in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Accountant prepare and submit monthly bank reconciliation statement together with its supporting documents in the prescribed method and format in order to prove the correctness of the balances reported in the financial statements and to comply with Section 74 of PD 1445, COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996 and National Guidelines on Internal Control System as circularized in DBM Circular Letter No. 2008- 8 dated October 23, 2008.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant regularly submits Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS).
observations.reason: No supporting documents were attached to the BRS. Likewise, account numbers for each bank account for each fund were not also indicated in BRS.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that henceforth, the Municipal Accountant should record all cash withdrawals for payment of wages, honoraria, prizes and other similar expenditures as a debit to Advances for Payrolls.  She should stop the practice of recording the transaction out rightly as expense.

Moreover, she should be instructed to strictly follow prescribed COA rules and regulations in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.
observations.managementAction: In CY 2018, most of the cash advances granted to the Municipal Treasurer were recorded as Advances for Payrolls.
observations.reason: In CY 2018, there were still payments of withdrawals for cash advances granted to the Municipal Treasurer that were outrightly recorded as Expenses instead of Advances for Payrolls.

observations.managementAction: Procurement of goods was recorded as Inventory. In the books.
observations.reason: Recording of inventories were already booked up as inventory account. No submission of the required inventory reports and schedules were submitted in CY 2018.

observations.recommendation:
In order to achieve the inclusive growth and poverty reduction as envisioned by the Bottom-up Budgeting Program, we recommend that management should:

Expedite the implementation of the projects in accordance with Section 5 of the  DBM-DILG-DSWD-NAPC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 7 dated November 3, 2015.

For projects with insufficient funds, submit the revise detail, design, specification and location of the project for approval in accordance with Section 5. 8 thereof; and

For unexpended balances of completed projects, return the amount to the funding agencies to fully liquidate the funds.
observations.managementAction: The LGU partially utilized some of the previous years’ funds for identified projects in CY 2018.
observations.reason: See related discussion in Part II, Finding No. 3 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that payments for wages and gratuity pay to contractual and job order employees should be supported with the required supporting documents such as DTRs, Accomplishment Reports and Contracts.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2018, payment of wages to job order workers were supported with necessary documents except for some observation mentioned Part II, Finding No. 6 of this report.
observations.reason: See related discussion in Part II, Finding No. 6 of this report.

observations.recommendation: To institutionalize the DRRM Office, we recommend that management create items for DRRM Officer and staff in compliance with RA 10121 or the Philippine Risk Reduction and Management Act of 2010 and NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014.
observations.managementAction: The LGU already appointed an LDRRM Officer with 4 staff- (1) permanent and (3) job orders.
observations.reason:
While there was already an appointed DRRMO with salary grade 15 and 1 permanent staff, but the other three (3) staff were not yet implemented.

See related discussion in Part II, Finding No. 2 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that all Disbursement Vouchers and its supporting documents should all be conspicuously stamped “PAID” by the Municipal Treasurer in compliance with COA Circular N0. 92-389.
observations.managementAction: Paid vouchers and its supporting documents for CY 2018 transactions were stamped “PAID”.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Accountant assign the JEV Numbers following the instructions set forth in the NGAS Manual for LGUs.  Furthermore, it is recommended that all JEVs should be approved by the assigned approving authority before transactions shall be recorded in the books of accounts.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant properly  assigned the JEV Numbers following the instructions set forth in the NGAS Manual for LGUs and JEVs were approved by the assigned approving authority before recording in the books of accounts.

observations.recommendation:
In order for the funds to be fully utilized and realize its purpose, we recommend that:

The LSB convene to thresh out their concerns and problems that are preventing performance of their duties and responsibilities

The LSB prepare the Annual SEF Budget following the procedure set forth in the Joint Circular and furnish the Local Accountant, Local Treasurer and Local Budget a copy of the budget

The Local Accountant prepare the quarterly and annual report on SEF Utilization using the format prescribed in the Joint Circular, and

Adhere to the Circular on the allowable expenses chargeable to SEF.
observations.managementAction: The unutilized funds as of 2018 were utilized in the following year. Bidding of certain priority projects of San Jose Central Elementary School San Jose Technical High School had already started.
observations.reason: See related discussion in Part II, Finding No. 7 of this report.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we recommend that management: 

Give priority to the development projects and programs that partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures. 

Optimize utilization of  the fund in conformity with the guidelines set forth by the DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 to pave the way for the realization of the desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes.

Copies of approved contracts and purchase orders of infrastructure projects and procured goods and services should be submitted within five (5) days from their execution/issuance;

Paid vouchers should be supported with the necessary documentary requirements to ensure the legality and propriety of the transactions.
observations.reason: See related discussion in Part II, Finding No. 1 of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend the following action to be implemented by management:

Stop disbursement of funds for purposes not within the line item budget in the approved budget proposal in compliance with the Memorandum of Agreement.
