Labor for the construction of the remaining coco coir shade houses and renovation of SJFA Training Center should be done by the members of the SJFA as their counterpart contribution to the project as provided in the MOA.

Propose realignment of some funds to be approved by the funding agency to procure decorticating machine for the use of the SJFA and allow the trained members to do the twining to enable them to increase their income in line with the objective of the project.

Fast track the implementation of the project to attain the Millennium Development Goal (MDG) of reducing poverty and attaining inclusive growth.
observations.managementAction: There were partially implementation made in CY 2018.
observations.reason: See related discussion in Part II, Finding No. 3 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that management exert extra effort to complete the conduct of physical count of its property, plant and equipment and prepare a complete and detailed Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment, in accordance with Section 156 of CAO Circular No. 92-386.
observations.managementAction: Was able to submit the RPCPPE for CY 2018.
observations.reason:
Reconciliation of GSO/Municipal Treasurer record not reconciled with that of Accounting Records.

See related discussion in Part II, Finding No. 10 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Municipal Budget Officer being the Chairman of the BAC be required to submit copies of approved contracts/purchase orders as provided for in COA Circular No. 2009-001 and comply strictly with the provisions of Section 4(6) of PD 1445 and RA 9184, its IRR and the relevant documents required in COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.
observations.managementAction: The BAC Chairman had complied on the submission of Purchase Orders but not on the Submission of Contracts
observations.reason: Contracts for the procurement of Infrastructure were not submitted in Calendar Year 2018

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that the Head of the Procuring Entity and the BAC should comply strictly with the provisions of Section 4(6) of PD 1445 and RA 9184, its IRR and the relevant documents required in COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

This recommendation is without prejudice to the issuance of Notices of Suspensions and/or Disallowances as a result of post audit of transactions
observations.reason: In calendar year 2018, there were still payments with no complete supporting documents.

observations.recommendation: Require the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to prepare and furnish the Municipal Accounting Office a duly certified list showing the names of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year to provide basis in setting up receivables at the beginning of the year, thereby complying the requirement of the provisions of Section 269 of RA 7160 and Section 20, NGAS, Volumes I for LGUs.

Direct the Bids and Awards Committee to conduct detailed bid evaluation process in accordance with the guidelines set under Section 32 of RA 9184 and GPBB Circular No. 02-2010 adding the cost of all taxes to the bid of cooperatives to ensure that fair and competitive bid comparison is provided to interested and qualified suppliers.
Direct the Municipal Accounting Office to prepare and submit regularly to COA the Monthly Status of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations to ensure timely review and verification of the same.

Direct the Municipal Agriculture Office to monitor and device a feedback or reporting mechanism on the progress of funded projects to be implemented in accordance with the Memorandum of Agreement. observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management, through the City Accountant, continue to enforce the settlement of the suspensions and disallowances, both for the current and prior years, from concerned personnel liable and responsible thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit intact all collections remitted to her by the liquidating officer/collectors including her own collections to ensure that these will not be used for personal and other matters.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Treasury and Accounting Offices to locate the supporting documents of the checks presented on the table above and henceforth, make sure that every check prepared has an approved disbursement voucher.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the PCF Custodian strictly follow the provision of Section 48 Volume I of the NGAS for LGUs regarding the maintenance of PCF and in the corresponding recording of pertinent transactions under the imprest system.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer faithfully adhere to the provisions of Section 175, Volume I of the GAAM and COA Circular No. 2012-001 regarding the withdrawal of cash advance for payroll fund to control utilization of the meager resource of the Municipality.

We reiterated the audit recommendation that the Municipal Accountant require the concerned accountable officers to immediately settle their unliquidated cash advances by submitting the liquidation report and settlement documents to the Accounting Office and/or by returning the unexpended balance of the cash advance to the Municipal Treasurer. 

We further recommended the application of Section 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 which states that the failure of the accountable officer to liquidate his cash advance within the prescribed period shall constitute a valid cause for the withholding of his salary.

We reiterated the audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive ensure the submission by the Municipal Accountant of all disbursement vouchers for confidential and intelligence cash advance, with complete required supporting documents in a sealed envelope to the ATL within seven working days from release of the check, as well as the liquidation thereof in accordance with Section 6.2 of the Joint Circular. 

Further, we recommended that the granted cash advance of P1,000,000.00 should not be used for reimbursement of paid expenses prior to the grant of cash advance.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

instruct the Treasury Office to prepare and furnish the Accounting Office the list of required under Section 20 Volume I of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs; and

instruct the Municipal Accountant to base the establishment of RPT and SET receivables on the list submitted by the Treasury Office for an accurate recognition of the said receivables; and make the necessary adjustments in the recording of the RPT/SET Receivables in CY 2016.

We reiterated the audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive create an inventory team to conduct actual physical count of all PPE of the Municipality in order to determine the existence and pinpoint the person/s responsible for the said assets.  The Inventory Team shall prepare and submit the result of the physical inventory that will be used by the Municipal Treasurer to start Property Cards. Consequently, require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their records. Further, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Treasurer to prepare, maintain and monitor AREs for every PPE issued to end users.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive furnish the Accounting Office of the Memorandum of Agreement with the PCSO and other pertinent documents to the received ambulances for the latter to record the same. Moreover, we recommended that the Accounting Office record the DILG funded dump truck in the General Fund books of accounts, and adjust corresponding depreciation. Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant review the disbursements from the Trust Fund in previous years and effect adjustments of accounts, if warranted.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Human Resources and Management Officer, the Accounting Office and other concerned offices to submit the supporting documents as required by COA Circular No. 2012-001.
We recommended that the Accounting Office adjust the accounting books and henceforth, record the subject benefit with the proper account.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, in coordination with the Sangguniang Bayan, adopt and implement clear policies concerning the provision of reasonable cellphone load allowance taking into consideration the provision of Section 2 of P.D. No. 1445 requiring the efficient and economical use of government resources. Non-implementation of the audit recommendation would compel the Audit Team to disallow the subject expense in the course of audit.

We reiterated the previous audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive, the Municipal Accountant, and the Municipal Treasurer seriously address the persisting issue on the delay in the submission of mandatory reports to COA by requiring that only those disbursement vouchers with complete supporting documents are processed and paid.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Accounting, Engineering and Planning Offices and the BAC and its Secretariat to submit the lacking documents and explain on the other observations. We further recommended that the same Offices be ordered to see to it that all necessary documents are attached to disbursement vouchers.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive –

instruct the Municipal Treasurer to prepare and issue Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) for every property issued to DepEd employees and instruct said employees to turn-over to the Municipality the issued properties upon transfer to other school or town; and

request the DepEd to inform the Municipality for every movement of DepEd personnel assigned in San Jose for the Municipal Treasurer to be able to monitor the proper turn-over of issued properties to the employees who will be moving out from a certain school.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive undertake the necessary actions to encourage and/or request women representatives from the NGO members in the local development council to achieve the goal of having an equitable representation of women in all spheres of society, particularly in the decision-making and policy-making processes in the government and to fully realize their role as agents of development.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) orient the Barangay VAW Desk officers on the preparation and submission of quarterly reports to the DILG and the MSWDO for the latter to take appropriate action based on the reports. We further recommended that the GFPS coordinate with other competent agencies on the conduct of formal training of the Barangay VAW Desk Officers.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive review the scholarship program of the Municipality and establish more detailed procedures to ensure the achievement of its objective which is to help poor but intellectually deserving students and addressing the problem on the out-of-school youth.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Sangguniang Bayan formulate the Municipality’s own implementing guidelines, policies and procedures regarding AICS, in conformity with the provisions set forth by the DSWD MC No. 02 S. 2014, as basis in the granting of financial or in-kind assistances to its constituents.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the GFPS to properly decide and plan PPAs that address GAD issues and concerns.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive –

instruct the Solid Waste Management Board to formulate, finalize and submit the Municipality’s Solid Waste Management Plan;

instruct concerned employees to operate the  MRF with compliance with R.A. No. 9003 to achieve optimum benefits; and

make order for the utilization of the equipments procured for their intended purposes, and the acquired dump truck for its solid waste management program.

We recommended that Management through the MDRRM Officer (a) ensure that all unexpended balance of the QRF and the DRRMF-Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses be transferred to Trust Liability-DRRM account in the Trust Fund books; and (b) see that LDRRMF is used solely for supporting disaster risk reduction and management programs/projects/ activities in accordance with RA No. 10121.
observations.reason: Lack of available cash.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record all fund transfers to implementing agencies as receivable account subject for liquidation and to recognize expenses only after its liquidation to ensure that public funds are used for its intended purpose.  We also recommended that fund transfers be supported with documentary requirements provided under Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to establish the regularity of the disbursements.
observations.reason: No justification was given by the Management. Related discussion in Observation No. 8, Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management strictly observe the general limitations on personal services cited under Section 325 (a) of R.A. No. 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 98.  The Municipal Mayor, should always be guided by the conditions of the agency’s annual and supplemental budgets to ensure that the budgeting requirements are completely complied with. As part of his fiscal responsibility, he should allow only payments of personal services costs and other expenditures expressly authorized by law.

The municipality should exert efforts in raising its revenues/receipts such as increasing tax collection efficiency and generating revenue related projects/activities to increase the base of PS limitation.
observations.reason: The municipality still exceeded its fifty-five percent (55%) Personal Services Limitation/Cap.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Municipal Accountant give priority to the timely submission of the accounts and financial reports within the reglementary period to enable the Audit Team to conduct timely audit and promptly inform the management of the deficiencies noted, if any.
observations.reason: Delay were still noted in the submission of accounts, financial reports and FS in CY 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the MDRRMO prepare and submit the LDRRMF Investment Plan in accordance with the format provided in Annex A of COA Circular No. 2012-002 to reflect the allocation for disaster mitigation, prevention and preparedness with details as to projects and activities to be funded, as well as the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years.
observations.reason: The MDRRMO still failed to prepare the LDRRMF Investment Plan in accordance with the format provided in Annex A of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the lacking documents to support the propriety and validity of the disbursements made and henceforth ensure that claims of the same nature be supported with complete documentation.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant failed to submit the lacking documents as required under Item 5.13 of COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management allocate 1% of their IRA for the strengthening and implementation of the programs of the LCPC pursuant to R.A. No. 9334.
observations.reason: The municipality still failed to allocate 1% of its IRA for LCPC.  Fund has been allocated for the LCPC through attribution only from the offices of MSWD and RHU.

Likewise, development projects included in the Annual Investment Plan should be implemented immediately to provide intended beneficiaries of its benefits thereby optimizing the desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes.

Likewise, reconciliation between the records of the municipal accountant and the municipal assessor should be regularly made to accurately establish the valuation of land in the book of accounts of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: Instruct the MPDC to construct a ground water monitoring well system in the appropriate locations within the sanitary landfill to continuously monitor the quality of ground water.
observations.managementAction: No Management action.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: (a) require the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board (MSWMB) to make a periodic review and re-evaluate the Ten-Year Municipal Solid Waste Management Plan as to its sustainability, viability, effectiveness and relevance in relation to local and international developments in the field of solid waste management, (b) direct the MSWMB to coordinate and assist all barangays in solid waste management, such as waste segregation, sorting, Tecovery, recycling and composting at their respective Material Recovery Facility (MRF); and (c) provide suitable storage metal containers with cover for biodegradable, recyclable and residual wastes in strategic places of residential areas, schools, commercial and market site to facilitate the collection of solid wastes by the garbage service trucks.

observations.reason: The LGU has not yet receive the SWM Plan duly approved by EMB.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management create an inventory committee to
conduct the physical count of PPE and prepare the RPCPPE annually in
accordance with Section 124 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal
Treasurer reconcile their records with the results of the actual count.

We recommended that management refrain from using the 20% EDF for
the payment of wages to casual and JO personnel assigned in the different offices
of the Municipality in accordance with DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated
February 22, 2017. We, however, recommended that these expenditures may be
charged to the General Fund Proper pursuant to existing rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipality utilize and implement its PPAs
under the LDRRMF to strengthen the capacity of the Municipality to avoid or
mitigate the adverse effects of disaster or calamity.

We recommended and management agreed to comply with the
documentary requirements under Section 5.7.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-001
dated June 14, 2012 before granting honoraria to its officials and employees.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to analyze and
review the composition of the balance of the Due to Philhealth account. We
further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer immediately remit to
Philhealth the amount withheld, or for the Municipal Accountant to revert the
long outstanding balance, if warranted.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed that he should see
to it that the PCF is not used for regular and recurring expenses and only use it in
the non-recurring, emergency and petty expenses.

We recommended that management create a GFPS and a GFPS-TWG and
prepare its GAD Plan and Budget in accordance with Joint Memorandum
Circular No. 2013-01 of the PCW, DILG, DBM and the NEDA so that gender
issues and concerns of the Municipality may be substantially addressed.

We recommended and Management agreed to (a) instruct the (i) City Treasurer and City Assessor to continuously coordinate for the updating of the list of taxpayers; and (ii) City Accountant to make appropriate adjustments in the receivable balances of each taxpayer, as warranted; and (b) address the inadequacy of personnel at the City Treasurer’s Office but taking into consideration the Personal Services limitations in the Local Government Code.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the (a) GSO to (i) fast track the recording of individual PPEs in the property cards; and (ii) based on PCs, extract a list of all PPEs that will be used in the physical validation of these assets; and (b) Inventory and Disposal Committee to facilitate the (i) conduct of physical count in CY 2018 in accordance with prescribed rules and timelines; and (ii) disposal of unserviceable items based on the guidelines set. Likewise, we advised, to which Management concurred, to ensure that all the functionalities of the proposed PPE inventory system meet their actual requirements.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the (a) City Engineer, City Budget Officer and the City Treasurer to resolve the identified causes of the missed timelines in project implementation and to act on these accordingly; and (b) City Planning and Development Officer or the concerned officer to henceforth ensure that the documentary requirements of future and similarly-funded projects are timely provided.

We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the (a) LDRRM Officer to monitor, evaluate and enhance performance on DRRM, as warranted, based on the Disaster Preparedness Minimum Standards on Disaster Risk Reduction and Management; (b) City Accountant to (i) cause the refund to LDRRMF from appropriate trust fund the amount paid for BAC honoraria; and (ii) refrain from using DRRMF for programs/projects/activities which are not directly related to disaster preparedness, mitigation, response and rescue operations; and (c) BAC to explore other legal procurement options such as “Ordering Agreement” in the procurement of relief goods and food packs.

We commended the effort exerted by the Management in the implementation of the SWM Plan to ensure protection of the environment and the general welfare of the City’s constituents.  Also, we recommended and Management agreed, thru the CENRO, to continue ensuring clean and healthy environment through waste reduction, recycling, diversion, proper disposal, segregation, collection, transport, storage, and treatment of solid waste as well as the formulation and adoption of the best environment practice in ecological waste Management as required under R.A. No. 9003.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Members of the City Local School Board  (a) ensure that future plans and budgets for SEF are consistent with DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 01, s. 2017 dated January 19, 2017 and other pertinent rules that may be issued henceforth; (b) secure certification from the DepEd Budget Officer that the charges to SEF were already net of the approved DepEd Budget;  and (c) refrain from accommodating expenditures for activities for which the DepEd has the direct mandate to execute. We also advised that the invalid charges to SEF be restored to the fund from the General Fund after the requisite legislation is secured.

We recommended and Management agreed to (a) reconstitute the LCPC after ensuring that the required membership qualification are met; (b) instruct the LCPC to (i) prepare a comprehensive plan on delinquency prevention, and to oversee its proper implementation; (ii) continuously ensure that at least 1% of the City’s annual IRA is attributed to child protection related activities and in the development of a child-friendly environment; and (iii) prepare accomplishment reports and other necessary documents required under the MC Nos. 2008-126 and 2012-120.

We recommended and Management agreed to (a) instruct the (i) the concerned City Engineer to submit the approved request for extension for the project, Construction of LDRRMO Building; otherwise, impose on the contractor liquidated damages; (ii) BAC and City Engineer to ascertain readiness of the project site and satisfaction of other requirements before the procurement proceedings are initiated; (iii) City Accountant, the BAC and the contractors concerned to immediately submit the above-indicated documents to the Audit Team for a facilitated audit action on the said transactions; and (b) ensure that the use of government funds are adequately documented and are in accordance with laws and regulations.

We recommended and Management agreed to look into the reason for, and act accordingly to address, the continued failure of the LGU in the timely submission of the reports, records, accounts, contracts, POs, DRs, and other documents required by regulations.

We commended Management for their effective planning and implementation of the Gender and Development programs, projects and activities. We also recommended and Management agreed to continue adhering to the requirements under Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA.

We recommended and Management agreed that the City Accountant and City Treasurer continue to be compliant in the deduction of GSIS premium contributions and the remittance thereof including the government share within the prescribed period.

We commended the City Accounting Office and City Treasurer's Office in their continuous compliance with existing laws, rules and regulations on proper deductions and remittances of Pag-IBIG fund contributions and loan amortizations in accordance with the provisions of RA No. 9679. We also recommend the Accounting Office to verify the negative balance signifying over-remittance and make the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Treasury and Accounting Offices to submit to the Audit Team the disbursement vouchers for the twenty paid checks together with all the relevant supporting documents to avoid disallowance or suspension in audit.

It was recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer strictly adhere to Section 48 of Volume I of the Manual on NGAS for LGUs.
We reiterated the audit recommendation that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer (PCF Custodian) strictly adhere to the provision of Section 48 Volume I of the NGAS for LGUs and relevant rules and regulations on the proper maintenance and recording of transactions of Petty Cash Fund.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct Treasury and Accounting Offices to comply with all the laws, rules and regulations on cash management.

We reiterated the audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive:
instruct the Treasury Office to prepare and furnish the Accounting Office the duly certified list required under Section 20 Volume I of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs; and
instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) base the recognition of RPT and SET receivables on the list submitted by the Treasury Office; and (ii) make the necessary adjustments in the misstatements of the RPT/SET Receivables.

We reiterated the audit recommendation that the Municipal Accountant require all Accountable Officers to immediately liquidate their cash advances by submitting required report and settlement documents to the Accounting Office and refund the unexpended balance to the Municipal Treasurer. We likewise recommended that the Municipality avoid granting cash advances to non-regular employees and additional cash advances to accountable officers with outstanding account.

We reiterated the audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive ensure the submission of all disbursement vouchers for confidential cash advances. We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant (i) certify the disbursements only after the completion of supporting documents before any payment of confidential funds is effected, and (ii) submit immediately all cash advances vouchers and their supporting documents to the Auditor.

We recommended that the management: (i) stop the practice of paying fixed allowance for communication without the submission of proof on the incurrence of expense showing or justifying the actual usage or consumption by the concerned officials and employees and (ii) consider the availment of contracts subscriptions with telephone companies offering discounts or service promotions.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and other concerned Officers (i) submit clarification on the designation of an employee with a first level item as OIC-HRMO and justification for the grant of RATA to said OIC-HRMO; and (ii) for future and similar personnel movement, observe the guidelines set forth by the CSC in the designation of personnel as OICs.

We recommended that the Management:
draft individual contract of services showing the rates, functions and expected outputs to its JOs and Casual employees;
ensure that each JO and Casual employee has DTRs and ARs duly approved and certified by their supervisors every payroll period; and
ensure the completeness of details in the DTRs of the said employees.
We recommended that the Municipality submit explanations/information/documents for the issues stated above.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the BAC and its Secretariat, the Accounting Office and the Engineering Office to submit the lacking documents in order to avoid the Auditor’s issuance of Notice of Suspension.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct lead offices for the programs under trust to secure and maintain complete copies of relevant documents for monitoring and evaluation purposes. We recommended further that the MOA with the DOT and the corresponding approved project/program, if any, be submitted immediately to facilitate the proper evaluation of the project/program’s implementation.

We recommended that the Management:
exert efforts to secure a copy of the MOA relative to the Community Based Livelihood Program funded by DSWD-RO III and submit the same to the Audit Team to further evaluate the implementation of the said program;
submit the corresponding detailed project proposal for the livelihood project; and
explain/justify the discrepancies in the items procured and the items distributed and provide specific specifications of the items procured for audit purposes.

We reiterated the audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive instruct concerned offices to establish a mechanism necessary to operate and manage the harvester including the scheme on income generation that could both benefit the farmers and the Municipality. We further recommended that the concerned officials conduct monitoring to ensure that the programs’ activities are implemented in accordance with the agreement or MOA.

We reiterated the previous audit recommendation that the:
GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) orient the Barangay VAW Desk officers on the preparation and submission of quarterly reports to the DILG and the MSWDO for the latter to take appropriate action based on the reports.
GFPS coordinate with other competent agencies on the conduct of formal training of the Barangay VAW Desk Officers.
Local Chief Executive review the scholarship program of the Municipality and establish more detailed procedures to ensure the achievement of its objective which is to help poor but intellectually deserving students and addressing the problem on the out-of-school youth.
Local Chief Executive and the Sangguniang Bayan formulate the Municipality’s own implementing guidelines, policies and procedures regarding AICS, in conformity with the provisions set forth by the DSWD MC No. 02 S. 2014, as basis in the granting of financial or in-kind assistances to its constituents.
We further recommended that (1) the LCE instruct the GFPS to properly monitor the GAD PPAs and coordinate with respective offices on implementing and reporting GAD plan and accomplishments; and (2) the BAC and its Secretariat and other concerned offices to submit explanation/justification/documents for the issues stated in paragraph 1.7.

We recommended that the Municipality continue to allocate the mandatory appropriation or funding for LCPC. We also recommended that the Municipality prepare its LDPC and LIPC in consideration of other relevant and comprehensive programs for children.
We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive (1) instruct the LCPC to consider in the formulation of plans and programs those which are culturally relevant and responsive and (2) direct the concerned officers to submit explanation/documents/information for the post audit findings indicated under paragraph 1.8.

We recommended that the Accounting Office (i) raise immediately the detected discrepancy in the billing or statement of account; (ii) regularly reconcile its records with the GSIS; and (iii) make the necessary adjustments, if warranted.

We reiterated the audit recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Treasury and Accounting Offices to reconcile their records.  We further recommended that the Treasury Office should prepare remittance schedules based on checks issued only for the Accounting Office will only recognize taxes withheld from the issued checks for a certain month.

We recommended that the Treasury Office (i) exert effort in preparing accurate remittances on time; and (ii) inquire from Pag-IBIG and PHIC on the proper treatment of contributions withheld from salary differential which were belatedly paid to employees.
We recommended that the Management to settle the above audit suspension through compliance with the requirements indicated on paragraph 29.2.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the persons responsible and liable to fully settle their respective accountabilities.

We recommended that the Accountant (i) strengthen his review controls over disbursements and ensure the completeness and propriety of the supporting documents before certifying the DVs; and (ii) deliver immediately the accounts and reports that are due for submission as of date. We further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer defer the approval or processing of the check if the DV does not bear signatures of the key officials.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive to:
instruct the Municipal Engineering Office to conduct comprehensive evaluation and implement necessary corrective measures so that the Municipality could benefit from capital investment put to the project; and
ensure that all projects are properly planned taking into consideration the competence and technical knowledge of the involved officers/employees.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal OIC-
General Services Officer to carry on and accelerate the process by which these
unserviceable properties could be disposed of, in accordance with the
abovementioned rules and regulations, so that the balances of Property, Plant and
Equipment accounts presented in the financial statements would only represent
those assets that are in existence and servicing the needs of the Municipality.

We recommended to the Municipal officials concerned to strictly observe the
austerity measures being implemented by the government in terms of trainings and
seminars and exercise due diligence on the use of the limited government funds
entrusted to them. Further, avoid expenditures that may qualify to irregular,
unnecessary, excessive, extravagant, unconscionable expenditures, otherwise these
expenditures will be disallowed in audit.

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant require the concerned
officials and employees to attach the Itinerary of Travel (Appendix A) together with
the Travel Order and other required documents in support to cash advances for travel,
and the Certificate of Travel Completed (Appendix B), tickets, e-tickets, boarding
pass on the corresponding liquidation vouchers in accord with COA Circular Nos. 96-
004 and 2005-003 dated April 19, 1996 and September 22, 2005, respectively.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal
Development Council and the Office of the Municipal Engineer to:
a. ensure that the mandatory 20% Development Fund appropriated out of Internal
Revenue Allotment be optimally utilized to implement identified priority
programs and activities to help achieve the desirable outcomes on socio-economic
development and environmental management pursuant to the DILG-DBM Joint
Circular No. 2011-1;
b. properly plan and coordinate with DPWH and other concerned officials which
have projects counterpart and coincide with the programmed projects of the
Municipality; and
c. come up with a well-defined guidelines in identifying priority programs and
projects in order to project a feasible and appropriate results that can be delivered
efficiently and effectively to the public on time.

We recommended to the Management the following courses of action:
The Inventory Committee conduct complete physical count of all inventories and submit the complete report thereon to the Auditor, copy furnish the Municipal Accountant to enable reconciliation with their records/ledgers;
The Municipal Accountant (i) record the receipt of supplies in the Supplies Ledger Cards including the complete details of the Inventories; (ii) record the receipts of medicines from the DOH; (iii) reclassify the Float Buoy Markers from Inventory account to proper PPE account, subject to depreciation, for the fair presentation of affected accounts in the financial statements; and
The OIC-General Service Office (i) assign stock number for each type of supplies upon receipt thereof and maintain stock cards per stock number of Inventories; (ii) require/prepare RIS for issuance of inventories to end-users and consolidate weekly all RIS using the SSMI for submission to the Municipal Accountant to enable recognition of expenses totaling P15,237,424.75 in the books of accounts; and (iii) regularly reconcile the balance of stock cards with the ledger cards of the Municipal Accounting Office to promptly address discrepancies, if any.

We recommended the following courses of actions:
the Municipal Treasurer (i) submit records to Municipal Accountant regarding the accurate tax liability due from OMECO for proper recording in the books of accounts; and (ii) communicate with OMECO the adjustments made on its business taxes for reconciliation of their records with that of the Municipality; and
the Municipal Accountant (i) compute the amount of unpaid electric bills due to OMECO and prepare the necessary Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the exact amount of obligation due to them; (ii) prepare JEV to adjust the  Other Deferred Credits account balance for its fair presentation in the Statement of Financial Position; and (iii) exert efforts in locating the documents/records pertaining to the outstanding loans receivables totalling P13,048,824.04 under Tulong Pangkabuhayan Loan Program to establish the validity and legality of claims against the debtors, facilitate its collection  and to prevent the possible loss of funds.

We recommended the Municipal Accountant transfer the unexpended/unutilized amount of QRF and DRRM-MOOE as Trust Liability-DRRM in the trust fund books solely for the purpose of supporting disaster risk reduction management activities of the LDRRM Council within the next five years.

We recommended that the (a) Municipal Accountant (i) strictly monitor the grant of cash advance by preparing certification that previous cash advances have been liquidated and accounted for in the books to prevent the grant of additional cash advances to those employees and officers with outstanding balances; (ii) practice prudence in the grant of cash advances to casual/job order employees whose connection and tenure with the Municipality is uncertain there being no employee-employer relationship;  and (iii) ensure that the grant and liquidation of cash advances are duly supported with proper and complete supporting documents, ObR included, to establish the validity, propriety and legality of the transactions; and (b) Municipal Treasurer pay regular and operating expenses by check and that only payments in small amount should be made through the petty cash fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) ensure that the Engineering technical staff conduct detailed engineering investigation, survey designs for infrastructure projects and duly approved in accordance with the standards and specifications prescribed by the HoPE and with Annex A of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184; (b) refrain from issuing unjustifiable suspension orders and time extensions to cover up the real cause of slippage or negligence on the part of the Management or the contractor; and (c) direct the Municipal Engineer to (i) strictly monitor the implementation of projects to ensure their timely completion to enable early enjoyment of benefits by intended users; and (ii) observe compliance with COA Circular No. 2013-003 on the display of signboards for infrastructure projects.
We further recommended that the (a) Bids and Awards Committee ensure that procurement of infrastructure projects are made strictly in accordance with the requirements of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR by verifying the regularity and completeness of eligibility requirements submitted by the bidders; (b) OIC – Municipal Accountant ensure that all claims are duly supported with proper and complete supporting documents required under Section 9.1. of COA Circular No. 2012-001; and (c) Municipal Treasurer ensure that checks are drawn only on duly approved disbursement vouchers and to require written authorization if the claimant is other than the payee.

We recommended that the Management, through the (a) Municipal Mayor and the OIC-Municipal Agriculturist (i) exercise prudence and restraint in granting financial assistance to NGOs/POs, NGAs and barangays; and (ii) adhere strictly with the guidelines provided under COA Circular Nos. 2007-001 and  94-013  on the availment, release and utilization of fund transfers to NGOs/POs and implementing agencies, respectively, to ensure the proper implementation of projects and the attainment of its objectives for the benefit of the intended beneficiaries.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant (i) review the regularity and completeness of supporting documents as required by COA Circular No. 2012-001, as amended by COA Circular No. 2016-002; and (ii) record funds released to NGOs/POs, NGAs and LGUs as receivable accounts subject to liquidation; and (iii) recognize expenses only after its liquidations to facilitate monitoring and ensure that public funds are used for its intended purpose.
We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer be more cautious in releasing checks by requiring official receipt to acknowledge receipt of payment especially from the barangay treasurers and to require written authorization and certified photo copy of valid Identification Card if the claimant is other than the payee or the authorized representative of the association.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, to remit all unused balance of funds to the source agencies and the Bureau of Treasury as soon as cash becomes available, pursuant to COA Circular No. 94-013 and Supreme Court Ruling dated November 19, 2013.

We recommended that Management, through the Solid Waste Management Board
Hasten the finalization of the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan that will place primary emphasis on the implementation of all feasible re-use, recycling, and composting programs of the Municipality;
Encourage the barangay officials  to establish MRF in their respective barangays which must be strategically located in  a certain parcel of land  where they can receive mixed waste for final sorting, segregation, composting and recycling before the resulting residual wastes are transferred to a long term storage or disposal facility or sanitary land fill; and
Allocate funds for the effective implementation of Ecological Solid Waste Management Plan especially on the construction of Materials Recovery Facility and conversion of its open dumpsite to long term disposal facility or sanitary landfill to ensure the health and safety of the people and the environment.

We recommended that the Municipal School Board strictly comply with the prioritization of expenditures to be charged against the SEF based on DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 01, s. 2017 and Section 272 of the Local Government Code of 1991.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, with the assistance of the Local Development Council, the Municipal Development Officer and the Municipal Engineer optimize the utilization of the 20% development fund through: a) proper prioritization and planning of projects including the timing of execution; b) strict monitoring of project implementation to ensure prompt completion of the projects; c) ensure that projects charged against the 20% Development Fund partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures that will contribute to the attainment of socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the Municipality; and d) ensure that expenditures such as, repair and maintenance of various vehicles and equipment, purchase of petroleum products and payment of salary of job order/casual employees are not charged against the fund in compliance with Item 4.0 of the DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-001 dated February 22, 2017.
We also recommended that the a) MBO and the Municipal Accountant coordinate with each other and reconcile their records regularly to ensure the accuracy and completeness of information reflected in their respective reports; and b) Municipal Treasurer and/or Municipal Accountant submit the 40 DVs totalling P9,969,031.95 and its supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor for verification and audit.

We recommended the following courses of action:
The Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (i) ensure the optimal utilization of 70% mitigation fund of LDRRMF to strengthen the resilience of the constituents in times of calamities as required under RA 10121; (ii) see that the stockpile procured are distributed/utilized only for the purpose for which it was purchased; and (iii) prepare the LDRRMFIP correctly showing the 30% allocation for QRF and the details of PPAs to be funded by the 70% mitigation fund as well as the PPAs charged to unexpended LDRRMF of previous years; and
The Bids and Awards Committee observe the procedure and requirements of Section 29 and 35.1 of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 with regard to bid opening and failure of bidding, including the procedures in negotiation (two failed bidding) provided under Annex H of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended for the settlement of audit suspension within 90 days from receipt of the Notice of Suspension, pursuant to Section 9.4 of the Rules and Regulations on Settlement of Accounts.  The head of the agency is responsible for ensuring that the requirements of transactions suspended in audit are complied with.

We reiterated our recommendation that the  Municipal  Mayor  require
   the concerned officials to submit  the  financial  reports/statements  of
   calendar years 1994 to 2002 to the Audit Team. The Municipal Accountant -
   Designee and the Municipal Treasurer in coordination with the former
   Municipal Accountant – Designee must render once and for all  the  actual
   and true status of financial accounts of the Municipality, otherwise, the
   responsible officials should be held liable under the Revised Penal  Code
   for their failure to render the accounts and reporting thereof.

Based  on  the  foregoing  observations,  we  reiterated  our
   recommendations that:
        a. the Municipal Mayor require  the  Municipal  Treasurer  to  exert
           extra effort to account for the unreconciled items  appearing  in
           the Bank Reconciliation  Statements  to  faithfully  present  the
           correct balances of the account for the period; and


        b. the Municipal Accountant prepare the Monthly Bank  Reconciliation
           Statements for  all  bank  accounts  and  submit  same  regularly
           together  with  the   paid   checks,   debit/credit   memos   for
           verification as provided for under Section 74 of PD No. 1445  and
           COA Circular No. 96-011.  Continue securing  bank  statements  of
           prior years to establish the correct balance of the Cash in  Bank
           and update and submit the subsidiary  ledgers  of  all  the  bank
           accounts.

Based on  the  foregoing  audit  observations,  we  reiterated  our
   previous recommendations that the Municipal Mayor:


        a.    require all accountable officers and employees to immediately
           liquidate their outstanding cash advances giving  more  emphasis
           to the long outstanding cash advances;


        b.    resort to the withholding of salaries  of  those  accountable
           officers and employees who still fail or persistently refuse  to
           settle/liquidate their cash advances;


        c. exert extra efforts to recover outstanding  cash  advances  from
           Accountable Officers who  are  no  longer  in  the  service  nor
           connected with the municipality;

        e.    direct the Municipal  Accountant  to  refrain  from  granting
           additional cash advances to  any  official  or  employee  unless
           previous cash advance given to him is first settled or a  proper
           accounting thereof is made; and


        f.    require  the  Municipal  Accountant  to  maintain  Subsidiary
           Ledgers and schedules to facilitate  monitoring  of  the  amount
           outstanding per employee, ensure that no additional cash advance
           will be granted to those with outstanding balances  and  support
           the amounts reflected in the financial statements.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management  strictly  observe
   the general limitations on personal services cited under Section 325  (a)
   of R.A. No. 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 98.  The Municipal  Mayor,
   being  the  Local  Chief  Executive,  should  always  be  guided  by  the
   conditions of the agency’s annual and supplemental budgets to ensure that
   the budgeting requirements are completely complied with. As part  of  his
   fiscal responsibility, he should allow only payments of personal services
   costs and other expenditures expressly authorized by law.


        The municipality should also increase its efforts  in  raising  its
   revenues/receipts  such  as  increasing  tax  collection  efficiency  and
   generating revenue related projects/activities to increase the base of PS
   limitation.

In view of the aforementioned observations, we recommended that  the
   Municipal Mayor direct the BAC to:


       a. strengthen internal control over eligibility checking,  evaluation
          of the bids and post-qualification of bidders by requiring the BAC
          and the TWG to ensure timeliness, completeness and veracity of the
          post-qualified Documents;


       b. ensure compliance to the procurement schedule, including deadlines
          and timeframes for different activities prescribed in Annex  C  of
          the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184;


       c.  ensure mathematical accuracy on the evaluation of bids;


       d. comply strictly with the requirements of the 2016 Revised IRR  of
          R.A. No. 9184; and


       e. comply strictly with Section 3.1.1 of COA  Circular  No.  2009-001
          dated February 12, 2009 in the submission of copies  of  contracts
          to the Audit Team.

In view of the foregoing audit observations, we recommended that:


        a. the Municipal Mayor instruct the LDRRMO to prepare and submit the
           LDRRMFIP to the Audit Team in accordance with Item 5.1.2  of  COA
           Circular No. 2012-002;


        b. the LDRRMO refrain  from  incurring  and  charging  expenses  not
           related to disaster risk reduction management activities against
           the DRRM fund; and


        c. the Municipal Accountant be guided  with  the  Revised  Chart  of
           Accounts for Local Government Units prescribed  by  COA  Circular
           No. 2015-009 in recording the transactions of the municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure  that  all  the
   forwarded RCDs with copies of ORs and validated deposit slips be prepared
   with the Journal of Entry  Voucher  and  recorded  in  the  Cash  Receipt
   Journal to avoid unrecorded collections and deposits.   Likewise,  ensure
   the accuracy of the amounts  of  recorded  transactions  and  adjust  the
   balance of the accounts affected by the miscalculated  collections  noted
   in CY 2016 audit.

We recommended that the BAC Secretariat, or other assigned  as  the
   case may  be  ensure  the  timely  submission  of  copies  of  government
   contracts and each of all the required documentary  requirements  forming
   part thereof within five (5) working days  from  execution  and  purchase
   orders within five (5) working  days  from  issuance,  to  ensure  timely
   review  and  evaluation  of  same,  so  that  the  management  could   be
   immediately notified of any defects/deficiencies  noted.   Likewise,  the
   Municipal Accountant  review  the  purchase  orders  and  its  supporting
   documents before preparation of vouchers to determine completeness of the
   documentary requirements as required under the 2016 Revised IRR  of  R.A.
   No. 9184 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:


        a. as the Local Chief Executive, strictly implement its solid  waste
           management plan;


        b. advise the  Solid Waste Focal Person to seek assistance from  the
           Provincial Environmental and  Natural  Resources  Office  in  the
           conduct of WACS;


        c. through the Liga ng mga Barangay, encourage active  participation
           of barangay officials  to  support  the  solid  waste  management
           program  of  the  government  by   educating   their   respective
           constituents on the proper segregation of reusable or  recyclable
           materials from compostable wastes;


        d. through SWMB, comply with the requirements of the  law  on  waste
           segregation by placing trash bins with proper labels;


        e. recommend to the Sangguniang Bayan  (SB)  policies  and  possible
           measures that will ensure full compliance by the barangays on the
           provisions of R.A. No. 9003; and


        f. through SWMB to fast track the processing of various requirements
           for the operation of  a  sanitary  landfill  in  accordance  with
           Sections 40 and 41 of R.A. No. 9003 the soonest possible time  to
           avert adverse environmental consequences.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor a)  direct  the
               Municipal Accountant to reconcile their  records  with  GSIS,
               Pag-IBIG and PHILHEALTH and make the  necessary  adjustments,
               if any; and b) instruct the Municipal Treasurer  to  identify
               the   employees   whose   GSIS,   Pag-IBIG   and   PHILHEALTH
               contributions and loan amortizations were still not  remitted
               and effect the immediate remittance thereof.


                 We  recommended  that  the  Municipal  Mayor  instruct  the
            employee who prepares the monthly payroll to be  more  aware  of
            the terms of the loan of each and every employee  especially  if
            there is a renewal of loan and see to it that the deductions are
            in accordance with the updated  billing  from  the  Pag-IBIG  to
            avoid under remittance and refund during the year.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant prepare and submit monthly bank reconciliation statement together with its supporting documents in the prescribed method and format in order to prove the correctness of the balances reported in the financial statements and to comply with Section 74 of PD 1445, COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996 and National Guidelines on Internal Control System as circularized in DBM Circular Letter No. 2008- 8 dated October 23, 2008.

We recommend that henceforth, the Municipal Accountant should record all cash withdrawals for payment of wages, honoraria, prizes and other similar expenditures as a debit to Advances for Payrolls.  She should stop the practice of recording the transaction out rightly as expense.

Moreover, she should be instructed to strictly follow prescribed COA rules and regulations in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant should be instructed to comply with required supporting documents prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 in the granting of cash advances for payments for salaries, wages, allowances, honoraria and other similar expenses.

In order to achieve the inclusive growth and poverty reduction as envisioned by the Bottom-up Budgeting Program, we recommend that management should:
Expedite the implementation of the projects in accordance with Section 5 of the  DBM-DILG-DSWD-NAPC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 7 dated November 3, 2015,
For projects with insufficient funds, submit the revise detail, design, specification and location of the project for approval in accordance with Section 5. 8 thereof; and
For unexpended balances of completed projects, return the amount to the funding agencies to fully liquidate the funds.

We recommend that payments for wages and gratuity pay to contractual and job order employees should be supported with the required supporting documents such as DTRs, Accomplishment Reports and Contracts.

To institutionalize the DRRM Office, we recommend that management create items for DRRM Officer and staff in compliance with RA 10121 or the Philippine Risk Reduction and Management Act of 2010 and NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014.

To correct the procedure, we recommend that the payment for registration fees for CLUP trainings should be acknowledged by an Official Receipt of the government (AF51) in accordance with the provisions of Executive Order No. 292 and Section 68(1) of PD 1445.

Since fiscal responsibility rests upon the Head of the Agency, we recommend that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to render her accounts and submit the required financial transactions together with the supporting documents to the Accounting Division, and the Municipal Accountant to submit the accounts and reports to COA on or before the 10th day of the succeeding month.

Likewise, the accountable officer and/or other officials concerned are reminded of the sanctions imposed under Section 122 of PD 1445 in relation to Article 218 of the Revised Penal Code on the automatic suspension of payment of salaries for failure to render accounts in a period of two (2) months.
We recommend that all Disbursement Vouchers and its supporting documents should all be conspicuously stamped “PAID” by the Municipal Treasurer in compliance with COA Circular N0. 92-389.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant assign the JEV Numbers following the instructions set forth in the NGAS Manual for LGUs.  Furthermore, it is recommended that all JEVs should be approved by the assigned approving authority before transactions shall be recorded in the books of accounts.

To correct the deficiencies, we recommend that the Municipal Mayor reorganize the LCPC/MCPC and thru the concerned officials to appropriate one (1%) from its IRA in the LGUs Annual Budget in compliance with RA 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012.   We further recommend that the MCPC propose and implement Programs/Projects/Activities that advocates child rights, plan and conduct monitoring/interventions on children’s programs and projects.

In order for the funds to be fully utilized and realize its purpose, we recommend that:
The LSB convene to thresh out their concerns and problems that are preventing performance of their duties and responsibilities
The LSB prepare the Annual SEF Budget following the procedure set forth in the Joint Circular and furnish the Local Accountant, Local Treasurer and Local Budget a copy of the budget
The Local Accountant prepare the quarterly and annual report on SEF Utilization using the format prescribed in the Joint Circular, and
Adhere to the Circular on the allowable expenses chargeable to SEF

In view of the foregoing, we recommend that management:
Give priority to the development projects and programs that partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures.
Optimize utilization of  the fund in conformity with the guidelines set forth by the DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 to pave the way for the realization of the desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes.
Copies of approved contracts and purchase orders of infrastructure projects and procured goods and services should be submitted within five (5) days from their execution/issuance;
Paid vouchers should be supported with the necessary documentary requirements to ensure the legality and propriety of the transactions.

We recommend the following action to be implemented by management:
Stop disbursement of funds for purposes not within the line item budget in the approved budget proposal in compliance with the Memorandum of Agreement.
Labor for the construction of the remaining coco coir shade houses and renovation of SJFA Training Center should be done by the members of the SJFA as their counterpart contribution to the project as provided in the MOA.
Propose realignment of some funds to be approved by the funding agency to procure decorticating machine for the use of the SJFA and allow the trained members to do the twining to enable them to increase their income in line with the objective of the project.
Fast track the implementation of the project to attain the Millennium Development Goal (MDG) of reducing poverty and attaining inclusive growth.

We recommend that procurement of materials for the construction of shed houses be made in bulk thru competitive public bidding in compliance with Republic Act 9184 or the Government Procurement Reform Act and its revised implementing rules and regulations.  Avoid splitting of contract in the guise of on a per shed house basis.

Require the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to prepare and furnish the Municipal Accounting Office a duly certified list showing the names of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year to provide basis in setting up receivables at the beginning of the year, thereby complying the requirement of the provisions of Section 269 of RA 7160 and Section 20, NGAS, Volumes I for LGUs.

Direct the Bids and Awards Committee to conduct detailed bid evaluation process in accordance with the guidelines set under Section 32 of RA 9184 and GPBB Circular No. 02-2010 adding the cost of all taxes to the bid of cooperatives to ensure that fair and competitive bid comparison is provided to interested and qualified suppliers.
Direct the Municipal Accounting Office to prepare and submit regularly to COA the Monthly Status of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations to ensure timely review and verification of the same.
Direct the MDRRM Office that utmost effort be exerted to recover immediately the L300 truck which was intended for disaster response purposes.

Direct the Municipal Agriculture Office to monitor and device a feedback or reporting mechanism on the progress of funded projects to be implemented in accordance with the Memorandum of Agreement. Consider to return the unexpended balance of the released funds if it is improbable that the project could continue.

We recommended Management to:
Direct the Municipal Planning and Development Office to monitor the implementation of the projects as embodied in the Annual Investment Plan;
Instruct the Municipal Engineering Office to coordinate with the departments concerned to fast track the implementation of the projects; and
Remind the Mayor’s Office to ensure compliance with the responsibilities provided for in paragraph 5.0 of DBM and DILG JMC No. 2017-1 to achieve desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes.
Refrain from charging unrelated expenses to the PCF in order to avoid disallowance in audit and charge the unrelated expenses to the appropriate account and fund.

Stop the payment of Hazard Pay Allowance to MSDO personnel. Follow strictly the provision of Section 16.10 of RA No. 9433, the laws, rules and regulations in payment of hazard pay allowance to employees.

However, we recommended that documentations should have to be attached to the claims that exposure to occupational risks really occurred to further justify the payment of hazard pay.

Direct Management to completely adhere to the guidelines set by DSWD in the implementation of the AICS program. If possible, a resolution or executive order can be issued by the LGU to set a clear-cut, systematic and uniform rules and procedures of the AICS which must be aligned with the above-cited guidelines.

Instruct the LCPC Secretariat to exert effort in the establishment and maintenance of a complete database for children in order to create an effective/responsive LCPC PAPs for the best interest of the children.

Direct the concerned officials and employees to place the eight (8) computer units at the appropriate and safe area. Likewise, fast track the utilization of the purchased IT equipment according to its intended purpose.

(b) the Municipal Accountant to immediately reconcile these reciprocal

record the SET receivables for CY 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the accounting office should update the Property, Plant and Equipment Ledger , Cards. Likewise, the municipal treasurer’s office should maintain property cards. Accordingly, the two offices should make a periodic reconciliation of these records. Moreover, the inventory committee shall conduct a reliable physical inventory count at least once a year to determine which of their properties are actually existing. A team composed of the General Services and Accounting Department personnel should conduct the physical inventory count and reconcile the book and property records. Accordingly, the Accounting Department shall effect the correcting entries, where applicable, when properly supported with documentation. Finally, management should drop the unserviceable properties from the inventory reports and dispose the same in accordance with the procedures outlined above.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Accounting, Treasury, Engineering Offices and the BAC to submit the documents relevant to the granted loan to the Municipality and to the implementation of the subject project.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to immediately return the unutilized PDAF to the National Treasury.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

direct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to monitor the effective and efficient implementation of the projects under the 20% Development Fund so that timely necessary interventions by the implementing offices may be made for their timely completion; 

ensure that development projects for inclusion in the 20% Development Fund have all gone thorough study and priority ranking including coordination with other LGUs/agencies to avoid double funding; and 

continue to invest in high–impact or developmental projects for inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan.

We recommended that:

the BAC members, Secretariat, units/officials who prepare the bidding documents and draft invitation to bid/request for expression of interest discuss, confirm and settle the procurement requirements of the agency, taking into consideration the inputs and comments of the implementing unit, before proceeding to the posting/publication of invitation to bid and the holding of the public bidding;

the BAC Chairman communicate in writing the agency’s demand from the contractor to complete its deliverables for a hybrid type of solar power system and set reasonable timeline within which the contractor is expected to complete its contractual obligations; and

the Mayor (i) require the BAC Secretariat and Technical Working Group to prepare a verified report showing the actual facts as would prove the delay in the performance of contractual obligation by the contractor in order to determine the propriety of imposing liquidated damages upon the contractor; (ii) if warranted by the verified report, instruct the Municipal Treasurer to deduct from any money due or which may become due the contractor and collect such liquidated damages from the retention money or other securities posted by the contractor whichever is more convenient to the procuring entity in compliance with Annex D or Annex E of the revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184; and (iii) in the succeeding projects of the agency, ensure that the implementing unit strictly monitors the faithful compliance by contractors with their deliverables.

We recommended that the Municipality continue implementing PPAs under the SEF and, if possible, maximize the utilization of the available P1,544,418.33 cash of the Fund as at year-end. We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Accounting, Treasury, Engineering Offices, the BAC and its Secretariat and other concerned offices to be aware of the issues observed and henceforth, prevent the same. Further, we recommended that the Official Receipt for the P15,000.00 Press Conference registration fee be submitted to the Audit Team.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the BAC and its Secretariat, the Accounting Office and the Engineering Office to submit the lacking documents.

We recommended that:

the BAC members, Secretariat and TWG (i) strictly observe the provisions of the procurement law and (ii) explain the reason for not complying with the pertinent sections of R.A. No. 9184 as discussed in paragraph 24.2;

the Mayor, Municipal Accountant, BAC members, Secretariat and TWG explain the reason for the noted breakdown in the internal control system of the agency as discussed in paragraph 24.5; and

the Municipal Administrator and the Municipal Agriculturist complete the mechanics necessary to operate and manage the harvester so that the purpose of the project, which is to help farmers increase their income, will be soonest achieved.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to evaluate and compare the amount of collectibles and payables with the records of the Provincial Government to ascertain the legality and validity of both claims. If claims are fully documented and establishes regularity of settlements/collections should be made by the Municipality. For those undocumented collectibles, where after all diligent efforts that had been exhausted by the Municipal Accountant still disclosed zero basis, then proper procedures leading to write-off of these accounts may be considered. As to the undocumented payables, where after conferring with the Provincial Accountant showed the absence of any documents to prove validity of claims, then the Municipal Accountant may seek approval from the Sangguniang Bayan for the reversion of these undocumented obligations to surplus accounts, where it could be used as a source of funds for succeeding appropriation to some municipal programs or projects. Said remedial measures would eliminate the books of accounts of balances that are not fully supported with valid documents to warrant continued fair presentation in the financial statements of the Municipality of San Jose.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant submitted a reply letter on the AOM dated July 19, 2017 and received on the same date.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer to reconcile the differences noted in order to arrive at the correct balance of the account so as to ensure fairness in the presentation of accounts and maintain integrity of information being reported in the financial statements of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Accounting Office was made by the OIC-MGSO per letter and attachment dated July 19, 2017 and received on the same date.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure full compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002, whereby the appropriation for 30% and Quick Response Fund be made in lump-sum appropriation, and that if remained unexpended at year-end be transferred in full to the Special Trust account of the Trust Fund books.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Budget Officer immediately submitted a reply letter on the AOM dated July 19, 2017 and received on the same date.

observations.recommendation: We strongly recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal OIC-General Services Officer to continue and expedite the process by which these unserviceable properties could be disposed of, in accordance with the said aforecited rules and regulations, so that the balances of Property, Plant and Equipment accounts presented in the financial statements would only represent those assets that are in existence and servicing the needs of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-MGSO implemented together with the Appraisal and Disposal Committee is in the course of appraising the unserviceable properties as stated on their letter dated July 21, 2017 and received by your office on July 24, 2017. A request letter dated September 7, 2017 for inspection was submitted and received on September 8, 2017.
observations.reason: A request letter dated September 7, 2017 for inspection was submitted and received on September 8, 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Local Accountant to on a monthly basis ensure full compliance with the Report on the Utilization of the LDRRMF and that the Municipal DRRM Officer (MDRRMO) to submit the same to the COA Audit Team within 15 days after the end of the preceding month as prescribed by the above regulation for monitoring and appropriate action of the concerned Office.
observations.managementAction: Immediate compliance that this will not happen again in the future, per letter addressed to the Office of the Audit Team Leader dated July 19, 2017 and received on the same date.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management insure all the properties of the Municipality with the General Insurance Fund of the GSIS, so that in case of loss or destruction of these properties, the Municipality could be compensated and recover the cost of these assets. We also recommended that the funds needed for the insurance premiums be included in the annual budget of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: The OIC-MGSO, on his letter dated July 21, 2017 and received on July 24, 2017 stated compliance on the AOM thru budget inclusion.
