observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Inventory Committee conduct the physical count of all PPE and Inventories, submit a complete and accurate report thereon, copy furnish the Municipal Accountant to facilitate reconciliation with accounting records. 

We further recommended that the OIC-General Service Officer maintain complete and updated perpetual inventory records which should always reconcile with the PPE ledger cards. Further, the Accounting Office and the GSO were likewise advised to maintain Supplies Ledger Cards and stock cards, respectively, to monitor receipt and issuance of Inventories. Any discrepancies between these records should be properly examined to facilitate adjustment of the affected Inventory and PPE account balances for their fair presentation in the Statement of Financial Position.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee conducted partial physical count of Municipality’s property, plant and equipment at the end of the quarter of CY 2017 and the report thereon was submitted to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.reason: Lack of man power to conduct complete physical count of all PPE including the Local Road Networks of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant (a) record the completed public infrastructure in CYs 2015 and 2016 totaling P77,654,472.53 to proper Infrastructure Assets accounts; and (b) comply with the transitional provisions regarding transfer of public infrastructure from the RPI to the books of accounts as prescribed under COA Circular Nos. 2015-008 and 2016-004 and pursuant to PPSAS 17 for the fair presentation of infrastructure assets in the financial statements. 

We likewise recommended for the conduct of physical inventory of local roads under the Municipality’s jurisdictions and reconcile inventory report with those recorded in the Registry of Public Infrastructures to ensure its accuracy and completeness.
observations.managementAction: Public Infrastructure from CY 2013 to 2017 were accounted in the books as Property, Plant and Equipment.
observations.reason: Lack of man power to conduct complete physical count including the Local Road Networks of the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that (a) the Municipal Treasurer submit certified list of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible to the OIC – Municipal Accountant as basis for setting up the RPT and SET Receivable at the beginning of the year; and (b) the OIC – Municipal Accountant.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer submitted the certified list of taxpayers to the Municipal Accountant which was used as basis for recording RPT/SET receivables and deferred credits in the books of accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant immediately effect the necessary adjusting entries in the books of accounts in order to reflect the true financial condition of the Municipality. Prospectively, record financial transactions and operations conformably with the PPSAS and in accordance with pertinent laws and regulations.
observations.managementAction: JEV No. 118-2017-01-01 was prepared to adjust the error in the recording of income under the 20% DF amounting to P7,777,745.50.
observations.reason: The Municipal Treasurer’s Office and the Municipal Accounting Office failed to reconcile their records regarding the unpaid taxes of OMECO due from them.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) analyze and reconcile the Due from Other Funds and Due To Other Funds to determine the discrepancies between these reciprocal accounts including the reversing entries made on the outstanding cash advances of the former Municipal Accountant; and (b) make the necessary corrections/adjustments for the fair presentation of the affected account balances in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Correcting entries have already been made on the cash advances of the former Municipal Accountant under JEV No. 10 dated January 31, 2017.

Notice to Liquidate has been issued to persons with cash advances from May 15 to June 20, 2017. Adjusting/correcting entries have been made to reclassify the collection of income taxes from Sangguniang Bayan Members. The Municipal Accounting Office and the Treasurer's Office reconcile their records and submitted the lacking documents.
observations.reason: Lapses in the internal control system in processing, granting, and liquidation of cash advances.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that Management exercise prudence in handling Municipal funds to prevent losses in operations pursuant to Section 2 of PD No. 1445 and to consider availability of funds before incurring obligations to maintain its ability to meet current obligations.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality’s performance for the CY 2017 has a surplus of P14,742,094.50 under the General Fund – Proper.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Inventory Committee to fast track the conduct of physical count and the preparation of the report thereon for submission to the GSIS to facilitate identification of insurable assets and comply with Property Insurance Law to indemnify or compensate the government in case of loss or damage to its properties in case of fire, earthquake, storm and other calamities.
observations.managementAction: 15 motor vehicles of the Municipality were insured with GSIS in September 2017 for one year. The OIC – GSO already went to the GSIS Office to inquire about the process on how to insured buildings and other insurable assets of the Municipality.
observations.reason: No budget for insurance of buildings and other insurable assets in CY 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Members of the Municipal Local School Board (a) prepare a balanced budget that is compliant with the legal and regulatory requirements on SEF budgeting; (b) refrain from accommodating expenditures on activities for which the DepEd has the direct mandate to execute; (c) request for inputs and feedbacks from the DepEd officials/School District Supervisors in the prioritization of SEF projects and expenditures and ensure that PPAs are in accordance with the DepEd criteria and the provisions of laws, rules and regulations prescribing the limitations on the use of the SEF; (d) maximize the utilization of SEF to attain its primary goal and objectives; (e) comply with the requirement of DILG Memorandum Circular 2012-141 on the posting of SEF utilization report 20 days after the end of each quarter for disclosure to the general public; and (f) monitor closely the implementation of the PPAs and take appropriate remedies, such as realignment or re-programming of funds, if necessary, thereby retaining in the LSB Budget only those relevant and priority activities in consultation with the school heads/project proponents.
observations.managementAction:
Local School Board Budget was in line with DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 01, s. of 2017. 

The non-priority expenditures was significantly reduced from last year’s P1,656,000.00 P221,377.80 this year.
observations.reason: P221,377.80 worth of disbursement were not in line with DepEd-DBM-DILG JC No. 01, s. of 2017. Related discussion in Observation No. 11, Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) prioritize allocation of funds  for the effective implementation of the Ecological Solid Waste Management Plan especially on the construction of Materials Recovery Facility and conversion of its open dumpsite to long term disposal facility or sanitary landfill to ensure the health and safety of the people and the environment; and (b) coordinate with the Barangay Officials concerned in the implementation of MRF in all barangays within their jurisdiction in consonance with RA No. 9003.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor issued a Memorandum Circular and conducted several meeting with all Punong Barangays under his jurisdiction regarding the establishment of their own MRF. The 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan was already prepared and submitted to National Solid Waste Commission for approval.
observations.reason: The market value of lots within the urban Barangays of the Municipality is significantly high. Their yearly budget was inadequate to procure lots for the establishment of their own MRF.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the (a) BAC ensure that procurement of infrastructure projects are made strictly in accordance with the requirements of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR; (b) OIC – Municipal Accountant ensure that all claims are duly supported with proper and complete documentary requirements before these are processed for payment to establish the regularity and legality of the disbursements; and (c) Municipal Engineer strictly monitor the implementation of projects to ensure their timely completion to enable early enjoyment of benefits by the intended beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction: The BAC tried their best to observe the rules and regulations on the procurement of infrastructure projects.
observations.reason: Related discussion in Observation No. 6, Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that concerned officials (a) ensure that the detailed engineering investigation, surveys and designs for the projects are carried out and duly approved in accordance with the standards and specifications prescribed by the HOPE and in accordance with Annex A of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 before bidding and award of the contract; and (b)refrain from issuing suspension orders just to cover up the real cause of slippage or negligence on the part of the Management or contractor.
observations.managementAction: The BAC tried their best to observe the rules and regulation on the procurement of infrastructure projects.
observations.reason: Related discussion in Observation No. 6, Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the following courses of action (a) the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator ensure that (i) the programs, projects and activities to be funded out of the 20% Development Fund are specifically itemized with the complete details to facilitate proper monitoring and evaluation; (ii) project implementation should be in accordance with the approved AIP and APP; and (iii) projects charged against the 20% Development Fund partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures that will contribute to the attainment of socio-economic development and environmental management outcome; (b) the Municipal Budget Officer certify the existence of appropriation in the appropriate portion of the Obligation Request before processing the claims to ensure that funds are available for the purposes; (c) the OIC-Municipal Accountant submit the 37 disbursement vouchers and its supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor for verification  and audit; and (d) the Municipal Budget Officer and the OIC-Municipal Accountant reconcile their records regularly to ensure the correctness and accuracy of reported data/information and enhance the reliability of their respective reports.
observations.managementAction: 31 lacking disbursement vouchers were submitted by the Municipal Accountant to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.reason: Inaction on the part of the concerned Officials. Related discussion in Observation No. 12, Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following courses of actions:
 
• The Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer prepare a Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Investment Plan following the prescribed format in Annex A of COA Circular No. 2012-002 indicating the expected output per project/activity and include under a separate caption the list of projects and activities chargeable against the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years;

• The Municipal Mayor ensure optimal utilization of LDRRMF, especially Mitigation Fund, to strengthen the local communities’ resilience to future disasters/calamities and see to it that LDRRMF is used solely for disaster risk reduction activities; and

• The OIC-Municipal Accountant submit the lacking disbursement vouchers and supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor for verification and audit.
observations.managementAction: Two lacking disbursement vouchers were submitted to the office of the Auditor.
observations.reason: Lack of quorum during LDRRM Council meetings. Related discussion in Observation No. 13, Part II of this Report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that once the finances of the Municipality improved, Management cause the immediate remittance of the unutilized balance of PDAF totaling P12,085,531.60 to the Bureau of Treasury in strict compliance with the Supreme Court ruling.
observations.managementAction: P235,086.35 worth of PDAF were already reverted back to the Bureau of Treasury in CY 2017
observations.reason: Lack of available cash.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor exercise his primordial responsibility to require that past transactions and financial reports of calendar years 1994 to 2002 be reported to the Audit Team by the concerned officials.  The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer must render once and for all the actual and true status of financial accounts of the Municipality.  Moreover, the responsible officials should be held liable under the Revised Penal Code for their failure to render the accounts and reporting thereof.
observations.managementAction: The present responsible Officials and Employees had already exerted extra effort but still no documents were found.
observations.reason: No financial reports pertaining to years 1994 to 2002 were submitted to the Audit Team due to unavailability of the related documents needed in the preparation of the reports.

observations.recommendation:
Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that: 

a.  the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to exert extra effort to account for the unreconciled items appearing in the Bank Reconciliation Statements to faithfully present the correct balances of the account for the period;




b.	the Municipal Accountant prepare the Monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements for all bank accounts and submit same regularly together with the paid checks, debit/credit memos for verification as provided for under Section 74 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 96-011. Continue securing bank statements of prior years to establish the correct balance of the Cash in Bank; and 
 
c.   the Municipal Accountant prepare subsidiary ledgers of all the bank accounts.
observations.managementAction:
The Municipal Treasurer was advised by the LCE to make a request to DBP Kalibo and LBP Odiongan for previous years’ copy of Bank Statements.




The Municipal Accountant-designee requested for the temporary Bank Statements from DBP Kalibo.




The Municipal Accountant had already prepared the subsidiary ledgers of all the bank accounts
observations.reason:
The unreconciled items amounting to P13,690,129.80 still exist in the Bank Reconciliation Statements submitted to the Audit Team as of December 31, 2017. Inquiry with the Municipal Accountant Designee revealed that no request yet has been made to the DBP Kalibo and LBP Odiongan for previous years’ copy of Bank Statements.



The Municipal Accountant commented that she regularly requests for the temporary copy of Bank Statements to DBP Kalibo.  However, delay was still incurred in the submission of Bank Reconciliation Statements.  Also, paid checks were not yet attached.





Subsidiary Ledgers of all bank accounts were already prepared according to the Municipal Accountant but not yet submitted to the Audit Team, thus verification cannot be made yet.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

require the Inventory Committee to conduct the complete physical count of all Inventories and Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) accounts of the Municipality so that the corresponding reports can be submitted to the Audit Team; 
	
thereafter, instruct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the resulting balance of the physical count with the accounting records. In the event that deficiencies/discrepancies exist between the Inventory Report and accounting records, the necessary adjustments/corrections should be made; and

require the Municipal Treasurer to make a report of   unserviceable/non-functional PPEs in the Inspection and Inventory Report of Unserviceable Properties (IIRUP) which could be undertaken simultaneously with the physical inventory for disposal and eventual proper relief of accountabilities.
observations.managementAction: The municipality had already prepared and submitted the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories and Report on the Physical Count of PPE.  Likewise, the   resulting balances of the physical count were reconciled with the accounting records.

observations.recommendation: Considering the foregoing, we recommended that the BAC strictly observe proper procedures in all its procurement activities. observations.recommendation:

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the reconciliation of PPE balances on both records of the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer. Regular conduct of reconciliation of property records by both municipal officials shall be strictly implemented and the necessary adjustments, if any, shall be effected in the books of accounts. Compliance to the accounting rules and regulations shall be strictly observed to avoid any misstatement in the financial statements. Unserviceable properties shall likewise be inventoried and inspected. These shall be appraised and be subsequently disposed following the procedures and modes of disposal prescribed in the Manual on Disposal issued by this Commission.

Moreover, we recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to maintain PC and PPELC, respectively, for all items under PPE. Periodic reconciliation of records shall be undertaken to avoid any misstatement in the quantity and amount of inventories and properties.

Justification on the above violations and deficiencies shall be submitted to the COA, and a copy of the said MOA and resolution, including other legal bases to support the payments, shall be attached thereto. 

Furthermore, require that all payments of honoraria be taxed and accordingly withheld, and be remitted to the BIR.

Should all efforts fail, request for a write-off to the COA of the subject account and support the letter of request with (a) proof or evidences that could substantiate efforts exerted to review and validate the account, and enforce collection thereof; (b) list of available records and extent of validation made on the said account; and (c) a certification ascertaining the reasons why pertinent records and documents could not be located, as the case may be.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the reconciliation of PPE balances on both records of the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer. Moreover, direct the Municipal Accountant to effect the necessary adjustments in reclassifying small tangible items as adjustment to Government Equity using the Prior Years’ Adjustment account. Unserviceable properties shall likewise be inventoried and inspected, and be reclassified to Other Assets. These shall be appraised and be subsequently disposed following the procedures and modes of disposal prescribed in the Manual on Disposal issued by this Commission.

Regular conduct of reconciliation of property records by both municipal officials shall be strictly implemented and the necessary adjustments, if any, shall be effected in the books of accounts. Compliance to the accounting rules and regulations shall be strictly observed to avoid any misstatement in the financial statements.

Moreover, we recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to maintain PC and PPELC, respectively, for all items under PPE. Periodic reconciliation of records shall be undertaken to avoid any misstatements in the quantity and amount of inventories and properties.
observations.managementAction: Reconciliationwas already done by both the Municipal Accountant and the Property Officer.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to stop the practice of charging expenditures that are not related to projects on social and economic development, and environmental outcomes, and do not partake the nature of investment/capital expenditures to the 20% development fund. Strictly adhere to the guidelines set under Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 of the DBM and DILG in the appropriation and utilization of the 20% Development Fund to ensure its optimal utilization.

Furthermore, we recommended that the Construction Period Theory be followed in its application for costing purposes on construction of agency assets and public infrastructures. During the construction period, these shall be taken up in the books as "Construction in Progress” with the appropriate asset classification.  As soon as the project is completed, the Construction in Progress for agency asset shall be closed to the appropriate asset account.

Likewise, expenditures such as payment of labor, direct materials and overhead pertaining to the construction, rehabilitation or repair of agency assets/government structures, which could be an addition, could contribute to the betterment, or could extend the life of such assets, should be capitalized. Also, use of appropriate accounts in recording fund transfers and other transactions shall be made to clearly disclose the nature of the transactions actually undertaken by the Municipality to avoid inaccurately reported account balances and misleading information in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Incurrence of operating expenses not chargeable against the 20% DF were eliminated.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Treasurer to retrieve all stale checks and the corresponding DVs together with all supporting documents, and furnish the Municipal Accountant of the said documents for the preparation of the journal entry voucher.  Replacement checks shall be issued following the procedures in accordance with the above provision of the MNGAS, Vol. I.

Furthermore, monitoring of outstanding checks shall be conducted monthly by both the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant. Notices to payees who have custody of checks, which will become stale, shall be sent by the Municipal Treasurer at least one (1) month before the same become stale to inform them of the status thereof.
observations.managementAction: Made adjustments to various recovered stale checks.

Only those projects that can contribute to the attainment of socio-economic and environmental development of the municipality as defined under the cited joint circular be funded out the 20% IRA.
observations.reason:
Expense items not related to development projects were still included in CY 2016 20% development fund budget
The finding was first raised in CY 2014 AAR

observations.recommendation: 7. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive always ensure that only eligible NGOs/POs will be granted cash advances thru strict compliance to documentary requirements enumerated under COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.  Also, always ensure that only projects/activities within the capability of the NGO/PO and in line with the SEF priority expenses as provided under DECS-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 01, 01-A, and 01-B are granted to NGO/PO. Further, cause the liquidation of the above subject cash advances and stop releasing funds to NGOs/POs with unliquidated cash advances.
observations.reason: CY 2016 Special Education Fund transfers to NGOs/POs were not supported with complete required documentations. Only project proposals and Memorandum of Agreement were attached to the voucher.

observations.recommendation: 10. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor secure the necessary travel authority from the Governor everytime he travels outside of his official station pursuant to Section 96 of R.A. 7160.  Also, persons responsible submit to the office of the Auditor lacking local travel documents enumerated above in compliance with Section 1.2.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 for local travels listed in Annex 3 of this report.
observations.reason:
The required documents were not submitted to the Auditor
The finding was first raised in CY 2013 AAR.

observations.recommendation: 11. We recommended that the provisions of Local Budget Circular No. 103 dated May 15, 2014 on the grant of transportation allowance be strictly observed. Ensure that claims of this nature shall bear the required supporting documents. We also recommend that persons responsible submit to the Office of the Auditor the lacking documentary requirements to support each claim of transportation allowance.
observations.reason: The required documents were not submitted to the Auditor.

observations.recommendation: 13. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer or concerned Department Head timely inform the Auditor through a letter that the appropriate signboard is already posted after award or before the start of a program/activity. Strict compliance to the prohibition on the display/affixture of items related to government officials on signboards and on maintaining and updating the project status portion of the signboards as provided in COA Circular No. 2013-004 likewise be observed.
observations.reason: Letter from the Municipal Mayor informing the auditor that the appropriate signboard is already posted was not received in CY 2016.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend the following:

19. Waste materials and unserviceable properties should be properly disposed according to applicable rules and regulations on supply and property management in LGUs.

20. The Municipal Mayor should constitute a Disposal Committee who will appraise the value of supplies or property to be disposed and require the Municipal General Services Officer to spearhead the disposal of waste materials and unserviceable properties of the Municipality.
21. Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) and Waste Materials Reports (WMR) should be diligently prepared, copies of which should be submitted to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.reason:
Preparation of required reports is on-going.  

Fully Implemented





Not Implemented
IIRUP and WMR were not prepared and submitted.

observations.recommendation: 36. We recommend that management strictly adhere to the provisions of RA 10121 and its IRR, and COA Circular 2012-002 in the accounting and reporting of the LDRRMF. The unexpended LDRRMF should be transferred to a special trust fund in the Trust Fund Books of the LGU. The Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF should be timely submitted to the Office of the Auditor monthly.
observations.reason: Only a Special Trust Fund in the General Fund was established. No separate Trust Fund books and bank account.

observations.recommendation: 37. We recommend that The Chief Executive issue a policy and the Sanggunian to pass a resolution with regard to mobile telephone usage and expenditures, review and set allocated amount of mobile telephone expenses allowed to elected and appointed officials with justifications, and course the procurement of cellphone call cards through the BAC and be guided with the provisions of RA 9184 and its revised IRR.
observations.reason: Disbursements of cellphone load allowances were still made thru reimbursement basis.

observations.recommendation: 39. We recommend that prudence and judiciousness should always be observed in the disbursement of funds for travelling and training expenses. Only necessary travels and trainings should be allowed.  The Human Resources and Development Office under the Municipal General Services Office should develop an annual comprehensive training and development plan for all the employees of the Municipality which will be the basis of allowed trainings and seminars for the year.
observations.reason: The Municipality has no annual comprehensive training program.

observations.recommendation: 40. We recommend that the Municipal Accountant investigate and exert extra efforts to review all the documents which are available in his office. In addition, he should interview some of the personnel who are still assigned in the Office before his assumption in order to get some information regarding these undocumented accounts.
observations.reason: These accounts remain unsupported by subsidiary ledgers.

observations.recommendation: 41. We recommend that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal General Services Officer be directed to strictly adhere to the accounting policies on inventories as provided for in the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs). The Perpetual Inventory System should be strictly followed as well as the Moving Average Method in costing inventories. Stock Cards, Supplies Ledger Cards and the Physical Count of Inventories should be maintained and reconciled. Physical count of inventories should be conducted semestrally and a report thereof should be made.
observations.reason:
The Perpetual Inventory System is not followed
The finding was first raised in CY 2008 AAR.

observations.recommendation: 43. We recommend that the Municipal Accountant, and other concerned department heads and LGU officials in charge of the disbursement and implementation of the funds require the recipient NGO/PO – SAME to comply with the requirements of COA Circular No. 96-003 as amended by COA Circular No. 2007-001 and to ascertain the propriety of expenditures as specified in the Memorandum of Agreement.
observations.reason: SAME has not submitted the required documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that management immediately dispose all unserviceable properties in accordance with the provisions of Section 79 of P. D. 1445.
observations.managementAction: AAR Management will create inspectorate team to inspect/evaluate unserviceable properties before its disposal.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the requirement on the submission of financial reports and transaction documents as provided in Section 6.05 of COA Circular 95-006 and submit financial reports and documents on or before due dates to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.managementAction: Financial reports and transaction documents were still submitted late.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to strictly comply with the requirement on the submission of financial reports and transaction documents as provided in Section 6.05 of COA Circular 95-006 and submit financial reports and documents on or before due dates to the Office of the Auditor.
observations.managementAction: All DVs and financial reports were submitted late.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management evaluate and monitor the status of implementation of the Kilos Asenso Program, and if the program is already completed, return the unused balance to the national government.
observations.managementAction: None.
observations.reason: The department concerned had no opportunity to implement any project under the program because of insufficient funds. Management is considering on returning the funds.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management install electric meters in places where a flat rate is being applied by NORECO II. We also recommended that management require the barangays to appropriate an amount for electricity expenses in their annual budgets, and oblige these barangays to pay for their own electric consumption.
observations.reason: The Municipality still paid for the electricity expenses of the barangays. In order to cover for the payment of such expenses, prior period obligations were authorized through an SB resolution in a supplemental budget.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Municipal Accountant to review the composition of payables which have been outstanding for 2 years an above amounting to ₱679,131.58 in the General Fund as of December 31, 2014 and prepare the necessary adjustments, where applicable, so that the payable accounts will be fairly presented in the financial statements.  In coordination with concerned officials, he shall verify the real status of the projects/activities relating to the funds Due to NGAs and Due to LGUs.

Accordingly, management shall adopt measures to ensure the immediate implementation or liquidation of the financial assistance from these agencies upon the completion of the intended purposes and impose the return of any unused balances upon completion of the project/program.  Otherwise, if there are any priority projects that need additional funding, request for authority to use the balances.  For unimplemented projects, evaluate whether the programs/activities are still needed, if so, pursue its implementation as soon as possible.
observations.reason: Management has yet to find and set aside time in evaluating these liabilities. However, for the due to NGAs and LGUs accounts, management is planning to return the unutilized funds.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to submit immediately the five (5) disbursement vouchers together with the supporting documents totaling R70,424.00, otherwise these shall be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that Management create a committee on appraisal and disposal of properties to facilitate the immediate disposal of the aforementioned unserviceable properties and waste materials to recoup any salvage value thereof.
observations.reason: The Inventory of unserviceable property is not yet completed.

observations.recommendation: niang Bayan enact an ordinance to update the Revenue Code and every five years thereafter in accordance with RA 7160, as basis for the collection of taxes, fees and charges.
observations.reason: The rates of fees, charge and taxes are still in the process of updating

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Management:
create an inventory committee to conduct physical count of property, plant and equipment annually and submit results thereon to the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year;
maintain property records in accordance with the New Government Accounting Manual to establish custodianship and assign accountability; and
determine unserviceable properties and recommend their immediate disposal to avoid continuous deterioration or disparagement.

We recommended that the City Treasurer: (i) furnish the City Accountant with certified list of taxpayers with stated amount due and collectible for the year which would be the basis of the latter to set-up RPT/SET receivables at the beginning of the accounting period; (ii) reconstruct his records on delinquent taxpayers and maintain those relevant information.
We also recommended that the City Treasurer and the Accountant to reconcile their records pertaining to balances of RPT/SET receivables.

We recommended that the officials concerned comply with the audit rules relevant to documentary requirements to avoid audit suspension and/or disallowance.  Further, the City Accountant should be more prudent in processing transactions and certifying that the supporting documents are complete.

We recommended that the LGU require the concerned officers to submit the copies of approved purchase orders, contracts and delivery receipts to the Audit Team within the period prescribed by existing rules and regulations and impose administrative disciplinary action as therein provided against the concerned employee with unjustified failure of compliance.

For the foregoing observations, we recommended that the City Accountant draw Journal Entry Voucher to (i) record items for adjustment in the books including the cancellation of stale checks and recognizing them as accounts payable; (ii) ensure that subsequent adjustments should be taken up in the books immediately; and (ii) reconcile her cash records with those maintained in the City Treasurer.

We recommended that the Management:
strictly observe the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002 in the granting, utilization and reporting of cash advances and exert prudent efforts to require immediate settlement/liquidation of the cash advances;
refrain from granting of additional cash advances to official/employee unless their previous cash advances were liquidated; and
demand the immediate settlement or liquidation from accountable officers and in case of patent disregard and refusal of the accountable officers in settling their obligations or accounts, the following remedial actions may be resorted to:
withhold the salaries and/or other benefits as provided for under Section 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002; and
file appropriate charges/cases, if warranted.

We recommended and the Management agreed to continue to observe              Section 324 (b) of the Local Government Code in the availment of credit facilities to augment its finances in the execution of its development programs and socio-economic activities.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
continue to adopt a systematic, comprehensive and ecological solid waste Management program which shall ensure the protection of public health and environment as required under Republic Act           No. 9003; and
continue to ensure that proper segregation, collection, transport, storage, treatment and disposal of solid waste through the formulation and adoption of the best environmental practices in ecological waste Management are observed.

We recommended that the City market officials exert extra effort to intensify collection of delinquent accounts; ensure that the lessees comply faithfully with their obligations of paying stall rentals in accordance with the contracts; and impose the corresponding penalties and sanctions against erring stall occupants as provided in the City Ordinance No. 11-063.
We recommended and Management agreed to continue comply with the prompt withholding and remittance of taxes due to BIR in conformity with Revenue Regulations No. 2-98 dated May 17, 1998.

We recommended and Management agreed to continue compliance with   Section 6(b) of Republic Act No. 8291, otherwise known as GSIS Act of 1997 which mandates that the monthly personal share of employees and the contribution for employer’s share covered by GSIS shall be remitted within the first 10 days of the calendar month to which the contribution apply.
An in-depth verification should be made on the Due to GSIS account under the Trust Fund account and effect the necessary adjustment where appropriate.

We recommended and Management agreed to continue the implementation of projects and activities related to GAD in order to ensure the promotion of public welfare and basic services addressing gender issues in accordance with the requirements of the Joint Memorandum Circular above cited.

We recommended that the City ensure that: (i) the 20% Development Fund is optimally utilized in delivering priority programs and projects predetermined and included in the AIP and annual budget; and (ii) only programs projects funded by the annual appropriation ordinance are to be implemented and accomplished during the budget year.

We recommended that the City Local School Board prepare a balanced SEF budget and implement PPAs with conscious and concerted efforts of observing compliance with the legal and regulatory requirement and refrain from accommodating expenditures for activities for which the DepEd has the direct mandate to execute.

We recommended that Management, through the City Accountant, continue to enforce the settlement of the suspensions and disallowances, both for the current and prior years, from concerned personnel liable and responsible thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit intact all collections remitted to her by the liquidating officer/collectors including her own collections to ensure that these will not be used for personal and other matters.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Treasury and Accounting Offices to locate the supporting documents of the checks presented on the table above and henceforth, make sure that every check prepared has an approved disbursement voucher.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the PCF Custodian strictly follow the provision of Section 48 Volume I of the NGAS for LGUs regarding the maintenance of PCF and in the corresponding recording of pertinent transactions under the imprest system.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer faithfully adhere to the provisions of Section 175, Volume I of the GAAM and COA Circular No. 2012-001 regarding the withdrawal of cash advance for payroll fund to control utilization of the meager resource of the Municipality.

We reiterated the audit recommendation that the Municipal Accountant require the concerned accountable officers to immediately settle their unliquidated cash advances by submitting the liquidation report and settlement documents to the Accounting Office and/or by returning the unexpended balance of the cash advance to the Municipal Treasurer. We further recommended the application of Section 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002 which states that the failure of the accountable officer to liquidate his cash advance within the prescribed period shall constitute a valid cause for the withholding of his salary.

We reiterated the audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive ensure the submission by the Municipal Accountant of all disbursement vouchers for confidential and intelligence cash advance, with complete required supporting documents in a sealed envelope to the ATL within seven working days from release of the check, as well as the liquidation thereof in accordance with Section 6.2 of the Joint Circular. Further, we recommended that the granted cash advance of P1,000,000.00 should not be used for reimbursement of paid expenses prior to the grant of cash advance.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
instruct the Treasury Office to prepare and furnish the Accounting Office the list of required under Section 20 Volume I of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs; and
instruct the Municipal Accountant to base the establishment of RPT and SET receivables on the list submitted by the Treasury Office for an accurate recognition of the said receivables; and make the necessary adjustments in the recording of the RPT/SET Receivables in CY 2016.

We reiterated the audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive create an inventory team to conduct actual physical count of all PPE of the Municipality in order to determine the existence and pinpoint the person/s responsible for the said assets.  The Inventory Team shall prepare and submit the result of the physical inventory that will be used by the Municipal Treasurer to start Property Cards. Consequently, require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile their records. Further, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Treasurer to prepare, maintain and monitor AREs for every PPE issued to end users.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive furnish the Accounting Office of the Memorandum of Agreement with the PCSO and other pertinent documents to the received ambulances for the latter to record the same. Moreover, we recommended that the Accounting Office record the DILG funded dump truck in the General Fund books of accounts, and adjust corresponding depreciation. Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant review the disbursements from the Trust Fund in previous years and effect adjustments of accounts, if warranted.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Accounting, Treasury, Engineering Offices and the BAC to submit the documents relevant to the granted loan to the Municipality and to the implementation of the subject project.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Human Resources and Management Officer, the Accounting Office and other concerned offices to submit the supporting documents as required by COA Circular No. 2012-001.
We recommended that the Accounting Office adjust the accounting books and henceforth, record the subject benefit with the proper account.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, in coordination with the Sangguniang Bayan, adopt and implement clear policies concerning the provision of reasonable cellphone load allowance taking into consideration the provision of Section 2 of P.D. No. 1445 requiring the efficient and economical use of government resources. Non-implementation of the audit recommendation would compel the Audit Team to disallow the subject expense in the course of audit.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to immediately return the unutilized PDAF to the National Treasury.

We reiterated the previous audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive, the Municipal Accountant, and the Municipal Treasurer seriously address the persisting issue on the delay in the submission of mandatory reports to COA by requiring that only those disbursement vouchers with complete supporting documents are processed and paid.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) direct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to monitor the effective and efficient implementation of the projects under the 20% Development Fund so that timely necessary interventions by the implementing offices may be made for their timely completion; (b) ensure that development projects for inclusion in the 20% Development Fund have all gone thorough study and priority ranking including coordination with other LGUs/agencies to avoid double funding; and (c) continue to invest in high–impact or developmental projects for inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Accounting, Engineering and Planning Offices and the BAC and its Secretariat to submit the lacking documents and explain on the other observations. We further recommended that the same Offices be ordered to see to it that all necessary documents are attached to disbursement vouchers.

We recommended that the Municipality continue implementing PPAs under the SEF and, if possible, maximize the utilization of the available P1,544,418.33 cash of the Fund as at year-end. We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Accounting, Treasury, Engineering Offices, the BAC and its Secretariat and other concerned offices to be aware of the issues observed and henceforth, prevent the same. Further, we recommended that the Official Receipt for the P15,000.00 Press Conference registration fee be submitted to the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive –
instruct the Municipal Treasurer to prepare and issue Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) for every property issued to DepEd employees and instruct said employees to turn-over to the Municipality the issued properties upon transfer to other school or town; and
request the DepEd to inform the Municipality for every movement of DepEd personnel assigned in San Jose for the Municipal Treasurer to be able to monitor the proper turn-over of issued properties to the employees who will be moving out from a certain school.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the BAC and its Secretariat, the Accounting Office and the Engineering Office to submit the lacking documents.

We recommended that:
the BAC members, Secretariat and TWG (i) strictly observe the provisions of the procurement law and (ii) explain the reason for not complying with the pertinent sections of R.A. No. 9184 as discussed in paragraph 24.2;
the Mayor, Municipal Accountant, BAC members, Secretariat and TWG explain the reason for the noted breakdown in the internal control system of the agency as discussed in paragraph 24.5; and
the Municipal Administrator and the Municipal Agriculturist complete the mechanics necessary to operate and manage the harvester so that the purpose of the project, which is to help farmers increase their income, will be soonest achieved.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive undertake the necessary actions to encourage and/or request women representatives from the NGO members in the local development council to achieve the goal of having an equitable representation of women in all spheres of society particularly in the decision-making and policy-making processes in the government and to fully realize their role as agents of development.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) orient the Barangay VAW Desk officers on the preparation and submission of quarterly reports to the DILG and the MSWDO for the latter to take appropriate action based on the reports. We further recommended that the GFPS coordinate with other competent agencies on the conduct of formal training of the Barangay VAW Desk Officers.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive review the scholarship program of the Municipality and establish more detailed procedures to ensure the achievement of its objective which is to help poor but intellectually deserving students and addressing the problem on the out-of-school youth.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Sangguniang Bayan formulate the Municipality’s own implementing guidelines, policies and procedures regarding AICS, in conformity with the provisions set forth by the DSWD MC No. 02 S. 2014, as basis in the granting of financial or in-kind assistances to its constituents.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the GFPS to properly decide and plan PPAs that address GAD issues and concerns.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive –
instruct the Solid Waste Management Board to formulate, finalize and submit the Municipality’s Solid Waste Management Plan;
instruct concerned employees to operate the  MRF with compliance with R.A. No. 9003 to achieve optimum benefits; and
make order for the utilization of the equipments procured for their intended purposes, and the acquired dump truck for its solid waste management program.

We recommended that (a) the management ensure that claims against government funds be supported with the required documents provided under the abovementioned regulations; (b) the Municipal Accountant make sure that the transactions are supported with complete documentation before certifying and effecting payment in order to avoid suspension and /or disallowance of the transactions; and (c) the BAC and its Secretariat, TWG and other concerned offices to submit the lacking documents to the Audit Team.

We recommended that:
the Municipal Accountant oversee the preparation of the summary of remittance to warrant the completeness and full payment of premiums and loan amortizations  to GSIS; and
the Municipal Treasurer ensure the remittance of premiums and loan amortizations to GSIS within prescribed time to prevent payment of penalties.

We reiterated the audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned personnel of the Accounting and Treasury Offices to tally their records on taxes withheld before the due date for payment to avoid surcharge and penalties imposed by the BIR. We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer immediately remit the under-remittance of P1,069,516.86 to the BIR.
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the persons responsible and liable to fully settle their respective accountabilities.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to evaluate and compare the amount of collectibles and payables with the records of the Provincial Government to ascertain the legality and validity of both claims. If claims are fully documented and establishes regularity of transactions, then settlements/collections should be made by the Municipality.  For those undocumented collectibles, where after all diligent efforts that had been exhausted by the Municipal Accountant still disclosed zero basis, then proper procedures leading to write-off of these accounts may be considered.
As to the undocumented payables, where after conferring with the Provincial Accountant showed the absence of any documents to prove validity of claims, then the Municipal Accountant may seek approval from the Sangguniang Bayan for the reversion of these undocumented obligations to surplus accounts, where it could be used as a source of funds for succeeding appropriation to some municipal programs or projects.
Said remedial measures would eliminate the books of accounts of balances that are not fully supported with valid documents to warrant continued fair presentation in the financial statements of the Municipality of San Jose.

We recommended that the Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer to reconcile the differences noted in order to arrive at the correct balance of the account so as to ensure fairness in the presentation of accounts and maintain integrity of information being reported in the financial statements of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure full compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-002, whereby the appropriation for 30% Quick Response Fund be made in lump-sum appropriation, and that if remained unexpended at year-end be transferred in full to the Special Trust account of the Trust Fund books.  This is to ensure that all unexpended LDRRMF will become available for use in the disaster risk reduction and management activities as provided in the approved LDRRMF Investment Plan of the Municipality for the next five years.

We strongly recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal OIC-General Services Officer to continue and expedite the process by which these unserviceable properties could be disposed of, in accordance with the aforecited rules and regulations, so that the balances of Property, Plant and Equipment accounts presented in the financial statements would only represent those assets that are in existence and servicing the needs of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Local Accountant to prepare on a monthly basis the Report on the Utilization of the LDRRMF and the Municipal DRRM Officer (MDRRMO) to submit the same to the COA Audit Team within 15 days after the end of the preceding month as prescribed by the above regulation for monitoring and appropriate action of the concerned Office.

We recommended that Management insure all the physical properties of the Municipality with the General Insurance Fund of the GSIS, so that in case of loss or destruction of these properties, the Municipality could be compensated and recover the cost of these assets.
We also recommended that the funds needed for the insurance premiums be included in the annual budget of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee conduct the physical count of all PPE and Inventories, submit a complete and accurate report thereon, copy furnish the Municipal Accountant to facilitate reconciliation with accounting records.
We further recommended that the OIC-General Service Officer maintain complete and updated perpetual inventory records which should always reconcile with the PPE ledger cards. Further, the Accounting Office and the GSO were likewise advised to maintain Supplies Ledger Cards and stock cards, respectively, to monitor receipt and issuance of Inventories. Any discrepancies between these records should be properly examined to facilitate adjustment of the affected Inventory and PPE account balances for their fair presentation in the Statement of Financial Position.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Accountant (a) record the completed public infrastructure in CYs 2015 and 2016 totaling P77,654,472.53 to proper Infrastructure Assets accounts; and (b) comply with the transitional provisions regarding transfer of public infrastructure from the RPI to the books of accounts as prescribed under COA Circular Nos. 2015-008 and 2016-004 and pursuant to PPSAS 17 for the fair presentation of infrastructure assets in the financial statements.
We likewise recommended for the conduct of physical inventory of local roads under the Municipality’s jurisdictions and reconcile inventory report with those recorded in the Registry of Public Infrastructures to ensure its accuracy and completeness.

We recommended that (a) the Municipal Treasurer submit certified list of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible to the OIC – Municipal Accountant as basis for setting up the RPT and SET Receivable at the beginning of the year; and (b) the OIC – Municipal Accountant strictly follow the rules and regulations in accounting for setting-up and collection of real property tax/special education tax receivables for their fair presentation in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant immediately effect the necessary adjusting entries in the books of accounts in order to reflect the true financial condition of the Municipality. Prospectively, record financial transactions and operations conformably with the PPSAS and in accordance with pertinent laws and regulations.

We recommended that Management through the MDRRM Officer (a) ensure that all unexpended balance of the QRF and the DRRMF-Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses be transferred to Trust Liability-DRRM account in the Trust Fund books; and (b) see that LDRRMF is used solely for supporting disaster risk reduction and management programs/projects/ activities in accordance with RA No. 10121.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) analyze and reconcile the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds to determine the discrepancies between these reciprocal accounts including the reversing entries made on the outstanding cash advances of the former Municipal Accountant; and (b) make the necessary corrections/adjustments for the fair presentation of the affected account balances in the financial statements.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management exercise prudence in handling Municipal funds to prevent losses in operations pursuant to Section 2 of PD No. 1445 and to consider availability of funds before incurring obligations to maintain its ability to meet current obligations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record all fund transfers to implementing agencies as receivable account subject for liquidation and to recognize expenses only after its liquidation to ensure that public funds are used for its intended purpose.  We also recommended that fund transfers be supported with documentary requirements provided under Section 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to establish the regularity of the disbursements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Inventory Committee to fast track the conduct of physical count and the preparation of the report thereon for submission to the GSIS to facilitate identification of insurable assets and comply with Property Insurance Law to indemnify or compensate the government in case of loss or damage to its properties in case of fire, earthquake, storm and other calamities.

We recommended that the Members of the Municipal Local School Board (a) prepare a balanced budget that is compliant with the legal and regulatory requirements on SEF budgeting; (b) refrain from accommodating expenditures on activities for which the DepEd has the direct mandate to execute; (c) request for inputs and feedbacks from the DepEd officials/School District Supervisors in the prioritization of SEF projects and expenditures and ensure that PPAs are in accordance with the DepEd criteria and the provisions of laws, rules and regulations prescribing the limitations on the use of the SEF; (d) maximize the utilization of SEF to attain its primary goal and objectives; (e) comply with the requirement of DILG Memorandum Circular 2012-141 on the posting of SEF utilization report 20 days after the end of each quarter for disclosure to the general public; and (f) monitor closely the implementation of the PPAs and take appropriate remedies, such as realignment or re-programming of funds, if necessary, thereby retaining in the LSB Budget only those relevant and priority activities in consultation with the school heads/project proponents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor (a) prioritize allocation of funds  for the effective implementation of the Ecological Solid Waste Management Plan especially on the construction of Materials Recovery Facility and conversion of its open dumpsite to long term disposal facility or sanitary landfill to ensure the health and safety of the people and the environment; and (b) coordinate with the Barangay Officials concerned in the implementation of MRF in all barangays within their jurisdiction in consonance with RA No. 9003.

We recommended that the (a) BAC ensure that procurement of infrastructure projects are made strictly in accordance with the requirements of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR; (b) OIC – Municipal Accountant ensure that all claims are duly supported with proper and complete documentary requirements before these are processed for payment to establish the regularity and legality of the disbursements; and (c) Municipal Engineer strictly monitor the implementation of projects to ensure their timely completion to enable early enjoyment of benefits by the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that concerned officials (a) ensure that the detailed engineering investigation, surveys and designs for the projects are carried out and duly approved in accordance with the standards and specifications prescribed by the HOPE and in accordance with Annex A of the Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 before bidding and award of the contract; and (b)refrain from issuing suspension orders just to cover up the real cause of slippage or negligence on the part of the Management or contractor.

We recommended that once the finances of the Municipality improved, Management cause the immediate remittance of the unutilized balance of PDAF totaling P12,085,531.60 to the Bureau of Treasury in strict compliance with the Supreme Court ruling.

We recommended for the settlement of audit suspension within 90 days from receipt of the Notice of Suspension, pursuant to Section 9.4 of the Rules and Regulations on Settlement of Accounts.  The head of the agency is responsible for ensuring that the requirements of transactions suspended in audit are complied with.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor exercise
his primordial responsibility to require  that  past  transactions  and
financial reports of calendar years 1994 to 2002  be  reported  to  the
Audit Team by the concerned officials.  The  Municipal  Accountant  and
the Municipal Treasurer must render once and for  all  the  actual  and
true status of financial accounts of the Municipality.   Moreover,  the
responsible officials should be held liable  under  the  Revised  Penal
Code for their failure to render the accounts and reporting thereof.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that:


a.  the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer  to  exert
   extra effort to account for the unreconciled items appearing in
   the Bank Reconciliation Statements to  faithfully  present  the
   correct balances of the account for the period;


b.   the   Municipal   Accountant   prepare   the   Monthly   Bank
   Reconciliation Statements for all bank accounts and submit same
   regularly together with the paid checks, debit/credit memos for
   verification as provided for under Section 74 of  PD  No.  1445
   and COA Circular No. 96-011. Continue securing bank  statements
   of prior years to establish the correct balance of the Cash  in
   Bank; and


c.   the Municipal Accountant prepare subsidiary  ledgers  of  all
   the bank accounts.

Based on the  foregoing  audit  observations,  we  reiterated  our
previous recommendation that the Municipal Mayor:


a.  require all accountable officers and employees to  immediately
   liquidate their outstanding cash advances giving more  emphasis
   to the long outstanding cash advances;


b.  resort to the withholding of  salaries  of  those  accountable
   officers and employees who still fail or persistently refuse to
   settle/liquidate their cash advances;


c. exert extra efforts to recover outstanding cash  advances  from
   Accountable Officers who are  no  longer  in  the  service  nor
   connected with the municipality;


d.  require strictly an Office Clearance from Money  and  Property
   accountabilities, pursuant to Sections 200 and 256, Vol. I  of
   the  Government  Accounting  and  Auditing  Manual   from   all
   personnel who seek retirement, resignation, transfer  or  claim
   for death benefits in order that all accountabilities could be
   appropriately settled;


e.  direct the  Municipal  Accountant  to  refrain  from  granting
   additional cash advances to any  official  or  employee  unless
   previous cash advance given to him is first settled or a proper
   accounting thereof is made, pursuant to Section  4.1.2  of  COA
   Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997; and


f.   require  the  Municipal  Accountant  to  maintain  Subsidiary
   Ledgers and schedules for the accounts used for  cash  advances
   to  facilitate  monitoring  of  the  amount   outstanding   per
   employee, to ensure that no additional  cash  advance  will  be
   granted to those with outstanding balances and to  support  the
   amounts reflected in the financial statements and  submit  same
   to the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:


a.  require  the  Inventory  Committee  to  conduct  the  complete
   physical count of  all  Inventories  and  Property,  Plant  and
   Equipment (PPE)  accounts  of  the  Municipality  so  that  the
   corresponding reports can be submitted to the Audit Team;


b. thereafter, instruct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile  the
   resulting balance of the physical  count  with  the  accounting
   records. In the  event  that  deficiencies/discrepancies  exist
   between  the  Inventory  Report  and  accounting  records,  the
   necessary adjustments/corrections should be made; and


c.  require  the  Municipal  Treasurer  to  make   a   report   of
   unserviceable/non-functional  PPEs  in   the   Inspection   and
   Inventory Report  of  Unserviceable  Properties  (IIRUP)  which
   could be undertaken simultaneously with the physical  inventory
   for disposal and eventual proper relief of accountabilities.

We  recommended  that  Management  strictly  observe  the  general
limitations on personal services cited under Section 325  (a)  of  R.A.
No. 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 98.  The Municipal Mayor, should
always  be  guided  by  the  conditions  of  the  agency’s  annual  and
supplemental budgets to ensure  that  the  budgeting  requirements  are
completely complied with. As part  of  his  fiscal  responsibility,  he
should allow  only  payments  of  personal  services  costs  and  other
expenditures expressly authorized by law.


The   municipality   should   exert   efforts   in   raising   its
revenues/receipts such as  increasing  tax  collection  efficiency  and
generating revenue related projects/activities to increase the base  of
PS limitation.

Considering the foregoing, we recommended that  the  BAC  strictly
observe proper procedures in all its procurement activities.   The  BAC
is also reminded to comply strictly with the requirements of  the  2016
Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 which has become  effective  last October
28, 2016 in their succeeding procurements.

We recommended that the BAC Secretariat, or other officer assigned
as the  case  may  be,  ensure  the  timely  submission  of  copies  of
government contracts and each of all the documents forming part thereof
within five (5) working days from execution and purchase orders  within
five (5) working days from issuance, pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-
001 dated February 12, 2009, to ensure timely review and evaluation  of
same, so that the management  could  be  immediately  notified  of  any
defects/deficiencies noted.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct
the Municipal Accountant to analyze the  debits  and  credits  to  said
reciprocal accounts and reconcile the  same  in  all  funds  to  fairly
present the balances of these accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare and  maintain
Subsidiary Ledgers in accordance with the NGAS Manual to enable  proper
analysis and evaluation of the balances of the accounts.

We  reiterated  our  previous  year’s  recommendation   that   the
Municipal Accountant give priority to  the  timely  submission  of  the
accounts and financial reports within the reglementary period to enable
the Audit  Team  to  conduct  timely  audit  and  promptly  inform  the
management of the deficiencies noted, if any.

We recommended that the  MDRRMO  prepare  and  submit  the  LDRRMF
Investment Plan in accordance with the format provided in  Annex  A  of
COA Circular No.  2012-002  to  reflect  the  allocation  for  disaster
mitigation, prevention and preparedness with details as to projects and
activities to be funded, as well as the list of projects and activities
charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years.

We  recommended  that  the  management   ensure   that   all
information required in the review of NSWMC be incorporated in the
plan and submit the same for final approval.  Section 46  of  R.A.
No. 9003 provides that LGUs are entitled to receive  funding  from
the Solid Waste Management Fund administered by the NSWMC  on  the
basis of their approved solid waste management plan.   We  further
recommend that the Municipal Officials concerned conduct  a  Waste
Analysis and Characterization Study or WACS.


We  recommended  that  the  Municipal  officials   concerned
effectively implement the  Municipal  Ordinance  on  proper  waste
collection and disposal to improve  and  attain  ecological  waste
management and adopt measures  to  mitigate  the  effects  of  the
current open dumpsite.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant  submit  the  lacking
documents to support the propriety and validity  of  the  disbursements
made and henceforth ensure that claims of the same nature be  supported
with complete documentation.

We recommended that:


 1.  the Municipal Treasurer:
    a. ensure timely deposit  of  collections  received  from  the
       collectors.
    b. instruct all collectors  to  prepare  RCDs  and  remit  all
       collections to the Municipal Treasurer in  accordance  with
       Section 29, Vol. 1 of the NGAS Manual.
    c. ensure correctness of figures presented on the face of  RCD
       and as much as possible avoid erasures.

    d. issue receipt for the transfer of cash withheld due to  BIR
       from Trust Fund to General Fund.


 2.   the Municipal Accountant ensure the accuracy of the amounts of
    recorded transactions and adjust the  balance  of  the  accounts
    affected by the miscalculated collections.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor allow only expenses  expressly
provided by the law to be charged against the SEF.

We recommended that the RPCPPE for inventory taking conducted in CY 2015 up to the last quarter of CY 2016 be prepared and updated up to December 31, 2016. The report shall be submitted to the Office of the Accountant for reconciliation in its records.  A copy shall be submitted to the Office of the Auditor for audit.

We recommended that the reconciliation of records on cash in bank account between the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer be conducted regularly at least quarterly to ensure that monthly balances of these accounts in both offices are reconciled with each other after adjustments. We also recommended that the proposed adjustments in the GL as shown in the tables in paragraph A and B be recorded in the books to correct the overstatement of cash in bank balance in the GF and the understatement in MEEDO Fund.  We further recommended that journal entry vouchers for adjustments made in the books be prepared and submitted to the office of the Auditor.

We recommended the following:
The accounting office communicate with the concerned depository banks the long outstanding bank reconciling items appearing in the Bank Reconciliation Statements for necessary adjustments in bank’s records.
Ensure that the balances reflected in the BRS specifically the unadjusted balance per books be reconciled with the GL and respective SL.
The accounting office revalidate all reconciling items specifically those that are long outstanding including errors in recording and returned checks.

We recommended that charges out of the BuB fund for wages of job order physicians/doctors be stopped.  Ensure exclusive use of the fund only for projects that partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures as enumerated under Section 3 of DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated Apri1 13, 2011.

We recommended that the Municipal Government through concerned department heads ensure that provisions on the period of implementation of BuB projects be provided in each project proposal and in the Memorandum of Agreement so that timeliness in projects implementation can be properly monitored.

We recommended the following:
The Municipal Accountant demand from NGOs/POs the unliquidated fund transfers as of December 31, 2016 through submission of the Fund Utilization Report together with the required supporting documents for proper evaluation and recording in the books;
The Municipal Government revisit and review the Memorandum of Agreement entered into with various NGOs/POs and provide provisions on the following:
Time schedule for release of funds, periodic inspection/evaluation, reporting requirements, date of commencement of the project/activity and date of completion
Specific period to liquidate the fund granted to NGO/PO
Visitorial audit by the officials and personnel of the Commission on Audit (COA) authorized to perform the audit under approved office order
Maintenance by the NGO/PO of separate savings account for each fund received from the Municipal Government
Institution of legal action by the Municipal Government against defaulting NGO/PO which fails to complete a project/activity covered by the MOA and its subsequent disqualification from applying for another project in any other government office.
c. The Municipal Accountant issue a certification stating that the previous fund releases to the NGO/PO has been liquidated for attachment to the disbursement on fund transfers to NGOs/POs; and
d. Submit liquidation or fund utilization reports with complete supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor.

We reiterated our recommendation that management comply with COA rules and regulations on the submission of accounts and financial reports to the Office of the Auditor for timely audit of transactions.

We recommended that complete supporting documents required under COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 be submitted together with the approved/perfected contract to the COA Auditor.

We recommended that the Municipal Government consider the following:
Establish separate and specific policies and procedures or guidelines on the use/operation of the Emergency Medical Service vehicles.  The guideline shall provide among others the following provisions to ensure smooth implementation of the program and avoid abusive use of vehicles:
Types of emergency cases covered
Scope of services of EMS
Areas covered
Duties and Responsibilities of MDRRMO on supervision of EMS
Qualifications in hiring of personnel to be assigned in the field
Duties and Responsibilities of assigned personnel
Responsibility of persons transported
Payment of services
Limitations on the use of EMS
Limitations on services
Authority on the use of EMS, and;
Administrative sanctions
Centralize filing/safekeeping of records generated by each team in every operation.

We recommended that the Municipal Government include in its DRRM Plan the construction of adequate drainage system, riprapping of creeks or rivers and other infrastructures projects that would maximize the prevention and mitigation of the direct impacts of calamity and reduce vulnerabilities and exposure of communities to all hazards.

We recommended that the Municipal Government consider the following:
Full implementation of the projects or activities of SWM regarding the capacity building of households/residential, commercial, industrial, institutional and other establishments in terms of wastes segregation and disposal of wastes in the temporary designated area; and
Conduct of training/seminar to educate the residents on proper wastes segregation in the households, commercial and other establishments, to enhance responsibility and importance of SWM in the attainment of the municipality’s objective of maintaining a clean environment.

We recommended that taxes withheld from salaries and wages of employees and the value added taxes, and expanded withholding taxes from suppliers and contractors be remitted in full to the BIR to avoid fines, penalties and surcharges that the concerned agency may impose.

We recommended that the withheld 9% Personal Share (PS) from the basic salary and loan repayments of its officials and employees together with the 12% Government Share (GS) be remitted in full to GSIS to avoid fines, penalties and surcharges that the concerned agency may impose.
We reiterated our recommendation that management cause the immediate settlement of unsettled suspensions and disallowances.

We recommend that management exert extra effort to complete the
conduct of the physical count of its property, plant and equipment and prepare
a complete and detailed Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and
Equipment, in accordance with Section 156 of COA Circular No. 92-386.

We recommend that management stop allocating the 20% Development
Fund for programs/projects/activities which do not partake the nature of investment
and capital expenditures as provided for in DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum No.
2011 -1 dated April 13, 2011. Likewise, development projects included in the
Annual Investment Plan should be implemented immediately to provide intended
beneficiaries of its benefits thereby optimizing the desired socio-economic
development and environmental management outcomes.

We recommend that the Municipal Accountant maintain a separate book of
accounts for the PhilHealth Capitation Fund and ensure that the fund is
strictly utilized for the purpose and properly documented. All payrolls should be
paid through a cash advance of the Municipal Treasurer and all procurement
should be in accordance with RA 9184.

We recommend that management revisit the stipulations in the
