Memorandum of Agreement, Trust Agreement or Memorandum of
Understanding between the LGU and source agency to properly identify the
required/applicable mode of disposition of the unutilized balances of funds
transferred and require the Accountant to immediately comply as stated.

We recommend that the Municipality create a Local Solid Waste
Management Board that will formulate a Solid Waste Management Plan to
provide an efficient and effective system of solid waste collection and disposal
within its area of jurisdiction.

We recommend that management create the mandatory DRRM
positions to complement the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management
Office (LDRRMO) and fill up the position in compliance with the provision
of Section 12 of Republic Act No. 10121 or the Philippine Disaster Risk
Reduction and Management Act of 2010 to carry out the setting of the
direction, development, implementation and coordination of disaster risk
management program.

We recommend that the Local School Board allocate the Special
Education Fund to program/project/activity specifically enumerated in
Section 271 and 100 of Republic Act 7160 and Section 7 (b) of RA No. 10410
to efficiently utilize the funds derived from the share on the proceeds of the
additional 1% real property tax. Moreover, strict compliance to the
reportorial requirements should be made in accordance with the
requirement of DECS-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 01-A
dated March 14, 2000.

We recommend that the Municipal Budget Officer being the Chairman of
the BAC be required to submit copies of approved contracts/purchase order as
provided for in COA Circular No. 2009-001 and comply strictly with the
provisions of Section 4(6) of PD 1445 and RA 9184, its IRR and the relevant
documents required in COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive direct the treasury
personnel to conduct intensive tax campaign to invite more taxpayers to pay
their current and delinquent real property taxes. We also recommend that the
Municipal Treasurer institute legal remedies if necessary as provided in Sections
247 and 256 of RA 7160 to compel taxpayers to pay their taxes.

1.5   Require the employees concerned to conduct regularly the reconciliation of PPE balances on both records of the Municipal Accountant and the Property Custodian. If there is a difference identified between the records, the same shall be adjusted in the books of accounts and/or reflected in the RPCPPE.

2.6   Instruct the Municipal Assessor to register all land owned by the Municipality under the Torrens Title System in conformity with COA Circular No. 92-386 and submit tax declarations which are duly signed by the Provincial Assessor to erase the doubts as to the genuineness of those documents.
2.7  Likewise, reconciliation between the records of the municipal accountant and the municipal assessor should be regularly made to accurately establish the valuation of land in the book of accounts of the Municipality.

3.5  Instruct the municipal accountant to prepare an adjusting entry provided below:
Account Title			      Dr		   Cr
Land 					16,300,000.00
Investment Property, Land 		     16,300,000.00

5.6  Require Management to dispose immediately the six (6) unserviceable vehicles in conformity with Section 79 of PD 1445 by following the guidelines of Section 503 of GAAM Vol. I.
5.7  Likewise, other unserviceable properties aside from those vehicles be disposed of properly also.
6.5   Instruct the MPDC to construct a ground water monitoring well system in the appropriate locations within the sanitary landfill to continuously monitor the quality of ground water.

7.6  Instruct the Municipal Engineer to submit to the Office of Auditor copies of suspension orders duly supported with the required documents for its immediate determination if the grounds for suspension are allowable or not.

8.3  Require Management to stop the practice of charging to the 20% development fund programs and projects that were not geared towards socio-economic and environmental outcomes.
8.4   Moreover, written explanation should be submitted to the Office of the Auditor relative to the above violation for us to make a decision in audit.

9.5  Instruct the MSWDO to transfer to another storage the relief goods, various materials and supplies stored in the said Women and Children Crisis Center and open the building to the public to cater women and children who are victims of abuses and suffering from different crises.

We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Treasurer require the Disbursing Officer to submit the lacking supporting documents and henceforth, strictly comply with the documentary requirements in accordance with Section 1.1 of CO Circular No. 2012-01.

We recommended that management utilize its LDRRMF strictly in accordance with the provisions of Republic Act No. 10121 and NDRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013. We also recommended that management should be keen in processing procurement documents to avoid errors.

We recommended and management agreed to refrain from charging expenses for entertainment purposes, specifically those which relate to the holding of beauty pageants, against government funds pursuant to Section 343 of R.A. 7160.

We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Council for the Protection of Children (MCPC) should focus and prioritize on the formulation and implementation of the PPAs of the LCPC in accordance with Section 15 of Republic Act No. 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012.

We recommended that management, henceforth, strictly adhere to the provisions of RA 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012 on the use of the 1% of IRA in the implementation of PPAs of the LCPC.

We recommended that Management require the Inventory Committee to submit a complete physical inventory report of all PPE of the Municipality classified by fund/class/type in accordance with the NGAS Manual as of December 31, 2016 and every year-end thereafter. We likewise recommend that Management direct the Accounting and the Inventory Committee of the Municipality to reconcile their records, in accordance with the aforementioned regulations.

‘We recommended that Management: (a) direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately prepare and submit the required monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) for the 12 bank accounts of the Municipality as of December 31, 2016; (b) require that the personnel assigned to prepare the BRS shall not be given other duties other than the preparation of BRS, to enable him/her to finish the assigned task promptly; and (3) comply with the requirements of Treasury Circular 01-2017 and Department Circular No.001-2015 to maintain account with PNB and PPSB otherwise close the accounts and transfer them to AGDB such as Land bank of the Philippines (LBP) or Development Bank of the Philippines (DBP).

We recommended that Management require:

(a.) The Municipal Treasurer deposit immediately to the authorized depository bank all her undeposited collections as of December 31, 2016 - and strictly comply with Section 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382;

(b.) reconcile the records of the Municipal Treasurer with that of the Accounting Section from date of assumption as Municipal Treasurer up to December 31, 2016, to determine the correct amount of the undeposited collections; and

(c) institute legal remedies to collect the undeposited collections from the three (3) accountable officers, namely: M.O. Millares, F.L. Bombase and Marieta V. Olivares.

We recommended that Management:

{a) require the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board (MSWMB) to make a periodic review and re-evaluate the Ten-Year Municipal Solid Waste Management Plan as to its sustainability, viability, effectiveness and relevance in relation to local and international developments in the field of solid waste management;

(b) direct the MSWMB to coordinate and assist all barangays in solid waste management, such as waste segregation, sorting, recovery, recycling and composting at their respective Material Recovery Facility (MRF); and

(c) provide suitable storage metal containers with cover for biodegradable, recyclable and residual wastes in strategic places of residential areas, schools, commercial and market site to facilitate the collection of solid wastes by the garbage service trucks.
We recommend that Management facilitate and speed up the implementation of the projects to hasten the poverty alleviation problems of the constituents.
We recommend that Management direct the Local School Board te ensure that programs and projects under the SEF are implemented during the year.

We recommended that LGU strictly comply with COA Circular No. 2005-002 in the management of small tangible items with estimated useful life of more than one year and with a unit cost of below ₱10,000.00.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2015 | The concerned agency officials agreed with our audit recommendations

The Accounting Office agreed to reclassify the account and effect the necessary adjustment.
observations.reason: Some inventory records of offices were revisited and reclassified small tangible items from the PPEs to expense accounts.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LGU strictly adhere with the provisions of the NGAS Manual in the accounting of government properties.
observations.managementAction:
The Accounting Office agreed to exert its best effort to reconcile the PPE accounts.

SL will be updated to facilitate periodic reconciliation of PPE reports with the books of accounts.
observations.reason: Records are being updated. Reiterated in Observation No. 1 of the 2016 AAR.

observations.managementAction: SL will be updated to facilitate periodic reconciliation of PPE reports with the books of accounts.
observations.reason: SL is being updated in compliance with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:

(a) Treasurer make a thorough assessment of the actual income collected for CYs 2011 to 2015 and re-compute the actual cash to be transferred from the General Fund to the Trust Fund for the fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements; 

(b) Local Chief Executive (i) require that the balances of the unutilized QRF and DRRMF-MOOE be transferred to the special trust fund account and (ii) ensure that the QRF and DRRMF-MOOE are used only for the intended purpose; and
 
(c) Accountant maintain a subsidiary record for the Trust Liability-DRRMF account by source and by year upon  transfer of cash.
observations.managementAction:
The balances were transferred to a special trust fund account and assured the Audit Team that the fund will be utilized only for the intended purpose.

Subsidiary ledgers will be updated by the accounting office by year and sources.
observations.reason: Subsidiary records are not available as basis for computing actual receipt and utilization of fund, thus, partial transfer only was made on the Trust Fund account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City officials exert extra effort to intensify collection of delinquent accounts and adopt all necessary measures to enable the collections of unpaid stall rentals and local taxes.  These will provide additional funds to support the projected expenditure of the City which may be beneficial to its constituents.
observations.managementAction: The agency assured the Audit Team that utmost effort will be exerted to lessen delinquent account.
observations.reason: There was a decrease in delinquent accounts of stall markets.

observations.managementAction:
Observatio
n No. 1
AAR
CY-2015

The Accountable Officers with outstanding cash advance were not on time submitting their supporting documents within the time frame prescribe by the COA and/or in some cases the Accountable Officers were not preparing their liquidation report and shall be submitted soon to the Accounting Officer for Journal Entry Voucher.

For the CY 2016, cash advances granted to Officials and Employees are now being liquidated on time, Journal Entry Voucher and Liquidation Report attached with supporting documents.

Observatio
n No. 7
AAR
CY-2015

The amount of P441,168.33 representing the shares of the barangays from the real property tax collected by the agency in CY 2015 was not fully remitted. Of the amount, P333,448.57 was remitted very late by the Municipal Treasurer because of the unclear division of responsibility between the Accounting and Treasury Offices, thus deprived the barangays of the opportunity to augment early its depleting cash to fund significant projects or programs that were pipelined during the year, contrary to Section 271 of the R.A. No. 7160, otherwise known as the Local Government Code of 1991.

Observatio
n No. 8
AAR
CY-2015

Transportation allowance amounting to P96,000.00 was paid to two (2) members of the Sangguniang Bayan of the Municipality of San Jose despite that government service vehicles were assigned to them, in violation of Section 8.2.1 of the Department of Budget and Management (DBM) Local Budget Circular (LBC) No. 103 dated May 15, 2013 and Section 56 of the General Provisions of the General Appropriations Act (GAA) for CY 2015.

We recommended that: a. the concerned members of the Sangguniang Bayan immediately return to the Municipal Treasurer the combined transportation allowances of P96,000.00 erroneously paid to them; and b. the Municipal Budget Officer, Accountant and Treasurer immediately stop the obligation and payment of transportation allowance to officials of the agency who are at the same time provided with government service vehicle.

Observatio
n No. 9
AAR
CY-2015

Common-use office supplies worth P581,336.85 for the different offices of the agency were procured from various local suppliers instead of availing them from the Procurement Service of the Department of Budget and Management (PS-DBM), contrary to Section 21 of the General Provisions of the General Appropriations Act (GAA) for Fiscal Year 2015.

We recommended that the MPDO and the Bids and Award Committee (BAC) comply with the rules requiring the availment of the services of the PS-DBM in the procurement of the office supplies requirement of the various offices of the LGU. We likewise recommended that the MPDO require the concerned staff of the agency’s various offices to monitor the three-month supply requirement of their respective offices and immediately request for replenishment when the stocks fall below the minimum threshold so that occurrence of emergency procurement is minimized, if not avoided.

Observatio
n No. 11
AAR
CY-2015

The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer were late in the submission of disbursement vouchers, payrolls, collection reports, official receipts and monthly trial balances due to delay in completing the required supporting documents/schedules, contrary to Section 7.2.1 (a) of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009 and Sections 70 and 72, Volume I of the Manual on the NGAS for Local Government Units (LGUs), thus hindered the conduct of the post-audit and the timely communication to the Management of audit deficiencies.

Observatio
n No. 12
AAR
CY-2015

Eleven (11) infrastructure projects costing P19,407,599.00 funded by the twenty percent (20%) Local Development Fund (LDF) and Grassroot Participatory Planning Budget (GPPB) were found existing and useful. However, the disbursement vouchers (DVs) for the payment of said infrastructure projects were not supported with complete documentation and signatures of concerned officials were not affixed thereto, contrary to Section 4 of Presidential Decree (P.D.) No. 1445, thereby exposed the disbursement transactions to possible issuance of audit suspension. Moreover, the Municipal Mayor failed to notify the Audit Team of the posting of project billboards at the project sites and the project billboards posted did not fully contain the information required in COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013, thus rendered the concerned agency officials susceptible to administrative action and the related billboard expenses subject to possible audit disallowance.

Observatio
n No. 13
AAR
CY-2015

The 20 percent (20%) local development fund (LDF) of the agency for CY 2015 amounting to P31,991,765.06 was under spent as only 49 percent (49%) thereof, or P15,647,888.89, was utilized to fund at least 13 completed and 11 on-going social, economic and environmental projects. Moreover, six (6) projects with total project cost of P3,028,500.00 were funded by the LDF, contrary to Section 2 of the DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011.

Observatio
n No. 14
AAR
CY-2015

The agency’s Gender and Development (GAD) Accomplishment Report for CY 2015 contained inadequate, incoherent and inaccurate information which were not timely supplied or corrected because of the lack of coordination among members of the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS), thus the report generated was not reflective of the real achievements of the agency. Moreover, there was no system installed for the effective monitoring of budget utilization and evaluation of the implementation, impact, and sustainability of GAD-related activities, contrary to the relevant provisions enshrined in the Republic Act (R.A.) No. 9710, otherwise known as the Magna Carta of Women (MCW) and Joint Circular No. 2012-01 of the Philippine Commission on Women (PCW), the National Economic and Development Authority (NEDA), and the DBM.

Observatio
n No. 15
AAR
CY-2015

Information about the implementation of the Agro-Processing Facilities and Expanded Agri-Business Activities (Milking Carabao) Project was not widely disseminated in the Municipality of San Jose, hence farmers who might be as willing and capable as those benefited agency officials’ relatives and caretakers to take care of the twenty (20) Bulgarian milking carabaos procured by the agency for P1,000.000.00 were not all considered in the selection process, thus defeated the purpose of the Bottom-up Budgeting (BUB) Program of the national government.

Observatio
n No. 16
AAR
CY-2015

The unutilized balance of Priority Development Assistance Fund (PDAF) and Development Acceleration Program (DAP) Fund amounting to P258,815.00 remained in the book of accounts of the agency as at December 31, 2015, contrary to the directive of the COA requiring the return of said unutilized balances to the National Treasury for reversion to the unappropriated surplus of the general fund of the national government.

Observatio
n No. 17
AAR
CY-2015

The over-all cleanliness of the Municipality of San Jose was maintained and the cooperation of the barangays in the management of solid wastes generated at source was regularly monitored through open communication, active information dissemination, and assistance in the establishment of Materials Recovery Facility (MRF). However, other salient provisions of the Republic Act (R.A.) No. 9003, or the Philippine Ecological Solid Waste Management Act of 2000, specifically those pertaining to the completion of local government Solid Waste Management Plan (SWMP) and ensuring compliance with the minimum requirements for effective handling of solid wastes were not given close attention because of the other priorities of the agency, thus impeded the full realization and enjoyment of benefits expected to be derived from the efficient implementation of the ecological solid waste management system in the municipality.

Observatio
n No. 18
AAR
CY-2015

The Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Office (LDRRMO), a mandatory office under Section 12 of the Republic Act (R.A.) No. 10121, also known as the Philippine Disaster Risk Reduction and Management (PDRRM) Act of 2010, was not established in the Municipality of San Jose despite pronouncement made on April 4, 2014 through the Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1 issued by the National Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (NDRRMC), DILG, DBM and CSC requiring every local government unit to create said office, thus there was no lead group working full time on the disaster risk reduction campaign of the agency which resulted in the failure of the agency to fully maximize the calamity fund appropriated during the year.

Observatio
n No. 20
AAR
CY-2015

The funds appropriated for the Special Education Fund (SEF) in CY 2015 totaling P815,490.20 were not efficiently utilized because only four (4) of the nine (9) programs/activities/projects were carried out at the cost of P219,021.50 of the total appropriation, leaving an unexpended balance of P596,468.70 as at December 31, 2015, thus the objectives of the projects/activities programmed for the year were not fully achieved.

Observatio
n No. 21
AAR
CY-2015

The agency regularly paid its loan repayment with the Local Government Finance and Development Office (LOGOFIND) of the Municipal Development Fund Office (MDFO) as resources in CY 2015 were adequate to cover the annual amortization of P482,005.66. Moreover, the debt service ceiling of 20 percent (20%) of the annual regular income was observed in compliance with Section 324 of the Local Government Code of 1991. However, while the debt service ceiling was satisfied, the appropriation for the agency’s debt service of P450,000.00 was not sufficient to cover the annual loan amortization.

Observatio
n No. 23
AAR
CY-2015

Applicable Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards (PPSAS) was not applied and proper classification of accounts was overlooked, thus required further adjustments in the financial statements of the agency.

Observatio
n No. 24
AAR
CY-2015

Except in one occasion, the Municipal Treasurer remitted the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS) premiums and loan amortizations withheld in CY 2015 within the timeline prescribed under Section 14 of the implementing rules and regulations (IRR) of the Republic Act (R.A.) No. 8291, otherwise known as the GSIS Act of 1997. However, an over-remittance of P34,998.41 was recorded at year-end because of the accommodated requests of various employees to apply other loan deductions as additional loan payment with the GSIS.

Observatio
n No. 25
AAR
CY-2015

Income tax for benefits paid in excess of the P82,000.00 ceiling for the 13th month pay and other benefits excluded from the computation of gross income for purposes of income taxation was not withheld from the salary of 23 agency personnel and consequently remitted to the Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) in violation of BIR Revenue Regulation No. 3-2015 dated March 9, 2015.

Observatio
n No. 26
AAR
CY-2015

The non-reconciliation of records between the Accounting and Treasury Offices due to lack of constant communication of concerned personnel assigned to tally the amount of taxes withheld for a given period resulted in the late and under-remittance of P469,889.13, contrary to Revenue Memorandum Order No. 23-2014 dated June 20, 2014 and Revenue Regulation No. 2-98 dated April 17, 1998.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended   
that the         
Municipal Mayor  
direct the       
Municipal        
Treasurer to     
prepare a yearly 
report of tax    
delinquencies and
provide copy to   
the Municipal    
Accountant and

and audit        
purposes.        
We also enjoined 
the Municipal    
Accountant to

entry voucher    
(JEV) for the    
difference that  
will be noted    
during

observations.managementAction:
Upon assumption to office of the    
newly elected Municipal Mayor, he   
called the attention of the         
Municipal Treasurer regarding the   
AOM. In response, the Municipal     
Treasurer stated that they have     
complied with the recommendation and
submitted to the Accounting Office  
the Real Property Tax Delinquencies 
as of December 31, 2015. The        
Statements of Account sent to       
taxpayers which started on August   
15, 2016 were based on the herein   
aforementioned report.              
Upon submission of the report on    
Real Property Tax Delinquencies as  
of December 31, 2015 of the         
Municipal Treasurer, the Municipal  
Accountant made the corresponding   
JEV No. 100-16-07-925a dated July 1,
2016 and JEV No. 200-16-07-169 dated
July 18, 2016 on the General Fund   
and Special Education Fund books of 
accounts, respectively, amounting to
P7,747,365.48 on each fund for the  
set-up of the tax delinquencies for 
CY 2013 and below.

observations.recommendation:

that the         
Municipal Mayor  
instruct the BPLO
to require all

submit the       
audited financial
statements       
together with the
books of accounts
and other

and information  
of the preceding 
year for the

Maintain

observations.managementAction:
Upon assumption to office of the    
newly elected Municipal Mayor, he   
immediately called a meeting for the
members of the Joint Inspection Team
(JIT) and discussed the compliance  
for this AOM. The BPLO concerned    
expressed her willingness to comply 
with the recommendations. Letters   
were sent to taxpayers informing    
them of their scheduled payment of  
business tax along with the         
instruction to bring the necessary 
documents including the ITR/ 1701Q 
for 1st-3rd Quarter 2016 to serve as
basis for the computation of        
business tax.
observations.reason:
Full implementation was made during the
processing of the application/renewal of business
permits on January 2017. Sampled application for
business permit showed compliance with the
recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended   
that the         
Municipal Mayor  
direct the DRRM  
Office to conduct
survey to

Municipality     
which will

result thereof   
shall be

the formulation  
of LDRRMFIP.
observations.managementAction:
Upon assumption to office of the    
newly elected Municipal Mayor, he   
instructed the LDRRMO II to         
immediately prepare the LDRRMFIP for
2017 (General Fund and Special Trust
Fund) and convene the MDDRMC for

We recommended   
that the         
Municipal Mayor  
instruct the     
Municipal        
Treasurer/OIC-GSO
to initiate the  
immediate        
disposal of those
unserviceable    
properties in

168 of the

92-386 dated

observations.managementAction:
Upon assumption to office of the    
newly elected Municipal Mayor, he   
called the attention of the Supply  
Officer/OIC-GSO to address the AOM.
The Supply Officer/OIC-GSO replied  
that they are about to finish       
consolidating all the unserviceable
properties of the municipality and  
placed in a single storage area.    
Considering that a single spacious  
storage area was not available      
before, most of the unserviceable   
properties were located in various  
offices and some other areas in the 
Municipal Hall. All of these        
unserviceable properties are now    
being transferred in the Eco Park at
Brgy. Banaybanay 1, this            
municipality upon instruction of the
